It stores pre-aggregated metrics (orders, quantity, gross/discount/tax/net sales) per year and customer_type and appears to be an ETL-produced snapshot (created_at). Its role is to provide fast, read-optimized answers to high-level questions about yearly B2B sales and to serve as a reconciliation / rollup layer against more granular tables (monthly/daily/orders).
b2b_sales_yearly.key_characteristics: This is an aggregated, small-cardinality table with year as the primary key. In the provided sample it contains a single row (2024) and all numeric measures are decimals with four fractional digits. customer_type has low cardinality (e.g., 'HORECA' in sample). Columns are nullable except year. created_at is an ETL timestamp (datetime2). The table is best used for fast reads and reporting rather than transactional queries. Expect one row per year (or per (year, customer_type) if multiple customer types are present in other data).
b2b_sales_yearly.analysis_capabilities: Enables year-level reporting and KPI retrieval (total orders, shipped quantity, gross/discount/tax/net sales), trend comparisons across years (when multiple years exist), quick KPI lookups for dashboards, and reconciliation against monthly/daily/product-level aggregates. Can be used to compute year-over-year change, percentage contributions by customer_type (if multiple types exist), and simple KPI ratios (e.g., discount as % of gross, tax rates).
business_questions: What were the total net sales and gross sales for B2B customers in year X?
How many B2B orders and how much quantity (kg) were sold in year X for a given customer_type?
What is the discount amount and discount percentage relative to gross sales for the year?
How did net sales change year-over-year (requires multiple rows/years)?
When was this yearly sales snapshot generated (ETL run time)?
b2b_sales_yearly.relationships: No explicit foreign keys, but logical joins are common: join on year (and customer_type if populated) to b2b_sales_monthly / b2b_sales_daily for drilldown and reconciliation; join to b2b_orders, b2b_order_lines, b2b_product_sales, and b2b_customers via year derived from order dates to validate and attribute totals; join to ecom / pos sales_yearly tables for cross-channel comparisons using year and customer_type (or channel mappings). These joins enable drill-down (year -> month -> day -> orders -> lines), validation of aggregated metrics, and cross-channel performance comparisons.

b2b_shipment_tracking_events.purpose: Stores individual shipment tracking events for B2B dispatches — timestamped location/status updates (picked, loaded, in_transit, out_for_delivery, delivered) for dispatches. Used to reconstruct shipment timelines, audit scans, and link location/device/actor information to a dispatch.
b2b_shipment_tracking_events.key_characteristics: Small table sample (50 rows). tracking_event_id and dispatch_id are nvarchar and currently nullable; tracking_event_id appears unique in this snapshot. No declared primary key. dispatch_id is a foreign key to zuna_etl.b2b_dispatches.dispatch_id. event_type is low-cardinality (5 values) and good for grouping. event_ts and created_at are datetime2 and capture event and record creation times. gps_lat/gps_long are decimal(18,4) with full coverage (gps_long has 49 distinct values). scan_photo_url and status_note are numeric typed (decimal) but have 100% nulls — likely schema/type issues. Many textual columns (scanned_by, location_name, device_id) are high-cardinality. Small dataset but pattern shows frequent distinct tracking_event_id and dispatch_id.
b2b_shipment_tracking_events.analysis_capabilities: Can reconstruct per-dispatch event timelines (ordered by event_ts), compute event counts by type, compute time differences between key events (e.g., LOADED -> DELIVERED), identify last-known GPS per dispatch, aggregate by location_name or scanned_by, analyze device usage, and produce simple delivery performance metrics. Cannot by itself determine order-level details without joining dispatches (and orders), but can be joined to enrich dispatch-level analytics.
business_questions: What is the average time from LOADED to DELIVERED per dispatch or per route?
Which dispatches are currently OUT_FOR_DELIVERY or IN_TRANSIT and what are their last-known GPS coordinates?
Which scanners (scanned_by) or devices generate the most events and are there anomalies (many events in short time)?
How many events of each event_type occur per day/week and are there location hotspots (location_name) with more delays?
For a given dispatch_id, what is the ordered timeline of events and the timestamp of the last event?
b2b_shipment_tracking_events.relationships: Primary relationship is dispatch_id -> zuna_etl.b2b_dispatches.dispatch_id which allows enriching events with dispatch metadata (order_id, customer, scheduled delivery window, route/driver). Joining to dispatches enables linking event timelines to order/customer KPIs, costs, and SLA checks. device_id can be joined to device registries if available in other tables. scanned_by could be joined to employee/user tables (not listed here) for operator-level metrics.

b2b_vendor_partners.purpose: Master list of B2B vendor partner records. Stores vendor identifiers and contact/metadata used by procurement, logistics, billing and partner-management processes across the B2B ecosystem.
b2b_vendor_partners.key_characteristics: Small table (45 rows). vendor_id and vendor_name are unique per row. service_type and credit_terms are low-cardinality categorical fields (5 and 3 distinct values respectively). city has moderate low-cardinality (9). gst_no appears unique per vendor and stored as bigint. created_at has a single distinct value (all rows share same timestamp), likely loaded/updated at once. Contact fields contain free-form text and some punctuation/formatting inconsistencies (emails contain commas in sample). Data types are nvarchar for textual fields and datetime2 for created_at.
b2b_vendor_partners.analysis_capabilities: Can be used as a dimension table for vendor-level slicing: counts of vendors by service_type, city, credit_terms; join-key for combining vendor metadata with transactional tables (orders, invoices, returns, shipments) to analyze vendor performance, billing/days-payable, logistics distribution and service partner mixes. Also supports contact lookups and basic data quality checks (missing gst_no, malformed emails/phones).
business_questions: How many vendors do we have by service type (Transport, Logistics, Quality Inspection, etc.)?
Which cities have the highest concentration of vendor partners?
How many vendors are on each credit_terms bucket (NET 7/15/30)?
Which vendors (vendor_id/vendor_name) provide a given service_type or operate in a given city?
Are there vendors missing GST numbers or contact details that need remediation?
b2b_vendor_partners.relationships: vendor_id is the primary key and should serve as the join key to transactional tables (orders, invoices, contracts, returns, shipments) that reference vendors. There are no declared foreign keys here, but related tables in the schema (b2b_orders, b2b_invoices, b2b_products, etc.) typically have vendor_id or vendor references allowing enrichment of transactions with vendor attributes (service_type, city, credit_terms). This table functions as a vendor dimension for NL2SQL queries and analytical joins.

delivery_slots.purpose: Represents delivery time slots available for order deliveries. Each row describes a unique slot (slot_id) for a specific date, start/end times, capacity and remaining availability for orders, associated with a fulfilment centre and the timestamp when the row was created. Used to manage and schedule order deliveries and to report utilization of delivery capacity.
delivery_slots.key_characteristics: Small table (80 rows). slot_id is unique (primary key). date has moderate cardinality (15 distinct values) with many rows concentrated on a few dates. start_time and end_time are stored as nvarchar but represent times with ~5 distinct values (few time windows). centre_id has low cardinality (3 centres). capacity_orders and available_orders are numeric (bigint) with varied values and some NULLs possible. created_at is unique per row (80 distinct) and useful for incremental loads and auditing.
delivery_slots.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: compute capacity and utilization by date/centre/time window; list available slots; detect fully booked or under-utilized slots; report historical slot creation activity; compare capacity distribution across centres and dates. You cannot attribute slots to specific orders or customers without joining order/dispatch tables.
business_questions: Which delivery slots are available for a given date and centre?
What is the utilization (filled orders) per slot / per centre / per date?
Which centres have the most capacity on a given day or time window?
What time windows are most frequently fully booked or under-utilized?
What are the most recent changes to delivery slot definitions (new/updated slots)?
delivery_slots.relationships: No explicit foreign keys exist. centre_id likely corresponds to a centre/store table (e.g., pos_stores, pos_centres or similar) in the schema; slot_id could be referenced by scheduling, dispatch, or order allocation tables (b2b_dispatches, b2b_order_allocations, shipments, etc.) even if not enforced. Joining by centre_id and by slot_id (if present in other tables) enables linking slots to orders, dispatches, shipments and capacity planning dashboards.

delivery_status.purpose: Event-level delivery status log for shipments and orders. Each row records a delivery-related event (timestamped) including state (event_type/status), location, GPS coordinates and the operator who updated it. It is used to track the lifecycle of deliveries and to join delivery events back to orders/shipments for operational reporting and troubleshooting.
delivery_status.key_characteristics: Small table (~232 rows) with event-level granularity. event_ts is unique per row (232 distinct). event_type has low cardinality (~6 values) and status has 3 values. location has 4 distinct cities. updated_by is high-cardinality (~231 distinct) indicating mostly unique operator names. gps_lat/gps_long are nearly fully populated decimals. photo_url and notes are mis-typed as decimal and are 100% NULL (data-quality issue). delivery_id/shipment_id/order_id are strings with moderate distinct counts (~100 each) and allow NULLs. No primary key defined and one FK: order_id -> zuna_etl.ecom_orders.order_id.
delivery_status.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports time-series and event-sequence analyses (e.g., time to delivery, event frequency by type or location, operator activity), geospatial point mapping (using gps_lat/gps_long), status-based aggregations (counts of FAILED/DELIVERED), and identifying problematic shipments/orders by event patterns. It can also be joined to orders/shipments/customers for customer-level metrics or revenue impact.
business_questions: How many delivery attempts and what proportion failed vs delivered over a given period?
What is the average time from LOADED to DELIVERED (or OUT_FOR_DELIVERY to DELIVERED) per city or per route?
Which couriers/operators (updated_by) have the highest failure rates or longest resolution times?
Which orders/shipments experienced FAILED_ATTEMPT events and what subsequent events occurred?
Where (gps coordinates or city) are delivery failures concentrated?
delivery_status.relationships: Primary relationship is order_id -> zuna_etl.ecom_orders.order_id; this enables joining to orders for customer, product and financial context. shipment_id/delivery_id can be joined to shipment/dispatch tables if present (e.g., shipments, b2b_dispatches) to connect to routing, carrier and planned delivery windows. These joins enable end-to-end delivery analytics (time-to-fulfillment, cost allocation, customer impact).

ecom_ab_test.purpose: Holds meta-data and results for e-commerce A/B tests (experiments). Each row records one experiment's identity, variants, timing, status, winner and summary conversion metrics. Acts as the canonical experiment registry for analytics and product decisions.
ecom_ab_test.key_characteristics: Small table (80 rows). test_id is unique primary key (high cardinality). test_name has low cardinality (3 values). variant_a/variant_b are categorical with only 'Control' and 'Treatment' in the sample. start_ts and end_ts are stored as nvarchar(50) (19-char timestamp strings) and should be cast to datetime for temporal analysis. created_at is a datetime2. status and winner_variant are low-cardinality categorical columns (RUNNING/CANCELLED/COMPLETED; NONE/A/B). conversion_rate_a and conversion_rate_b are decimal(18,4) and have many distinct values (good for numeric comparisons). Some nulls exist for optional fields.
ecom_ab_test.analysis_capabilities: By itself this table can: count experiments by status; compare aggregate conversion metrics (mean/median) for A vs B across experiments; identify winners distribution and frequency; compute test durations after casting start/end timestamps; filter experiments by date ranges and status; produce trend analyses of experiment launches (using created_at). It cannot attribute per-user or per-order effects without join to assignment or orders tables.
business_questions: How many experiments are currently RUNNING, COMPLETED, or CANCELLED?
Which experiments resulted in variant A vs variant B winning and what is the distribution of wins?
What is the average conversion rate for variant A and variant B across completed experiments?
Which experiments ran during a given date range and how long did they run (duration)?
Which tests had the largest absolute or relative difference between conversion_rate_a and conversion_rate_b?
ecom_ab_test.relationships: There are no declared foreign keys. Typical joins: link to an experiment-assignment or session-level table (if present) that maps user/order/session to test_id (not present here) to attribute conversions. Can be joined to ecom_orders, ecom_customers, ecom_product_events, or other ecom_* tables in the schema if those tables contain a test_id (or an assignment table) to enable per-user or per-order impact analysis. Recommended: maintain a separate mapping table (experiment_assignments/test_exposures) with (test_id, subject_id, assignment_variant, timestamp) to enable accurate join-based analysis.

ecom_acquisition_agents.purpose: Repository of e-commerce customer acquisition agents (people/resources) used to acquire customers. Stores identity, contact, channel, geography, lifecycle timestamps and current active status. Supports attribution, channel performance, regional coverage, and agent-level reporting within the ecom analytics/operations ecosystem.
ecom_acquisition_agents.key_characteristics: Very small table (55 rows). agent_id is primary key and unique (55 distinct). Most columns are unique per row (name, phone, email, join_date, created_at). acq_channel has low cardinality (5 values) with Social Media, Influencer, Referral, WhatsApp Outreach, Facebook Ads dominating. area has moderate cardinality (9). status is highly skewed: INACTIVE 60%, ACTIVE 40%. Dates are full datetime2; join_date and created_at are distinct per agent. All identifier columns are nvarchar; agent_id values have effective max length ~10.
ecom_acquisition_agents.analysis_capabilities: Alone, the table can: compute counts of agents by acquisition channel, area, and status; analyze join_date/cohort distributions; identify contactability (phone/email present); support agent lists for operations; provide small-dimension lookups for joins to transactional tables; build agent-level filters for dashboarding and segmentation.
business_questions: How many acquisition agents are active vs inactive and how has that changed over time (by join_date or created_at)?
Which acquisition channels have the most agents and what is the geographic distribution of agents by area?
Which agents joined within a specific date range (e.g., hires in Q1 2022)?
Which channels or areas should be prioritized for recruiting more agents based on current agent counts?
Is contact information available for each agent and which agents are missing phone or email?
ecom_acquisition_agents.relationships: No declared foreign keys. The natural join key is agent_id which can be joined to transactional or analytic tables that store an agent reference (for example ecom_orders, ecom_sales_daily/monthly, ecom_customer_segmentation or any orders/lead tables that have an acquisition_agent or agent_id column). Joins enable agent-attributed revenue, conversion, retention and per-agent performance metrics. Because agent_id is nvarchar, ensure target tables use compatible types/collations or cast/trim when joining.

ecom_activity_log.purpose: Event / audit log for ecommerce operations. Tracks actions (CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE/STATUS_CHANGE) performed on entities (CUSTOMER, ORDER, PAYMENT, PRODUCT), who performed them, when, and source IP. Serves operational auditing, security monitoring, and behavioral analytics.
ecom_activity_log.key_characteristics: Small table (~200 rows). Most columns are nvarchar despite representing categorical or datetime data. entity_type and action are low-cardinality categorical fields. performed_by has 3 distinct values. entity_id and ip_address are high-cardinality. action_ts stored as text (yyyy-mm-dd HH:MM:SS) and appears to be consistent datetime strings but is not a native datetime type. notes is decimal but contains 100% nulls. There is no primary key or foreign key constraints; audit_id appears unique per row in practice (distinct=200) but not enforced.
ecom_activity_log.analysis_capabilities: Can analyze activity volumes over time, distribution of actions by entity_type and performer, detect high-frequency IPs or actors, identify recent deletes/updates, and correlate with other tables by entity_id (when prefixes/types match). Can support security/audit queries (who performed X on Y), operational metrics (actions per hour/day), and basic anomaly detection.
business_questions: Which users or system actors performed the most DELETE or STATUS_CHANGE actions in the last 7/30 days?
How many CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE actions happened per entity_type (ORDER/CUSTOMER/PAYMENT/PRODUCT) over a period?
Which entity_ids (orders/customers) had the most status changes or deletions in the last month?
From which IP addresses are most destructive actions (DELETE) being performed?
When did a specific entity_id (e.g., EORD2073 or CUST1022) undergo CREATE, UPDATE, STATUS_CHANGE or DELETE and who performed them?
ecom_activity_log.relationships: No enforced foreign keys, but entity_id can be joined to domain tables in the schema by matching id strings and entity_type: e.g., entity_type=ORDER -> join ecom_orders.order_id or ecom_order_lines; entity_type=CUSTOMER -> join ecom_customers.customer_id; entity_type=PAYMENT -> join ecom_payments.payment_id; entity_type=PRODUCT -> join products/product_variants. Joining requires string matching and sometimes prefix inspection (IDs like 'EORD...' or 'CUST...'). These joins enable combining audit events with full entity details (order amounts, customer segments, product metadata) for richer analytics and root-cause investigations.

ecom_browsing_history.purpose: Stores event-level browsing interactions on the e-commerce site (searches, product views, category clicks) for analytics, product & UX optimization, and attribution.
ecom_browsing_history.key_characteristics: Small table (300 rows) with high-cardinality primary key (browse_id), moderate cardinality customer_id (~95 distinct), very low cardinality categorical fields (event_type: 3, device_type: 3, referrer_source: 4), and a small set of SKUs (7). Timestamps are event-level and creation-level; many columns can be NULL. Frequent values in search_query and referrer_source indicate concentrated user behavior.
ecom_browsing_history.analysis_capabilities: Can compute visit/event counts, unique visitors, device/referrer/event-type distributions, top search queries, product-level interest (sku views), time-series trends at event granularity, and simple attribution by referrer or device. Enables funnel and engagement analyses (search → view), short sessionization, and customer-level browsing profiling when joined to customer or product tables.
business_questions: Which referrer sources drive the most product views and searches?
What are the top search queries and how do they convert to product views for specific SKUs?
How does browsing activity vary by device type and time of day?
Which customers have the most browsing events and which products are they viewing/searching for?
What is the distribution of event types (SEARCH, VIEW_PRODUCT, CATEGORY_CLICK) over a given date range?
ecom_browsing_history.relationships: customer_id can join to ecom_customers or b2b_customers for enrichment (demographics, segments). sku can join to products, product_variants, or ecom_products for product metadata and pricing. Joins enable conversion analysis (browsing → orders/payments), cohort analysis, and linking events to revenue tables (ecom_orders, ecom_payments). Because browse_id is primary key and there are no foreign keys defined, joins rely on customer_id and sku as logical keys; consider adding FK constraints or indices for performance in larger datasets.

ecom_cart_items.purpose: Stores line‑level items placed into e‑commerce carts. Each row represents a single cart item (sku/variant, quantity, price snapshot and reservation state) and is used to understand cart composition, item-level activity and pre-order/reservation behavior prior to checkout.
ecom_cart_items.key_characteristics: Small table (150 rows). cart_item_id is unique (150 distinct). cart_id has moderate cardinality (81 distinct) indicating multiple items per cart. sku has low cardinality (10 distinct) with strong skew to a few SKUs. qty and price_at_add are numeric with limited distinct values indicating repeated quantities/prices. added_at and updated_at are fully distinct timestamps (150 distinct). reserved_flag is boolean with roughly equal distribution. notes is always null in sample. Variant_id has moderate distinct values (21).
ecom_cart_items.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports item-level analysis for carts: distribution of quantities and prices, frequency of SKUs in carts, time-based patterns for when items are added/updated, reservation status analysis, counts of items per cart, and identifying carts containing specific SKUs or variants. It can provide input metrics for conversion funnel analysis when joined to carts/orders. It is also useful for inventory reservation and pre-order analytics when combined with product/inventory tables.
business_questions: Which SKUs or variants are most frequently added to carts (top-of-cart items)?
What is the distribution of quantities and price snapshots for cart items?
How many items per cart do users typically add and which carts contain >N items?
What proportion of cart items were marked reserved and how does reservation vary by SKU or time?
When are items typically added/updated (daily/hourly trends) before checkout?
ecom_cart_items.relationships: Foreign key cart_id -> zuna_etl.ecom_carts.cart_id links items to the parent cart (customer, cart state, cart-level timestamps). sku and variant_id are natural keys to join product-related tables (e.g., products, product_variants, product_pricing, inventory) to enrich with product attributes and inventory/price history. Joins enable conversion analysis (cart items -> carts -> orders), stock/reservation reconciliation (cart items -> inventory_items), and price/discount attribution (cart items -> coupons/coupons usage/orders).

ecom_carts.purpose: Represents shopping carts (active, checked-out or abandoned) captured by the e-commerce ETL. Each row is a cart instance tracked by cart_id with meta data about owner (customer_id), session, timing, value, coupon usage, device and abandonment status. It is used for customer behavior analysis, cart abandonment analysis and as a join source to cart items, orders and customers.
ecom_carts.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows). cart_id is primary key and fully distinct (100). customer_id is present for all carts but nullable; distinct ~100 indicating one cart per customer in this sample. created_at/last_updated_at have full cardinality and are date/time typed. total_items has low cardinality (8 distinct) with common values: 5 (21%), 2 (17%), 8 (13%), 1 (12%), 6 (12%). total_amount is continuous (100 distinct). coupon_code is sparse (37% null) with only 2 values (NEWUSER, FRESH10). currency is single-valued (INR). device_type has 3 values (web, ios, android). abandoned_flag is boolean with ~30% True; abandoned_at has 59% nulls and aligns with abandoned_flag.
ecom_carts.analysis_capabilities: Alone the table enables cart-level analytics: cart counts, abandoned vs non-abandoned rates, average order/cart value (AOV), distribution of total_items, coupon usage effectiveness at the cart-level, device segmentation, time-based trends of cart creations/abandons, and cohorting by customer or session. It can provide conversion signals when joined to orders, cart items and customers.
business_questions: What percentage of carts are abandoned vs completed?
What is the average total_amount and total_items by device_type or coupon_code?
How does abandonment rate vary over time (daily/weekly) based on created_at or session?
Which coupons are being used and what is the average cart value for each coupon?
Which customers/sessions generate the highest cart totals and how many carts per customer exist?
ecom_carts.relationships: Has a foreign key customer_id -> zuna_etl.ecom_customers.customer_id enabling joins for customer demographics, lifetime value and segmentation. Commonly joined to ecom_cart_items (item-level breakdown), ecom_orders/ecom_payments (to check conversion from cart to order), ecom_coupons (coupon metadata), and session/customer tables for behavioral analysis. These joins enable item-level revenue, conversion funnel and coupon effectiveness analyses.

ecom_coupon_usage.purpose: Records instances when coupons were applied to orders (coupon redemptions). It captures which coupon was used by which customer on which order, when it was applied, the discount amount given and when the usage row was created. In the schema it serves as the bridge between coupon definitions (ecom_coupons) and orders (ecom_orders) for coupon analytics and attribution.
ecom_coupon_usage.key_characteristics: Small table (~140 rows). No declared primary key. coupon_usage_id has 120 distinct values => not fully unique. coupon_id, customer_id and order_id are moderately high-cardinality relative to table size (54, 78, 84 distinct). applied_ts is stored as nvarchar(50) but contains timestamp strings (140 distinct). created_at is datetime2 and also fully distinct. discount_given is decimal(18,4) with many unique values (135 distinct) and some repeated common discount amounts (e.g., 30.0000, 25.0000). Several nullability flags: all columns are nullable per DDL. Foreign keys exist to ecom_coupons.coupon_id and ecom_orders.order_id (logical, declared in metadata).
ecom_coupon_usage.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table lets you: count coupon redemptions, compute total and average discount given (sum/avg), analyze redemption frequency by coupon/customer/order, do time-series of redemptions using created_at (or applied_ts after casting), detect multiple redemptions per order or customer, and identify outlier discounts. For richer analysis (redemption rates, revenue impact, coupon metadata like rules, or order totals to compute discount percentage), joins to ecom_coupons and ecom_orders are required.
business_questions: How many times was each coupon redeemed in a given date range?
What is the total and average discount provided by each coupon or across all coupons?
Which customers redeem the most coupons and what is the average discount they receive?
When (time-series) are coupons being applied most often (daily/weekly/monthly patterns)?
Which orders used coupons and how much discount was applied per order (requires join to orders for order totals to compute relative discount)?
ecom_coupon_usage.relationships: coupon_id -> ecom_coupons.coupon_id: join to get coupon metadata (campaign, type, validity, discount rules). order_id -> ecom_orders.order_id: join to obtain order total, order status, customer billing and shipping attributes and calculate discount as % of order value. These relationships enable computing redemption rate (need coupon issuance counts from ecom_coupons or campaign tables), revenue impact per coupon, and attribution of discounts to orders/customers. Because coupon_usage_id is not a guaranteed primary key, join predicates should prefer coupon_id/order_id for many-to-one relationships; de-duplication or additional business keys (order_id + coupon_id + applied_ts) may be needed to identify unique redemption events.

ecom_customer_addresses.purpose: Stores customer postal and geolocation addresses for the e-commerce platform. Each row is one address belonging to a customer and is used for deliveries, analytics (geography, urban concentration), and customer-contact functions.
ecom_customer_addresses.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). address_id is primary key and unique. customer_id is a foreign key (one-to-many relationship expected). Low-cardinality columns: label (2 values), state and country are constant (Gujarat, India). Many near-unique fields: addr_line1, pincode, lat/long. addr_line2 has ~27% nulls and only 3 distinct values (common modifiers). created_at is populated and has full datetime precision. Latitude/longitude are present and allow basic geospatial calculations at city/local scale.
ecom_customer_addresses.analysis_capabilities: Can compute number of addresses per customer, distribution of addresses by city/label, basic geospatial clustering and nearest-store/delivery calculations, recent address additions (by created_at), and address-level joins to customers/orders for delivery and retention analysis.
business_questions: How many saved addresses does each customer have and which customers have multiple delivery locations?
What is the distribution of customer addresses by city and by label (Home vs Work)?
Which addresses are new in the last X days (recently added addresses) for outreach or fraud checks?
What are the geographic clusters of customers (using latitude/longitude) to inform delivery zone planning?
Which customers' addresses are near a given store or warehouse (proximity / nearest location)?
ecom_customer_addresses.relationships: customer_id references zuna_etl.ecom_customers.customer_id enabling joins to customer demographics, orders and payments. Joining to ecom_orders and ecom_payments allows mapping addresses to order deliveries and payment behavior. Relationships enable funneling address-level insights into customer segmentation, delivery performance, and regional sales analysis.

ecom_customer_segmentation.purpose: Stores one-row-per-customer segmentation metrics derived from transactional data (RFM segment, loyalty score, avg order value, purchase frequency, last purchase and create timestamps). Used to drive marketing, personalization, retention and analytics without re-computing base transactions each query.
ecom_customer_segmentation.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows) with customer_id as the logical primary key (all rows distinct). rfm_segment is low-cardinality (4 values) and suitable for grouping/filters. loyalty_score is integer-like (59 distinct values). avg_order_value and purchase_frequency are high-cardinality decimals (100 and 84 distinct respectively). last_purchase_date and created_at are full datetime2 values and can be used for range filters; many columns allow NULLs. Data appears pre-aggregated per customer.
ecom_customer_segmentation.analysis_capabilities: Enables segmentation analytics (counts, distributions) and joins to customer master or orders for enrichment. Can answer segment-level summaries (mean AOV, avg loyalty score), identify high-value or lapsed customers, compute counts for campaigns, and filter customers for targeted actions. Useful for cohorting by created_at or last_purchase_date without touching raw orders.
business_questions: How many customers are in each RFM segment and what is their average order value and loyalty score?
Which customers are 'Champions' with high avg_order_value and high loyalty_score for VIP outreach?
Which customers are 'Hibernating' or 'At Risk' and had last_purchase_date more than X days ago for re-engagement campaigns?
What is the distribution of purchase_frequency across segments and which segments drive the highest revenue per order?
How did the composition of RFM segments change for customers created in the last N months?
ecom_customer_segmentation.relationships: Natural key customer_id can be joined to ecom_customers (customer master) and transactional tables such as ecom_orders, ecom_payments, ecom_product_wise_sales, product_sales, ecom_reviews to enrich segmentation with address, order history, item-level data and revenue. Although no foreign key is declared, customer_id is the expected join key for marketing, order-level drilldowns and cohort analyses.

ecom_customers.purpose: Customer master for the e-commerce domain: stores identity, contact, demographic and engagement metadata for individual customers used by front-end, marketing, reporting and joins to transactional tables (orders, payments, addresses).
ecom_customers.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows). customer_id is primary key and fully distinct. Many columns have high cardinality (email, phone, external_customer_code, dob). Several categorical columns have low cardinality and skew (gender: 3 values with 'Other' 40%; preferred_language: 3 values; status: ACTIVE/INACTIVE). created_at and last_login are datetime2 with full uniqueness - useful for time-range queries. dob is stored as nvarchar in ISO-like YYYY-MM-DD format and needs casting for age calculations. marketing_opt_in is bit and skewed toward True (63%). No declared foreign keys but natural joins to orders/addresses via customer_id are expected.
ecom_customers.analysis_capabilities: By itself: profiling (demographics, language, gender, status), churn/engagement proxies (last_login, created_at), marketing segmentation (marketing_opt_in, preferred_language, source_channel), single-customer lookups and identity resolution. Limited transactional analysis without joins (no spend/orders data here).
business_questions: How many active vs inactive customers do we have and their language/gender breakdown?
Which acquisition channels (source_channel) brought the most customers and what percent opted into marketing?
What is the distribution of last login recency (e.g., active users in last 30/90 days)?
What are the counts of customers by preferred_language and gender for targeted campaigns?
Which customers lack email or phone contact information?
ecom_customers.relationships: Primary join key is customer_id; typical joins: ecom_orders, ecom_payments, ecom_customer_addresses, ecom_cart_items, ecom_reviews, ecom_returns. Joining enables order-level revenue, lifetime value, address-level geography, cart behavior, review sentiment. external_customer_code can be used to link to external systems. No declared FK means joins are logical; add foreign key constraints or indexes when possible for referential integrity and query performance.

ecom_frequently_bought.purpose: Holds precomputed pairwise 'frequently bought together' relationships between SKUs for the e‑commerce domain. Each row represents a mapping (map_id) that links a base SKU (sku) to another SKU frequently bought with it (freq_with_sku), with an associated co_purchase_rate and a timestamp of the last update. This table is used to drive recommendations, cross-sell analyses, and product bundling heuristics across the ecom schema.
ecom_frequently_bought.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows) with map_id effectively unique per row (100 distinct). sku and freq_with_sku are low-cardinality categorical fields (6 distinct SKUs) with skewed frequency distribution (top SKUs cover large share). co_purchase_rate is numeric (decimal(18,4)) with high cardinality (96 distinct values) and suitable for ranking/sorting. last_updated stored as nvarchar but holds ISO-like timestamps; requires casting to datetime for temporal operations.
ecom_frequently_bought.analysis_capabilities: Can identify top co-purchased SKUs per SKU (ranking by co_purchase_rate), compute top global co-purchases, generate recommendation lists (top N), detect changes if compared across snapshots (if historical tables exist), and join to product master data for names/pricing. Supports filtering by SKU, thresholding by co_purchase_rate, and ordering by recency after casting last_updated.
business_questions: What are the top N SKUs most frequently bought with a given SKU (ranked by co_purchase_rate)?
Which SKU pairs have the highest co-purchase rates across the catalog (potential bundling candidates)?
How do co-purchase relationships change over time (requires snapshots or comparing last_updated across exports)?
Which SKUs should be prioritized for cross-sell recommendations on product pages based on co_purchase_rate and recency?
Are there low co_purchase_rate pairs that still appear frequently and might indicate pricing or inventory issues when combined with sales data?
ecom_frequently_bought.relationships: Primary join keys are sku and freq_with_sku which can be linked to product master tables such as products, ecom_products, product_variants, b2b_products, or product_sales tables in the schema to enrich with product name, category, price, inventory or sales metrics. Joining to order_items / ecom_orders / product_sales enables validation and further signals (actual co-purchase counts, revenue impact). last_updated can be used to join or filter by recency when combined with time-series snapshots or event tables (ecom_activity_log, ecom_product_events).

ecom_notifications.purpose: Stores outbound notifications (email, SMS, push) sent to customers, with message content, delivery status, and linkage to orders. Used for monitoring communication effectiveness, delivery reliability, and auditing notification history.
ecom_notifications.key_characteristics: Small table (~150 rows). notification_id is unique (150 distinct). Several low-cardinality categorical fields (notification_type: 3, title: 3, message: 3, delivery_status: 3). customer_id and linked_order_id have moderate cardinality (78 and 75 distinct). sent_ts is stored as nvarchar though values appear to be timestamps (150 distinct). created_at is datetime2 and matches sent/creation events. Repeated message/title text indicates templated notifications. Distribution: notification_type roughly evenly split among PUSH/EMAIL/SMS; delivery_status shows ~36% FAILED, 33% DELIVERED, 31% SENT — useful for reliability metrics.
ecom_notifications.analysis_capabilities: Can compute notification volume over time, delivery success/failure rates by channel/type/title, per-customer notification frequency, notifications linked to orders, and temporal patterns (hour/day). With joins to customers or orders tables, can analyze message impact on orders, refunds, or customer behavior. Useful for operational reporting and A/B analysis of notification templates.
business_questions: What is the delivery success rate (DELIVERED vs FAILED) overall and by notification_type (EMAIL/SMS/PUSH)?
Which customers received the most notifications in a given period and what are their delivery outcomes?
How many notifications are linked to orders and what is the distribution of delivery_status for order-related notifications?
Which notification templates (title/message) lead to higher delivered rates or more order conversions?
What are the hourly/daily patterns of sent notifications and peak sending times?
ecom_notifications.relationships: No enforced foreign keys, but logical joins exist: customer_id -> ecom_customers.customer_id (customer profile, segmentation); linked_order_id -> ecom_orders.order_id or ecom_order_ids in order tables (order status, conversion); notification_type/delivery_status/title can be joined to reference/look-up tables if introduced. These joins enable analysis of notification impact on orders, customer segmentation, and support troubleshooting delivery issues. Because relationships are not enforced, NL2SQL systems should validate existence when joining and handle missing matches (LEFT JOIN).

ecom_orders.purpose: Represents ecommerce orders (one row per order) and serves as the central fact table for order-level metrics (amounts, status, channel, timestamps). It is used to analyze sales, payments, fulfillment and campaign attribution and to join to customers, addresses, order lines and shipment tables.
ecom_orders.key_characteristics: 601 rows. order_id is primary key (high cardinality). customer_id has moderate cardinality (98). Several datetime-like fields exist: order_ts stored as nvarchar (mixed) and created_at/updated_at as datetime2. Many monetary columns (decimal(18,4)) with high distinct counts. Categorical columns channel, order_status, payment_status and fulfillment_centre_id are low-cardinality. coupon_code and source_campaign have nulls. delivery_address_id is high-cardinality. Table is small but contains important analytical dims and measures.
ecom_orders.analysis_capabilities: Time-series sales and order count analyses, channel and campaign attribution, conversion/funnel by order_status/payment_status, average order value / tax / discount / shipping metrics, customer-level order frequency (when joined to customers), fulfillment center and delivery-slot performance, refund and cancellation rate analysis (via payment_status and order_status).
business_questions: What is total revenue (net_amount / total_amount) and order count by day/week/month and by channel?
Which acquisition channels or campaigns produce the highest average order value and repeat customers?
What are cancellation and refund rates by fulfillment centre, delivery slot or channel?
How much discount and shipping cost is given per order and how do they affect net revenue?
Which customers generate the most orders and revenue (when joined to customers)?
ecom_orders.relationships: FK customer_id -> zuna_etl.ecom_customers (enables customer-level joins for demographic/segment analysis). delivery_address_id can join to ecom_customer_addresses; order items / line-level details are in related order_items/ ecom_order_lines / order_items tables for product-level revenue and quantities. Fulfillment/shipments can join to shipments or delivery_slots tables for logistics analysis.

ecom_payment_status_log.purpose: Event / audit log of payment status transitions for e-commerce payments. Each row records a single status change attempt for a payment, including source of change, response from payment gateway, retry attempts, timestamps and processing flag. Used for debugging, monitoring payment reliability and retry behavior.
ecom_payment_status_log.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows). Primary key is log_id (100 distinct). payment_id is also unique in this snapshot (100 distinct) but in production could repeat as payments retry. status_before and status_after are low-cardinality enums (3 values each) with PENDING/PAID/INITIATED and FAILED/PENDING/PAID respectively. source is binary (Manual, PG Webhook). response_code is low cardinality (200, 402, 500). Two numeric columns (response_body, notes) are defined as decimal(18,4) but are 100% NULL in current data—likely wrong type or unused. retry_count stored as decimal(18,4) but values are small integers 0-3. created_at and updated_at are full-precision datetime2 and have unique timestamps per row in this sample. processed_flag is boolean ~53% true.
ecom_payment_status_log.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table enables analysis of payment status transition counts, status-flip patterns (before->after), failure rates by source and response_code, retry behavior and correlation with processed_flag, and timing analysis (updated_at vs created_at) if created_at reflects event insertion. It can feed dashboards on payment reliability, mean retries before success/failure, and identify problematic payment gateways or manual workflows.
business_questions: What percentage of payment status transitions end in FAILED vs PAID in a given date range?
Which payment source (Manual vs PG Webhook) has higher failure rate or higher retry counts?
How many retries are attempted on average before a payment becomes PAID or FAILED?
Which response_code (200/402/500) is associated with most FAILED transitions?
Which payments had multiple status changes (frequent toggling) and need manual review?
ecom_payment_status_log.relationships: No declared foreign keys, but payment_id is a natural join key to ecom_payments (schema contains ecom_payments) and can connect to ecom_orders via payments -> orders relationship. Joining on payment_id enables enrichment with payment amount, currency, order_id, customer_id and merchant/gateway metadata. Joining to ecom_customers/ecom_orders allows customer-level failure and retry analysis. Typical joins: zuna_etl.ecom_payment_status_log.payment_id -> ecom_payments.payment_id -> ecom_orders.order_id -> ecom_customers.customer_id.

ecom_payments.purpose: Stores one row per payment attempt / transaction for e-commerce orders. Tracks amount, timing, gateway, status and settlement details to enable reconciliation, payment-failure analysis and settlement reporting within the ecom domain.
ecom_payments.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). Primary key payment_id (distinct=100). Low-cardinality categorical columns: payment_mode (4 values), payment_gateway (3), status (3), settlement_status (2), refund_eligible_flag (2). Several datetime-like fields: payment_ts stored as nvarchar(50) (values follow 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' pattern), settlement_date and created_at are datetime2. Many unique identifiers (gateway_ref_no distinct=100). processed_by is constant ('system'). notes column typed as decimal but entirely null. order_id is FK (nullable). Good coverage for time-based and categorical analyses but small volume.
ecom_payments.analysis_capabilities: By itself can produce payment volumes and amounts by time window, mode, gateway and status; settlement vs unsettled amount summaries; refund-eligible payment counts and amounts; failed/PENDING vs success ratios; compute time-to-settlement when payment_ts is parsed as datetime and settlement_date exists; identify payments missing orders. Cannot provide product-level or customer-level context without joining ecom_orders (or customers).
business_questions: What is the total payment amount and count by payment_mode and payment_gateway for a given date range?
What percentage and count of payments are SUCCESS vs FAILED vs PENDING, and how does that vary by gateway?
What is the total unsettled amount and number of UNSETTLED payments as of a given date?
How long (median/average) does it take for payments to move from payment_ts to settlement_date (time-to-settlement)?
Which orders have payments that are refund-eligible and what is the total potential refund exposure?
ecom_payments.relationships: order_id is a foreign key to zuna_etl.ecom_orders.order_id enabling joins to order-level details (customer_id, order_total, items, shipping). Joins to ecom_payment_status_log (if present) allow time-series status history per payment. Joining to ecom_refunds/ecom_return_items enables reconciliation of refunds against original payments. These relationships enable answering questions about payment impact on orders, customer-level payment behavior, refund reconciliation and linking payments to products/regions/stores via orders.

ecom_product_events.purpose: Tracks discrete product-related events (stock and price changes) emitted by e‑commerce systems and bots. It is an audit/telemetry table used to analyze inventory and pricing lifecycle events for SKUs and to join with product/catalog/inventory tables for enrichment.
ecom_product_events.key_characteristics: Small table (~250 rows). event_id is the primary key and high-cardinality (unique per row). sku is low-cardinality (7 distinct values) with a few SKUs dominating volume. event_type and triggered_by each have 3 distinct values and are ideal for grouping/filters. event_ts and created_at are fully populated and distinct (can be used for ordering/timeseries). old_value and new_value are decimal(18,4) but currently 100% NULL (no numeric deltas captured). Overall distribution is heavily skewed to a few SKUs and two main event types (BACK_IN_STOCK, PRICE_DROP, OUT_OF_STOCK) and three trigger sources (system, pricing_bot, inventory_bot).
ecom_product_events.analysis_capabilities: Alone, the table can: count events by type and trigger, compute timeseries of event frequency (by event_ts or created_at), identify most frequently affected SKUs, compute time gaps (event_ts vs created_at) to measure ingestion latency, and produce latest-event-per-sku snapshots. It cannot provide product metadata (names, categories, prices) without joins; numerical delta analysis is not possible until old_value/new_value are populated.
business_questions: Which SKUs experienced the most product events in a given period?
What is the breakdown of event types (BACK_IN_STOCK / PRICE_DROP / OUT_OF_STOCK) over time?
Which agent (system, pricing_bot, inventory_bot) triggered the majority of events?
What is the average latency between event_ts and created_at (ingestion delay)?
For each SKU, what was the most recent event and when did it occur?
ecom_product_events.relationships: No declared foreign keys, but logical joins: sku -> products / ecom_products / product_variants tables to enrich with product name, category, vendor, or current price; event_ts/created_at can join/compare with other event timelines in ecom_activity_log or b2b_events_stream to correlate cross-system events. These joins enable analyses such as correlating price drops to orders, linking stock events to inventory levels, and joining triggered_by to agent configuration tables.

ecom_product_wise_sales.purpose: Daily product-level sales metrics for e-commerce, storing time series KPIs (orders, customers, quantities, and monetary metrics) for product-level reporting and trend analysis. It appears to be a daily snapshot of sales metrics for a product across time.
ecom_product_wise_sales.key_characteristics: Small table (710 rows). Primary key is date (unique per row). product_id has extremely low cardinality (only 1 distinct value: 'TOM001'), indicating this table currently contains a single product's daily time series. All other measures are decimal(18,4) with full distinctness across rows for most metrics. Many columns contain continuous numeric values suitable for aggregation. Date values are daily (datetime2 with midnight times in top values) and are ideal for range filters and time-series grouping. No declared foreign keys.
ecom_product_wise_sales.analysis_capabilities: Time-series trend analysis (daily/rolling sums/averages), calculation of revenue and margin-like metrics, per-day KPIs (orders, customers, qty), aggregation across date ranges (weekly/monthly), detecting seasonality/anomalies, and comparison of gross_sales vs net_sales, discounts, and delivery/tax impacts. Can compute conversion-like metrics (orders/customers), average order size (qty/kg or gross/net per order/customer) and rolling averages. Enrichment with other tables (products, orders) enables product metadata and transaction-level drilldown.
business_questions: How have daily net sales trended over the last N days/weeks/months for the product?
What is the average daily number of orders and customers, and how are they correlated with net sales?
How much revenue is lost to discounts and what percentage of gross sales do discounts represent over a period?
What is the average sold quantity (kg) per order or per customer and has it changed over time?
How much of gross_sales is consumed by delivery fees and taxes over time (breakdown of components of net_sales)?
ecom_product_wise_sales.relationships: No foreign key constraints are declared. Logical joins: product_id => products or b2b_products/ecom_products to fetch product attributes (name, category, SKU details). date => can join to daily snapshots tables (ecom_sales_daily, ecom_sales_monthly, b2b_sales_daily) for rollups/compare aggregated KPIs. Joining to orders/order_lines/invoices (ecom_orders, order_items, b2b_order_lines) enables drilldown to transaction-level details and reconciliation. These joins enable questions like product contribution to total sales, per-order itemization, and attribution to campaigns or customers when combined with customer/order tables.

ecom_refunds.purpose: Holds refund-level records for e-commerce orders. Each row represents one refund event (refund_id) with references to the original order and payment, timestamps, amounts, status and administrative metadata. It supports tracking refunds, reconciliation and refund-rate analytics.
ecom_refunds.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows). refund_id is primary key and distinct for all rows. order_id and payment_id are strings with moderate reuse (order_id distinct 39), refund_mode and status have low cardinality (2 and 3 distinct values respectively). refund_ts is stored as nvarchar containing datetime strings; processed_at is datetime2. notes column is decimal but entirely NULL. Values concentrate in a few top timestamps and identifiers. Foreign key to ecom_orders.order_id exists.
ecom_refunds.analysis_capabilities: Can compute refund rates, total refunded amount, average refund per order, refunds by mode/status/reason, time-series of refunds (if refund_ts parsed to datetime), and link refunds to orders/payments for deeper joins (order details, customer, items). Good for reconciliation and operational KPIs (failed vs completed refunds, processing lag between refund_ts and processed_at).
business_questions: What is the total refunded amount over a given period (day/week/month)?
What proportion of refunds are ACCOUNT_CREDIT vs PG_REFUND and how does that vary by month?
Which orders have the highest number/amount of refunds or repeat refunds?
What is the breakdown of refund status (COMPLETED, INITIATED, FAILED) and trends over time?
What are the top reasons for refunds and their associated average refund amounts?
ecom_refunds.relationships: Primary key refund_id uniquely identifies refund rows. order_id is a foreign key to zuna_etl.ecom_orders.order_id, enabling joins to order-level info (customer, order value, items). payment_id can join to ecom_payments to reconcile gateway transactions. These joins enable combined analyses like refund rate by customer segment, item-level return and refund mapping, and linking refund lifecycle to payment gateway outcomes.

ecom_return_items.purpose: Stores one row per returned item for e-commerce returns. Each record represents a specific item on a return (line-level), tracking quantity, inspection status, whether it can be restocked, and the final disposition. It is used to analyze return details, restocking decisions, and inspection outcomes.
ecom_return_items.key_characteristics: Very small table (60 rows). Primary key return_item_id is unique (60 distinct). return_id has moderate cardinality (29 distinct) indicating many returns have multiple items. sku has low cardinality (10 distinct) with clear top SKUs. disposition is highly categorical (3 values). inspected_flag is mostly true (~77%). restockable_flag is roughly balanced (~53% true). inspected_at values are unique per row indicating time-stamped inspections. notes column is numeric type but entirely NULL (100%). Data types are all nullable except return_item_id. max lengths for nvarchar columns are short (max len observed <= 50, actual values much shorter).
ecom_return_items.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports analysis of returned item volumes and quantities by SKU, disposition, inspection status and timing. It can compute counts and sum(qty) per sku/disposition/restockable_flag, measure inspection throughput (counts/times), calculate proportions of restockable vs discarded items, and identify top SKUs by return volume. It cannot provide customer-level or order-level context without joins to orders/returns/order_items/products tables.
business_questions: Which SKUs generate the most returned quantity and how much of that quantity is restockable?
What proportion of returned items are inspected and what are the typical inspection times?
How are dispositions distributed (RESTOCK, DISCARD, RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER) overall and by SKU?
Which returns (return_id) contain the largest quantity of returned items?
How many items are restocked versus discarded over a given date range of inspection?
ecom_return_items.relationships: Logical join keys: return_id can join to a returns table (e.g., returns or ecom_returns) to get return-level metadata (customer, return reason, created_at). order_item_id can join to order_items / ecom_order_lines / b2b_order_lines to map to original order/order_line and to get price, product_id, order_id. sku can join to products / product_variants / ecom_products to fetch product attributes and categories. disposition and restockable_flag results can be combined with inventory_items or product_inventory to drive restocking and valuation flows. Joins enable analyses tying returns to customers, orders, refunds, inventory adjustments, and supplier returns.

ecom_reviews.purpose: Customer product reviews for the e-commerce platform; stores review metadata, textual content, ratings, and minimal response/administrative fields. Used to measure product satisfaction, surface review text and titles, and link feedback to customers and SKUs.
ecom_reviews.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). review_id is primary key (unique). customer_id references ecom_customers. sku has low cardinality (10 distinct), rating has 3 distinct values (3,4,5) with distribution skewed to 4. title and review_text are highly templated (4 distinct values each). review_ts is stored as nvarchar but appears to contain full timestamp strings (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). created_at is proper datetime2. verified_purchase_flag is always True (no variance). responded_by and response_text are incorrectly typed as decimal(18,4) and contain 100% nulls (data-quality issue). helpful_count has moderate distinct values (21 distinct).
ecom_reviews.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports: distribution of ratings, counts of reviews by SKU/customer, top positive/negative templated titles and review_text frequency, time-series of reviews after casting review_ts or using created_at, helpfulness aggregation, and basic verified-purchase reporting (note: flag has no variance). It cannot effectively measure company responses (response fields empty) without data fixes. Joins extend capability to customer demographics and product metadata.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the most reviews and what are their average ratings?
How has average rating (or review volume) changed over time (daily/weekly/monthly)?
Which customers have submitted the most reviews and what are their typical ratings?
What percentage of reviews are 4 or 5 stars versus 3 stars (overall and by SKU)?
Which reviews are most helpful (highest helpful_count) and which titles/reviews are most common?
ecom_reviews.relationships: Foreign key: customer_id -> zuna_etl.ecom_customers.customer_id. Connects to product/product_variant tables via sku (products, product_variants, ecom_product_wise_sales). Enables combining review sentiment/ratings with customer demographics, order history (ecom_orders/order_items) and product sales to analyze impact of reviews on conversions, repeat purchases, and product returns. Related tables allow enriching reviews with product names, categories and customer segment attributes.

ecom_sales_daily.purpose: Daily e-commerce sales summary aggregated at the date level for the Website order channel. It stores daily KPIs (orders, customers, quantities, revenue components and timestamps) used for reporting, trend analysis and aggregation to higher-level metrics.
ecom_sales_daily.key_characteristics: 710 rows (one row per date primary key). date is the primary key and has full cardinality (710 distinct). Numeric metrics are stored as decimal(18,4) including counts (total_orders/total_customers) and monetary/weight fields. order_channel is constant ('Website') so it has no discriminatory power. created_at is an ingestion timestamp with only 2 distinct values (load batches). Most monetary/quantity fields have near-row-level uniqueness making the table well suited to time-series analysis.
ecom_sales_daily.analysis_capabilities: Time-series trend analysis (daily/rolling/period-over-period), calculation of daily KPIs (AOV = gross_sales/total_orders, average quantity per order, discounts as % of gross), aggregation to weekly/monthly/yearly levels, correlation between weight and sales, decomposition of gross -> net by discount/delivery/tax. Because the table is already aggregated daily, it cannot produce order-level or product-level breakdowns without joining to other tables.
business_questions: How have gross sales and net sales trended day-by-day over a time window?
What is the daily average order value (AOV) and how has it changed over time?
What portion of gross sales is being lost to discounts and how does that vary daily?
How many unique customers purchased per day and how does customer count correlate with orders?
What is daily delivered weight (kg) and how does quantity relate to revenue and delivery/tax costs?
ecom_sales_daily.relationships: No declared foreign keys. Typical relationships are joins on the date column to other daily or time-series tables in the schema (e.g., ecom_sales_monthly/ecom_sales_yearly, b2b_kpi_daily_snapshots, ecom_orders aggregated by date). Joining on date enables enrichment with product-level, order-level, customer or channel data stored elsewhere. Because order_channel is present, it can be used to align channel-specific metrics with other channel tables, but here it contains only 'Website'.

ecom_sales_monthly.purpose: Monthly aggregated e-commerce sales metrics for the Website channel. This table stores one row per year/month (monthly rollup) and is intended for reporting and trend analysis of orders, customers, quantities and monetary metrics at a monthly grain.
ecom_sales_monthly.key_characteristics: Small table (12 rows) representing one full year of monthly aggregates. Primary key is composite: (year, month). year has only one distinct value (2024) in the sample. month has 12 distinct values (1..12). order_channel is single-valued ('Website') in the sample. Monetary and quantity columns are decimal(18,4) and many fields are nullable. created_at is constant in this snapshot (likely ETL timestamp). No foreign keys. Values look pre-aggregated (sums/counts) at monthly grain.
ecom_sales_monthly.analysis_capabilities: Can be used alone to compute monthly trends, year-to-date aggregates, month-over-month (MoM) growth, basic margin-ish calculations (if combined with additional columns), KPI ratios (e.g., average order value = gross_sales/total_orders), and channel-level monthly reporting. Useful for fast lookups by year and month and for dashboarding of high-level sales metrics.
business_questions: What were monthly gross and net sales for each month in 2024?
How did total orders and total customers trend month-over-month and which month had highest orders?
What is the average order value per month (gross_sales / total_orders) and how did it change?
What was the total discount and delivery fee incurred each month and their share of gross sales?
What are year-to-date totals for gross_sales, net_sales, and total_qty_kg for 2024?
ecom_sales_monthly.relationships: No direct foreign keys. It conceptually links to other sales/fact tables (ecom_sales_daily, ecom_sales_yearly, ecom_product_wise_sales, ecom_orders, ecom_payments) for drilldowns and reconciliation. Joining on (year, month) or date-derived keys to daily/product/order-level tables enables drill-down from monthly aggregates to daily or transactional data, validation of rollups, and combining with product or customer dimensions for segmentation.

ecom_sales_yearly.purpose: Annual aggregated e-commerce sales metrics by year and order channel. It provides a pre-aggregated, year-level snapshot of orders, customers, quantities and monetary metrics to support high-level reporting and KPI calculations in the ETL layer.
ecom_sales_yearly.key_characteristics: Very low cardinality in the provided sample (single-row snapshot for year=2024 and order_channel='Website'). Primary key is year. All monetary and quantity fields are stored as decimal(18,4) (already aggregated). created_at appears to be an ETL/timestamp. order_channel is nvarchar(50) but currently constant. Columns may be NULLABLE (except year). This table is an aggregation table intended for fast read queries at the year level rather than transactional detail.
ecom_sales_yearly.analysis_capabilities: Excellent for fast year-level summaries (totals, averages, rates derived from totals). Supports KPI dashboards (yearly revenue, customers, avg order size, discounts as percent of gross). Not suitable for trend analysis within a year, detailed drill-downs (by customer/product/day) or deriving distributions without joining to lower-level tables (monthly/daily/orders).
business_questions: What were the total/net/gross sales for a given year (or multiple years) and order channel?
How many orders and unique customers did the business have in a given year, and what is the average order value?
What proportion of revenue was given away as discounts or paid as delivery fees and taxes in a year?
How does year-over-year net sales growth look (requires multiple rows / multiple years in table)?
When was this yearly aggregate last generated (ETL run time) and is the snapshot up to date?
ecom_sales_yearly.relationships: No explicit foreign keys. Typical joins: join to ecom_sales_monthly / ecom_sales_daily / b2b_sales_yearly /pos_sales_yearly by matching the year column (or by extracting YEAR(created_at) when needed). Can join to transactional tables (ecom_orders, ecom_payments, ecom_customers, product_sales) by converting the aggregate 'year' into a filter on the transaction date (e.g., WHERE YEAR(order_date)=year). These joins enable drill-down, validation, reconciliation and attribution (channel, product, customer segments). Because order_channel is present, it can be used to align or compare channel-level aggregates across other channel-dimensioned tables.

ecom_search_keywords.purpose: Holds recorded search keywords from an e-commerce/search analytics pipeline with per-keyword metrics (search volume, conversion rate) and the top associated SKU. Used to track keyword performance over time for SEO/SEM and merchandising decisions.
ecom_search_keywords.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows). keyword_id is unique (PK) and non-null; keyword and top_sku are low-cardinality categorical fields (keyword: 9 distinct values, top_sku: 7 distinct values). Numeric fields search_volume and conversion_rate are mostly unique per row (search_volume distinct 100, conversion_rate distinct 97). created_at has full datetime precision and 100 distinct timestamps (likely recent events). Many columns allow NULLs except keyword_id. Patterns: a few keywords dominate (potato, apple, tomato, organic veg, cheap veg) and a few top SKUs account for most associations (APP009, CAR004, TOM001, ONI003, COW005).
ecom_search_keywords.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports: ranking keywords by search_volume or conversion_rate, identifying top-performing keywords and associated SKUs, simple time-based snapshots of keyword performance (by created_at), conversions-per-impression style metrics (conversion_rate) and correlation checks between search_volume and conversion_rate. It can produce aggregates (sum/avg/count), percentiles, and trends across created_at timestamps.
business_questions: Which keywords generate the highest search volume over the recorded period?
Which keywords have the highest conversion rate and are they also high-volume?
Which SKU is most frequently the top SKU for high-converting or high-volume keywords?
How has search volume or conversion rate for a given keyword changed over time?
Which low-volume keywords have unexpectedly high conversion rates (potential niche opportunities)?
ecom_search_keywords.relationships: No declared foreign keys. Practical joins: top_sku can be joined to product/product_variant or sales tables (products, product_variants, product_sales, ecom_product_wise_sales, pos_products) to enrich SKU metadata, pricing, and sales performance. created_at can join to a date/time dimension for reporting. keyword can be reconciled with other search or events tables (ecom_product_events, ecom_browsing_history, ecom_search_logs if present) to connect keyword exposure to user behavior. These joins enable linking keyword intent to actual product sales and revenue.

ecom_ticket.purpose: Records customer support tickets for the e-commerce system. Each row represents one ticket raised by a customer (optionally tied to an order), its current status/priority, timestamps, and which agent owns it. The table supports operational monitoring of support workload and basic customer-service analytics.
ecom_ticket.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows) with high-cardinality primary key (ticket_id distinct=100). Many categorical columns with low cardinality (subject 4, status 4, priority 3, assigned_to 3). customer_id and order_id are semi-cardinal (customer_id distinct=60, order_id distinct=53) enabling joins to customer/order dimension tables. created_at/updated_at are fully distinct (100) enabling time-series queries. Descriptions are templated; many repeated values.
ecom_ticket.analysis_capabilities: Can compute ticket volume by status/priority/agent, SLA/age metrics (time between created_at and updated_at, open ticket age), trends over time (tickets/day, tickets by week/month), customer-level ticket counts (join to customers), order-related issue analysis (join to orders), and basic agent load balancing. Limited for deep conversation-level analytics without ecom_ticket_messages.
business_questions: How many tickets are open / in progress / resolved over a given time range?
Which agents have the highest number of high-priority tickets assigned?
What is the average time to update or resolve tickets (created_at -> updated_at) by priority or subject?
Which customers submit the most tickets and what issues do they report most often?
How many tickets are linked to orders and which orders generate the most tickets?
ecom_ticket.relationships: FK customer_id -> zuna_etl.ecom_customers.customer_id (enables enrichment with customer profile, segmentation, lifetime value). order_id can be joined to ecom_orders (or order tables) to analyze order-related issues. ecom_ticket_messages is a related table that would provide message-level chronology and resolution timestamps. These relationships enable customer-centric and order-centric NL2SQL queries (e.g., join to customers for demographic filters or to orders for order date correlation).

ecom_ticket_messages.purpose: Stores individual messages exchanged on support tickets (both customer and support messages). Acts as the conversation log for e-commerce tickets and is used to understand ticket timelines, response behavior, and message content.
ecom_ticket_messages.key_characteristics: Small table (293 rows). No declared primary key. ticket_message_id and message_ts are high-cardinality (unique per row). ticket_id has moderate cardinality (100 distinct values) and is a foreign key to ecom_ticket. sender is low-cardinality (2 values: 'support' and 'customer'). message_text is highly repeated (4 distinct messages making up all rows). attachment_url column is decimal and 100% NULL (likely incorrect type or unused). created_at is a true datetime2 and has unique values per row. message_ts is stored as nvarchar (19 chars) and appears to be a timestamp string—duplicate conceptually with created_at or a message-level timestamp.
ecom_ticket_messages.analysis_capabilities: Can analyze ticket-level conversation counts, message timelines, response split by sender (support vs customer), time-to-response (if message_ts or created_at are parsed as timestamps), distribution of message volume across tickets and over time, and basic content pattern detection (given only 4 message_text values). Cannot analyze attachment usage (attachment_url null) until populated. Joins to ecom_ticket allow enriching messages with ticket status, customer id, priority, etc.
business_questions: How many messages are associated with each ticket and which tickets have the most messages?
What is the distribution of messages by sender (support vs customer) and average messages per ticket by sender?
What is the typical time between successive messages on a ticket (median/average response time) and support response latency?
Which tickets had the most recent activity in a given date range (by created_at or message_ts)?
What are the common message texts or patterns and how often do automated/common responses occur?
ecom_ticket_messages.relationships: ticket_id -> zuna_etl.ecom_ticket.ticket_id. This FK enables joining to ticket metadata (status, priority, assigned agent, customer id) to answer agent performance and ticket lifecycle questions. Via ecom_ticket further joins to ecom_customers or orders allow analysis of messaging by customer segments, order issues, or vendor-related tickets.

ecom_vendors.purpose: Master vendor reference for e-commerce/pos/B2B systems. Stores vendor identity, contact, tax identifiers, location and lifecycle status used to enrich transactions, payments, contracts and operational records across the schema.
ecom_vendors.key_characteristics: Small (50 rows) high-cardinality identifier (vendor_id distinct 50). vendor_name ~49 distinct. vendor_category low-cardinality (5 values; Ad Agency/Courier/Influencer/Marketing/Tech) useful for grouping. status binary (ACTIVE/INACTIVE) with ~54% INACTIVE. contact fields (phone/email) are nearly unique, billing_address fully unique. gst_no stored as bigint with 50 distinct values; pan_no stored as nvarchar but fixed-length-like (~10). created_at has a single distinct value (ETL/load timestamp) so not useful for temporal analysis. No enforced foreign keys in DDL, but vendor_id is logical PK.
ecom_vendors.analysis_capabilities: Alone, the table supports vendor-level inventory of partners, segmentation by category/city/status, contact lists, compliance checks (GST/PAN presence), and simple counts. It cannot by itself show sales, returns, invoices or temporal trends but is ideal to join into transactional tables to attribute those events to vendors.
business_questions: How many vendors do we have by category and by city?
How many vendors are ACTIVE vs INACTIVE, and which ones are inactive?
Which vendors are missing or have malformed tax identifiers (GST/PAN) or contact info?
List contact details (phone/email/billing address) for a specific vendor or set of vendors.
Which vendors belong to a given category (e.g., Influencer) so we can target communications?
ecom_vendors.relationships: Logical join key is vendor_id. Although DDL does not declare foreign key constraints, many schema tables are likely to reference vendors (e.g., b2b_vendor_partners, pos_vendors, ecom_orders/pos_transactions/product_sales/invoices/returns/payments). Joining vendor_id to those transaction/contract tables enables attribution of orders, revenue, refunds, shipments, and payments to vendors for KPI, operations and reconciliation use-cases.

ecom_wishlist.purpose: Customer wishlist records for the e-commerce pipeline; captures which SKUs customers have added to their wishlist, when they added them, and whether they marked them as priority. Serves as a lightweight behavioral / intent dataset that can be joined to customer and product master tables to drive personalization, marketing, and inventory planning.
ecom_wishlist.key_characteristics: 100 rows, no declared primary or foreign keys. Columns are nullable. Distinct cardinalities: wishlist_id = 100 (appears unique per row), customer_id = 65, sku = 7 (very low cardinality), added_at = 100 (timestamp per row), priority_flag = 2 (boolean distribution ~46% True, 54% False), notes = all NULL (decimal(18,4) but unused). SKUs show strong skew (top SKU TOM001 = 20%, POT002 = 16%, CAR004 = 15%, ONI003 = 13%, APP009 = 12%). Customer usage is moderately dispersed (top customer ~4%). Timestamp values span recent dates (examples are in Oct 2025).
ecom_wishlist.analysis_capabilities: By itself the table can identify which SKUs are most wished-for, per-customer wishlist counts, priority-weighted wishlist counts, and temporal patterns of wishlist additions (e.g., daily or hourly spikes). It supports simple ranking and cohorting of customers by wishlist activity. To answer conversion or inventory questions it should be joined with orders, products, and customers tables.
business_questions: Which SKUs are most commonly added to wishlists (overall and in a given time window)?
Which customers have the most items in their wishlist, and which customers have high-priority wishlist items?
How many wishlist additions occurred per day / week / month, and are there temporal spikes tied to events or campaigns?
Which priority wishlist items are yet to convert to purchases (requires join to orders/order_lines)?
What proportion of customers add items to wishlists (requires join to customer base) and which segments are most engaged?
ecom_wishlist.relationships: No explicit foreign keys, but natural joins: customer_id → ecom_customers.customer_id (or b2b_customers.customer_id depending on dataset); sku → products.sku or product_variants.sku or ecom_products.sku (multiple product tables exist in schema). Joining enables enrichment: map wishlist sku → product metadata (category, price) and customer_id → customer attributes (segment, location). Because there are multiple product/customer tables in the schema, queries should explicitly choose the canonical table and column for joins and/or ask the user which master to use. Joins will enable analyses like conversion funnel, inventory reservation needs, targeted marketing lists, and lifetime value of wishlisted customers.

inventory_items.purpose: Inventory items ledger capturing per-item stock, batch, date, supplier and cost metadata. It is the canonical table for on-hand inventory details used for stock valuation, expiry/age tracking, and linking physical inventory to product master and downstream sales/returns records.
inventory_items.key_characteristics: Small table (100 rows) with high-cardinality identifiers (item_id, batch_id), low-cardinality categorical supplier (3 values), moderate-cardinality sku and name (10 values each), numeric continuous stock and cost (decimal with 4 fractional places), and many datetime fields (received_at, created_at, updated_at) useful for time-range filters. expiry_date has limited distinct values (29) indicating many items share common expiry buckets. item_id is primary key and unique.
