Use equality/IN for filtering (e.g., customer_id = 'CUST1001'). Consider indexing/join hints for performance if queries frequently filter by customer_id.

addr_line1.description: Primary address text (street, building number, road name).
addr_line1.analysis_use: Used for display, grouping by common streets, approximate deduplication (matching addr_line1 + city/pincode), and quality checks (missing or placeholder lines).
business_questions: What are the most common street addresses among customers?
Which addresses on 'MG Road' belong to which customers?
Are there addresses that look like duplicates based on addr_line1 + city + pincode?
addr_line1.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). For NL2SQL prefer exact or prefix matches (WHERE addr_line1 LIKE '%MG Road%'). Normalize whitespace/casing when matching. Use combined predicates with city/pincode for deduplication queries.

addr_line2.description: Secondary address line (landmark, locality note); many repeated limited values and some NULLs.
addr_line2.analysis_use: Quality and completeness checks; grouping/filtering by landmark descriptors (e.g., 'Near Bus Stop'); improves delivery instructions when present.
business_questions: Which addresses are missing addr_line2 (to improve delivery instructions)?
How many addresses include 'Near Bus Stop' as a landmark?
Which customers rely on landmark-based addr_line2 values in city Z?
addr_line2.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with ~18% NULLs and low cardinality. Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL to check completeness. For text matching use equality or LIKE; beware of repeated generic values. Consider coalescing (COALESCE(addr_line2,'') ) in NL2SQL when needed.

city.description: City name for the address (regional segmentation).
city.analysis_use: Primary field for geographic aggregation, grouping and regional reporting; common filter for queries and joining to city-level metadata or delivery zones.
business_questions: How many addresses are in Vadodara vs Gandhinagar?
Which customers have addresses in Nadiad?
What is the address count per city for planning distribution centers?
city.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with ~10 distinct values. Use equality/IN for city filters (WHERE city = 'Vadodara'). Consider normalization for spelling/casing. Good candidate to index for frequent city-level queries.

state.description: State name for the address (all rows contain 'Gujarat' in this dataset).
state.analysis_use: Mostly constant here but useful for validation, regional roll-ups in multi-state datasets and future-proofing if dataset expands.
business_questions: Are all addresses in the expected state (Gujarat)?
How many addresses would fall under a particular state-level SLA?
state.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but currently single-value. Filters on state will usually return all rows; consider omitting state in NL2SQL if known constant. Still include condition templates (WHERE state = 'Gujarat') for completeness/validation.

pincode.description: Numeric postal code (regional postal index) for routing and grouping.
pincode.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation by postal area; join to external pincode metadata (delivery zone, courier availability); numeric range or exact-match filters for serviceability queries.
business_questions: How many addresses are in each pincode?
Which customers are in pincode 365103?
Which pincodes have the highest concentration of customers for last-mile planning?
pincode.nl2sql_hints: bigint numeric; use numeric equality or IN lists (WHERE pincode = 365103). Avoid string operations; when using NL2SQL ensure numeric parsing of user-supplied pincodes. Consider indexing if pincode-based queries are frequent.

country.description: Country name for the address (all rows contain 'India').
country.analysis_use: Validation and multi-country roll-ups if dataset expands; currently constant and primarily informational.
business_questions: Are all B2B addresses in India?
If multi-country data is added later, how many addresses belong to each country?
country.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but currently single-value. Filters on country are rarely selective here; include for completeness in multi-country queries. Use equality (WHERE country = 'India').

latitude.description: Latitude of the address for geospatial calculations (decimal(18,4)).
latitude.analysis_use: Geospatial grouping, distance calculations (with longitude), bounding-box filters, clustering for delivery zones and mapping addresses to nearest warehouse/driver.
business_questions: Which addresses lie within X kilometers of coordinate (lat, lon)?
Cluster addresses geographically to plan delivery routes.
Find the nearest hub/warehouse to each address based on lat/long.
latitude.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Use numeric comparisons and bounding box queries for performance (WHERE latitude BETWEEN x AND y). For distance computations use appropriate spatial functions or convert to geography types if supported. Watch precision: storing 4 decimal places ~ 10-11m precision—suitable for regional routing but not very fine-grained.

longitude.description: Longitude of the address for geospatial calculations (decimal(18,4)).
longitude.analysis_use: Paired with latitude to compute distances, bounding-box filters, map plotting and spatial joins to service areas or delivery regions.
business_questions: List addresses within a given rectangular bounding box (lat/lon ranges) for a delivery area.
Which addresses are closest to a given longitude/latitude (for routing)?
Map addresses for visualization of customer geographic spread.
longitude.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Use with latitude in spatial calculations. Prefer WHERE longitude BETWEEN a AND b for quick filters; for accurate radial distance use Haversine or native spatial types. Consider converting these columns to a geography/spatial index for frequent proximity queries.

customer_id.description: Surrogate natural identifier for each B2B customer (primary key).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to transactional and reference tables; common filter and grouping column for per-customer aggregations (orders, invoices, payments).
business_questions: What are the transaction totals (orders, invoices, payments) for a specific customer_id?
List customers created within a date range (use customer_id to join transactions).
Which customers exceed their credit_limit?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as exact-match string key. Use equality or IN for joins/filters. Use consistent casing and trimming. When generating SQL use WHERE customer_id = 'CUST1001' or WHERE customer_id IN (...). Avoid LIKE unless user asks for partial id matching.

name.analysis_use: Human-readable label used in reporting, search and fuzzy matching. Useful for grouping when customer names include branch indicators; used with LIKE or full-text for search queries.
business_questions: Find customers whose name contains 'Organic' or 'Mahadev'.
Show top customers (by order value) with their names for reporting.
name.nl2sql_hints: Use LIKE '%term%' for partial search; support case-insensitive comparison. When returning lists, include name for clarity. Beware of duplicate or similar names — join by customer_id if uniqueness required.

account_type.description: Categorical type of account (Retailer, Wholesale, Hotel/Catering, Exporter).
account_type.analysis_use: Low-cardinality grouping/filtering dimension for segmentation, cohort analysis and aggregations (counts, revenue by type).
business_questions: How many retailers vs wholesalers do we have?
What is the average credit_limit by account_type?
account_type.nl2sql_hints: Use equality or IN for filters (e.g., WHERE account_type = 'Retailer'). Possible NULLs: handle with IS NULL in NL2SQL. Good candidate for indexed/dimension column in queries — map to read-friendly labels.

gst_number.description: Tax registration number (GST) for the customer; appears unique per row.
gst_number.analysis_use: Identity validation, deduplication across channels, and regulatory reporting. Can be used to join to external tax/partner datasets if available.
business_questions: Which customers are missing GST numbers?
Find customers by GST number (for compliance or KYC).
gst_number.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match string comparisons are typical. Use equality for lookups (WHERE gst_number = '24ABZKH2931Y9ZN'). Validate fixed-length patterns in preprocessing; consider indexing if often queried.

primary_contact_name.description: Name of the primary contact person at the customer.
primary_contact_name.analysis_use: Reporting and contact-level segmentation; joins to contact-related activity logs or CRM tables; used in notifications and outreach analyses.
business_questions: List customers and their primary contacts for a given sales rep.
Find customers whose primary contact name contains 'Aaina' or another name.
primary_contact_name.nl2sql_hints: Use LIKE for partial name searches and equality for exact matches. Watch for duplicates and name normalization. May need to join to CRM/contact tables on contact name or a contact id if available.

primary_contact_phone.description: Phone number of the primary contact (often includes country code).
primary_contact_phone.analysis_use: Customer contactability analysis, deduplication and matching with communication logs; quality checks for formatting and missing values.
business_questions: Which customers do not have a phone number on record?
Find a customer by their contact phone number.
primary_contact_phone.nl2sql_hints: Phone stored as string — normalize (strip spaces, punctuation) before equality comparisons. Use LIKE for partial matching. Consider pattern matching for country code extraction (e.g., phone LIKE '+91%').

email.description: Primary contact email address for the customer.
email.analysis_use: Communications, campaign targeting, deduplication and joining with marketing/campaign datasets. Useful for identifying active contact channels.
business_questions: How many customers have email addresses on file?
List customers with emails from a particular domain (e.g., '@guha.com').
email.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; use LOWER(email) for case-insensitive filters. Use LIKE '%@domain' for domain filters. Validate format in upstream; treat NULLs explicitly.

billing_address_id.description: Pointer to the customer's billing address record (address id).
billing_address_id.analysis_use: Join key to address table(s) to obtain city/state/postcode for tax, invoicing and regional reporting. Use for grouping customers by billing geography.
business_questions: Show customers grouped by billing city/state.
Find customers whose billing and delivery addresses differ.
billing_address_id.nl2sql_hints: Join via billing_address_id to b2b_customer_addresses (or equivalent address table). Use exact-match string equality for joins. Ensure consistent id formats and consider COALESCE if either billing or delivery id is used.

delivery_address_id.description: Pointer to the customer's delivery/shipping address record.
delivery_address_id.analysis_use: Join to address table to determine delivery regions, routing, and logistics metrics. Compare to billing_address_id to detect differences for delivery charge calculations.
business_questions: How many customers have different delivery and billing addresses?
What are the top delivery cities by customer count?
delivery_address_id.nl2sql_hints: Join using delivery_address_id to address tables. Use equality for joins and handle NULLs. When users ask for 'address' clarify whether they mean billing, delivery, or both.

customer_segment.analysis_use: Segmentation for performance tracking, targeted campaigns and KPI rollups. Low cardinality makes it ideal for GROUP BY.
business_questions: How many customers are in each tier and what is their average credit_limit?
Compare average payment_terms_days by customer_segment.
customer_segment.nl2sql_hints: Use equality/IN for filters (WHERE customer_segment = 'Tier-2'). Watch for NULLs. Map segments to human-friendly labels in NLP to avoid ambiguous synonyms ('tier 2' vs 'Tier-2').

credit_limit.description: Numeric credit limit assigned to the customer (decimal(18,4)).
credit_limit.analysis_use: Financial exposure analysis (sum, avg, max, min), risk assessment, credit limit utilization when combined with receivables. Sort and filter for high-credit customers.
business_questions: What is the total credit_limit exposure across all customers?
List top 10 customers by credit_limit.
credit_limit.nl2sql_hints: Treat as numeric in SQL aggregations (SUM, AVG). Beware of NULLs — use COALESCE(credit_limit,0) where appropriate. Use appropriate formatting for decimals in results.

payment_terms_days.analysis_use: Group customers by payment terms, compute receivable aging expectations, and business credit policy compliance. Low-cardinality column used heavily in GROUP BY.
business_questions: How many customers have 30-day payment terms?
What is the average credit_limit for customers on 60-day terms?
payment_terms_days.nl2sql_hints: Numeric integer type; use equality/IN and GROUP BY. Consider presenting as categorical labels in NL2SQL output. Handle NULLs explicitly (e.g., WHERE payment_terms_days IS NULL).

assigned_sales_rep_id.description: Identifier of the sales representative assigned to the customer (sales rep id).
assigned_sales_rep_id.analysis_use: Join to sales rep master (b2b_sales_agents) to attribute revenue, customer counts, and performance metrics to reps. Useful for territory and workload balancing analyses.
business_questions: Which sales rep is responsible for a given customer?
How many customers and what total credit_limit is assigned to each sales rep?
assigned_sales_rep_id.nl2sql_hints: Joinable key to b2b_sales_agents (use exact string match). When users refer to 'sales rep name' convert to id via join. Use IN for multi-rep filters. Handle unassigned (NULL) reps explicitly.

created_at.description: Timestamp of when the customer record was created/ingested (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Time-series analyses: customer acquisition trends, cohorting by sign-up date, windowed joins and period-based filters. Useful for TTL and recency-based segmentation.
business_questions: How many customers were created each month in the last 12 months?
List customers created after a certain date.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Use date/time functions (CAST/FLOOR/DATEPART) for grouping by day/month/year. Normalize timezone context if necessary. Be explicit in NL2SQL about inclusive/exclusive ranges (e.g., >= vs >). Support relative expressions ('last 30 days') by translating to created_at >= DATEADD(day, -30, GETDATE()).

dispatch_id.description: Unique identifier for the dispatch event (primary key).
dispatch_id.analysis_use: Used as the primary key for deduplication and exact lookups; base for joins to shipment events or logs; grouping rarely needed since it's unique.
business_questions: Show the full dispatch record for dispatch_id = 'DISP11001'.
Has a particular dispatch_id been processed in shipment tracking events?
dispatch_id.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match key; treat as string in queries. Use it for WHERE dispatch_id = '<id>' or JOIN ON dispatch_id. No aggregation on this column except COUNT(DISTINCT) if verifying uniqueness.

order_id.description: Reference to the originating B2B order (FK -> b2b_orders.order_id).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to bring in order metadata (customer, order amount, lines); group/aggregate dispatch metrics by order attributes; filter by order_id for tracing.
business_questions: Which dispatches belong to order_id = 'B2BORD4001'?
How many dispatches relate to high-value orders (join with orders to filter by order amount)?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join predicate to zuna_etl.b2b_orders. Stored as nvarchar; ensure consistent casing/whitespace. May be NULL — handle with IS NOT NULL when joining or use LEFT JOIN to preserve unmatched dispatch records.

vehicle_id.description: Identifier for the vehicle assigned to the dispatch.
vehicle_id.analysis_use: Group/filter to measure vehicle utilization, count dispatches per vehicle, identify vehicles with late dispatches.
business_questions: Which vehicle_id handled the most dispatches last week?
What is the average freight_amount for dispatches by vehicle_id?
vehicle_id.nl2sql_hints: String field; may require normalization if multiple formats exist. Use GROUP BY vehicle_id. Consider filtering NULLs and treating short codes (e.g., 'VH106') as categorical identifiers.

driver_id.description: Identifier for the driver assigned to the dispatch.
driver_id.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation to analyze driver performance (counts, delays), and to join to driver master data if available.
business_questions: Which drivers have the highest on-time dispatch rates?
List dispatches assigned to driver_id = 'DR104' in the last 30 days.
driver_id.nl2sql_hints: String column; not declared FK here — confirm driver master table name before joining. Use GROUP BY driver_id and joins on driver reference tables to display driver names.

carrier_partner_id.description: Identifier for the carrier partner (logistics provider) responsible for the dispatch.
carrier_partner_id.analysis_use: Low-cardinality grouping to compare carrier performance (delivery status distribution, average freight, delays). Useful for SLAs and partner KPIs.
business_questions: Compare average freight_amount and delivery rates between LOCAL_TRUCKERS and PRIVATE_LOGISTICS.
Which carrier has the highest fraction of DELIVERED dispatches?
carrier_partner_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality (2 values). Use equality filters and GROUP BY carrier_partner_id. Watch out for inconsistent values / whitespace; consider normalizing/canonicalizing. Good candidate for indexes or inclusion in small dimension tables.

scheduled_dispatch_ts.description: Planned dispatch timestamp stored as nvarchar(50) (appears in 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss' format).
scheduled_dispatch_ts.analysis_use: Key for time-based analyses: schedule adherence, time-window filters, daily/hourly aggregations. Must be cast to datetime for correct comparisons and ordering.
business_questions: How many dispatches were scheduled per day in the last month?
What proportion of dispatches were scheduled during off-peak hours?
scheduled_dispatch_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — always CAST/TRY_CONVERT/CONVERT to datetime in NL2SQL generated queries (e.g., TRY_CONVERT(datetime2, scheduled_dispatch_ts, 120)). Handle invalid or NULL text safely (use TRY_ casts). Use date functions after casting.

actual_dispatch_ts.description: Actual dispatch timestamp recorded (stored as nvarchar(50) but contains datetime-like strings).
actual_dispatch_ts.analysis_use: Used to compute delays, on-time metrics (actual vs scheduled), dispatch duration analysis and event timelines. Requires casting to native datetime for math/filters.
business_questions: Which dispatches were delayed (actual_dispatch_ts > scheduled_dispatch_ts)?
Average delay in minutes/hours between scheduled and actual dispatch times.
actual_dispatch_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also nvarchar — cast with TRY_CONVERT to datetime2 before arithmetic/aggregation. Beware of NULLs for pending/in-transit dispatches. Use COALESCE or conditional logic when computing delay metrics.

seal_no.description: Seal number applied to the consignment/container for security/integrity tracking.
seal_no.analysis_use: Identification/traceability of physical seals; can be used to detect duplicates or mismatches across dispatches and delivery records.
business_questions: Are there any duplicate seal_no values across dispatches (possible reuse)?
List dispatches with missing or suspicious seal numbers.
seal_no.nl2sql_hints: String column; treat as identifier. Use GROUP BY/COUNT to detect duplicates. Normalize whitespace/casing if any inconsistent inputs.

dispatch_status.description: Current lifecycle status of the dispatch (SCHEDULED, IN_TRANSIT, DELIVERED).
dispatch_status.analysis_use: Primary categorical filter and grouping for operational dashboards — status distributions, transitions, SLA calculations.
business_questions: How many dispatches are currently IN_TRANSIT vs DELIVERED?
Trend of dispatch_status over time (daily counts by status).
dispatch_status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field; use in WHERE and GROUP BY. Be explicit about status values in NL queries (e.g., WHERE dispatch_status = 'DELIVERED'). Consider mapping synonyms in NL2SQL layer (e.g., 'in transit' -> 'IN_TRANSIT').

freight_amount.description: Monetary freight cost charged for the dispatch (decimal(18,4)).
freight_amount.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM, AVG), cost-per-dispatch KPIs, cost comparisons by carrier/vehicle/status, joins to orders to compute freight as % of order value.
business_questions: Total freight cost for the last month and average freight per dispatch.
Which carriers have the highest average freight_amount?
freight_amount.nl2sql_hints: Numeric type — safe for SUM/AVG. Watch out for NULLs. When joining to orders, ensure consistent currency/units. Use ROUND/CAST in output if required by NL phrasing (e.g., two decimals).

tracking_no.description: Carrier tracking number for the dispatch (string).
tracking_no.analysis_use: Link to shipment tracking events, external carriers; filter for missing tracking numbers; lookup tracking history.
business_questions: List dispatches without a tracking_no (potential manual shipments).
Show tracking history for tracking_no = 'TRK126499' (join with tracking events table).
tracking_no.nl2sql_hints: String identifier — join key to shipment/tracking tables. May be NULL; treat empty/NULL as missing. Use exact matches for lookup queries.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the dispatch record was created in the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion-time analysis: record age, creation trends, lag between creation and scheduled dispatch. Native datetime allows efficient time filters and indexing.
business_questions: How many dispatches were created each day in the previous month?
What is the average time between created_at and scheduled_dispatch_ts?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Native datetime2 — prefer using created_at for time-range filters to leverage indexes. When comparing to scheduled/actual (which are nvarchar), cast scheduled/actual to datetime for comparisons (or cast created_at to string if necessary, but casting strings to datetime is preferred).

remarks.description: Free-text short remarks/instructions for handling the dispatch (e.g., 'Keep refrigerated', 'Fragile').
remarks.analysis_use: Categorical grouping for special handling requirements, filtering for compliance or packaging instructions. Low-cardinality here — good for quick segmentation.
business_questions: How many dispatches require refrigeration vs fragile handling?
List DELIVERED dispatches that had 'Handle with care' remarks.
remarks.nl2sql_hints: Text field with few distinct values in this dataset. Use exact-match or LIKE for partial matches. Consider normalizing common phrases in preprocessing for consistent NL mapping.

created_by.description: Name of the user who created the dispatch record.
created_by.analysis_use: Audit trail and productivity metrics by creator (counts, errors), detect patterns in who creates dispatches and potential training needs.
business_questions: Which users created the most dispatch records last quarter?
Show dispatches created by 'Aaryahi Dalal' that are still IN_TRANSIT.
created_by.nl2sql_hints: High-cardinality string. Use exact matching for user names or join to a users table (if available) using normalized user id rather than free-text name. Be careful of spelling/casing differences.

event_uuid.description: Unique identifier for the event in the stream (string). Currently distinct for each row in the sample.
event_uuid.analysis_use: Primary key candidate for deduplication and identifying individual events; used in counts, existence checks, and tracing an event through processing steps.
business_questions: Has a particular event (by event_uuid) been processed successfully?
Are there any duplicate event_uuids within a time window (indicating duplicate ingestion)?
event_uuid.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a unique string identifier. Use equality filters (event_uuid = '...') or IN lists. When translating natural language queries, map phrases like 'specific event id' to this column. Consider SELECT DISTINCT event_uuid for dedupe checks. Watch for NULLs — column is nullable in DDL but appears populated in sample.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the domain event occurred (source event time).
event_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for event-time analytics: filtering by time range, grouping by day/hour to compute event rates, computing event-to-processing latency (processed_at - event_ts).
business_questions: How many order events occurred by day/hour during the last month?
What is the distribution of event arrival times for shipment events?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Use date/time functions and range predicates (BETWEEN, >=, <=). When NL queries reference 'events in last 7 days' or 'events between X and Y', translate to filters on event_ts. For latency compute TIMESTAMPDIFF or processed_at - event_ts. Ensure timezone handling if the system expects UTC vs local.

aggregate_type.description: Category of the domain object the event pertains to (e.g., inventory, order, shipment).
aggregate_type.analysis_use: Low-cardinality categorical filter or grouping key for splitting event analyses by domain. Drives conditional joins to different domain tables based on aggregate_type.
business_questions: How many inventory vs order vs shipment events were produced this week?
Which aggregate_type has the highest failure/retry rate?
aggregate_type.nl2sql_hints: Because aggregate_type determines which domain table to join, NL2SQL should implement conditional join logic (CASE/UNION or dynamic join) when queries ask for domain details. Use equality filters or IN lists (aggregate_type IN ('order','inventory')). Normalize casing if users input synonyms like 'orders' vs 'order'.

aggregate_id.description: Identifier of the domain aggregate instance (e.g., order id like B2BORD4008). Likely maps to ids in domain tables depending on aggregate_type.
aggregate_id.analysis_use: Join key to domain tables for enrichment (orders, dispatches, inventory). Used for grouping by aggregate id, identifying top aggregates by event counts, and tracing event history for a specific domain entity.
business_questions: What events relate to order B2BORD4008 and what is their processing status?
Which orders have generated the most events or retries in the last 30 days?
aggregate_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string join key; when translating NL queries that reference order/shipments by id, map to aggregate_id plus filter on aggregate_type='order' (or appropriate). Because there is no enforced FK, make joins conditional and be prepared for missing matches. Use equality predicates for exact id lookups or LIKE for pattern-based requests.

event_type.description: Concrete event name/type (INVENTORY_UPDATED, ORDER_CREATED, SHIPMENT_CREATED) — a higher fidelity event label.
event_type.analysis_use: Grouping/filtering dimension for event-event type analytics, funneling, event counts, and processing metrics by event type.
business_questions: How many ORDER_CREATED events were produced daily in the last month?
Are INVENTORY_UPDATED events being processed slower than SHIPMENT_CREATED events?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; map NL terms (e.g., 'order created') to these canonical values. Use equality/IN filters and GROUP BY event_type. Consider synonyms and pluralization in NL2SQL parser (e.g., 'order creation events' -> event_type='ORDER_CREATED').

payload_json.description: JSON payload for the event content. In the current sample this column contains only '{}' (empty JSON).
payload_json.analysis_use: If populated, used to extract event details (nested attributes) for enrichment or filtering. With current empty payloads, it's not useful for analytics until real payloads are present.
business_questions: What specific attribute values (e.g., quantity changed) were included in inventory update events?
Which events include an error message in the payload?
payload_json.nl2sql_hints: Column is nvarchar(50) and currently contains '{}'. If JSON content exists, use JSON functions (JSON_VALUE / OPENJSON in SQL Server) to extract fields. NL2SQL should only reference JSON fields when non-empty payloads are expected; include guards (payload_json IS NOT NULL AND payload_json <> '{}'). Consider increasing size / changing type to native JSON if richer payloads are expected.

source_system.description: Origin system of the event (e.g., B2B_PORTAL, ERP).
source_system.analysis_use: Filter/group events by source system to compare ingestion rates, error rates, and processing behavior across systems.
business_questions: Which source system generated the most failed events last week?
Are events from ERP being processed slower than those from the B2B portal?
source_system.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string. Map typical NL phrases ('from ERP', 'portal events') to these values. Use equality or IN predicates. If joining to system config tables, this column is the join key.

processed_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the event has been processed by downstream consumers.
processed_flag.analysis_use: Binary filter/grouping: select unprocessed events for replay, compute processed rate, and SLA attainment. Combined with timestamps to compute processing latency and backlog.
business_questions: How many events are currently unprocessed (processed_flag = False)?
What is the processed/unprocessed ratio by event_type this month?
processed_flag.nl2sql_hints: Column type is bit; SQL predicates should use processed_flag = 1/0 or processed_flag = TRUE/FALSE depending on dialect. NL2SQL must map phrases like 'unprocessed' to processed_flag = 0. When combining with processed_at, handle NULL processed_at for unprocessed rows.

processed_at.description: Timestamp when the event was processed by the consumer (nullable if not processed).
processed_at.analysis_use: Used to compute processing latency (processed_at - event_ts or created_at), SLA metrics, and time-to-process distributions. Also useful to identify processing batches and trends over time.
business_questions: What is the average time between event arrival and processing for shipment events?
Which events processed in the last 24 hours had processing time greater than X minutes?
processed_at.nl2sql_hints: Nullable datetime2; for latency calculations guard against NULLs (only compute for processed_flag = 1 or processed_at IS NOT NULL). Use DATEDIFF/TIMESTAMPDIFF or timedeltas in NL2SQL translations. For queries like 'events processed yesterday', filter on processed_at date range.

retry_count.description: Number of retry attempts for processing the event. Stored as decimal(18,4) but acts like a small integer in practice.
retry_count.analysis_use: Measure failure patterns, identify problematic events or aggregate_ids with high retries, and compute retry distributions. Can be used to filter 'events retried more than N times'.
business_questions: Which events have been retried more than 2 times?
What is the average retry_count by aggregate_type or source_system?
retry_count.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as decimal, treat as integer for comparisons. Cast/convert to integer when performing integer logic (e.g., CAST(retry_count AS INT)). Translate natural language phrases 'more than 2 retries' to retry_count > 2. Handle NULLs — interpret NULL as 0 or unknown per business rules.

partition_key.description: Logical partition identifier used for distribution/ingestion (e.g., pk1, pk2).
partition_key.analysis_use: Used to filter or group events by ingestion partition for operational troubleshooting, detecting skew, and partition-level replays.
business_questions: Which partition contains the majority of unprocessed events?
Is there event volume skew across partition_key values?
partition_key.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; use equality or IN. Map NL references like 'partition pk1' to partition_key = 'pk1'. If physical partitioning exists, prefer filters on this column for performance.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the event row was created in this ETL table (ingestion time).
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for ingestion latency analysis (created_at - event_ts), windowed processing, and for time-based retention or cleanup queries. Distinct from event_ts which is the source event time.
business_questions: How long after the source event time are events being landed into the ETL table on average?
Which events were ingested in the last N hours?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Use for 'ingested last X hours/days' type queries. When building NL2SQL translations, differentiate user intent between 'when event occurred' (event_ts) vs 'when ingested' (created_at). Use created_at for retention/deletion predicates.

invoice_id.description: Unique identifier for each invoice (nvarchar). Acts as the primary key for the table.
invoice_id.analysis_use: Primary key for deduplication, exact lookups, joins to payment/credit-note/dispatch tables; used for row-level retrieval and linking.
business_questions: Retrieve full invoice details for a specific invoice ID.
How many distinct invoices exist in a time period or for a customer?
invoice_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) (observed max len 8). Use equality/IN for lookups (WHERE invoice_id = 'INV13001' or IN (...) ). Avoid LIKE for performance. When joining use exact match to zuna_etl.b2b_invoices.invoice_id. Treat as unique; ensure queries expect non-null.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the invoice; nullable (FK to b2b_orders.order_id).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders for reconciliation (invoice ↔ order), aggregate order vs invoice amounts, identify orders with missing invoices, group by order for order-level reporting.
business_questions: Which orders have been invoiced and which have pending invoices?
How do order totals compare to invoice_amounts for order-level reconciliation?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Nullable — handle NULLs (WHERE order_id IS NULL). Use joins: LEFT/INNER JOIN zuna_etl.b2b_orders ON b2b_invoices.order_id = b2b_orders.order_id. Avoid text wildcards for IDs; use exact matches.

invoice_number.description: Business-facing invoice number string (may include formatting like INV-200000); not necessarily unique but likely unique in practice.
invoice_number.analysis_use: Useful for reporting, display, and external reconciliation; can be used to search invoices by human-facing number or prefix patterns.
business_questions: Find invoice(s) by invoice number used in customer communications.
List invoices with a number matching a pattern (e.g., a series prefix).
invoice_number.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Often searched by exact match; support pattern searches (LIKE 'INV-%') but expect slower scans. Consider normalizing/preferring invoice_id for programmatic joins. Watch for NULLs.

invoice_date.description: The date/time when the invoice was issued (datetime2).
invoice_date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for reporting: filter/range (WHERE invoice_date BETWEEN ...), group by day/week/month/quarter for trends, compute month-to-date/period comparisons.
business_questions: How many invoices were issued each month and what is the monthly invoiced revenue?
Show invoices issued in the last X days or within a date range.
invoice_date.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2. Use BETWEEN or >=/<= for range filters; use DATEADD/DATEDIFF or SQL Server functions (CONVERT(date, invoice_date) or FORMAT) for bucketing. Consider indexing invoice_date for time-series queries. Be explicit about timezone if needed; truncate time when grouping by date.

due_date.description: The date when payment for the invoice is due (datetime2).
due_date.analysis_use: Used to compute receivables aging (days past due), classify overdue invoices, and calculate payment terms performance.
business_questions: Which invoices are past due and by how many days?
How much outstanding value is due in each aging bucket (0-30, 31-60, etc.)?
due_date.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2. Use DATEDIFF(day, due_date, GETDATE()) or similar to compute days overdue. Handle NULL due_date (absence of terms). When building aging buckets use CASE expressions or width_bucket logic. Consider indexing if frequent.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer billed on the invoice (FK to b2b_customers.customer_id).
customer_id.analysis_use: Join key for customer-level rollups (revenue by customer), segmentation, churn/AR analysis, and customer collections prioritization.
business_questions: Which customers contribute the most invoice_amount / taxable_value over a period?
Show overdue invoice totals grouped by customer.
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Nullable — handle NULLs. Use as JOIN key: zuna_etl.b2b_invoices.customer_id = zuna_etl.b2b_customers.customer_id. For filters use equality or IN lists. Consider indexing for frequent customer aggregations.

taxable_value.description: Monetary base amount subject to tax (decimal(18,4)).
taxable_value.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM/AVG), per-invoice ratio calculations (tax rate = total_tax / taxable_value), compute totals by customer/time/ status.
business_questions: What is the total taxable_value billed in a period and by customer?
What is the average taxable_value per invoice and how does it trend over time?
taxable_value.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use SUM/AVG/COUNT for aggregations. Protect against division by zero when computing rates. When filtering by monetary ranges, ensure proper decimal literals (e.g., WHERE taxable_value >= 1000.00).

igst.description: Integrated GST component (bigint). In this dataset igst is constant 0.
igst.analysis_use: Usually aggregated as part of total_tax breakdown; currently does not vary and can be ignored for analytics or used to validate tax composition.
business_questions: What is the total IGST collected in a period?
Are there invoices with non-zero IGST (indicating inter-state transactions)?
igst.nl2sql_hints: Type: bigint. Note low variance (distinct=1) — may be historical placeholder. If stored as zero often, queries aggregating tax may be simplified by using total_tax. Still include in SELECT when tax breakdown is required.

cgst.description: Central GST component for the invoice (decimal(18,4)).
cgst.analysis_use: Aggregation and breakdown of tax by component; compute tax ratios per invoice; sum cgst by customer/time/status.
business_questions: What is the total CGST collected by month?
What proportion of total_tax is CGST vs SGST for domestic invoices?
cgst.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use SUM(cgst) and SUM(sgst) to validate total_tax. Handle NULL values and use COALESCE(cgst,0) when summing. Maintain precision for financial reporting.

sgst.description: State GST component for the invoice (decimal(18,4)).
sgst.analysis_use: Same as CGST — component-level aggregation and validation against total_tax.
business_questions: How much SGST was collected in the last quarter?
Compare CGST and SGST totals by region if customer location is joined.
sgst.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use COALESCE(sgst,0) in aggregations. When validating invoices, ensure cgst + sgst + igst ≈ total_tax (allowing for rounding).

total_tax.description: Total tax amount applied to the invoice (decimal(18,4)), typically sum of IGST/CGST/SGST.
total_tax.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liabilities, compute tax-to-sales ratios, validate tax components, or calculate net amounts.
business_questions: What is the total tax collected over a period and by customer?
What is the average tax rate (total_tax / taxable_value) per invoice?
total_tax.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use SUM(total_tax) for reporting. Validate with SUM(cgst + sgst + igst) and account for rounding differences. Use COALESCE when aggregating.

invoice_amount.description: Final invoice amount payable including taxes (decimal(18,4)).
invoice_amount.analysis_use: Primary monetary metric for revenue and AR reporting — SUM(invoice_amount), AVG, per-invoice ranking, time-series and status-based totals.
business_questions: Total invoiced revenue for a period and breakdown by status (PAID/OVERDUE/ISSUED).
Which invoices contribute the top 10% of invoice_amount (top customers/invoices)?
invoice_amount.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use SUM(invoice_amount) and GROUP BY status/customer/date. Beware of NULLs — use COALESCE(invoice_amount, 0). For currency formatting, apply presentation layer formatting rather than SQL.

status.description: Invoice lifecycle status (e.g., PAID, ISSUED, OVERDUE). Low-cardinality textual field.
status.analysis_use: Filtering and grouping to identify AR exposure, counts by status, funnel analyses (issued -> paid), and aging worklists.
business_questions: How many invoices (and what value) are overdue vs paid vs issued?
What is the time-to-pay distribution for invoices that moved from ISSUED to PAID?
status.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) but values are short (max len 7). Use exact equality (WHERE status = 'PAID') or IN lists. Consider mapping to a normalized status table for constrained vocab. Beware of casing/collation — default SQL Server collation is often case-insensitive.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the invoice record was created in the system (datetime2) — may mirror invoice_date for many rows but useful for ETL/incremental loads.
created_at.analysis_use: ETL incremental extraction (WHERE created_at > last_run_timestamp), audit/time of ingestion analysis, and supplemental time-dimension for reporting when invoice_date differs.
business_questions: Which invoices were created within a specific ETL window?
Are there invoices where created_at significantly differs from invoice_date (indicating late entry)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2. Use for incremental extraction filters (WHERE created_at > @last_run). If using both invoice_date and created_at in queries, be explicit which is required. Indexing created_at helps incremental queries.

snapshot_id.description: Unique identifier for each daily KPI snapshot row (primary key).
snapshot_id.analysis_use: Use for row-level identification, deduplication, and safe updates/deletes. Not typically used for grouping or aggregation.
business_questions: Was a specific snapshot_id successfully ingested or updated?
When did snapshot SNAP18002 get created and what were its KPIs?
snapshot_id.nl2sql_hints: Primary key is nvarchar(50). Use exact equality predicates when referencing snapshot_id. Watch for trailing/leading whitespace in string literals; use = 'SNAP18001'. Avoid substring matching unless necessary. Snapshot ids are stable unique keys — use for point lookups.

centre_id.description: Identifier for the operational centre/location the snapshot belongs to.
centre_id.analysis_use: Common grouping/filter column for per-centre aggregation, comparisons, and filtering in WHERE clauses. Useful for joins to a centre/store dimension if available.
business_questions: Which centre had the highest gross sales last month?
How does on-time delivery percentage compare across centres?
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with only a few distinct values — good selective filter. When writing NL2SQL, map user phrases like 'centre', 'store', 'location' to centre_id. If the user asks for 'all centres' or 'top centre', translate to GROUP BY centre_id and ORDER BY aggregate DESC. If mapping to another table is needed, confirm join key (centre_id -> pos_stores.store_id or a centre lookup) because no FK is declared.

date.description: The business date the snapshot covers (snapshot date). Stored as datetime2 but likely represents a date-only value at midnight.
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trend analysis, filtering by ranges, and window calculations. Use for grouping by day/week/month after truncation or casting to date.
business_questions: What were total orders and gross sales between 2025-09-01 and 2025-09-30?
Show weekly averages of on-time percentage for the last 12 weeks.
date.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 column often contains 00:00:00. For NL2SQL, support natural language date ranges (e.g., 'last 7 days', 'September 2025') by translating to WHERE date >= ... and date < ...; use CAST(date AS date) or CONVERT(date, date) for day-level grouping. Provide default timezone/interpretation rules if users don't specify. For performance, prefer range filters on the datetime column.

total_orders.description: Aggregated count of orders for the centre on that date (stored as decimal(18,4) rather than integer).
total_orders.analysis_use: Primary aggregation metric: SUM/AVG for totals, trend lines, per-order KPIs (sales per order). Can be used as denominator for rates.
business_questions: How many orders did centre CTR_VDR_001 process yesterday?
What is the average total_orders per day over the last month?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Although numeric, value is conceptually an integer; watch for decimal type when formatting. Cast/convert to INT for counts in NL2SQL responses or aggregate expressions. When users ask 'count of orders' ensure they are not asking for transactional order counts (this is aggregated snapshot).

total_kg.description: Total weight (kg) of goods handled/sold at the centre on that date.
total_kg.analysis_use: Used in volume-based analyses, ratio metrics (returns_kg/total_kg, wastage_kg/total_kg), and correlation with gross_sales or orders.
business_questions: What was the total weight sold by CTR_AHD_001 last week?
What percentage of shipped weight was returned last month?
total_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) precision; use SUM(total_kg) for aggregates and safe division with NULL/zero checks for percentage calculations. When computing rates, coalesce denominator to avoid division by zero (e.g., NULLIF(SUM(total_kg),0)).

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (monetary amount) for the centre on that date.
gross_sales.analysis_use: Monetary KPIs: revenue trend, sales-per-order, centre performance, and contribution to company totals. Useful as numerator/denominator for financial ratios.
business_questions: Which date had the highest gross_sales for CTR_SUR_001 in Q4?
What is the average gross_sales per order for each centre?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) used for currency. For NL2SQL, map user terms like 'revenue', 'sales' to gross_sales. Use formatting functions for currency display and aggregate functions (SUM, AVG). Watch for nulls; coalesce to 0 where appropriate.

returns_kg.description: Total weight (kg) of returns for the centre on that date.
returns_kg.analysis_use: Assess reverse logistics, compute return rates (returns_kg / total_kg), and identify centres/dates with elevated returns.
business_questions: Which dates had the highest returns_kg and what centres were affected?
What is the monthly return rate (%) across all centres?
returns_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4). Use ratio calculations with total_kg and protect against zero denominators. When users request 'return rate' translate to 100 * SUM(returns_kg) / NULLIF(SUM(total_kg),0). Consider smoothing (rolling averages) for noisy daily returns.

wastage_kg.description: Weight (kg) of wastage (loss/spoilage) recorded for the centre on that date.
wastage_kg.analysis_use: Used to monitor quality/efficiency and compute wastage rate relative to total_kg. Compare wastage across centres or over time to spot issues.
business_questions: How much wastage_kg did CTR_RJT_001 record last month?
Is wastage increasing over time for a specific centre?
wastage_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4). Treat similar to returns_kg for ratio computations. When NL2SQL parsing detects 'waste' or 'spoilage' map to wastage_kg. Use COALESCE/NULLIF patterns when computing percentages.

avg_delivery_time_min.description: Average delivery time in minutes for deliveries from the centre on that date.
avg_delivery_time_min.analysis_use: Performance metric for logistics: compute averages, trends, compare against SLAs, and filter for outliers. Useful as a numeric KPI for ranking centres.
business_questions: What is the average delivery time for CTR_VDR_001 over the last 30 days?
Which dates had average delivery times exceeding 240 minutes?
avg_delivery_time_min.nl2sql_hints: bigint integer type. When users ask 'average delivery time' map to this column directly. For time-window aggregation (hour/day/week) use AVG(avg_delivery_time_min) with appropriate GROUP BY. For human-friendly output, convert minutes to HH:MM if requested. Watch out for NULLs.

on_time_pct.description: Percentage of deliveries that were on-time for the centre on that date (stored as decimal, likely 0-100 scale).
on_time_pct.analysis_use: SLA/quality monitoring: track percentage over time, compute compliance rates, and identify centres with low on-time performance.
business_questions: Which centres consistently maintain on_time_pct above 90%?
Show on_time_pct trend for CTR_SUR_001 over the past quarter.
on_time_pct.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) representing percent values (examples ~70). Map user terms 'on time', 'timely deliveries', 'delivery punctuality' to on_time_pct. When calculating averages of percentages use weight-aware methods if necessary (e.g., weighted by total_orders) — clarify with user whether they want simple average or weighted average.

created_at.description: Timestamp when this snapshot row was created/ingested into the ETL table.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit/ETL monitoring: identify ingestion time, lag between business date and load time, and for incremental refresh diagnostics.
business_questions: When was the snapshot for 2025-10-05 created?
Show rows created in the last 24 hours (ETL runs).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 type. For NL2SQL map phrases like 'loaded', 'created', 'ETL time' to created_at. Use range filters (created_at >= ...). For timezone concerns, confirm server timezone; if comparing to 'today' use server-local datetime semantics or convert to UTC as appropriate.

notes.description: Freeform notes field intended for annotations about the snapshot; currently all values are NULL (100% nulls).
notes.analysis_use: If populated, could provide human comments about anomalies or ETL issues; today it has no analytical value.
business_questions: Are there any notes explaining anomalies for a specific snapshot_id?
Show snapshots with non-empty notes.
notes.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) incorrectly typed for a notes field and currently all NULLs. NL2SQL should treat it as effectively empty — avoid exposing as text. If users ask for notes, clarify whether a textual notes column exists or whether notes are stored elsewhere. Consider a schema correction: change to nvarchar(max) if textual notes are required.

allocation_id.description: Unique identifier for each allocation record (primary key).
allocation_id.analysis_use: Row-level identity; used for deduplication, joining to logs or events, and as the natural primary key for select-by-id queries.
business_questions: Show details for allocation ALLOC9001 (who allocated, qty, status).
How many unique allocations were made in a given time window?
allocation_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key (nvarchar). Use equality filters for point lookups. Consider indexing/primary key enforcement if not present. No casting needed.

order_id.description: Identifier of the B2B order this allocation belongs to (FK to b2b_orders).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders for customer, order date, and fulfillment status; group/filter by order to compute allocated totals per order.
business_questions: Which orders currently have active allocations and what are their allocated totals?
For a given order (B2BORD4001), list all allocations and allocation history.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Nullable nvarchar key — use LEFT/INNER JOIN depending on whether orphan allocations expected. Values appear consistent (B2BORD*). Add explicit JOIN to zuna_etl.b2b_orders on order_id. Consider using EXISTS/IN for membership checks to optimize plans.

order_line_id.description: Identifier of the specific order line the allocation is against (links to b2b_order_lines).
order_line_id.analysis_use: More granular join to order lines to compare requested vs allocated quantities; group allocations by order line to detect partial allocations or duplicates.
business_questions: Which order lines are partially allocated or over-allocated?
How much allocation (kg/units) exists per order line across inventory sources?
order_line_id.nl2sql_hints: Nullable string. Join to b2b_order_lines.order_line_id for line-level attributes. Watch for multiple allocations per line (one-to-many). Use GROUP BY order_line_id to aggregate.

inventory_id.description: Identifier of the inventory record (stock item / location) used to satisfy the allocation.
inventory_id.analysis_use: Join to inventory/products to determine SKU, warehouse, lot; aggregate allocations per inventory item to spot hot SKUs or stock pressure.
business_questions: Which inventory items (INV_1029) have the highest allocated quantity?
Which warehouses/lots are frequently used for allocations for a SKU?
inventory_id.nl2sql_hints: String key — joinable to product_inventory or inventory_items. Normalize values if different prefixes exist. Use equality joins and GROUP BY inventory_id for counts/aggregations.

allocated_qty_kg.analysis_use: Primary numeric measure for aggregation (SUM, AVG), used in capacity planning, allocation rate metrics, and shortage calculations.
business_questions: Total allocated kilograms by day/week/month or by inventory_id.
Average allocation size (kg) per allocation or per allocator.
allocated_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type — use SUM() and ROUND() as needed. Watch NULLs. For ratio metrics combine with allocated_qty_units or ordered quantity from orders. Use appropriate numeric casting in expressions if combined with integers.

allocated_qty_units.analysis_use: Alternate numeric measure for unit-based SKUs; used in counts, reconciliation with ordered unit quantities and unit-based KPIs.
business_questions: How many units are currently allocated per product or order?
Are there discrepancies between allocated units and allocated kg for mixed-measure SKUs?
allocated_qty_units.nl2sql_hints: Integer type — use SUM() and COUNT(). May be NULL for weight-only SKUs; handle with COALESCE(allocated_qty_units,0). Prefer explicit casts if performing arithmetic with decimals.

allocated_at.description: Timestamp when the allocation was made (datetime2).
allocated_at.analysis_use: Filtering and grouping by time (hour/day/week), calculating allocation age, SLA measurements (time from order to allocation), and seasonality analysis.
business_questions: How many allocations were made in the last 24 hours / 7 days?
What is the distribution of allocations over time (peak allocation times)?
allocated_at.nl2sql_hints: Proper datetime column — use date functions (CAST, CONVERT, DATEPART) for grouping. Use range filters (BETWEEN) for optimal performance. Consider creating date-sharded indexes for large tables.

allocated_by.description: Name or identifier of the person/system that performed the allocation.
allocated_by.analysis_use: Group/filter by allocator to measure productivity, error rates, or patterns (e.g., who creates most shortages). Joinable to users/agents tables for richer attributes.
business_questions: Which allocators allocate the most volume or most frequently trigger releases?
Are allocations by a particular allocator more likely to be marked as Shortage?
allocated_by.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar — may contain full names; prefer joining on a stable user_id if available elsewhere. For text matches consider exact equality; avoid LIKE on allocator names if possible. Normalize casing if user IDs vary.

expiry_ts.description: Expiration timestamp for the allocation (stored as nvarchar). Likely indicates when reservation expires if not converted to an order release or consumed.
expiry_ts.analysis_use: After casting to datetime, used to compute time-to-expiry, to find allocations nearing expiry, and to automatically release or reassign allocations.
business_questions: Which allocations will expire within the next X hours/days?
How many allocations expired without being fulfilled in the last month?
expiry_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — must CAST/CONVERT to datetime for time-based filters (e.g., TRY_CONVERT(datetime2, expiry_ts)). Watch inconsistent formats and NULLs. Prefer storing as native datetime in future for efficient filtering and indexing.

status.description: Allocation lifecycle status (e.g., ACTIVE, RELEASED). Low-cardinality indicator of whether allocation is currently holding inventory.
status.analysis_use: Filter and group to separate active reservations from released ones; critical for current inventory availability calculations.
business_questions: How many allocations are currently ACTIVE vs RELEASED?
What proportion of allocations end in RELEASED due to Shortage vs Normal allocation?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical nvarchar with small dictionary — use equality filters (status = 'ACTIVE'). Consider mapping to enumerated values in NL2SQL normalization layer. Low-cardinality values are good candidates for bitmap or filtered indexes.

reason.description: Reason for the allocation (e.g., Normal allocation, Shortage).
reason.analysis_use: Group by reason to analyze root causes (shortage-driven vs normal), and to correlate reason with release/expiry behavior.
business_questions: What percentage of allocations are due to Shortage vs Normal allocation?
Are shortage allocations more likely to get released or to expire?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Categorical nvarchar — use exact matches. Normalize synonyms/casing. Use reason in GROUP BY and WHERE clauses; consider creating a small lookup table for reasons to enforce consistency.

release_ts.description: Timestamp when allocation was released (stored as nvarchar in this table).
release_ts.analysis_use: After casting, used to compute allocation duration (release_ts - allocated_at), rate of releases over time, and to understand lifecycle completion times.
business_questions: What is the average time between allocation and release?
When were allocations released (trend by day/week)?
release_ts.nl2sql_hints: Currently nvarchar — must TRY_CONVERT/CAST to datetime for time arithmetic. Note sample shows a single repeated value; confirm real data distribution. For queries use WHERE TRY_CONVERT(datetime2, release_ts) IS NOT NULL before comparisons. Recommend converting column to datetime type in ETL for efficiency and correctness.

event_id.description: Unique identifier for this event (primary key).
event_id.analysis_use: Used to uniquely identify events, de-duplicate event streams, and as a row-level anchor; rarely used for grouping but essential in joins and event-level filtering.
business_questions: Are there duplicate event_id values indicating ingest issues?
Retrieve full event details for a given event_id for auditing.
event_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar key (max 50) but actual max len 7 in sample. Use equality filters for direct lookups (WHERE event_id = 'EVT8001'). Avoid LIKE for PK lookups. Expect unique results; use SELECT TOP 1 or LIMIT 1 patterns in NL2SQL when returning a single event.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order this event relates to (FK to b2b_orders.order_id).
order_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to orders table; used to aggregate events per order, compute event counts per order, and reconstruct event timelines (ordered by event_ts).
business_questions: Which orders have more than N events within 24 hours?
Show the event history for order B2BORD4001.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar; nullable. Join patterns: JOIN zuna_etl.b2b_orders o ON events.order_id = o.order_id. Use GROUP BY order_id for per-order aggregations and ORDER BY event_ts for timelines. Handle NULLs when events are not linked to an order.

event_type.description: Categorical type of the event (CREATED, ALLOCATED, PICKED, DISPATCHED, CONFIRMED, etc.).
event_type.analysis_use: Key dimension for grouping and filtering; useful for frequency counts, trend analysis, and funnel metrics. Low cardinality — efficient GROUP BY.
business_questions: What proportion of events are DISPATCHED vs CREATED in the last week?
How have counts of PICKED events changed month-over-month?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar low-cardinality; prefer equality/in-list predicates (WHERE event_type IN ('CREATED','DISPATCHED')). When translating natural language, map common synonyms (e.g., 'sent' -> DISPATCHED). Consider upper-case normalization for free-text matches.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the event occurred (event source time).
event_ts.analysis_use: Used for time-window filtering, time-series aggregation (hour/day/week), latency calculations between events, and ordering events chronologically.
business_questions: How many events occurred per day in the last 30 days?
What is the average time from CREATED to DISPATCHED per order?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: datetime2; use range queries (WHERE event_ts BETWEEN X AND Y). For NL2SQL, translate phrases like 'last 7 days', 'previous month', 'between date A and date B' to explicit datetime ranges. Use ORDER BY event_ts when reconstructing sequences and use DATEDIFF or TIMEDIFF equivalents for latency.

event_by.description: Human or system actor who triggered the event (user name or service name).
event_by.analysis_use: Group and rank actors by event volume, identify top contributors or outliers, and audit actions by actor. Useful for security/audit and workload allocation.
business_questions: Which users generated the most DISPATCHED events last month?
List events performed by Aarush Kapoor within a date range.
event_by.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar; high-cardinality. Matching should consider exact equality or prefix/suffix searches if partial names provided. Watch for varying name formats; may require normalization. Combine with event_type or source_system filters for targeted queries.

prev_status.description: Status of the order prior to this event.
prev_status.analysis_use: Used to compute transitions and state-change analytics (prev_status -> new_status counts). Low cardinality - efficient grouping.
business_questions: What are the most common previous statuses before DISPATCHED?
How often do orders move from PLACED directly to DISPATCHED?
prev_status.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar low-cardinality; use in GROUP BY and in transition window queries (e.g., count of rows WHERE prev_status = 'PLACED' AND new_status = 'DISPATCHED'). Consider NULL handling if status unknown.

new_status.description: Status of the order after this event.
new_status.analysis_use: Key for determining current/target state and computing status transition frequencies and success/failure rates.
business_questions: How many events resulted in DELIVERED status in the last quarter?
Which events transition orders into CONFIRMED status?
new_status.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar low-cardinality; commonly paired with prev_status for transition analysis. Use WHERE new_status = 'DELIVERED' for filtering outcome events.

details_json.description: JSON payload with event-specific details. Currently appears to contain '{}' in sample.
details_json.analysis_use: When populated, can contain structured metadata (error details, reason codes, allocation specifics). Can be parsed for richer attributes; currently provides little value in sample data.
business_questions: Extract error_code from details_json for events where processing failed.
What allocation details are present in allocation events?
details_json.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar storing JSON text. In SQL Server use JSON_VALUE/OPENJSON to extract fields. Because sample shows empty objects, NL2SQL should be conservative about assuming fields exist; guard with ISJSON or NULL checks. Keep text equality simple if only '{}' values exist.

source_system.description: Origin system that produced the event (ERP, MOBILE_APP, PORTAL, etc.).
source_system.analysis_use: Dimension for source attribution, monitoring system-specific volumes, and diagnosing issues by source. Low-cardinality and useful for grouping.
business_questions: Which source_system produced the most failed/unprocessed events?
Trend of events coming from MOBILE_APP vs ERP over time?
source_system.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar low-cardinality. Map common synonyms (e.g., 'mobile' -> MOBILE_APP). Use equality or IN-list filters. Consider joining with system registry if exists for richer metadata.

ip_address.description: IP address of the actor/system at the time of the event (string).
ip_address.analysis_use: Used for security/audit analysis (identify suspicious IPs), geolocation enrichment, and detecting correlated activity from same IP.
business_questions: Which IP addresses generated the most events in the last 24 hours?
Are there IP addresses producing many failed events indicating an integration issue?
ip_address.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar; treat as string for equality and prefix filters. For geo-analysis, extract and join to IP-to-location datasets. Beware of IPv6 variations and padded formats; normalize when necessary. Use COUNT(DISTINCT ip_address) to measure actor dispersion.

processed_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether this event has been ingested/processed by downstream pipelines.
processed_flag.analysis_use: Filter for unprocessed events for backlog monitoring, measure processing rates, and trigger reprocessing logic.
business_questions: How many unprocessed events exist right now?
What percentage of events in the last day remain unprocessed?
processed_flag.nl2sql_hints: bit/boolean. Use WHERE processed_flag = 0 or = 1 (or IS FALSE/TRUE depending on SQL dialect). For NL2SQL, map 'processed'/'unprocessed' natural language to boolean filters. Consider null semantics (NULL treated as unknown) and explicitly coalesce if needed (COALESCE(processed_flag, 0)).

created_at.description: Ingest timestamp when this event row was created in the ETL system.
created_at.analysis_use: Used to monitor ingestion latency (created_at vs event_ts), identify delayed ingests, and filter by ingestion windows. Useful for troubleshooting ETL issues.
business_questions: What is the average ingestion lag (created_at - event_ts) across events?
List events created in the ETL in the last hour that are unprocessed.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2. Use range filters and DATEDIFF between created_at and event_ts for latency. When users ask about 'recent' or 'latest', prefer created_at for freshness and event_ts for event chronology—be explicit which is requested in NL2SQL translations.

order_line_id.description: Primary key: unique identifier for each order line.
order_line_id.analysis_use: Point lookup, deduplication, counting distinct lines; join key if other tables reference line-level records.
business_questions: How many distinct order lines were created in a period?
Show details for a specific order line id for investigation.
order_line_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), NOT NULL. Use for equality filters (WHERE order_line_id = '...'). Avoid LIKE scans unless necessary. Because it's unique, GROUP BY order_line_id is rarely meaningful except diagnostic queries.

order_id.description: Identifier of the parent order (links to orders table).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to b2b_orders for customer/order-level attributes; group or filter to aggregate per order; count lines per order.
business_questions: How many lines are associated with a given order?
What is the total line value per order?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), nullable. Use exact-match joins: JOIN zuna_etl.b2b_orders o ON b2b_order_lines.order_id = o.order_id. Watch for NULLs — INNER JOIN drops lines without order_id; use LEFT JOIN if preserving lines is required.

sku.description: Stock-keeping unit code for the product on the line (links to products table).
sku.analysis_use: Primary product grouping/filtering key for sales, price and quantity analytics; join to product master for attributes like category or vendor.
business_questions: Which SKUs contribute most to revenue or volume?
What is average unit price per SKU over time?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), nullable. Join key: JOIN zuna_etl.b2b_products p ON b2b_order_lines.sku = p.sku. Because distinct SKUs are low (10), GROUP BY sku is efficient. Consider handling NULL sku values explicitly (WHERE sku IS NOT NULL) in NL2SQL translations.

item_description.description: Human-readable description of the item (likely derived from product master).
item_description.analysis_use: Labeling in reports, grouping when SKU not provided, deduplication of similar descriptions; useful for user-facing outputs.
business_questions: Show top-selling item descriptions by revenue.
Are there multiple descriptions for the same SKU?
item_description.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), nullable. Use in SELECT and GROUP BY for readable outputs. Beware of duplicates/spelling variations — consider joining to products for canonical naming.

qty_units.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) for unit volume, average units per line, filtering for large orders, per-SKU totals.
business_questions: What is total units sold per SKU or warehouse?
Which order lines exceed a threshold number of units?
qty_units.nl2sql_hints: Type: bigint, nullable. Use SUM(qty_units) and COUNT for aggregates. Handle NULLs with COALESCE(qty_units,0) in expressions. Use integer comparisons for thresholds.

qty_kg.analysis_use: Weight-based aggregation (SUM, AVG), conversions to per-unit weight, picking/packing workload estimates.
business_questions: What is total kg shipped by warehouse or by day?
Which SKUs are highest weight contributors to revenue?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4), nullable. Use SUM(qty_kg) and AVG(qty_kg). Handle NULLs with COALESCE(qty_kg,0). For comparisons or bucketing, be mindful of scale and use appropriate rounding/casting.

unit_price.analysis_use: Pricing analytics: average, min/max, price variance; input to compute line_total when missing or to validate data.
business_questions: What is the average unit price per SKU or batch?
Are there outlier unit prices that need investigation?
unit_price.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4), nullable. Use AVG(unit_price), MIN/MAX, and join to pricing tables for expected price. If line_total inconsistent, compute qty_kg * unit_price or qty_units * unit_price depending on business rule. Handle NULLs with COALESCE.

line_total.description: Monetary total for the order line (decimal) — revenue contribution for the line.
line_total.analysis_use: Primary measure for revenue SUM(line_total); used in ranking, top-N SKU revenue, margin calculations (when cost available elsewhere).
business_questions: Which order lines or SKUs generate the most revenue?
What is total revenue in a period or per warehouse?
line_total.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4), nullable. Prefer SUM(line_total) for revenue aggregations. Validate with computed value (unit_price * qty) if needed. Be null-safe: COALESCE(line_total,0). Monetary rounding/presentation may require CAST/ROUND.

allocation_id.description: Identifier for the inventory allocation attempt/reservation that served this line.
allocation_id.analysis_use: Analyze allocation efficiency, allocation reuse frequency, correlate allocations with fulfillment success or delays.
business_questions: Which allocations serve the most lines or volume?
Do certain allocation_ids correlate with higher cancellations or returns (when joined to returns)?
allocation_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), nullable. Use grouping and counts (GROUP BY allocation_id). JOIN to order_allocations or allocation events if available. Watch for many distinct values; handle NULL allocation_id explicitly.

promised_by.description: Promised delivery/due timestamp stored as nvarchar (contains datetime-like strings).
promised_by.analysis_use: Delivery promise SLA calculations, lead-time computations when compared to created_at, filtering by promised delivery window.
business_questions: How many lines are promised within the next 24/48 hours?
What is average lead time between promise and created_at, or between promise and actual fulfillment?
promised_by.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), nullable but contains datetime strings. NL2SQL should cast to datetime: TRY_CAST(promised_by AS datetime2) or parse with specific format. Handle invalid/missing strings gracefully (ISDATE/TRY_PARSE) and prefer created_at for reliable timestamps.

warehouse_allocated_id.description: Identifier of the warehouse or center allocated to fulfill this line.
warehouse_allocated_id.analysis_use: Group/filter to analyze throughput, capacity, and revenue per warehouse; routing optimizations; performance SLAs per warehouse.
business_questions: Which warehouse ships the most revenue/weight?
Are there warehouses with higher average lead-times or failed allocations?
warehouse_allocated_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), nullable and low cardinality (4 values). Good candidate for grouping and filter predicates; prefer equality filters (WHERE warehouse_allocated_id = 'CTR_VDR_001'). Consider indexing for frequent filters.

batch_id.description: Batch or consignment id associated with this line (traceability / pickup grouping).
batch_id.analysis_use: Grouping for pick/pack optimization, batch-level yield or quality analytics, traceability for recalls.
business_questions: Which batches contain the highest volume or revenue?
Are defects/returns concentrated in particular batches?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), nullable. Use GROUP BY batch_id for batch-level metrics. Join to batch or dispatch tables where available. Null-safe handling required.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the order line record was created (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time-based aggregations (daily/weekly/monthly), freshness/throughput metrics, trend analysis.
business_questions: How do order line volumes and revenue trend by day/week/month?
What is average time from created_at to fulfillment or promised_by?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2, nullable. Use date truncation functions for grouping (CAST(created_at AS DATE) or DATEADD/DATEDIFF in SQL Server). Favor created_at over promised_by for reliable time filtering. Handle timezone or implicit offsets if needed.

remarks.description: Short note about the line (e.g., packaging; limited set of values).
remarks.analysis_use: Categorical grouping, filtering for special handling (Bulk crate, Prewashed), operational reporting for packing instructions.
business_questions: How much revenue/weight is associated with 'Prewashed' or 'Bulk crate' items?
Do certain remarks correlate with higher unit_price or special handling?
remarks.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50), nullable and very low cardinality. Use WHERE remarks = 'Prewashed' or GROUP BY remarks. Consider normalizing if business needs expand (small lookup table).

order_id.description: Unique identifier for each B2B order (primary key).
order_id.analysis_use: Primary key for row-level lookups and joins to order-line, invoice, dispatch and event tables. Use for counting distinct orders and identifying order-level metrics.
business_questions: How many unique orders were placed in a timeframe?
Show order details and status for a given order_id
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string/varchar key. Exact-match lookups (=) are common. Use as join key to b2b_order_lines, b2b_invoices, b2b_dispatches. Ensure queries use equality rather than LIKE. If user asks for 'order B2BORD4001', match exact.

external_order_code.description: Order code used by external systems (mapping/reference to external ERP/partner).
external_order_code.analysis_use: Join or reconcile with external systems; lookups for mappings; de-duplication checks if external system sends duplicate codes. Can be used for filtering when a user references an external code.
business_questions: Find the internal order corresponding to external order code EXT100000
How many internal orders have external codes missing vs present?
external_order_code.nl2sql_hints: Nullable string. Expect exact equality searches. When users refer to external identifiers, map to this column. Watch for variations/prefixes; consider trimming/case normalization.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who placed the order; FK to b2b_customers (nullable).
customer_id.analysis_use: Group and filter orders by customer for per-customer revenue, frequency, lifetime value; join to customer demographic/segment data. Useful for cohorts and retention analyses.
business_questions: What is total net revenue for customer CUST1030?
Which customers placed the most orders last month?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Nullable string. Use as join key to zuna_etl.b2b_customers.customer_id. Queries should handle NULLs (e.g. exclude or label as unknown). Support equality and IN filters for multi-customer queries.

order_ts.description: Timestamp when the order was placed, stored as nvarchar string (format appears 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
order_ts.analysis_use: Primary business timestamp to analyze order placement trends, hourly/daily/weekly aggregations, late/early ordering patterns and time-to-delivery calculations when combined with requested_delivery_ts/created_at. Requires casting to datetime for range filters and date grouping.
business_questions: How many orders were placed on 2025-09-18?
Show daily order counts and gross amounts over last 30 days
order_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — always cast/convert to DATETIME/DATETIME2 before doing range comparisons, date truncation (e.g. CAST(order_ts AS datetime2) or TRY_CAST). Validate format consistency; handle NULLs and invalid strings using TRY_CAST. Prefer created_at if user asks for ingestion/created time.

requested_delivery_ts.description: Requested delivery datetime by the customer, stored as nvarchar string (ISO-like format).
requested_delivery_ts.analysis_use: Used to measure requested delivery windows, SLA planning, compare requested vs actual delivery. Useful for scheduling demand by requested date and backlog planning. Requires casting to datetime for temporal operations.
business_questions: How many orders requested delivery for 2025-09-30?
What is the distribution of requested delivery dates for urgent orders?
requested_delivery_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also nvarchar — cast to datetime for filtering/aggregation. Watch for timezone or format inconsistencies. Use TRY_CAST to avoid failures on malformed values.

order_channel.description: Channel via which the order was placed (WhatsApp, Email, Phone, Sales Rep).
order_channel.analysis_use: Group/filter to analyze channel performance (counts, revenue, cancellation rate). Low-cardinality categorical column — good for GROUP BY and pivoting.
business_questions: Which channel generates the highest net revenue?
What is the cancellation rate by order_channel?
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with small set of values; expect equality/IN filters and GROUP BY. Normalize casing/whitespace when interpreting NL phrases (e.g., 'whatsapp' => 'WhatsApp'). Consider mapping synonyms (e.g., 'phone orders' -> 'Phone').

order_status.description: Current lifecycle status of the order (PLACED, CANCELLED, CONFIRMED, DISPATCHED, DELIVERED).
order_status.analysis_use: Filter and group for funnel/flow analyses (conversion, cancellations), compute rates by time/channel/centre, and SLA metrics. Useful as a dimension in cohorts and operational reports.
