business_questions: How many orders are currently DISPATCHED vs DELIVERED?
Show cancellation percentage over the last quarter.
order_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with defined values — expect exact matches. When parsing natural language, map verbs to statuses (e.g., 'delivered', 'canceled'). Be careful about synonyms/spelling. Use IN lists for multi-status filters (e.g., active orders: PLACED, CONFIRMED, DISPATCHED).

currency.description: Currency code for monetary fields (three-letter ISO). Currently all rows are 'INR'.
currency.analysis_use: Useful if multiple currencies appear in the future; validate currency consistency before aggregating. For now can be used to assert that amounts are INR and no conversion required.
business_questions: Are there any orders in a currency other than INR?
Show net_amount by currency
currency.nl2sql_hints: String, mostly constant. When users ask currency-specific totals, filter by this column. If multiple currencies may appear, ensure conversion logic is applied in queries.

gross_amount.description: Order gross amount (decimal(18,4)) before discounts and taxes.
gross_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to compute total sales, AVG for AOV, GROUP BY channel/centre/status. Compare with discount and tax to validate net calculations. Watch for NULLs.
business_questions: What is the total gross sales by pickup_centre_id this month?
What is the average gross_amount for orders placed via WhatsApp?
gross_amount.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal — use SUM/AVG, and format rounding. For integrity checks compute gross - discount + tax and compare to net_amount. Handle NULL values (COALESCE to 0 when summing if appropriate).

tax_amount.description: Tax component of the order (decimal). Current data shows 0 for all rows.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to report tax collected; audit tax presence. Currently may not affect totals but retain for future changes. Check if taxes are captured elsewhere.
business_questions: How much tax was collected on orders last quarter?
Are there orders with non-zero tax_amount?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal numeric. Because current distribution is all zeros, queries filtering tax_amount > 0 may return none; use EXISTS checks. Handle NULLs and prefer ISNULL/COALESCE when aggregating.

discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to measure discount spend, compute discount_rate = discount_amount / gross_amount, and analyze promotions effectiveness by channel/centre/time.
business_questions: What is the total discount given by pickup centre this month?
Which channels give orders with the highest average discount rate?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal numeric. When computing ratios guard against division by zero (gross_amount = 0 or NULL). Use COALESCE for aggregation. NULLs may indicate no data; treat missing as 0 if domain-appropriate.

net_amount.description: Net payable amount for the order after discounts and taxes (decimal(18,4)).
net_amount.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric to SUM for revenue reports and AVG for AOV. Use to compute contributions by customer/channel/centre and to reconcile with invoices/payments.
business_questions: What is total net revenue by day/week/month?
List top 10 orders by net_amount in the last 90 days
net_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal numeric. Use SUM/AVG and ensure consistency with gross/discount/tax (validate net = gross - discount + tax). Handle NULLs. Use rounding/formatting when presenting monetary values.

pickup_centre_id.description: Identifier of the pickup/fulfilment centre fulfilling the order (4 distinct centres).
pickup_centre_id.analysis_use: Group by centre to analyze throughput, revenue, cancellation and priority mixes. Useful for operational capacity planning and per-centre KPIs.
business_questions: Which pickup centre handled the most URGENT orders?
Show net revenue by pickup centre for the last month
pickup_centre_id.nl2sql_hints: String categorical with small domain — good candidate for GROUP BY and JOIN to a centres/locations lookup table. Normalize values (trim/case) and map synonyms if natural language references centre names.

fulfillment_priority.description: Priority assigned to the order for fulfillment (URGENT, NORMAL, HIGH).
fulfillment_priority.analysis_use: Filter/group to prioritize operations (counts and revenue of high-priority orders), SLA reporting and response-time analyses.
business_questions: How many URGENT orders were placed last week and what was their average net_amount?
Is cancellation rate higher for NORMAL vs URGENT orders?
fulfillment_priority.nl2sql_hints: Categorical low-cardinality. Map natural language priority words (urgent, high) to these exact values. Use IN lists for multi-priority queries. Validate spelling/variants.

created_at.description: Datetime2 capturing when this row was created/loaded into the system (ingestion/audit timestamp).
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for ETL audits, incremental loads, and time-of-ingestion analyses. Use for system-level time filtering (e.g., 'orders ingested last 24 hours'). Can be used as fallback if order_ts is missing or malformed.
business_questions: How many orders were ingested into the system yesterday?
Show orders created_at between X and Y for incremental syncs
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Native datetime2 — prefer using created_at for efficient range filters and partitioning. When users specify 'orders placed on date' prefer order_ts but if they ask ingestion/audit use created_at. When comparing order_ts and created_at, cast order_ts to datetime2.

payment_id.description: Surrogate/unique identifier for the payment record (primary key).
payment_id.analysis_use: Used as primary key to deduplicate and reference single payment rows; not typically aggregated but used in joins and to count distinct payments.
business_questions: How many unique payments were recorded in a period?
Retrieve full payment details for a given payment_id for audit or troubleshooting.
payment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string identifier. Use equality or IN filters for precise lookup. It is the PK so prefer WHERE payment_id = 'PAY1234'. No casting required. When users ask 'payment id' map directly to this column.

invoice_id.description: Identifier of the invoice this payment is applied to (FK to b2b_invoices).
invoice_id.analysis_use: Join key to b2b_invoices to relate payments to invoice totals, due dates, order IDs and customer context; group by invoice to compute amount paid per invoice; filter to payments for a specific invoice.
business_questions: How much of invoice INV123 has been paid and what payments applied to it?
List payments for a given invoice_id and their statuses.
invoice_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; commonly used in JOIN to zuna_etl.b2b_invoices ON invoice_id. Watch out for NULL invoice_id values. When translating NL, map 'invoice number', 'for invoice', 'payments against invoice' to invoice_id.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who made/received the payment.
customer_id.analysis_use: Group and aggregate payments by customer (total amount, count, failed/pending counts); join to customers table for customer attributes or segmentation.
business_questions: What is the total payment amount collected from customer CUST123 over the last quarter?
Which customers have the most failed or pending payments?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; map natural language terms 'customer', 'client' to this column. Use JOIN to customer dimension when needed. Ensure consistent casing/format for filters.

payment_ts.description: Timestamp when payment was initiated/recorded, stored as nvarchar (text) in table.
payment_ts.analysis_use: Primary field for time-based analyses: time-series aggregations (day/week/month), ordering, computing time deltas (requires casting to datetime). Also used to filter date ranges.
business_questions: How many payments were received each day?
Show payments between 2025-10-01 and 2025-10-31.
payment_ts.nl2sql_hints: This is stored as text — always CAST/CONVERT to datetime/datetime2 in NL2SQL queries when doing comparisons, grouping by date parts, or computing intervals (e.g., CONVERT(datetime2, payment_ts) or TRY_CONVERT with fallback). Normalize accepted user date phrases ('today', 'last month') to concrete date ranges before generating SQL. Consider trimming whitespace and handling NULLs.

amount.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM, AVG), min/max, ranking; used to compute collected amounts, average payment size, and outstanding amounts when joined to invoices.
business_questions: What is the total amount collected in the last month?
What is the average payment amount by payment_mode?
amount.nl2sql_hints: Treat as numeric; use SUM(amount), AVG(amount), etc. Mind rounding and currency context (no currency column present). When answering queries like 'payments greater than X', ensure numeric literal is used and consider decimal precision.

payment_mode.description: Method used for the payment (CHEQUE, RTGS, UPI, NEFT).
payment_mode.analysis_use: Grouping/filtering and categorical analysis of payment volumes, failures and reconciliation rates per mode.
business_questions: Which payment mode has the highest failure rate?
How much value is collected via UPI vs RTGS this month?
payment_mode.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string field — ideal for GROUP BY and CASE mappings. Map user phrases like 'by mode', 'payment method' to this column. Use IN(...) for multi-mode filters (e.g., payment_mode IN ('UPI','NEFT')).

bank_ref_no.description: Bank or gateway reference number for the payment (trace/transaction id).
bank_ref_no.analysis_use: Useful for reconciling with bank statements, deduplication detection, and troubleshooting payment gateway mismatches; can be used to group or find specific payment traces.
business_questions: Find payment with bank ref BR12345 and show its status and reconciliation info.
How many payments share the same bank_ref_no (possible duplicates)?
bank_ref_no.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Often used in equality filters for audits. Watch for leading/trailing spaces and consistent formatting. Use LIKE only if partial matching is requested.

status.description: Outcome/status of the payment: SUCCESS, PENDING, FAILED.
status.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate counts and amounts by status; analyze failure rates by mode/customer/time; compute successful collection amounts.
business_questions: How many payments failed in the last week and what was their total amount?
Show pending payments older than X days.
status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as low-cardinality string. Map synonyms: 'successful' -> SUCCESS, 'failed' -> FAILED, 'pending' -> PENDING. Use equality or IN. Beware of inconsistent capitalization — normalize to upper() if necessary.

reconciled_flag.description: Boolean indicating whether the payment has been reconciled to accounting/bank records (bit).
reconciled_flag.analysis_use: Compute reconciliation rates, filter unreconciled payments, and analyze reconciliation backlog and SLA compliance.
business_questions: What percentage of payments are reconciled this month?
List unreconciled payments older than 7 days.
reconciled_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as bit — treat as boolean. In SQL use WHERE reconciled_flag = 1 or = 0. When translating NL, map 'reconciled', 'settled', 'matched' to this column. For nulls, decide whether NULL means false/unset.

reconciled_at.description: Datetime when the payment was reconciled (datetime2).
reconciled_at.analysis_use: Compute reconciliation latency (reconciled_at - payment_ts), measure time-to-reconcile distributions, and filter by reconciliation date ranges.
business_questions: What is the average time to reconcile payments?
Show payments reconciled in the last 30 days and their amounts.
reconciled_at.nl2sql_hints: This is a datetime2 type — use directly for range filters and arithmetic. When computing latency against payment_ts, cast payment_ts to datetime2 first. Handle NULLs where payments are not reconciled. Use DATEDIFF or TIMESTAMPDIFF equivalents depending on SQL dialect.

created_by.description: Name of the user/operator who recorded or created the payment entry.
created_by.analysis_use: Audit and accountability: who recorded payments; group to find operator-level patterns (manual entry errors, volume by operator).
business_questions: Which operator recorded the most payments last month?
Show payments entered by 'Aarush Batra' that are still unreconciled.
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Map NL terms 'entered by', 'created by' to this column. Consider normalizing names for joins with user tables. Use equality or LIKE for partial name matches.

notes.description: Freeform notes about the payment; currently stored as decimal(18,4) but entirely NULL—likely mis-typed column or unused.
notes.analysis_use: If intended as textual notes, could be used for qualitative filters (searching comments). In current schema it's unusable (numeric and null); ignore in numeric analyses until schema fixed.
business_questions: Show payments with notes containing 'dispute' (if notes were text).
List any manual comments recorded against payment PAY123.
notes.nl2sql_hints: Column type is decimal but content is NULL — treat as unavailable. If schema change occurs (to nvarchar), map 'notes', 'comment' to this column and use LIKE '%keyword%'. For now do not rely on this column in NL2SQL outputs.

picking_batch_id.description: Unique identifier for each picking batch (string).
picking_batch_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for grouping, counting batches, joining to allocation/order/dispatch tables if those reference this id. Use for deduplication and drill-down to batch-level metrics.
business_questions: What are the details (centre, picker, timings, lines, weight) for a given picking_batch_id?
How many orders/lines were associated with a specific picking_batch_id after joining to order/allocation tables?
picking_batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (nvarchar). Often unique per row (distinct count == row count here). Use equality joins (WHERE picking_batch_id = 'PB10001') for precise retrieval. If matching external tables, ensure exact string normalization (no extra whitespace) and consider indexing if used frequently as a join key.

centre_id.description: Identifier of the fulfilment centre or warehouse where the batch was picked.
centre_id.analysis_use: Filter and group by centre for performance comparisons, heatmaps, and capacity planning. Low cardinality — good for grouping and efficient aggregation.
business_questions: Which centre has the highest average actual_lines per batch?
How do average pick durations compare across centres?
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: String column with 4 distinct values — ideal for GROUP BY. When joining to centre metadata (pos_stores or similar), expect equality join on centre_id. Use centre_id filters to prune data early in queries for speed.

picker_id.description: Identifier for the picker (employee) who performed the batch.
picker_id.analysis_use: Group and attribute performance metrics (throughput, accuracy, speed) to individual pickers. Useful for leaderboards and outlier detection.
business_questions: Which picker had the highest average lines per hour over the last month?
Which pickers have the highest rate of batches where actual_lines < expected_lines?
picker_id.nl2sql_hints: High-cardinality string; join to employee/user tables (b2b_sales_agents, pos_users) on equality to fetch names/roles. Use careful aggregation (e.g., SUM(actual_lines) / SUM(duration)) to compute per-picker rates. Consider limiting picker list in NLSQL input (top N) to avoid large GROUP BYs.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the picking batch record was created in the system.
created_at.analysis_use: Filter by creation date ranges, compute creation-to-start latency, time-series analysis (daily/hourly volumes). Good for activity and ingestion-time audits.
business_questions: How many picking batches were created each day in the last 30 days?
What is the average time from created_at to started_at?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime type: use date functions (DATE(created_at), CAST/CONVERT, DATE_TRUNC) depending on SQL dialect for grouping by day/week/month. Beware timezone assumptions if present. Use BETWEEN or >=/<= for range filters. When asked for 'recent' or 'last N days', translate to created_at >= CURRENT_DATE - N.

started_at.description: Timestamp when picking for the batch actually started.
started_at.analysis_use: Compute pick duration (finished_at - started_at) and wait times (started_at - created_at). Use for performance and SLA calculations and to detect delays.
business_questions: What is the average and median pick duration (finished_at - started_at) per centre?
Are there significant delays between creation and start during peak periods?
started_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime; durations require subtraction and appropriate units (seconds/minutes/hours). Handle NULLs when batch hasn't started. Use CASE/COALESCE to avoid NULLs. For grouping by time-of-day, extract hour(started_at).

finished_at.description: Timestamp when picking for the batch finished.
finished_at.analysis_use: Used to compute total pick duration and to mark completion. Filter COMPLETED batches by finished_at date for historical performance.
business_questions: How many batches were finished yesterday and what was their average duration?
What is the distribution of pick durations for COMPLETED batches?
finished_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime; may be NULL for IN_PROGRESS/CREATED. Use WHERE finished_at IS NOT NULL to restrict to completed batches. Compute duration as DATEDIFF(second/minute, started_at, finished_at) or equivalent. Consider outliers where finished_at < started_at and validate data.

status.description: Lifecycle state of the picking batch (CREATED / IN_PROGRESS / COMPLETED).
status.analysis_use: Filter by workflow stage, compute completion rates, SLA compliance and current backlog counts (IN_PROGRESS/CREATED). Useful for dashboards and alerts.
business_questions: What percentage of batches are COMPLETED vs IN_PROGRESS today?
How many CREATED batches have not been started for more than X hours?
status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; ideal for WHERE or GROUP BY. Use explicit IN ('COMPLETED','IN_PROGRESS','CREATED') checks. Status should be combined with timestamps (e.g., status='COMPLETED' AND finished_at IS NOT NULL) to ensure consistency.

expected_lines.description: Number of order lines expected to be picked in the batch (planned count).
expected_lines.analysis_use: Compare planned vs actual to measure pick accuracy/efficiency. Aggregate for workload forecasting and capacity planning (SUM(expected_lines)).
business_questions: What is the accuracy rate (actual_lines / expected_lines) aggregated by picker or centre?
How much planned workload (expected_lines) was scheduled per day/shift?
expected_lines.nl2sql_hints: Integer (bigint) used in arithmetic; watch for zero or NULL expected_lines when computing ratios to avoid divide-by-zero. Use SUM(expected_lines) and aggregate ratios with proper denominators (e.g., SUM(actual_lines)/SUM(expected_lines)).

actual_lines.description: Number of order lines actually picked in the batch (observed count).
actual_lines.analysis_use: Primary measure for accuracy and throughput. Use in aggregations (SUM, AVG) and comparisons with expected_lines to compute over/under-picks.
business_questions: Which batches had significant discrepancies between actual_lines and expected_lines?
Who are the top performers by total actual_lines picked over a period?
actual_lines.nl2sql_hints: Integer; use SUM/AVG for throughput metrics. For per-batch accuracy use actual_lines - expected_lines or (actual_lines/expected_lines). Handle expected_lines = 0 and NULLs. When filtering on ranges, use >= / <=.

total_kg.description: Total weight (kilograms) of items in the picking batch (decimal).
total_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate weight by route, centre, picker, or time to understand load distribution and transport capacity needs. Combine with route/priority to optimize vehicle load planning.
business_questions: What is the total weight dispatched by route_id each day?
Are high-transport_priority batches heavier on average than NORMAL batches?
total_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) — maintain precision in SUM and AVG. Use ROUND when presenting results. Be mindful of units and conversion if queries join with other weight columns with different scales. Use SUM(total_kg) for capacity planning queries.

route_id.description: Identifier for the delivery/dispatch route associated with the batch.
route_id.analysis_use: Group and filter by route to analyze weight, lines and pick performance per route. Useful to correlate picking to dispatch performance and to plan route loads.
business_questions: Which routes carry the most weight (total_kg) per day?
Do certain routes have higher rates of pick discrepancies (actual vs expected)?
route_id.nl2sql_hints: String with many distinct values; when joining to dispatch or shipment tables, expect equality join on route_id. Use IN lists for filtering multiple routes and consider limiting large GROUP BYs when route cardinality is high.

transport_priority.description: Priority label for transport/dispatch associated to the batch (e.g., HIGH, NORMAL).
transport_priority.analysis_use: Segment performance and SLA calculations by priority; prioritize HIGH batches for monitoring. Useful for compliance and expedited handling analysis.
business_questions: Are HIGH priority batches completed faster than NORMAL ones?
What proportion of total weight or lines are assigned HIGH priority?
transport_priority.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string (2 values). Good for WHERE and GROUP BY. Pay attention to case and exact strings. Combine with created_at/finished_at for SLA checks.

created_by.description: Human-readable name of the user who created the batch record.
created_by.analysis_use: Audit and traceability (who created the batch), attribution for operational process analysis, monitoring for training or data-entry issues. Can be grouped for creator-level activity counts.
business_questions: Which users create the most picking batches per week?
Do batches created by specific users tend to have different completion rates or discrepancies?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Free-text nvarchar; may contain full names. When joining to user tables, prefer joining on a stable user id if available — names can change and may not be unique. Use LIKE for partial name matching in NL2SQL. Consider normalization (trim/case) when matching.

api_client_id.description: Unique identifier for the API client/application. Serves as the logical ID used to reference a client in logs and joins.
api_client_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filtering key for client-level reports, de-duplication, joins to event/audit tables, and selecting single-client records. Often used in equality filters (WHERE api_client_id = '...').
business_questions: Which api_client_ids are currently active?
What is the last_used timestamp for a given api_client_id?
api_client_id.nl2sql_hints: Treated as nvarchar/varchar. Although the DDL has no PK, api_client_id is unique in practice — use equality filters or IN lists for targeted lookups. Prefer indexing this column for joins/filters. Avoid substring searches unless necessary.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer/account that owns this API client. Links the client to a customer record.
customer_id.analysis_use: Used for grouping clients by customer, counting clients per customer, and joining to customer master tables to enrich with business metadata (e.g., company name, tier).
business_questions: How many API clients does a given customer have?
Which customers have no active API clients?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treated as nvarchar. This is the recommended join key to b2b_customers or ecom_customers. Because there is no FK defined, queries should use explicit INNER/LEFT JOINs on customer_id. Ensure types and formatting match across tables; consider trimming whitespace before join (RTRIM/LTRIM) if needed.

api_key_hash.description: Hash of the client's API key used for authentication. Stored as opaque hashed value for security.
api_key_hash.analysis_use: Not useful for business analytics other than detecting duplicates (same hash across rows). Useful for security audits: count unique keys, detect reused keys across clients.
business_questions: Are there duplicate api_key_hash values indicating key reuse?
How many clients have api_key_hash present vs null?
api_key_hash.nl2sql_hints: Sensitive: avoid returning raw hashes in reports. Treat as opaque nvarchar; equality comparisons are valid (WHERE api_key_hash = '...') but hashes should not be exposed. If searching for a key, compute the hash in application/SQL the same way before comparing. Consider indexing if security audits need frequent lookups.

secret_hash.description: Hashed secret associated with the API client (secondary credential). Stored hashed for security.
secret_hash.analysis_use: Similar to api_key_hash: used for auditing, detecting missing secrets or duplicates, and verifying presence of credentials.
business_questions: How many clients have secrets configured (non-null secret_hash)?
Are there any duplicated secret_hash values across clients?
secret_hash.nl2sql_hints: Sensitive and opaque. Use equality checks only, avoid selecting in UI. Treat as nvarchar. For audit queries that need to check a raw secret, hash the secret using the same algorithm before comparing.

allowed_ips_json.description: JSON array listing allowed client IPs or IP ranges for this client. In this dataset it is always an empty array ([]).
allowed_ips_json.analysis_use: Used to check whether IP restrictions are configured. Can be parsed to extract IP counts or ranges when populated. In current data it flags that no clients have IP restrictions.
business_questions: How many API clients have IP allowlists configured?
Which clients allow only specific IP ranges?
allowed_ips_json.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar JSON. When populated, use JSON parsing functions (JSON_VALUE/OPENJSON in SQL Server) to extract values. Because current values are constant '[]', queries should check for that literal and treat nulls vs empty arrays consistently.

rate_limit.description: Numeric rate-limit tier assigned to the client (requests per unit time). Low cardinality: typical values 100, 200, 500.
rate_limit.analysis_use: Group and aggregate counts by rate_limit to understand distribution, enforce usage policies, and analyze correlation between rate_limit and usage or business tier.
business_questions: How many clients are on each rate_limit tier?
Do clients on higher rate_limit tiers use the API more frequently?
rate_limit.nl2sql_hints: Stored as bigint. Good candidate for GROUP BY and histogram aggregations. Join to a rate_limit metadata table if available to translate numeric tiers into plan names. Watch for NULLs and treat them as 'unassigned' in queries.

scopes_json.description: JSON array of permission scopes granted to this client (e.g., ["orders","inventory"]). Currently identical for all rows.
scopes_json.analysis_use: Used to determine what areas of the API a client can access. When varied, can be parsed and exploded to count clients per scope and to filter clients with a given permission.
business_questions: How many clients have access to the 'orders' scope?
Which clients have both 'orders' and 'inventory' scopes?
scopes_json.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar containing JSON. Use JSON parsing functions (OPENJSON/JSON_VALUE) to test membership. Because this column is constant in the current data, consider normalizing scopes into a child table for efficient filtering when scope variability increases.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the API client was created (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Time-series analyses: cohorting by creation date, trends in new client creation, measuring age of clients. Good for retention and onboarding metrics.
business_questions: How many API clients were created in the last 30 days?
What is the monthly trend of newly created API clients?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as datetime2 — use range filters (WHERE created_at >= ...) and date functions (CAST/CONVERT, DATEPART) for grouping. Consider indexing created_at for time-range queries. Ensure timezone assumptions are documented if UTC vs local times matter.

last_used.description: Timestamp or marker indicating when the client was last used. Stored as nvarchar(50) in this schema, though values look like ISO datetimes.
last_used.analysis_use: Critical for recency analysis (e.g., identifying stale clients). Requires parsing/casting to datetime before comparisons or ordering. Useful to compute days-since-last-use and to drive cleanup or deactivation policies.
business_questions: Which API clients have not been used in the past 90 days?
What is the most recent usage timestamp across all clients?
last_used.nl2sql_hints: Because this is nvarchar, always CAST or TRY_CAST to datetime/datetime2 for date math (TRY_CAST(last_used AS datetime2)). Protect queries from invalid formats and NULLs using TRY_CAST/ISDATE checks. Normalizing to a datetime column is recommended to avoid parsing overhead and errors.

active_flag.description: Boolean indicating whether the API client is active (True) or deactivated (False).
active_flag.analysis_use: Primary filter for operational queries (show active clients only), used in counts and to scope audits. Combine with last_used to identify active-but-unused or inactive-but-recently-used clients.
business_questions: How many active API clients exist today?
Which inactive clients were used recently and might need investigation?
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as bit. Use WHERE active_flag = 1 (or = 0) for filtering. Consider indexing or including in a composite index for typical query patterns (e.g., active_flag + created_at). Watch for NULLs — treat them explicitly (WHERE active_flag = 1 OR active_flag IS NULL) depending on intended semantics.

price_list_id.description: Primary key identifier for a price list entry (unique per row).
price_list_id.analysis_use: Used for identifying and deduplicating records; useful for retrieving/updating a specific price entry. Not typically aggregated but used in WHERE and JOIN when referencing a single record.
business_questions: What are the details (sku, customer, validity, price) for price_list_id = X?
When was a specific price_list entry created and by whom?
price_list_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (nvarchar). Unique index exists / implied PK. Use exact equality in WHERE clauses. When constructing updates or deletes reference this column. Watch for unexpectedly short max_len (6) even though declared nvarchar(50) — values are compact.

sku.description: Product SKU to which the price entry applies; FK to b2b_products.sku.
sku.analysis_use: Common filter and GROUP BY column for product-level price analysis; join key to product metadata. Because only 10 distinct SKUs exist here, grouping by sku will be low-cardinality.
business_questions: What prices are defined for SKU X across customers and time?
Which SKUs have the greatest variation in price_per_unit across customers or time?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar. Use JOIN on zuna_etl.b2b_products.sku for product details. When filtering by SKU, simple equality or IN-list is efficient given low distinct values. Be mindful of NULLs — some rows may not have sku (nullable).

customer_id.description: Customer identifier for whom the price was set; FK to b2b_customers.customer_id. Nullable — absence implies customer_group or general price.
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter and join column for customer-specific pricing; used to compare customer-specific vs. group prices and to compute per-customer price history. Useful in WHERE clauses for per-customer reports.
business_questions: Does customer Y have any custom prices that differ from the group price for SKU X?
Which customers have the most price list entries or most recently updated prices?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar; join to zuna_etl.b2b_customers.customer_id. Null indicates non-customer-specific (group-level) price. For NL2SQL, prefer explicit IS NULL or IS NOT NULL when asking about group vs customer-specific prices. Consider using LEFT JOIN when enriching with customer info.

customer_group.description: Customer group classification for which this price applies (e.g., RETAIL or WHOLESALE).
customer_group.analysis_use: Low-cardinality grouping key for comparing prices across groups; used to apply group-level pricing rules or to filter rows that apply to all members of a group.
business_questions: What is the average price_per_unit for RETAIL vs WHOLESALE for SKU X?
Are there group-level prices that are more favorable than customer-specific prices?
customer_group.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar with only two main values; treat absent customer_group and non-null customer_id as indicators of a customer-specific record. Use equality filters (customer_group = 'RETAIL'). Consider prioritization logic in NL2SQL: customer-specific price should override group price when both exist — express explicitly.

start_ts.description: Validity start timestamp for the price entry, stored as an nvarchar (ISO-like datetime string).
start_ts.analysis_use: Key for temporal filtering (which price is active at a given time), ordering price history, and computing durations. Must be cast to datetime for date comparisons and range calculations.
business_questions: Which price entries become active within a given date range?
What was the effective price for SKU X on date D (requires start_ts <= D < end_ts)?
start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as nvarchar, values look like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' and must be CAST/CONVERT to datetime in queries (e.g., TRY_CAST(start_ts AS datetime2)) to avoid string-sort pitfalls. Be defensive: use TRY_CAST to handle malformed values. Indexes on start_ts are not effective until converted — consider adding a native datetime column for performance.

end_ts.description: Validity end timestamp for the price entry, stored as an nvarchar (ISO-like datetime string).
end_ts.analysis_use: Used with start_ts to determine active windows; used for temporal joins and filtering out expired entries. Needs conversion to datetime for range checks and overlap calculations.
business_questions: Which price entries have expired before date D or are active after date D?
Which upcoming price changes (end_ts in future) will affect a customer or SKU?
end_ts.nl2sql_hints: Same considerations as start_ts: cast to datetime (TRY_CAST/CONVERT) before comparisons. Handle open-ended validity (NULL or special values) if present. For active-price queries use condition: TRY_CAST(start_ts AS datetime2) <= @date AND TRY_CAST(end_ts AS datetime2) > @date.

price_per_unit.description: Decimal price per unit (currency indicated in currency column).
price_per_unit.analysis_use: Primary numeric measure for aggregations (AVG, MIN, MAX), comparisons, trend analysis, and revenue modelling when joined with quantities from orders. Used to detect discounts and price anomalies.
business_questions: What is the average, minimum and maximum price_per_unit for SKU X in period Y?
Which customers pay the highest/lowest price_per_unit for SKU X?
price_per_unit.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) — safe to aggregate. When comparing group-level vs customer-specific prices, use explicit COALESCE/priority logic. Watch for currency consistency (currency column is uniformly INR here). Use numeric comparisons and rounding as needed (e.g., ROUND(price_per_unit, 2)).

currency.analysis_use: Used to ensure consistent currency when aggregating prices or comparing across price entries; can act as a filter if multiple currencies exist. In this dataset it's constant (INR).
business_questions: Are there price list entries in currencies other than INR?
Convert price_per_unit to a base currency for cross-currency comparisons (requires FX rates from another table).
currency.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but max_len = 3 in practice. Because currency is single-valued here, many NL2SQL queries can safely assume INR but should still include currency checks if the schema allows other currencies in future. For cross-currency questions join to an FX rates table.

min_order_qty.description: Minimum quantity required to place an order at this price (decimal).
min_order_qty.analysis_use: Filter or group by threshold requirements; aggregate to find common MOQ values; combine with order quantity data to find orders that qualify for a given price.
business_questions: Which price entries require min_order_qty above N and which customers are affected?
What proportion of price entries have non-zero minimum order quantities?
min_order_qty.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4). Use numeric comparisons (>=, >). Watch for NULLs — treat NULL as no MOQ or unknown depending on business semantics. For NL2SQL, explicitly state whether NULL means 'no minimum' or 'not set'.

created_by.description: User who created the price_list entry (audit field).
created_by.analysis_use: Used for auditing and change-tracking; filter to see entries created by specific users or systems. Here it is single-valued ('admin').
business_questions: Which price entries were created by user X?
How many price entries were created by automated processes vs manual users?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar. Expect low cardinality. If auditing changes over time is required, consider joining to an audit/history table — created_by only shows initial creator. If values are single-valued, queries can omit this column for most analyses unless auditing is required.

created_at.description: Datetime when the price list entry was created (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for auditing, determining recency of entries, and temporal analyses (e.g., how frequently price lists are created). Use for ordering and filtering by creation date.
business_questions: How many price entries were created in month M?
When was the most recent price entry added for SKU X or customer Y?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Native datetime2 — use directly for range filters and ordering (e.g., WHERE created_at BETWEEN ...). Prefer this column over start_ts for queries about creation time. Beware of timezone assumptions if data spans zones.

notes.description: Freeform notes about the price entry. Declared as decimal(18,4) in the DDL but all values are NULL — appears mis-typed or unused.
notes.analysis_use: If populated with textual notes it would be used for audit/contextual information and filtered via LIKE searches; currently no analytical value since 100% NULL.
business_questions: Are there special conditions or comments associated with price_list entries (e.g., promotional codes, contract references)?
Which price entries include notes indicating manual overrides?
notes.nl2sql_hints: DDL shows decimal(18,4) but expected to be text; confirm actual data type before constructing NL2SQL. Currently always NULL — NL2SQL systems should avoid assumptions about content. If notes becomes populated as text, use CONTAINS or LIKE for keyword search; if numeric, treat accordingly.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit identifier for the product. Serves as the primary key in this table and the natural key to link to product master and transactional tables.
sku.analysis_use: Grouping key for all aggregations and joins. Use for filtering (e.g., particular SKUs or SKU lists), grouping when computing rank or contribution, and joining to product dimension tables to get names/categories/attributes.
business_questions: What are the top 10 SKUs by net_sales?
For a given SKU, what are its gross_sales, discount_amount, tax_amount and net_sales?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Data type nvarchar(50) but actual length observed small (<=6) — trim and match casing consistently when joining (use exact match unless joins are normalized). Use IN (...) for multi-SKU filters, and ensure you do not aggregate the SKU column (group by sku when aggregating measures). When writing NL2SQL, treat sku as the primary join key to product master and order lines; be careful about whitespace and null safety (sku is NOT NULL).

gross_sales.description: Total gross monetary sales for the SKU (pre-discounts and before applying tax semantics used in downstream reporting). Stored as decimal(18,4).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for ranking, contribution, and trend reconciliation (where time data is available in other tables). Used in ratios (discount/gross, tax/gross). Typical operations: SUM, AVG, RANK, PERCENT_RANK, and percent-of-total calculations.
business_questions: Which SKUs generate the highest gross sales?
What percentage of total gross sales is contributed by each SKU?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) — use SUM(gross_sales) for totals and COALESCE(gross_sales,0) to avoid nulls. When dividing to compute rates, guard against division-by-zero (e.g., CASE WHEN gross_sales = 0 THEN NULL ELSE discount_amount/gross_sales END). If joining to transactional tables that also have gross values, avoid double counting by understanding grain (this table is pre-aggregated per SKU).

discount_amount.description: Total discounts (monetary amount) applied to the SKU. Stored as decimal(18,4).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute discount rates and discount impact on revenue (discount_amount as absolute and relative measure). Useful for filtering SKUs with unusually high discounts and for grouping by discount band.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the largest absolute discount amounts?
Which SKUs have the highest discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales)?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal with possible NULLs — use COALESCE(discount_amount, 0) when summing. To compute a discount percentage use: CASE WHEN gross_sales IS NULL OR gross_sales = 0 THEN NULL ELSE discount_amount / gross_sales END. When phrasing NL2SQL, clarify whether discount is absolute or percent; avoid assuming discount is always less than gross (data errors may exist).

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected or applied for the SKU. Stored as decimal(18,4).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute tax rates (tax_amount / gross_sales), to isolate tax impact on net sales, and to reconcile with tax reporting. Can be aggregated or compared across SKUs to see tax burden patterns.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest tax amounts in absolute terms?
What is the tax percentage by SKU (tax_amount / gross_sales)?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal and nullable — use COALESCE(tax_amount, 0) for arithmetic. When computing ratios, guard against zero or null gross_sales. Clarify tax definition in NL2SQL prompts (is tax included in gross or added on top?) because that affects net calculations. Use SUM(tax_amount) when calculating totals across SKUs and ensure joins don’t multiply tax totals by joining to transactional aggregates.

net_sales.description: Net sales (monetary) for the SKU after accounting for discounts/taxes according to the ETL logic. Stored as decimal(18,4). Represents the end metric for revenue reporting at SKU level.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary KPI for ranking and performance analysis; used for contribution-to-total calculations, trend comparisons (if combined with time-series tables), and margin inferences when combined with cost data. Aggregate with SUM or use as-is if querying a single SKU.
business_questions: Which SKUs contribute most to net revenue?
How much net revenue would be lost if the top N discounted SKUs reduced their discounts by X%?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal nullable — use COALESCE(net_sales, 0) in totals. Because net_sales may already be computed in ETL, do not recompute net as gross - discount + tax unless verifying or reconciling (specify the expected formula in NL2SQL). When aggregating across other grains, ensure aggregation mismatch is avoided (this table is at SKU grain). Use appropriate numeric formatting and casting when returning results to end users.

date.description: The business date for the aggregated row (datetime2). Acts as the primary key in this table; each row represents metrics for this date.
date.analysis_use: Primary dimension for time-series analysis, filtering by ranges, grouping by day/week/month/quarter/year, window functions (moving averages), time-based joins to other tables.
business_questions: What were net sales on a given date or over a date range?
Which dates (days of week/month) have the highest average order quantity or sales?
date.nl2sql_hints: SQL Server type datetime2 NOT NULL. When users ask for daily/monthly aggregations, cast or truncate appropriately (e.g., CAST([date] AS date) for day; DATEPART/DATETIME functions for week/month in SQL Server). Use BETWEEN for inclusive ranges. Because date is unique, GROUP BY date will return original rows — to aggregate across products you must group by CAST(date AS date) if time portion exists.

product_id.description: Identifier for the product (nvarchar(50)). In the snapshot this is effectively a single product (TOM001). Column is nullable in DDL but dataset shows consistent value.
product_id.analysis_use: Filter or group by product. Join key to product master tables to enrich with product name, category, vendor. Useful for multi-product roll-ups if table contained more products.
business_questions: What are the daily sales for product TOM001?
How does this product perform compared to other products (when joined to other product-wise tables)?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: String column, nullable. In NL2SQL, match product identifiers or names via exact matches or JOIN to product master for name-based filters. Be aware this table currently holds a single product — queries filtering by product may be implicit. Use equality (product_id = 'TOM001') or IS NOT NULL when checking presence.

total_orders.description: Aggregated count/measure of orders for the product on the date (stored as decimal(18,4) rather than integer).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM), average (AVG), trending, compute per-order metrics (e.g., total_qty_kg / total_orders). Used as denominator for per-order KPIs and capacity planning.
business_questions: How many orders were placed for this product each day?
What is the average number of orders per week/month and how is that trending?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal(18,4). Although it represents a count, it is stored as decimal; when writing NL2SQL, avoid integer-only assumptions and handle decimals (e.g., ROUND or CAST to INT if you need whole order counts). Protect against division by zero when using as denominator (use NULLIF(total_orders,0) or CASE).

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the product on the date (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Used for volume trend analysis, inventory planning, compute average kg per order, and revenue per kg (gross_sales / total_qty_kg). Aggregate by sum/avg over ranges and compute rolling averages.
business_questions: How many kilograms of the product were sold each day and how does that vary seasonally?
What is the average kg per order and has it changed over time?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4). Use SUM(total_qty_kg) for multi-day aggregates. When computing per-unit metrics (e.g., gross_sales / total_qty_kg), guard against zero or null quantities. Consider unit conversions if downstream consumers expect grams or units.

gross_sales.description: Gross sales amount before discounts and taxes for the product on that date (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for top-line analysis, aggregation across periods, comparisons, and computing discount/tax rates. Useful for verifying invoice totals and revenue recognition.
business_questions: What is the daily gross revenue for this product?
How much gross revenue was lost to discounts over a given period?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4). When computing rates use COALESCE or NULLIF to avoid divide-by-zero (e.g., discount_amount / NULLIF(gross_sales,0)). For currency formatting or rounding use ROUND(gross_sales,2). When querying ranges, be aware of negative values (returns/credit notes) if present in source.

discount_amount.description: Total discount applied to the product sales on that date (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Compute discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales), total discount impact over periods, detect promotional effects, and reconcile discounts to promotions or coupon tables.
business_questions: How much discount was applied to this product each day?
What percentage of gross sales was given away as discount this month?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4). Use as SUM/AVG and compute derived rate with NULLIF(gross_sales,0). When presenting percentage, multiply by 100 and round. Watch for cases where discount_amount exceeds gross_sales (data quality) and flag anomalies.

tax_amount.description: Total tax charged on the product sales on that date (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Compute effective tax rate (tax_amount / gross_sales), reconcile tax collections to finance systems, and aggregate taxes for reporting and remittance.
business_questions: How much tax was collected from sales of this product each day?
What is the effective tax percentage applied to this product over a period?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4). Compute rates using NULLIF(gross_sales,0). Tax may need to be joined or compared with invoice-level tax records (b2b_invoices) for reconciliation. Beware rounding differences between line-level and invoice-level tax computations.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount after discounts and taxes for the product on that date (decimal(18,4)).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line revenue metric used for trend analysis, KPI computation, variance analysis against gross sales, and as target/actual comparisons. Sum/average and period comparisons.
business_questions: What were the net sales per day and how do they trend month-over-month?
Are there days where net_sales deviates unexpectedly from gross_sales - discount + tax?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4). Expect net_sales ≈ gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount; include tolerance when validating. Use SUM(net_sales) for period totals. When users ask for growth, compute percentage change using LAG() over ordered dates or compare period aggregates.

sku.description: Primary product identifier (alphanumeric code used as the table's primary key).
sku.analysis_use: Primary join key to transactions and other product-centric tables; used for exact-match filtering, grouping, and lookups. Typically used in WHERE sku = '...' or IN (...) clauses.
business_questions: What is the current price and stock_qty for SKU 'APP009'?
Show product details for a list of SKUs returned by an order or sales query.
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as exact-match string key. Use equality or IN for filters. Watch for case-sensitivity depending on DB collation; prefer case-insensitive comparisons or normalize input. Use sku for all joins to order_lines, product_sales, inventory tables.

plucode.description: Numeric PLU/code commonly used at POS systems; alternative product identifier (bigint).
plucode.analysis_use: Can be used to join/lookup records from POS systems or legacy tables that reference PLU instead of SKU. Useful for grouping or mapping to PLU-based reports.
business_questions: Which SKUs map to a given PLU code (e.g., plucode = 4011)?
Generate a price list keyed by PLU for POS integration.
plucode.nl2sql_hints: Numeric type — use numeric comparisons. May be NULL; guard joins/filters accordingly. When users say 'PLU' or 'POS code' map to this column. Convert or cast if user input arrives as text.

name.analysis_use: Used for display, text filters, fuzzy or partial matches (LIKE, ILIKE), grouping by product family if naming conventions exist, and exporting product lists.
business_questions: List all products containing 'Apple' in the name.
Return names for a set of SKUs in a purchase order for printing.
name.nl2sql_hints: Free-text column — support case-insensitive partial matches (LIKE '%...%') and exact match. Be prepared to map natural language queries with synonyms (e.g., 'apples' → name LIKE '%apple%'). Watch for NULLs and trim whitespace.

uom.analysis_use: Group and filter products by packaging/measure; useful to normalize inventory and aggregate stock_qty by unit type. Low cardinality, good for GROUP BY.
business_questions: How much total stock do we have for items sold by 'kg'?
How many SKUs are sold in 250g vs 500g packets?
uom.nl2sql_hints: Small set of distinct values — prefer equality or IN filters. Be mindful of synonyms (e.g., 'kilogram' vs 'kg') and normalization. Use uom when aggregating stock_qty to avoid mixing incompatible units unless converted.

pos_price.description: Point-of-sale price for the product (decimal with 4 fractional digits).
pos_price.analysis_use: Main pricing metric used for revenue estimations, price-list exports, price-change comparisons, and tax-inclusive price calculations. Aggregations (MIN, MAX, AVG) and arithmetic (pos_price * quantity) are common.
business_questions: What is the current POS price for SKU 'BRN007'?
Which products have pos_price above 25.00?
pos_price.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type — use numeric comparisons and ROUND when formatting. Map natural-language 'price', 'selling price', or 'rate' to this column. Watch NULLs for items without a set price. For currency displays include rounding to 2 decimals if needed.

tax_rate.description: Applicable tax rate for the product (decimal). In this sample it is constant at 0.05.
tax_rate.analysis_use: Compute tax amounts (pos_price * tax_rate), aggregate tax liability, or display tax-inclusive price. Typically treated as a multiplier in pricing calculations.
business_questions: What is the tax amount for a product with a price of X?
List products with tax_rate different from the default (to find exceptions).
tax_rate.nl2sql_hints: Decimal multiplier — apply directly in arithmetic. If tax_rate is constant, queries can use a literal; still prefer column for clarity. When users ask for 'tax' map to this column; check for NULLs or missing rows where default tax should apply.

stock_qty.description: On-hand stock quantity for the SKU (decimal to allow fractional units).
stock_qty.analysis_use: Inventory-level reporting and aggregation (SUM across SKUs), threshold checks vs reorder_point, stock valuation when multiplied by cost/pos_price, and trend snapshots when joined to history tables.
business_questions: Which products have stock_qty less than reorder_point?
What is the total stock_qty across all active SKUs?
stock_qty.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type; use numeric comparisons and aggregations (SUM, AVG). Be careful when comparing with reorder_point due to type differences (reorder_point is bigint). Handle NULLs as zero or unknown depending on business rule. For unit conversions, consider uom.

reorder_point.description: Reorder threshold for the SKU (bigint) — if stock_qty <= reorder_point, consider reordering.
reorder_point.analysis_use: Filter for replenishment candidates, generate procurement lists, and calculate safety stock alerts. Often used in WHERE stock_qty <= reorder_point queries.
business_questions: Which SKUs should be reordered today (stock_qty <= reorder_point)?
How many SKUs have reorder_point set above 50?
reorder_point.nl2sql_hints: Integer type — compare with stock_qty (cast if necessary). Null reorder_point implies missing threshold; treat according to policy (exclude or flagged). Map natural-language 'reorder level', 'reorder threshold', 'safety stock' to this column.

active_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating if the product is active/available for sale.
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter active vs inactive products for catalog exports, pricing updates and inventory checks. Useful to exclude inactive SKUs from sales or ordering reports.
business_questions: How many active products do we have?
Show active SKUs with stock_qty below their reorder_point.
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Bit/boolean type — in SQL Server use = 1 or = 0; other DBs may use TRUE/FALSE. Sample shows all True; still include this filter when users ask for 'active' or 'available'. Account for NULLs (treat as inactive or unknown depending on rules).

updated_at.description: Timestamp of the last update to the product record (datetime2).
updated_at.analysis_use: Recency filters (changed in last N days), incremental sync or CDC checks, and audit or SCD processing. Useful for ordering results by most recently updated.
business_questions: Which products were updated in the past 7 days?
List top 10 most recently updated products.
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime type — use range predicates (>=, BETWEEN) and date arithmetic. Consider timezone normalization if necessary. Map user phrasing like 'last week', 'yesterday', 'in November' to appropriate date ranges. Handle NULL updated_at as unknown/never-updated.

inspection_id.description: Unique identifier for each quality inspection event (primary key).
inspection_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping key for row-level queries. Use for identifying unique inspections, counts, deduplication, and as the basis for joins back to this table.
business_questions: How many inspections were performed in a given period?
Retrieve the full inspection record given an inspection_id.
inspection_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar primary key. Use equality filters or IN-lists. Expect short strings (max len ~8). No casting needed.

batch_item_id.description: Identifier for the batch item being inspected (likely links to a specific item in a picking/packing batch).
batch_item_id.analysis_use: Filter/group to analyze inspections per batch item. Use as a join key to batch or order-allocation tables to trace handling context. Useful for deduping multiple inspections on same item.
business_questions: Which batch items have the highest rejection counts?
Are rejections concentrated in particular batches or picking runs?
batch_item_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar. Values resemble order-line IDs (e.g., B2BOL...). Not declared as FK; NL2SQL should allow optional joins to b2b_order_lines or b2b_order_allocations on matching string fields. Use safe left joins when the foreign relationship is not enforced.

order_line_id.description: Identifier of the order line associated with the inspected item (maps to order line records).
order_line_id.analysis_use: Group and aggregate quality metrics at the order-line or product level after joining to order/order_line/product tables. Filter to investigate quality issues for specific orders.
business_questions: Which order lines (or SKUs) have repeated inspections or high rejection rates?
For a given order_line_id, show all inspection outcomes and corrective actions.
order_line_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar containing order-line keys; common join target is b2b_order_lines.order_line_id or order_items/order_lines tables in the schema. Use left join and avoid inner join unless referential integrity is known. Treat as exact-match string join.

inspector_id.description: Identifier of the inspector who performed the inspection.
inspector_id.analysis_use: Group by inspector to compute counts, acceptance rates, average visual_score, and identify outliers or training needs. Filter to examine an inspector's historical inspections.
business_questions: Which inspectors have the highest rejection/acceptance rates?
How does average visual_score per inspector compare to peers?
inspector_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with high cardinality (~49 distinct for 50 rows) — often used in GROUP BY. If staff user table exists, join on string key (use left join). Avoid assuming stable enumerations; treat as free strings in NL2SQL parsing.

inspection_ts.description: Timestamp when the inspection occurred; stored as nvarchar but values appear to be datetime strings.
inspection_ts.analysis_use: Critical for trend analysis, time series (daily/weekly/monthly) grouping, SLA monitoring (time between created_at and inspection_ts). Must be cast to datetime for range filters, ordering, and date-based grouping.
business_questions: How many inspections occurred per day/week/month?
What is the trend of acceptance_rate over time?
inspection_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — NL2SQL should cast to a datetime type (e.g., TRY_CAST(inspection_ts AS datetime2) or parse with safe functions) before applying time filters, GROUP BY date_trunc operations, or comparisons. Handle parsing errors and nulls; use TRY_CAST or ISDATE checks. When generating SQL, prefer converting once in a CTE to avoid repeated casts.

temp_c.description: Measured temperature in Celsius at inspection time (decimal(18,4)).
temp_c.analysis_use: Numeric metric for aggregations (avg/min/max/stddev), correlation analysis with acceptance_flag and visual_score, and threshold-based filtering (e.g., temp_c > X indicating potential spoilage).
business_questions: What is the distribution of inspection temperatures and do high temperatures correlate with rejections?
How often are temperatures outside acceptable thresholds?
temp_c.nl2sql_hints: Numeric type — use in aggregations and WHERE comparisons. Be aware of scale (4 decimal places) but typical values are small. Use aggregate functions (AVG, MAX, MIN, STDDEV) and joins for conditional averages (AVG(temp_c) FILTER/CASE WHEN acceptance_flag=1).

visual_score.description: Quantitative visual inspection score (bigint) representing appearance or quality rating.
visual_score.analysis_use: Aggregate (avg, median, percentiles), group by bins (score ranges), correlate with acceptance_flag and temp_c, and monitor inspector scoring patterns.
business_questions: What is the average visual_score for accepted versus rejected items?
Which items (or inspectors) produce the lowest visual_scores?
visual_score.nl2sql_hints: Integer type; common aggregations and groupings. Consider bucketing into ranges for NL2SQL (e.g., CASE WHEN visual_score >= 90 THEN 'Excellent' ...). Watch for NULLs; include IS NOT NULL filters when computing averages.

acceptance_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the inspected item was accepted (True) or rejected (False).
acceptance_flag.analysis_use: Primary target for quality metrics (acceptance/rejection rates), groupings, and conditional aggregates. Use as outcome variable in comparisons and correlations.
business_questions: What is the overall acceptance rate across inspections?
Which corrective actions are associated with rejected inspections?
acceptance_flag.nl2sql_hints: Bit/boolean. Use direct boolean filters (WHERE acceptance_flag = 1 or = 0). When generating natural-language queries, map words like 'accepted', 'rejected' to these boolean values. Consider NULL semantics (null means unknown) — include explicit IS TRUE / IS FALSE checks.

rejection_reason.description: Categorical reason for rejection (e.g., 'Spoilage'); heavily null where acceptance_flag is true or not rejected.
rejection_reason.analysis_use: Counts and distributions of rejection reasons, root-cause analysis for rejections, and cross-tabulation with corrective_action and temp_c. High null rate indicates only populated for rejected rows.
business_questions: What are the top reasons for rejection and their frequencies?
How often is 'Spoilage' cited and what temperatures or products accompany it?
rejection_reason.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with many nulls and low distinct values. Use WHERE rejection_reason IS NOT NULL to restrict to rejections. Normalize text comparisons (trim, upper/lower) if values may vary. For NL2SQL, map human terms like 'spoilage' to exact string matches.

photo_url.description: Intended to store URL to inspection photo but currently typed as decimal(18,4) and 100% null in this dataset.
photo_url.analysis_use: When populated, useful for audit and visual review; currently unusable. Should be a varchar/URL type; until fixed, cannot be meaningfully aggregated or filtered.
business_questions: Provide the photo link for a given inspection_id (when available).
How many inspections include a photo attachment?
photo_url.nl2sql_hints: Currently mis-typed as decimal and fully null — NL2SQL should treat this column as unavailable. If schema corrected to nvarchar/varchar, treat as string and return as-is; do not attempt numeric comparisons. Use IS NOT NULL to find available photos.

microbial_test.description: Boolean indicator whether a microbial test was performed/positive (True/False).
microbial_test.analysis_use: Filter/group by microbial test result, compute rates of microbial positives among rejections, and correlate with temp_c/visual_score.
business_questions: What proportion of inspections had microbial_test = True and how many of those were rejected?
Are microbial test positives concentrated by inspector or over time?
microbial_test.nl2sql_hints: Bit/boolean. Use direct filters (WHERE microbial_test = 1). Be explicit about what True means in business context (performed vs positive) — check documentation. Handle NULLs if present.

corrective_action.description: Categorical corrective action taken after inspection (e.g., Discount, Dispose, Repack).
corrective_action.analysis_use: Aggregate distribution of corrective actions, link corrective actions to rejection reasons and costs, and track effectiveness or frequency by inspector or product.
business_questions: What corrective actions are most commonly applied to rejected items?
Which corrective action is used most frequently for 'Spoilage' rejections?
corrective_action.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with low cardinality (three main values). Use GROUP BY to get distribution. Map natural-language requests like 'how many were disposed' to exact string values; consider trimming/case normalization when matching.

created_at.description: Datetime when the record was created in the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion timing analysis, time between inspection_ts and record creation, and time-based joins or partitions. Use for slow-change tracking and data pipeline monitoring.
business_questions: How long after inspection are records created in the system on average?
Are there ingestion delays or spikes in created_at timestamps?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Proper datetime2 type — use directly for date filtering, ordering, and grouping. When combining with inspection_ts (string), cast inspection_ts to datetime for accurate interval calculations (DATEDIFF or TIMESTAMPDIFF).

remarks.description: Intended free-text remarks for the inspection, but currently typed as decimal(18,4) and 100% null in this dataset.
remarks.analysis_use: When populated, useful for qualitative insights and NLP on rejection context; currently unusable due to type and nulls.
business_questions: Show remarks for inspection_id X to understand inspector notes.
How often do remarks mention specific causes (e.g., 'packaging', 'supplier')?
remarks.nl2sql_hints: Currently mis-typed and null. Treat as unavailable until schema corrected to text type. For NL2SQL, prefer text search functions (LIKE, CONTAINS, full-text search) if remarks become populated.

return_id.description: Unique identifier for the return record (primary key). Example pattern: 'RET#####'.
return_id.analysis_use: Used to de-duplicate, reference single return events, and as a join key for any return-level detail tables if present.
business_questions: What is the history/details of return RET15001?
How many unique returns occurred in a period (count of return_id)?
return_id.nl2sql_hints: Primary key NVARCHAR(50) with short max length (actual max 8). Exact-match lookups are common. Use equality on return_id for single-record retrieval. Expect prefix patterns like 'RET'. No need to aggregate this field except COUNT(DISTINCT return_id) which is equal to COUNT because it's PK.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the return. FK to zuna_etl.b2b_orders.order_id. Pattern example: 'B2BORD####'. Nullable.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders for enrichments (order date, order amount, customer_id confirmation, product lines). Filter or group returns by order attributes (sales channel, order date, store).
business_questions: Which orders have the highest number/value of returns?
What is the return rate by order creation date or sales channel?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR(50) but actual values shorter (max len ~10). Nullable -> handle IS NULL in NL2SQL. Use joins to b2b_orders for product-level and order-level attributes. When matching, prefer equality on full string; remember to trim/case-sensitive issues depending on DB collation. Suggest adding an index on order_id for faster join/filter if table grows.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who initiated/owned the return. Pattern example: 'CUST####'. Nullable.
customer_id.analysis_use: Group returns by customer, compute per-customer return counts, average refund per customer, identify repeat returners, and join to customer demographic/segment tables.
business_questions: Which customers have the highest number or value of returns?
What percentage of returns come from repeat customers vs one-time returners?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR(50) with short values (max len ~8). Nullable — queries should handle missing customer_id. Join to customer tables on exact string match. Use GROUP BY customer_id to identify top returners.

requested_at.description: Timestamp when the return was requested by the customer or recorded in the system (datetime2).
requested_at.analysis_use: Filter by request date, group by day/week/month, compute trends over time, and measure lead times (requested -> received). Useful for time-series aggregations and window functions.
business_questions: How many return requests arrive per day/week and how is this trending?
Are there seasonal patterns or spikes in return requests (by day of week or month)?
requested_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 — use DATE(requested_at) or CAST(requested_at AS DATE) (SQL Server) for day-level grouping. Be explicit about timezones if present; treat NULLs carefully. For NL2SQL, expect phrases like 'in last 30 days', 'by month', 'between X and Y' — map to appropriate date truncation or BETWEEN on requested_at.

received_at.description: Timestamp when the returned goods were received by the warehouse/inspector (datetime2).
received_at.analysis_use: Used to compute processing time (received_at - requested_at), SLA compliance, and to analyze inbound return handling throughput and bottlenecks.
business_questions: What is the average time from request to receipt for returns?
Which returns exceeded the target receipt SLA (e.g., > 5 days)?
received_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 — compute intervals with DATEDIFF (SQL Server) or equivalent. Some rows may have NULL received_at if not yet received; NL2SQL should support 'received' vs 'pending' filters and handle NULLs in comparisons.

return_status.description: Categorical status of the return lifecycle (e.g., REQUESTED, APPROVED, REJECTED, COMPLETED).
return_status.analysis_use: Primary filter/grouping column to report return pipeline stage counts and ratios, funnel analysis and status-based SLA metrics.
business_questions: How many returns are in each status today?
What proportion of requested returns are ultimately approved or rejected?
return_status.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR(50) but low cardinality (4 values). Use IN (...) for filters or GROUP BY return_status. Matching should be case-insensitive depending on DB collation. Consider mapping synonyms in NL2SQL (e.g., 'open returns' -> REQUESTED).

reason_code.description: Categorical reason for the return (EXCESS_QUANTITY, QUALITY, DAMAGED, WRONG_ITEM).
reason_code.analysis_use: Group and pivot analyses to determine top reasons, root cause trends, and to prioritize quality/fulfillment fixes. Useful in joins to quality_inspections or vendor data.
business_questions: What are the top reasons for returns and how much do they cost?
Are certain reason_codes increasing or tied to specific suppliers/products?
reason_code.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality NVARCHAR(50). Use exact-match strings in filters (e.g., reason_code = 'DAMAGED'), or IN lists. Consider normalizing mapping in NL2SQL templates (e.g., synonyms like 'damage' -> 'DAMAGED').

qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate sum for total returned weight, compute average per return, normalize refunds per kg, and flag large returns by weight.
business_questions: What is the total returned weight by period or by reason?
Which returns exceed a weight threshold (e.g., > 20 kg)?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type — use SUM(qty_kg), AVG(qty_kg), and numeric comparisons. When generating NL2SQL, ensure numeric literals are not quoted. Consider unit consistency (kg) in natural language (users might say 'kgs', 'kilograms').

refund_amount.description: Monetary refund issued for the return (decimal(18,4)).
refund_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate financial impact (SUM, AVG), compute refund per kg or per order, and reconcile against credit notes. Important for cost-of-returns analysis and financial reporting.
business_questions: What is the total refund amount associated with returns this quarter?
Which return reasons or products drive the highest refund costs?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type — use SUM(refund_amount), AVG(refund_amount). When formatting results for NL2SQL answers, present currency formatting separately. Watch for NULLs; use COALESCE(refund_amount,0) when summing to avoid NULL results.

credit_note_id.description: Identifier of the credit note issued for the return, if any (links to b2b_credit_notes).
credit_note_id.analysis_use: Join to credit note table for accounting details, verify refunds were issued and reconciled, count unmatched returns without credit notes.
business_questions: Which returns have an associated credit note and what are the credit amounts?
How many returns are pending credit notes (credit_note_id IS NULL)?
credit_note_id.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR(50) with moderate cardinality. Nullable — queries should explicitly check IS NULL/IS NOT NULL. Join to b2b_credit_notes on exact string equality; consider normalization if credit_note_id uses different prefixes. Use left joins to find missing credit notes.

disposition.description: What happened to the returned items: RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER, RESTOCK, or DISCARD.
disposition.analysis_use: Group by disposition to measure return value recapture vs write-offs, compute disposition-based cost adjustments, and inventory impact (restock vs discard).
business_questions: What percentage of returned weight/value is restocked vs discarded vs returned to supplier?
Which vendors or reasons lead to more discards compared to restocks?
disposition.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality NVARCHAR(50). Use GROUP BY disposition or conditional aggregates (SUM(CASE WHEN disposition='RESTOCK' THEN refund_amount END)). Map natural language queries like 'were restocked' to disposition = 'RESTOCK'.

inspected_by.description: Name or identifier of the inspector who processed/approved the return.
inspected_by.analysis_use: Workload and performance metrics per inspector, quality control metrics (inspector-level rejection/approval rates), and to detect inconsistent inspection patterns.
business_questions: Which inspectors processed the most returns and what are their approval/rejection rates?
Are there inspectors who consistently mark returns as DISCARD or RESTOCK?
inspected_by.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR(50), many distinct values. For NL2SQL, expect filter by inspector name; support partial matching (LIKE 'Aarav%') and exact matches. Consider normalizing inspector IDs in source systems; be mindful of spelling variations.

notes.description: Short free-text note about the return (e.g., 'Packaging torn', 'Spoiled', 'Customer complaint'). Low cardinality in this sample.
notes.analysis_use: Quick categorical insight into common issues; can be used for text-based grouping or tagging. For deeper analysis, needs NLP/keyword extraction for broader free-text fields.
business_questions: What are the most common notes/comments associated with returns?
How many returns cite 'Packaging torn' vs other notes?
notes.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR(50) free-text but low cardinality here. Use equality or LIKE for matching phrases. For NL2SQL, prefer explicit known phrases; fuzzy text matching should be translated into LIKE '%term%' or full-text search if available.

agent_id.description: Unique identifier for each sales agent (primary key). Stored as nvarchar up to 50 but actual max length ~9.
agent_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to link agents to orders, contracts and payment tables; used for grouping and counting distinct agents; essential for agent-level aggregations.
business_questions: Which agents generated the most revenue (when joined to orders)?
How many active agents are in a given region or specialization?
agent_id.nl2sql_hints: Use exact equality (=) for joins and filters. Trim and normalize input when users supply agent ids (remove whitespace/case differences). Consider indexing (clustered PK) on agent_id. When generating SQL, treat as a string column (nvarchar); avoid numerical casts. Example predicate: WHERE agent_id = 'B2BAG2001'.

name.description: Full name of the agent for display and human identification.
name.analysis_use: Used for reporting and labeling results; can be used in free-text filters (search) but not ideal as a join key. Useful for deduplication and data quality checks.
business_questions: What are the top-performing agents by name (after joining to sales data)?
Are there duplicate or inconsistent name records that require cleanup?
name.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; when supporting natural language queries that reference names, implement case-insensitive LIKE searches (e.g. WHERE LOWER(name) LIKE '%aarna%'). Beware of partial matches returning multiple agents—prefer agent_id for precise selection.

phone.description: Agent contact phone number stored as string (includes country code formatting).
phone.analysis_use: Used for contact completeness metrics and as an alternate identifier in support or outreach workflows; can be used for data quality checks (format/length).
business_questions: Which agents are missing phone numbers?
How many agents have phone numbers formatted with country code (+91)?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Keep as string; normalize phone formats before using for joins or deduplication. Use pattern matching (LIKE or regex where supported) for validation. Watch out for leading/trailing spaces and non-numeric characters when filtering.

email.description: Agent email address used for notifications and identification.
email.analysis_use: Use to measure contactability, to identify missing/duplicate emails and to support joins to authentication or API client tables if present.
business_questions: Which agents are missing or have invalid email addresses?
How many agents use corporate email domain (e.g. dailygreens.in)?
email.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; use LIKE '%@domain' for domain-based filters. Validate format with pattern matching. Treat NULLs explicitly in NL2SQL (IS NULL) when users ask for missing contact info.

region.description: Geographic region or city the agent is assigned to (e.g., Vadodara, Ahmedabad).
region.analysis_use: High-value grouping and filter key for regional headcount, performance and allocation analyses. Low cardinality -> efficient GROUP BY and useful as a partitioning/indexing candidate.
business_questions: How many agents are in each region?
What is the distribution of active/inactive agents by region?
