region.nl2sql_hints: Use equality filters and GROUP BY on region. Since cardinality is low, allow NLP to accept synonyms or common misspellings; consider mapping aliases. Use strict equality for exact region matches; use IN for multiple regions.

specialization.description: Agent's market specialization (Caterer, HORECA, Retail Chain, Wholesale).
specialization.analysis_use: Important grouping and segmentation key for commission structures, hiring strategy and performance comparisons. Low cardinality makes it ideal for categorical grouping and segmentation.
business_questions: What is the average commission rate by specialization?
How many agents specialize in HORECA vs Retail Chain?
specialization.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; use GROUP BY specialization. Map synonyms (e.g., 'Retail' vs 'Retail Chain') in NL2SQL preprocessing. Use IN(...) for multi-value filters.

commission_rate_pct.description: Commission rate for the agent expressed as percentage stored as decimal(18,4) (e.g., 3.2300 means 3.23%).
commission_rate_pct.analysis_use: Numeric aggregations (AVG, MIN, MAX, STDDEV), histogram/binning, filtering by threshold (e.g., agents with > 3%). Useful for calculating commission liabilities when multiplied by revenue from transactional tables.
business_questions: What is the average commission rate across all agents and by specialization?
Which agents have commission rates above X%?
commission_rate_pct.nl2sql_hints: Treat as numeric decimal. When users specify percentage thresholds, allow both fractional (0.0323) and percentage (3.23) inputs—normalize to match stored scale. Use numeric comparisons in WHERE (e.g. WHERE commission_rate_pct > 3.0 if stored as percent) but confirm unit convention. For aggregation, use CAST if necessary to control precision.

join_date.description: Date the agent joined (datetime2). Represents tenure/cohort entry.
join_date.analysis_use: Time-series and cohort analyses (agents joined per month/quarter/year), tenure calculations (current_date - join_date), and join-date based filtering (recent hires).
business_questions: How many agents joined in the last 6 or 12 months?
What is the retention/cohort size of agents by join month?
join_date.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 type — use DATE or CAST(join_date AS DATE) for date-level grouping. Support relative time expressions in NL2SQL (e.g., last 30 days, last quarter). Beware of NULL join_date values in filtering or cohorts.

status.analysis_use: Filter active workforce, compute active/inactive counts and percentages, and join to performance metrics to exclude inactive agents from current KPIs.
business_questions: How many active agents are currently assigned to each region?
What percent of agents are inactive overall?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string with a small set of values. Use equality (status = 'ACTIVE') or IN for multiple statuses. Normalize case in user queries. Because INACTIVE is the majority, clarify whether inactive agents should be excluded by default in NL2SQL templates.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the agent record was created in the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and data lineage checks, temporal filters for recent records, or to reconcile import times vs join_date. Useful to find recently added records or migration artifacts.
business_questions: Which agent records were created in the last 30 days?
Are there agents with created_at much earlier than their join_date indicating data issues?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 type — use date truncation or casting for grouping (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATE) or DATEPART functions). When NL2SQL encounters 'recent' or 'last X days', map to created_at for record recency questions. Handle NULLs explicitly.

date.description: The date for the aggregated metrics (datetime2). Each row corresponds to a single distinct date value.
date.analysis_use: Primary time index used for filtering time ranges, grouping by day/week/month/year, ordering time series, and computing deltas / growth rates / rolling windows.
business_questions: What were net_sales and gross_sales between 2024-01-01 and 2024-03-31?
Show weekly totals of total_orders for the last 12 weeks.
Which dates are statistical outliers for gross_sales?
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a date/time column — queries commonly truncate or cast to DATE for grouping (e.g., CAST(date AS date) or CONVERT(date, date)). Support date range filters (>=, BETWEEN). Use DATEPART/FORMAT functions for month/year grouping. Be careful with timezones if application expects local dates; the column is datetime2 so comparisons should use consistent datatypes. Because date is unique per row, joins on date to transactional tables must account for aggregation (e.g., join on transaction_date aggregated to date).

customer_type.description: Customer segment/type for which the daily metrics are aggregated (nvarchar(50)). In this snapshot the column contains a single value 'HORECA'.
customer_type.analysis_use: Used for segmentation and filtering when multiple customer types exist. In this dataset it currently offers no segmentation because it's constant, but future data may include multiple customer types allowing group-by comparisons.
business_questions: Compare net_sales by customer_type for a selected month.
Filter daily KPIs to only include HORECA customers (or another type when available).
customer_type.nl2sql_hints: String column — use equality filters or IN lists (e.g., WHERE customer_type = 'HORECA'). Note current cardinality = 1; NL2SQL should avoid generating unnecessary GROUP BY on this column unless user explicitly requests segmentation. Not a reliable join key to customers table unless normalized values and mappings exist.

total_orders.description: Number of orders aggregated for the date and customer_type (decimal(18,4) although it represents a count).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) across date ranges or average per day; can be used to compute average order size (total_qty_kg / total_orders) and orders per period. Because stored as decimal, treat as numeric integer-like values in queries.
business_questions: What is the average number of orders per day in the last quarter?
Which days had zero or unusually low order volume?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as decimal, this is a count. Use SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX and cast/round where integer semantics are required (e.g., CAST(total_orders AS INT) or ROUND). Avoid joins keyed on this column. When filtering for 'orders = 0', consider numeric tolerance for decimals.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the date and customer_type (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Sum/average over ranges, compute per-order average (total_qty_kg/total_orders), analyze demand volume trends and seasonality, and detect outliers in physical volume moved.
business_questions: How has daily sold quantity (kg) trended over the last 6 months?
What is the average kg per order for a given week or month?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal column — use SUM/AVG and calculations combining with total_orders and gross_sales. Beware of division by zero when computing per-order metrics. Consider unit conversions or rounding when presenting results. Use appropriate aggregation when joining to transactional tables to avoid double counting.

gross_sales.description: Gross sales amount (before discounts/taxes) for the date and customer_type (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for trend analysis, percentage calculations (discount/gross_sales), and aggregation to higher-level periods (weekly/monthly). Commonly used in SUM, AVG, TOP-N or ranking queries.
business_questions: What were the top 10 highest gross sales days in 2024?
What is the month-over-month percentage change in gross_sales?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal financial column — use SUM and ROUND for display. When computing percentages, handle cases where gross_sales = 0. If joins to transactional data are used, ensure consistent currency/scale and that aggregated transactional sums match this pre-aggregated metric (data quality check).

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied for the date and customer_type (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Calculate absolute discount spend and discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales). Used to analyze promotion effectiveness and margin impact over time.
business_questions: How much discount was applied in the last 30 days and what percentage of gross sales did it represent?
Are there days with unusually high discount spend relative to sales?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal column — compute ratios like (discount_amount / NULLIF(gross_sales,0)) to avoid division by zero. When users ask for 'discount %', present as 100 * discount_amount / gross_sales. Consider negative/positive sign conventions in data (confirm whether discounts are stored as positive numbers).

tax_amount.description: Tax collected for the date and customer_type (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax by period, compute tax as percentage of gross or net sales, and reconcile tax totals with transactional tax data.
business_questions: What is total tax collected month-to-date?
What is the average tax per order or per kg sold?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal column — use SUM and percent calculations. If users ask to compute tax rates, clarify denominator (gross_sales or net_sales). Ensure consistent rounding rules for tax reconciliation queries.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount after discounts and tax adjustments for the date and customer_type (decimal(18,4)).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary KPI for revenue after adjustments — used in trend analysis, growth calculations, profitability overlays and ranking. Frequently aggregated to weekly/monthly/yearly levels and compared to gross_sales to compute effective discounts/tax impact.
business_questions: What were net sales per month and how do they compare to the same period last year?
Show a 7-day rolling average of net_sales to smooth daily volatility.
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal financial column — use SUM, rolling window functions (AVG(...) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)) or GROUP BY DATEPART. Confirm formula or relationship to gross_sales and discount_amount if required by the user (do not assume a specific derivation unless documented).

created_at.description: ETL load timestamp when this row (or the dataset) was created/loaded (datetime2). In this snapshot it has a single constant value (one ETL run time).
created_at.analysis_use: Metadata for freshness checks and ETL auditing; rarely used in reporting filters because it is constant in this dataset. Useful to detect last load time or stale data.
business_questions: When was this daily sales table last refreshed?
Is the data current as of a specific timestamp?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 metadata column — use for freshness checks (e.g., SELECT MAX(created_at)). Because distinct=1, avoid using it for filtering unless user explicitly requests ETL load time. If present with multiple values in future, use range filters or compare to system time; consider timezone interpretation.

year.description: Calendar year for the monthly aggregate (bigint). Serves as the high-level time partition key.
year.analysis_use: Primary filter and group-by key for time-based analysis, used with month to order time-series. Useful to restrict queries to a particular year before further aggregation.
business_questions: What were the monthly net sales in 2024?
Filter to compare 2024 months with other years (if other years are added later).
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as integer-type filter/group-by. When generating SQL, always pair with month for ordering: ORDER BY year, month. If users ask for 'this year' or 'last year', map to literal year values (e.g., WHERE year = 2024). Note that current data contains only 2024 — queries expecting multiple years should handle empty results.

month.description: Numeric month (1-12) representing the calendar month of the aggregate (bigint).
month.analysis_use: Group-by and order-by key within a year for time-series. Can be converted to month name for presentation or used to filter seasonal queries (e.g., WHERE month IN (...)).
business_questions: Which month had the highest gross_sales in 2024?
Show net_sales per month for the year ordered by month name.
month.nl2sql_hints: Use as integer in WHERE/GROUP BY/ORDER BY. When users request month names, map 1..12 to names (e.g., DATENAME(MONTH, DATEFROMPARTS(year, month, 1))). Ensure ORDER BY uses numeric month to preserve chronological order.

customer_type.description: Categorical descriptor of the customer segment for the aggregated row (nvarchar(50)). Present data contains 'HORECA' only.
customer_type.analysis_use: Filter or group-by to segment KPIs by customer type. Serves as a dimension for comparing segments if multiple values exist in future snapshots.
business_questions: What are monthly net sales for the HORECA segment?
Compare gross_sales across customer types (if additional types are populated later).
customer_type.nl2sql_hints: Treat as case-sensitive depending on DB collation; matches should use exact strings or ILIKE/LOWER for fuzzy matching. Currently single distinct value — queries should handle the potential of additional types. Not a reliable join key unless a dedicated customer_type dimension exists; consider normalizing in the data model.

total_orders.description: Total number of B2B orders in the month (decimal to allow fractional stored values but semantically integer count).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) when combining months/years, AVG for average orders per month, used to compute average order value: gross_sales / total_orders. Use as numerator/denominator in rates and KPIs.
business_questions: How many orders were placed each month in 2024?
What is the average order value (gross_sales / total_orders) per month?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as decimal(18,4), treat as integer counts in calculations. Guard against division by zero when computing per-order metrics (e.g., CASE WHEN total_orders = 0 THEN NULL ELSE gross_sales/total_orders END). Use SUM(total_orders) when aggregating multiple months.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the month (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate/compare monthly volumes; compute unit-mix KPIs such as kg per order (total_qty_kg / total_orders) and revenue per kg (net_sales / total_qty_kg).
business_questions: How many kilograms were sold each month in 2024?
What is the revenue per kg each month (net_sales / total_qty_kg)?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal precision is important — use appropriate rounding in outputs. Handle division by zero when computing rates. When joining to product-level tables, ensure time alignment by year+month.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (pre-discount, pre-tax) for the month (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for trend analysis, aggregation, and calculation of discount and tax rates (discount_amount/gross_sales, tax_amount/gross_sales). Used for YoY and MoM growth calculations.
business_questions: What were the gross sales per month in 2024?
What is the month-over-month percentage change in gross_sales?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Use SUM(gross_sales) when aggregating. For percentage changes use LAG() OVER (ORDER BY year, month) to compute MoM deltas. Guard against NULL or zero when computing ratios (e.g., discount_rate = discount_amount / NULLIF(gross_sales, 0)).

discount_amount.description: Total discount value applied in the month (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Compute discount rates and impact on revenue (discount_amount / gross_sales), trend discounting patterns, and contribution to net sales calculations.
business_questions: How much discount was given each month and what is the discount rate relative to gross sales?
Did discount amounts increase or decrease month-over-month?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Divide by gross_sales using NULLIF to avoid division-by-zero. When generating natural-language queries about 'discount percentage' translate to (discount_amount/gross_sales)*100. Watch for negative values if refunds/credits are represented differently in other datasets.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected or applied for the month (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Compute tax rates (tax_amount / gross_sales or tax_amount / net_sales), analyze tax trends and month-level tax liabilities.
business_questions: What was the tax collected each month and how does it relate to gross sales?
How did tax amounts change across months in 2024?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Similar to discounts, use NULLIF for ratio calculations. Be explicit whether tax_amount is included in gross_sales or net_sales when interpreting queries; use net_sales column for pre-computed net values.

net_sales.description: Net sales for the month after discounts/taxes as stored in the snapshot (decimal(18,4)).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line monthly revenue metric for reporting, aggregation, trend analysis, and KPI calculation. Compare to gross_sales to compute effective discount/tax impacts.
business_questions: What were the net sales per month in 2024?
Which month had the highest net_sales and how much did it grow versus prior month?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Use SUM(net_sales) when aggregating. When users ask for 'revenue' clarify whether they mean gross or net. For MoM% use LAG() with ORDER BY year, month. Validate that net_sales = gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount (or other formula) if consistency checks are requested.

created_at.description: Timestamp when this row/snapshot was created in the ETL (datetime2). Present data shows a single refresh timestamp.
created_at.analysis_use: Metadata for freshness of the snapshot, used for detecting last refresh time in dashboards and to validate staleness. Not used for primary time-series analysis (year + month are used instead).
business_questions: When was this monthly snapshot last refreshed?
Has the data been updated since a given date (e.g., 'show only snapshots refreshed after X')?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Treat as metadata; filters against created_at are valid but will often return the same timestamp for all rows. When users ask for 'latest data' use ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 1 or filter WHERE created_at = (SELECT MAX(created_at) ...). Avoid joining on created_at as a business key.

year.description: The calendar year for the aggregated metrics (bigint). Acts as the primary key for the row(s).
year.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filter column for time-based queries; used in WHERE and GROUP BY to select specific years or ranges; join key to monthly/daily tables by extracting their order date year.
business_questions: What are the sales KPIs for year 2024?
Compare net sales between 2023 and 2024.
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as an integer/number. Use equality (year = 2024) or range (year BETWEEN 2020 AND 2024). When joining to tables with datetime order dates, extract the year (e.g., YEAR(order_date) = year) or use date range filters (order_date >= '2024-01-01' AND order_date < '2025-01-01') for better index usage. Note: primary key implies at most one row per year (or per year+customer_type if modeled).

customer_type.description: Categorical label for the customer segment (nvarchar(50)), e.g., 'HORECA'. May be NULL.
customer_type.analysis_use: Filter or group by customer segment to compare performance across segments; low-cardinality dimension for grouping; can be used to join/match to other customer-dimension tables if mapping exists.
business_questions: What were the yearly sales for the HORECA customer_type?
Which customer_type contributed most to gross sales in 2024?
customer_type.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string; use exact matches or IN lists (customer_type = 'HORECA' or IN ('HORECA','WHOLESALE')). Watch for casing/whitespace; consider trimming and normalizing. Because there is no FK, joins to customer master require mapping logic (customer_type value -> customer table). Nulls possible — use IS NULL checks or COALESCE when needed.

total_orders.description: Aggregated count of orders (decimal(18,4) stored as numeric — though conceptually an integer count).
total_orders.analysis_use: Primary metric for order volume; used in aggregations (SUM, AVG), ratios (orders per kg, orders per sales), and trend KPI calculations; can be compared to counts from order-level tables for reconciliation.
business_questions: How many orders were placed in year X?
What is the average orders per customer_type in year X (requires customer counts)?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as decimal, treat as an integer count when constructing queries. Use SUM/ROUND or CAST to integer when presenting counts. Be careful when joining to order-level tables to avoid double-counting — use the ETL snapshot as a single-row source rather than summing again unless reconciling.

total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Measure of physical volume sold; used for volume-based KPIs, unit price computations (gross_sales / total_qty_kg), and inventory planning. Useful in GROUP BY/year or customer_type to compare volume across segments.
business_questions: What was the total quantity sold (kg) in year X?
What is the average selling price per kg (gross_sales / total_qty_kg) for the year?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal arithmetic — watch for division-by-zero when computing per-kg metrics. Use COALESCE(total_qty_kg,0) and NULL-safe checks. For derived metrics, cast to appropriate numeric scale and round for presentation.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales amount before discounts and taxes (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Top-line revenue metric used in financial KPIs, trend analysis, and contribution calculations. Used with discount_amount and tax_amount to reconcile to net_sales. Useful in GROUP BY/year and customer_type.
business_questions: What were gross sales for year X?
How much did gross sales grow year-over-year?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal precision is important; preserve scale in SUMs and arithmetic. For percentage calculations, use safe division and round for display. When comparing across tables, ensure currency and scale consistency. Use aliases like gross_sales to avoid ambiguity in multi-table queries.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied in the period (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute net impact of discounts, percentage of gross (discount_amount / gross_sales), and to analyze promotional cost. Can be used in reconciliation against invoice-level discounts.
business_questions: How much was given in discounts in year X?
What percent of gross sales were discounts in 2024?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal arithmetic; expect NULLs — use COALESCE(discount_amount,0). When asking for percentages, guard against zero gross_sales. If discount breakdowns are needed, join to detailed discount tables; this table only holds the aggregate.

tax_amount.description: Total taxes charged for the period (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used for tax liability estimation, reconciliation to tax reports, and calculating net revenue post-tax. Combined with gross and discount for validation of net_sales.
business_questions: What was the total tax collected in year X?
What effective tax amount per unit (kg) or per order was collected in the year?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type with scale. Use COALESCE(tax_amount,0) in arithmetic and reconciliation queries. Beware of differing tax treatments in underlying invoices — use detailed tax tables for breakdowns. When constructing NL2SQL intents, map 'tax' to this column and clarify whether user means VAT collected vs tax-inclusive prices.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount after discounts and taxes (decimal(18,4)). Likely the final revenue metric used in reporting.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line sales metric for profitability and reporting; used in trend comparisons, YoY growth, and as a reconciliation target for detailed sales tables.
business_questions: What are the net sales for year X (after discounts and taxes)?
How much did net sales change compared to prior years?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal arithmetic and rounding. Ensure consistent sign conventions (positive amounts). When users ask for 'revenue' or 'sales', confirm whether they mean gross_sales or net_sales. Use aliases and explicit column references to avoid ambiguity in multi-table queries.

created_at.description: ETL snapshot timestamp indicating when this row was created/updated (datetime2). Not a transaction date for sales — metadata for the snapshot.
created_at.analysis_use: Used to identify recency of the snapshot, validate ETL runs, and filter for the most recent aggregates (e.g., select latest created_at per year). Not used for grouping time-series analysis of sales data.
business_questions: When was the yearly sales snapshot for 2024 generated?
Which snapshot is the most recent for use in reporting?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a datetime; when selecting latest snapshot use ORDER BY created_at DESC or MAX(created_at). Do not confuse created_at with transaction dates — for temporal filters on sales use the year column or join to order-level dates. Consider timezone handling if comparing with application times.

tracking_event_id.description: Unique identifier for each tracking event (string).
tracking_event_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de-duplication, retrieving a single event, or indexing. Useful to detect duplicate events and to partition or order data when combined with timestamps.
business_questions: Is a particular tracking_event_id present and what are its details?
Are there duplicate tracking_event_id values (data quality) across the table?
tracking_event_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. In NL2SQL use exact equality or IN when selecting specific events. Consider that column is currently nullable in schema; queries that expect uniqueness should guard for NULL. If used in joins, prefer equality predicates. When ordering or selecting latest per tracking_event_id, use ORDER BY event_ts DESC. Recommend indexing/primary key on this column if it is indeed unique.

dispatch_id.description: Identifier of the dispatch this event belongs to (foreign key to b2b_dispatches.dispatch_id).
dispatch_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to link events to dispatch-level metadata. Used for grouping (events per dispatch), filtering (events for a given dispatch), and window functions (latest event per dispatch).
business_questions: What is the full event timeline for a given dispatch_id?
How many tracking events were recorded for each dispatch in the last X days?
dispatch_id.nl2sql_hints: Common NL2SQL filter will reference dispatch_id = '...'. Use JOIN on zuna_etl.b2b_dispatches USING dispatch_id or ON events.dispatch_id = dispatches.dispatch_id. For performance include equality predicates or IN lists. Consider adding index on dispatch_id and NOT NULL constraint if appropriate. Watch out for leading/trailing whitespace due to nvarchar.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the tracking event occurred (datetime2).
event_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for sequencing events, filtering by date ranges, aggregations by day/hour, and calculating durations between events (time-to-delivery). Essential for window functions (first/last event).
business_questions: When was this event recorded and what is the timeline for events between two dates?
What is the average time between PICKED and DELIVERED event_ts across dispatches?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Use BETWEEN or >=/< predicates with proper datetime literals. When generating natural language queries referencing relative times (today, last 7 days), translate into event_ts >= DATEADD(day, -7, CAST(GETDATE() AS date)), etc. For latest-event queries use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dispatch_id ORDER BY event_ts DESC). Be careful to use event_ts (actual event time) rather than created_at for elapsed-time calculations.

event_type.description: Categorical event type describing the tracking state (DELIVERED, LOADED, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, PICKED).
event_type.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering dimension for event counts, funnel analysis (sequence of states), SLA breach detection (missing DELIVERED), and state-based aggregation per dispatch or period.
business_questions: How many DELIVERED events occurred in the last 24 hours?
What proportion of events are OUT_FOR_DELIVERY vs IN_TRANSIT by region?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — use GROUP BY event_type or conditional aggregation (SUM(CASE WHEN event_type='DELIVERED' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)). For sequence queries, combine with event_ts and ROW_NUMBER() to find first/last occurrence of specific event_type per dispatch. Normalize case/surrounding spaces when matching (e.g., UPPER(TRIM(event_type)) = 'DELIVERED').

gps_lat.description: Latitude of the event's GPS capture (decimal(18,4)).
gps_lat.analysis_use: Used to compute last-known location per dispatch, map visualizations, cluster by area, compute distances/travel paths, and detect deviations from planned route.
business_questions: What are the last-known latitude/longitude coordinates for dispatch X?
Which events had GPS coordinates outside expected delivery region (possible routing issues)?
gps_lat.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal with 4 fractional digits — ensure consistent precision when comparing. Combine gps_lat and gps_long in SELECT or WHERE. For spatial calculations, either export to GIS functions or compute Haversine in SQL using floats/decimals. Guard against NULLs. When filtering by bounding box, use range predicates on gps_lat and gps_long for performant queries.

gps_long.description: Longitude of the event's GPS capture (decimal(18,4)).
gps_long.analysis_use: Companion to gps_lat for mapping and location-based aggregation, last-known location, and path reconstruction.
business_questions: Show all events within a geographic bounding box or near a given coordinate.
Which dispatches had last-known longitudes indicating they reached the targeted city?
gps_long.nl2sql_hints: Same considerations as gps_lat. Use BETWEEN for bounding boxes, and combine with gps_lat. There is one fewer distinct value in this sample; handle NULLs. For geo proximity queries, use index-friendly bounding-box checks first then optional precise distance calculations.

scanned_by.description: Name or identifier of the person who scanned the shipment/event (string).
scanned_by.analysis_use: Operator-level analytics: counts per scanner, performance, potential fraud/unusual activity, and accountability for events. Useful for grouping and filtering.
business_questions: Which scanners recorded the most DELIVERED events last week?
Are there scanners with unusually high event volume in a short timespan (possible duplicate scans)?
scanned_by.nl2sql_hints: Text matching may need normalization (TRIM, UPPER). Use GROUP BY scanned_by for aggregates. If integrating with a user/employee table, join on a normalized user identifier — avoid joining on free-text names unless canonicalized. For NL2SQL, allow fuzzy matches or exact matches depending on user intent.

scan_photo_url.description: Intended to store a photo URL for the scan (currently defined as decimal(18,4) but 100% nulls — likely mis-typed; should be nvarchar).
scan_photo_url.analysis_use: When populated correctly, used to retrieve proof-of-delivery images, count how many events have photo evidence, and link to external storage/CMS. In current state it provides no data.
business_questions: How many delivered events include a scan_photo (proof of delivery)?
Retrieve photo URLs for events of dispatch X for audit.
scan_photo_url.nl2sql_hints: Schema currently uses decimal and is all NULL — NL2SQL should treat this column as potentially empty. If the source is fixed to a string, queries should SELECT scan_photo_url where NOT NULL to find pictures. Avoid numeric predicates. Consider CAST if historical data has mixed types. Recommend schema fix to nvarchar and a NOT NULL / presence boolean if photos expected.

location_name.description: Human-readable location name associated with the event (city/town or facility).
location_name.analysis_use: Aggregation by location for hotspot analysis, filtering events by city, region-level metrics, and joining to location reference tables for enrichment.
business_questions: Which locations have the highest frequency of OUT_FOR_DELIVERY events?
How many DELIVERED events occurred in Nadiad in the last month?
location_name.nl2sql_hints: Use exact match or ILIKE/LOWER(TRIM(...)) for more flexible NL2SQL matching. location_name has limited distinct values — good for GROUP BY. If joins to a canonical locations table exist, normalize values before join; otherwise expect spelling/case variants.

status_note.description: Freely-typed status note for the event (intended to be text) — currently decimal(18,4) and 100% NULL in snapshot, indicating schema mismatch or unused field.
status_note.analysis_use: When populated, it can provide qualitative context for deviated deliveries (e.g., 'customer absent', 'damaged'). Useful for text search, categorizing exceptions, and audit trails.
business_questions: Which events include an exception note mentioning 'damaged' or 'customer not available'?
How many events have non-empty status notes in the last X days?
status_note.nl2sql_hints: Currently numeric and empty — treat as missing. For NL2SQL that searches text, ensure the column is varchar/nvarchar and use LIKE '%keyword%'. Recommend schema correction and possibly an exceptions flag for faster filtering; otherwise queries should guard for NULL.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the event record was inserted into the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for ingestion latency metrics (delay between event_ts and created_at), troubleshooting late writes, and auditing when the record entered the database.
business_questions: What is the average data ingestion delay (created_at - event_ts) for events?
Which events were created in the last X hours (recent inserts)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Differentiate between event_ts (occurrence time) and created_at (ingest time) in NL2SQL prompts. For latency calculations use DATEDIFF/MINUTES. When asking for recent inserts, use created_at filter. Because both are datetime2, ensure timezone considerations are addressed if relevant.

device_id.description: Identifier for the scanning device that recorded the event (string).
device_id.analysis_use: Device-level analytics for failures, firmware issues, device assignment, and security. Grouping by device_id to identify hotspots or device-specific anomalies.
business_questions: Which devices recorded events that were later corrected or duplicated?
List the most active devices in the last 7 days and their event counts.
device_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string. Use exact equality or IN for filtering. If there is a device registry table in schema, join on device_id for metadata. Consider device_id in WHERE predicates for troubleshooting queries. Normalize case/whitespace for reliable matches.

vendor_id.description: Unique identifier for each vendor partner (string). Designated primary key.
vendor_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to transactional tables; used for lookups, deduplication, per-vendor aggregations (counts, sums after join). High-selectivity filter for retrieving single vendor records.
business_questions: What is the vendor_name, contact and GST number for vendor_id = 'B2BV2003'?
Which vendors had activity (orders/invoices) in a given period when joined to transactions?
vendor_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as exact-match string (nvarchar). Use equality or IN list for filtering. Ensure normalization/trim when matching user input (case/whitespace). Prefer using vendor_id for joins (avoid joining on vendor_name).

vendor_name.description: Display name of the vendor partner.
vendor_name.analysis_use: Grouping/labeling in reports; de-duplication checks; human-readable filter. Can be used for LIKE searches or fuzzy matching for user queries.
business_questions: Show vendor details where vendor_name contains 'Balan'.
List top vendors alphabetically or return full contact info for a given vendor_name.
vendor_name.nl2sql_hints: Text field: support substring/ILIKE searches; consider using exact match or startswith for performance. Normalization (trim, collapse multiple spaces) may be required for robust matching.

service_type.description: Categorical service classification for the vendor (e.g., Quality Inspection, Transport, Logistics Partner).
service_type.analysis_use: High-value grouping/faceting (counts per service type). Good for filtering vendors by capability when joining to service-specific transactions.
business_questions: How many vendors provide 'Quality Inspection' services?
Which vendors are categorized as 'Cold Storage'?
service_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality nvarchar: use equality, IN lists or GROUP BY. Consider normalizing common synonyms. Useful as a facet in NL2SQL (e.g., 'vendors that provide Transport').

contact_name.description: Primary contact person's name for the vendor.
contact_name.analysis_use: Display/contact lookup; grouping by contact for relationship/coverage analysis; identify duplicate contacts across vendors.
business_questions: Who is the contact person for vendor_id = 'B2BV2001'?
Which vendors are managed by contact_name = 'Aarna Bajwa'?
contact_name.nl2sql_hints: Free text nvarchar: support exact and LIKE queries. Watch for inconsistent spacing/characters; normalize when matching. Not suitable as a join key.

phone.description: Contact phone number for the vendor's primary contact (string to preserve formatting/country code).
phone.analysis_use: Contact retrieval and data quality checks (missing/invalid formats). Can be used to de-duplicate vendors by phone.
business_questions: Which vendors are missing phone numbers?
Provide phone numbers for all vendors in Ahmedabad.
phone.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; expect varying formats (+91, spaces). Use pattern matching (LIKE) or regexp cleaning in NL2SQL translations. Avoid numeric aggregation; treat as string.

email.description: Primary contact email for the vendor (string).
email.analysis_use: Contact lookup and validity checks; de-duplication; targeted communications lists.
business_questions: List emails of all logistics partners.
Which vendors have malformed or missing emails?
email.nl2sql_hints: Text field may contain punctuation issues (samples contain commas). Use trimming and simple validation (contains '@') when generating NL2SQL conditions. Use LIKE '%@%' or regex to validate; avoid assuming unique emails.

credit_terms.description: Payment terms offered to/used by the vendor (categorical: NET 7/15/30).
credit_terms.analysis_use: Group and filter vendors by payment terms; combine with financial transactional data to analyze days payable, ageing and cashflow impact.
business_questions: How many vendors are on NET 30 versus NET 7?
List vendors with NET 15 credit terms in Rajkot.
credit_terms.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality nvarchar: use equality/IN for filtering and GROUP BY for aggregation (counts). If joining to financial tables, align semantics (term vs actual payment days).

gst_no.description: Vendor GST number stored as bigint (tax identifier).
gst_no.analysis_use: Unique tax identifier for vendor verification, regulatory reporting and joining to external tax/finance datasets. Useful for detecting duplicate vendors or missing tax IDs.
business_questions: Which vendors are missing GST numbers?
Find vendor by GST number 240353331220156.
gst_no.nl2sql_hints: Numeric bigint: exact numeric equality preferred. However, some GST values may be longer than bigint or contain formatting in other systems—cast carefully. Nulls indicate missing GST. When accepting user input, allow string input to be cast to bigint or perform string-based match if formatting varies.

city.description: City where the vendor operates or is registered.
city.analysis_use: Geographic grouping and filtering; distribution analysis (vendors per city), logistics planning and regional reporting.
business_questions: How many vendors are based in Vadodara and Ahmedabad?
List transport vendors located in Anand.
city.nl2sql_hints: Categorical nvarchar: case-insensitive equality or IN filters and GROUP BY. Consider mapping synonyms/alternate spellings. Useful to precompute city facets for NL2SQL suggestion lists.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the vendor record was created or loaded (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Change tracking or ingestion-time analyses. In this dataset created_at is constant (single distinct value) so it has limited analytical use unless future rows vary.
business_questions: When was this vendor master loaded?
Show vendors created after 2025-01-01 (if differing timestamps exist).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2: use range filters and comparisons. Note: current data shows a single timestamp for all rows, so time-based filters may return either all or none. Ensure timezone/context handling and prefer comparing against datetime literals. If created_at is constant, avoid relying on it for temporal slicing.

slot_id.description: Unique identifier for the delivery slot (primary key).
slot_id.analysis_use: Key for row-level lookup, deduplication, and joins to other scheduling/dispatch/order tables if they store slot_id. Useful for returning slot detail and counting unique slots.
business_questions: What are the details for slot_id = 'DSLT03001'?
How many distinct slots exist in the system for a given date range?
slot_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar primary key; treat as string. Exact matches (WHERE slot_id = '...') are common. Support LIKE for pattern searches but beware performance if not indexed. Use as join key only if related tables store the same string value. Slot IDs are short (max len 9) so comparisons are cheap.

date.description: Date of the delivery slot (datetime2 stored, effectively a date representing the slot day).
date.analysis_use: Primary filter and GROUP BY dimension for daily analyses (sums of capacity/availability, counts of slots). Use for date-range queries, daily/weekly/monthly aggregation, and trend analysis.
business_questions: How much capacity is available per date for the next 7 days?
Which dates have the most fully booked slots?
date.nl2sql_hints: Stored as datetime2; when writing NL2SQL map natural language dates to SQL Server date expressions and use CONVERT(date, date) or CAST(date AS date) for grouping by day. Prefer parameterized date ranges (date BETWEEN 'YYYY-MM-DD' AND 'YYYY-MM-DD'). Consider timezone semantics separately — values appear to be local date-only usage.

start_time.description: Scheduled start time of the slot stored as nvarchar (formatted like '9:00', '11:00').
start_time.analysis_use: Filter and group by time window; order slots within a day; combine with end_time to compute slot durations or to find overlapping/conflicting windows.
business_questions: Which time windows (start_time) see the highest utilization?
Which morning/afternoon slots still have available capacity?
start_time.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar and not consistently zero-padded (e.g., '9:00' vs '09:00'). For numeric/time comparisons, cast to SQL time: TRY_CONVERT(time, RIGHT('0' + start_time, 5), 108) or normalize/pad strings before comparison. Lexicographic comparisons may be incorrect unless padded to HH:MM. Use CAST/CONVERT or PARSE to time for BETWEEN and ordering.

end_time.description: Scheduled end time of the slot stored as nvarchar (formatted like '13:00', '17:00').
end_time.analysis_use: Used with start_time to compute slot duration, detect overlapping slots, and to filter by slot end boundaries (e.g., find slots ending before or after a given time).
business_questions: What is the duration of each slot and which durations are most common?
Which slots end after 17:00 and how much capacity remains in those slots?
end_time.nl2sql_hints: Same issues as start_time: nvarchar format potentially not zero-padded. Convert to time type for reliable comparisons and duration calculations: TRY_CONVERT(time, RIGHT('0' + end_time, 5), 108). Watch for NULLs; ensure both start_time and end_time are valid before computing durations.

capacity_orders.description: Total capacity (max orders) allowed for the slot (bigint).
capacity_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to get total capacity by date/centre/time; compute utilization and fill rates; identify capacity bottlenecks and plan resource allocation.
business_questions: What is the total delivery capacity per centre for a given day?
Which slots have the highest capacity and are they being utilized?
capacity_orders.nl2sql_hints: Numeric (bigint). Handle NULLs (use COALESCE(capacity_orders,0)). When computing ratios, guard against division by zero (CASE WHEN capacity_orders > 0 THEN ...). Use SUM(capacity_orders) for aggregation and casts to decimal for percent calculations.

available_orders.description: Remaining available order capacity for the slot (bigint).
available_orders.analysis_use: Directly indicates remaining capacity; can be subtracted from capacity_orders to get filled orders. Aggregate to find total available slots across centres/dates and identify slots that are sold out or nearly full.
business_questions: Which slots are sold out (available_orders = 0) on a given date?
How many total order slots remain available across all centres for tomorrow?
available_orders.nl2sql_hints: Numeric (bigint). Use COALESCE(available_orders,0). To compute booked orders use (capacity_orders - available_orders) but ensure both values are present and capacity_orders is not NULL. For percent available, cast to decimal to avoid integer division.

centre_id.description: Human-readable identifier/name of the fulfilment centre or collection point (nvarchar).
centre_id.analysis_use: Dimension for grouping and filtering (capacity and availability by centre). Useful in regional performance comparisons and routing strategies.
business_questions: Which centre has the most available slots on a given date?
How does utilization compare across centres for peak time windows?
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with low cardinality; treat as categorical. Exact matching is typical (WHERE centre_id = 'Surat Distribution Centre'). If joining to a canonical centres table, trim and normalize strings (RTRIM/LTRIM) to avoid mismatches. Consider mapping to centre_id keys if an ID-based centres table exists for more robust joins.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the slot row was created in the ETL process or system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Used for auditing, incremental ingestion logic, freshness checks, and identifying recently added/updated slots. Can be used to detect changes in slot definitions over time.
business_questions: When was this slot definition created or last refreshed?
What slots were added or modified in the last 24 hours?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 type; use comparison operators (>=, <=) for incremental queries. Use ORDER BY created_at DESC to find newest rows. For NL2SQL, translate phrases like 'recent' to explicit time expressions (e.g., created_at >= DATEADD(day, -1, GETDATE())). Consider timezone normalization if data crosses zones.

delivery_id.description: Identifier for the delivery event or logical delivery unit (string).
delivery_id.analysis_use: Grouping/identifying events by delivery. Useful to de-duplicate or aggregate events that belong to the same delivery lifecycle. Can be used to filter for specific deliveries.
business_questions: Which delivery_ids experienced multiple FAILED_ATTEMPT events?
Show the sequence of event_type changes for a given delivery_id.
delivery_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as varchar/text. May be nullable and not globally unique—do not assume primary key. Use equality or IN filters. If users supply partial IDs, support prefix or LIKE patterns; otherwise match exact. Consider indexing if queries filter by delivery_id frequently.

shipment_id.description: Identifier for the shipment associated with the delivery event (string).
shipment_id.analysis_use: Join key to shipment-level tables (if they exist). Filter/group by shipment to measure shipment-level metrics (e.g., events per shipment). Useful for timeline queries ordered by event_ts.
business_questions: What events occurred for a particular shipment_id and in what order?
How many shipments had an OUT_FOR_DELIVERY event but never a DELIVERED event?
shipment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as varchar. Common joins or filters by shipment_id should use exact match; support composite filters with event_ts for time-bounded queries. Consider index on (shipment_id, event_ts) for efficient per-shipment timelines.

order_id.description: Order identifier linking the delivery event to the original order (string). Foreign key to zuna_etl.ecom_orders.order_id.
order_id.analysis_use: Use for joins to order table to get customer, order date, items, and revenue. Grouping by order_id allows mapping delivery events to order lifecycle or customer impact. Filter to show events for a specific order.
business_questions: Which orders failed to be delivered or had multiple delivery attempts?
List delivery events for order(s) placed within a date range, joined with order details (customer, value).
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Primary join key to ecom_orders—use equality join. Watch for NULL order_id rows. Joins should be explicit (LEFT JOIN if you want events without an order). Normalize case if order ids can have inconsistent casing. Consider index on order_id for frequent joins/filters.

event_ts.analysis_use: Primary dimension for time-series analysis: filtering by period, ordering events to build timelines, computing durations between events, and window aggregations (e.g., time from LOADED to DELIVERED).
business_questions: What is the distribution of delivery events over time (hour/day/week)?
What is the average time between OUT_FOR_DELIVERY and DELIVERED events?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Use as range filter (>=, <=) and ORDER BY for timeline queries. Consider timezone normalization; assume stored in server timezone—clarify with stakeholders. For NL2SQL, prefer ISO datetime input and explicit casting; support BETWEEN for inclusive ranges. Index on event_ts or composite with shipment_id for timeline retrieval.

event_type.description: Type of delivery event (categorical): e.g., DELIVERED, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, FAILED_ATTEMPT, LOADED, etc.
event_type.analysis_use: Categorical grouping for event counts, sequences and funnel analyses. Filter to select only terminal events (DELIVERED) or problematic events (FAILED_ATTEMPT).
business_questions: How many events of each event_type occurred last week?
Which shipments had a FAILED_ATTEMPT followed by DELIVERED vs FAILED_ATTEMPT followed by no subsequent DELIVERED?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Treat as low-cardinality string; comparisons should be case-insensitive and trimmed. Consider storing canonical enum list. Support IN lists and equality checks. When generating sequences, combine with event_ts and partition by shipment_id or order_id.

location.description: City/location name where the event occurred (string).
location.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering by geography for aggregation (counts/rates) and mapping to regional performance. Combine with gps_lat/gps_long for finer geospatial analysis.
business_questions: Which cities have the highest delivery failure rates?
How does average delivery time vary by city?
location.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical string—use equality/IN filters. Normalize common aliases (case, spelling). Use with GROUP BY. If geospatial joins are needed, prefer joining gps coords or a location mapping table.

status.description: High-level delivery status classification (FAILED, IN_TRANSIT, DELIVERED).
status.analysis_use: Aggregation by status for KPIs (failure rate, delivered share). Filter to identify problem cases or completed deliveries.
business_questions: What percentage of events/shipments are in FAILED status in the last month?
How many unique shipments currently show IN_TRANSIT vs DELIVERED?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string with few values—use equality or IN. For questions about 'current' status, combine with window functions or pick latest event_ts per shipment_id/order_id. Consider materialized current status view for efficient 'current state' queries.

updated_by.description: Name or identifier of the operator/person/system who recorded the event (string). High cardinality.
updated_by.analysis_use: Attribution and accountability: measuring operator performance, error rates, and activity counts. Use with caution for privacy or PII-sensitive reporting.
business_questions: Which operators recorded the most FAILED events in the past month?
How many events did a particular operator record over a date range?
updated_by.nl2sql_hints: High-cardinality string—avoid grouping over entire column in large-result queries unless limited. Support exact matches and possibly fuzzy/contains queries for partial names. Consider joining to a users table for canonical IDs; avoid using as join key unless normalized.

gps_lat.description: Latitude coordinate where the event occurred (decimal(18,4)).
gps_lat.analysis_use: Geospatial analysis: mapping event locations, clustering failures by coordinates, computing distance between events or to store/hub, and visualizations.
business_questions: Plot FAILED events on a map to find hotspots by GPS coordinates.
Calculate average distance traveled between consecutive events for a shipment.
gps_lat.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal—use as numeric type in calculations. Combine with gps_long for geospatial functions (Haversine). Beware of coordinate precision; decimal(18,4) is ~10m precision. Handle NULLs. For NL2SQL, expect numeric literals and support bounding-box queries (lat between x and y).

gps_long.description: Longitude coordinate where the event occurred (decimal(18,4)).
gps_long.analysis_use: Used with gps_lat for mapping, proximity rules, and route verification. Enables geofencing checks and cluster analyses.
business_questions: Which deliveries had event coordinates outside expected delivery zones?
Find all events within X km of a given coordinate (e.g., hub).
gps_long.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal—use in pair with gps_lat for spatial queries. Support distance calculations and bounding-box filters. Consider spatial index or storing as geometry type if spatial queries increase.

photo_url.description: URL to delivery photo/evidence (expected as string), but currently mis-typed as decimal and completely NULL in this dataset (data-quality issue).
photo_url.analysis_use: Would be used to fetch or validate proof-of-delivery images, count photo submissions, and audit visual evidence for disputes. Currently unusable until corrected.
business_questions: What proportion of DELIVERED events have a photo_url (proof-of-delivery)?
Show photo_url for a given delivery_id to review proof image.
photo_url.nl2sql_hints: Data type is incorrect—should be varchar/text and nullable. NL2SQL should treat this column as optional text URL. Expect many NULLs; use IS NOT NULL to find present photos. If values become populated, beware of long URLs—support LIKE/substring searches if users query by filename.

notes.description: Free-text notes about the event (expected as string), but currently mis-typed as decimal and all NULL (data-quality issue).
notes.analysis_use: Would provide context on failures, exceptions and manual remarks; useful for qualitative analysis, root-cause tagging and NLP extraction of reasons for fails.
business_questions: List notes for FAILED_ATTEMPT events to identify common failure reasons.
Search notes for specific keywords (e.g., 'customer not available', 'wrong address').
notes.nl2sql_hints: Data type is incorrect—should be text/varchar. For NL2SQL, treat as full-text or LIKE searchable field. Expect many NULLs; consider integrating basic NLP or tagging to structure common reasons. Use caution with large free-text aggregates.

test_id.description: Unique identifier for each A/B test (primary key).
test_id.analysis_use: Primary filter/group key for experiment-level analyses; used to join to an exposure/assignment table or experiment metadata tables. Ideal for row-level lookups.
business_questions: Show full details for experiment ABT003001.
How many experiments have been created in the last quarter (group by test_id)?
test_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (nvarchar). Use equality or IN clauses for lookup. It's the natural join key if an exposures/assignments table exists. Avoid LIKE unless searching patterns; use exact match for performance.

test_name.description: Human-readable name/classification of the experiment (e.g., Pricing Banner Test).
test_name.analysis_use: Grouping and labeling in reports; aggregate metrics by test type; detect frequent experiment types. Low cardinality — suitable for GROUP BY or filter.
business_questions: How many 'Checkout Flow Experiment' tests have been run and what are their outcomes?
What is the average conversion uplift for each test_name?
test_name.nl2sql_hints: String field; use straightforward equality or GROUP BY. Because cardinality is low, GROUP BY test_name is efficient. Watch for inconsistent naming (trim/case).

variant_a.description: Label for variant A in the test (usually 'Control').
variant_a.analysis_use: Used for labeling comparisons and ensuring consistent mapping when joining to variants in assignment data. Low cardinality categorical filter or group field.
business_questions: Which tests used 'Control' as variant A?
Are conversion rates for tests where variant_a = 'Control' systematically different?
variant_a.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string. Expect limited unique values; use equality filters. If variant names vary across tests, consider normalizing. Not a join key unless exposures reference variant labels directly.

variant_b.description: Label for variant B in the test (usually 'Treatment').
variant_b.analysis_use: Same as variant_a: labeling comparisons, grouping by variant type, and cross-checks with assignment data.
business_questions: Which tests had 'Treatment' as variant B?
For tests with variant_b = 'Treatment', what is the average conversion uplift?
variant_b.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string. Use equality/grouping. Consider normalization if multiple variant naming conventions exist.

start_ts.description: Start timestamp of the experiment stored as nvarchar (e.g., '2025-08-04 15:17:40').
start_ts.analysis_use: Filter experiments by start date, compute experiment duration (with end_ts), timeline visualizations, cohorting tests by start period. Must be parsed/cast to datetime for correct temporal arithmetic and orderings.
business_questions: Which experiments started between 2025-08-01 and 2025-08-31?
How many tests were launched each week (bucket by start date)?
start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar: cast to datetime/datetime2 (e.g., CAST(start_ts AS datetime2) or TRY_CAST) in WHERE, ORDER BY or DATEDIFF calculations. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to avoid errors with malformed strings. Consider materializing a proper datetime column or index on parsed value for frequent temporal queries.

end_ts.description: End timestamp of the experiment stored as nvarchar (e.g., '2025-08-18 08:59:06').
end_ts.analysis_use: Compute test duration (end_ts - start_ts), filter completed tests by end date, and analyze time-to-result. Must be cast to datetime for arithmetic.
business_questions: Which completed experiments ended in July 2025?
What is the average duration (days) of completed tests?
end_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar: cast with CAST/TRY_CAST for date calculations. For duration use DATEDIFF with cast values. Nulls present for ongoing tests; guard against nulls in calculations.

status.description: Current lifecycle status of the test (RUNNING, CANCELLED, COMPLETED).
status.analysis_use: Filter experiments by lifecycle state (e.g., only COMPLETED for outcome analysis), compute counts by status, and monitor active experiments.
business_questions: How many experiments are currently RUNNING?
What proportion of experiments get CANCELLED vs COMPLETED?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string with small set of values. Use equality or IN. Good candidate for an index if status-based queries are frequent. Normalize values or enforce domain to avoid typos.

winner_variant.description: Recorded winner of the experiment: 'A', 'B', or 'NONE' (no winner or inconclusive).
winner_variant.analysis_use: Aggregate winner counts, compute win rates, cross-tabulate winner by test type or time period, and to quickly identify successful experiments.
business_questions: How many experiments had B as the winner in the last 6 months?
For which test_names is variant A more likely to win?
winner_variant.nl2sql_hints: Categorical; treat 'NONE' as a meaningful value (no winner). Use equality/IN and GROUP BY. If deriving winners from conversion rates, prefer recomputing or verifying stored winner_variant against conversion_rate_a/b for auditability.

conversion_rate_a.description: Summary conversion rate metric for variant A (decimal percentage, e.g., 0-100 or 0-1 depending on convention; stored as decimal(18,4)).
conversion_rate_a.analysis_use: Aggregate and compare performance between variants (mean, median, delta); compute uplift = conversion_rate_b - conversion_rate_a; rank experiments by lift. Use for statistical checks and dashboards.
business_questions: What is the average conversion_rate_a across completed tests?
Which experiments had conversion_rate_a above 5%?
conversion_rate_a.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal type—safe for arithmetic. Confirm unit (percentage vs fraction) before interpreting. Use COALESCE to handle nulls. Consider creating computed columns for delta and percent lift to accelerate repeated queries.

conversion_rate_b.description: Summary conversion rate metric for variant B (decimal(18,4)).
conversion_rate_b.analysis_use: Same as conversion_rate_a: compare to A, compute lift, filter top-performing experiments, and drive winner calculations.
business_questions: Which experiments had conversion_rate_b higher than conversion_rate_a by more than 2 percentage points?
What is the median conversion_rate_b across all completed experiments?
conversion_rate_b.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type; validate units. Use expressions like (conversion_rate_b - conversion_rate_a) AS lift. Handle nulls. For ranking use ORDER BY conversion_rate_b DESC. Consider indexing if used in WHERE frequently (less common).

created_at.description: Datetime when the test record was created (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and timeline analysis for when experiments were registered vs launched; cohort experiments by creation date; sort and filter creation activity.
business_questions: How many experiments were created each month?
Is there a delay between created_at and start_ts for tests?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Proper datetime2 type—use directly in WHERE / GROUP BY / ORDER BY. If comparing to string timestamps (start_ts/end_ts), cast those to datetime for accurate comparisons. Consider indexing created_at for time-series queries.

agent_id.description: Unique identifier for the acquisition agent (primary key).
agent_id.analysis_use: Key for lookups and joins to transactional/analytics tables; grouping/aggregation by agent; deduplication and entity counts.
business_questions: Which agents generated X number of customers or orders (when joined to orders)?
List the top-performing agents by revenue or conversions (after joining to sales data).
agent_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as primary join key. Stored as nvarchar(50) but actual max len ~10: match types when joining (avoid implicit conversions). Use exact equality filters (=) for single-agent queries, IN lists for small sets. Normalize spacing/case if inconsistent. Consider indexing/PK for fast lookup.

name.description: Full name of the acquisition agent used for display and human-readable reports.
name.analysis_use: Used for labeling results, de-duplicating human records, exporting lists, and operator-level reporting; not ideal for grouping at scale but useful in final presentation layers.
business_questions: What is the contact name for agent ECOMAG3001?
Provide a list of active agents and their contact details for outreach.
name.nl2sql_hints: Treat as descriptive string; avoid grouping by name if agent_id is available (use agent_id for deterministic grouping). Be mindful of NULLs. Use LIKE for partial-name search; prefer LIMIT when returning search results.

phone.analysis_use: Contactability checks, detect missing/invalid phone numbers, channel for outreach, join/match to external contact systems when normalized.
business_questions: Which agents do not have a phone number on record?
How many agents have phone numbers in a specific country code (e.g., +91)?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Contains PII — apply masking/redaction in reporting. Phone is nvarchar; normalize formatting for filters/search (trim, remove spaces/characters) before matching. Use LIKE '+91%' for country-code filters.

email.description: Agent email address used for notifications and login/communication.
email.analysis_use: Contactability audits, identify missing emails, deduplicate via domain analysis, map to corporate domains for role-based filtering.
business_questions: Which agents are missing email addresses?
How many agents use corporate domain (e.g., dailygreens.in) vs personal domains?
email.nl2sql_hints: PII — mask when exposing. Use LOWER() for case-insensitive comparisons. Use LIKE '%@domain' to aggregate by domain. Validate presence via IS NOT NULL and LENGTH checks.

acq_channel.description: Acquisition channel through which the agent recruits or operates (e.g., Social Media, Influencer, Referral).
acq_channel.analysis_use: Primary dimension for channel-level aggregation, performance comparisons, pivoting and filtering; low cardinality makes it efficient for GROUP BY and indexing.
business_questions: How many agents belong to each acquisition channel?
Which channels have the highest number of active agents?
acq_channel.nl2sql_hints: Categorical, low-cardinality field — ideal for GROUP BY and WHERE filters. Beware of spelling/casing variations; normalize values. When converting NL to SQL, map synonyms (e.g., 'WhatsApp' -> 'WhatsApp Outreach'). Consider indexing this column for fast GROUP BY and filter performance.

area.description: Geographic area or city where the agent operates (e.g., Surat, Rajkot).
area.analysis_use: Geographic aggregation and filtering, regional headcount, regional cohort analysis and mapping to demand or revenue when joined to transactional data.
business_questions: How many agents are operating in Surat vs other areas?
Which areas have the highest concentration of inactive agents?
area.nl2sql_hints: Moderate cardinality: GROUP BY area is common. Normalize naming (e.g., consistent city spellings). Use exact matches or IN lists for regional filters. Consider spatial joins or mapping table if standardizing regions.

join_date.description: Date the agent joined (onboarded). Stored as datetime2; often represents cohort membership.
join_date.analysis_use: Time-based cohorting, trend analysis (agents per month/quarter/year), filtering by hire date, age-of-agent metrics. Useful for cohort retention when joined with activity/sales tables.
business_questions: How many agents joined in Q1 2022?
What is the monthly trend of agent onboarding over time?
join_date.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 — use DATE(), CAST or CONVERT to extract date/month/year for grouping. For NL2SQL, map natural language time expressions (e.g., 'last 6 months') to explicit date ranges. Beware of time component when filtering for a date (use >= and < next_day).

status.analysis_use: Primary filter for operational lists and reporting; used in segmentation (active pool vs inactive), retention/re-activation analysis and headcount snapshots.
business_questions: How many active acquisition agents are currently available?
What percentage of agents are inactive and in which areas are inactivity rates highest?
status.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality, few distinct values — ideal for quick filters. Normalize values to expected tokens (ACTIVE/INACTIVE). Use equality checks; when using temporal snapshots, pair with created_at/join_date to build status-as-of-date logic (status may not reflect historical changes if not time-versioned).

created_at.description: Timestamp when the agent record was created in the system (ingestion/registration time).
created_at.analysis_use: Auditing and freshness checks, ordering by creation time, filtering recent records, deriving time-to-first-activity when joined to activity tables.
business_questions: Which agents were added in the last 30 days?
Are there spikes in agent creation on particular dates (e.g., campaigns or onboarding drives)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 — use for recency filters and ORDER BY. When implementing NL2SQL date filters, convert natural language windows (e.g., "last 7 days") to explicit BETWEEN or >=/<= conditions. Distinguish created_at from join_date (created_at may reflect record creation, not actual onboarding date).

audit_id.description: Identifier for the audit event. nvarchar(50). Appears unique per row in the sample (distinct=200) but not enforced as PK.
audit_id.analysis_use: Row-level identifier for deduplication, event-level filtering, and referencing specific audit records. Useful for fetching or displaying a single audit entry.
business_questions: What is the full detail of the audit event with audit_id = 'AUD004002'?
Are there duplicate audit_id values indicating ingestion issues?
audit_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a unique string identifier. Use equality filters (WHERE audit_id = '...') or IN lists. No numeric operations. If ensuring uniqueness in NL2SQL, check COUNT(*) GROUP BY audit_id to detect duplicates.

entity_type.description: Type/category of the entity acted upon (PAYMENT, CUSTOMER, ORDER, PRODUCT). nvarchar(50) but low cardinality (4).
entity_type.analysis_use: Primary grouping/segmenting field for aggregations (counts per entity_type), conditional joins to domain tables, and filtering to scope queries (e.g., only ORDER events).
business_questions: How are actions distributed across entity types (orders vs customers vs payments vs products)?
For a given entity_type, what proportion of actions are DELETE vs CREATE/UPDATE?
entity_type.nl2sql_hints: Use exact string matches (WHERE entity_type = 'ORDER') or IN lists. Consider normalizing casing (UPPER/LTRIM) if source may vary. Use entity_type to decide join target table in generated SQL.

entity_id.description: Identifier of the entity instance the action targeted (e.g., CUST1022, EORD2086). nvarchar(50) with many distinct values (126).
entity_id.analysis_use: Key for joining to domain tables (customers, orders, payments, products) when combined with entity_type. Used for grouping by specific entity or identifying frequently changed entities.
business_questions: Which entity_ids had the most actions in the past month?
What sequence of actions happened for a given entity_id (audit trail) and who performed them?
entity_id.nl2sql_hints: Entity_id is a string — use exact matches or prefix matching (LIKE 'EORD%') if needed. When joining, ensure join expressions match the target table's id column and use entity_type to select the correct target. Beware of whitespace or case differences; apply TRIM()/UPPER() if necessary.

action.description: Type of action performed on the entity: CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, STATUS_CHANGE. nvarchar(50) with 4 distinct values.
action.analysis_use: Aggregate by action type to measure volumes of creates/updates/deletes, filter for destructive actions (DELETE) or state transitions (STATUS_CHANGE), and compute action rates over time.
business_questions: How many DELETE actions occurred in the last 30 days and which entities were affected?
What is the trend of STATUS_CHANGE events over time for orders?
action.nl2sql_hints: Use exact string filters (WHERE action = 'DELETE'). Consider mapping or grouping similar actions in NLP (e.g., STATUS_CHANGE implies UPDATE of status). When generating NL2SQL, translate natural language synonyms ('removed' -> DELETE).

action_ts.description: Timestamp (as text) when the action occurred in format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'. Stored as nvarchar(50) not a native datetime.
action_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for event-time analyses: ordering events, windowing (hour/day/week), and time-based aggregations. Must be cast to datetime for proper time functions.
business_questions: Show all actions on entity_id = 'EORD2073' ordered by action_ts.
Count actions per day over the last 14 days.
action_ts.nl2sql_hints: Always CAST/TRY_CAST action_ts to a datetime (e.g., TRY_CAST(action_ts AS datetime) or CAST(action_ts AS datetime) depending on DB). Use alias like event_time for downstream grouping. Guard against malformed strings with TRY_CAST or ISDATE checks. For ordering use ORDER BY CAST(action_ts AS datetime) DESC. When limiting by date range, apply CAST in WHERE (or best, persist a native datetime column).

performed_by.description: Actor who performed the action (system / support_agent / admin). nvarchar(50) with 3 distinct values.
performed_by.analysis_use: Segment actions by actor to attribute changes to system vs human agents, compute per-actor activity and detect misuse or unusual activity patterns.
business_questions: Which support_agent performed the most updates in the last week?
What percentage of DELETEs were executed by 'system' vs human users?
performed_by.nl2sql_hints: Use equality or IN filters (WHERE performed_by = 'support_agent'). For natural-language queries asking for 'human' vs 'automated', map 'system' to automated. Consider role mapping if performed_by values expand. Watch for case/spacing differences.

ip_address.description: Source IP address of the action. nvarchar(50) but values look like IPv4 strings (max length 15). High cardinality.
ip_address.analysis_use: Security and forensic analysis: identify suspicious IPs, group actions by IP, detect repeated destructive actions from same IP, geolocation enrichment in downstream steps.
business_questions: Which IP addresses generated the most DELETE actions in the past 30 days?
List IP addresses that performed actions against more than N distinct entity_ids (potential automation).
ip_address.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; use equality, GROUP BY, and COUNT(DISTINCT entity_id). For pattern matching (subnets) use LIKE '101.20.%' or substring functions. Consider normalizing IPv6/IPv4 formats. If geolocation is required, join or enrich externally — NL2SQL should return distinct IPs for enrichment.

notes.description: Numeric notes field declared decimal(18,4) but contains 100% nulls in the sample. Possibly reserved for future numeric metadata or mis-typed column.
notes.analysis_use: Currently unused due to all-null values. If populated later, could be used for numeric metadata (e.g., reason codes, numeric flags) — treat carefully because DDL type is numeric not text.
business_questions: Are there numeric notes or metadata associated with certain audit events (if populated)?
Which audit events have non-null notes and what are their statistics (min/max/avg)?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Because notes is numeric and currently null, guard queries with WHERE notes IS NOT NULL before numeric aggregations. If natural-language mentions 'notes' expecting text, clarify that this column is numeric and may be mis-typed; look for a different textual notes column if required. Use CAST/CONVERT carefully if data ingestion changes type.

browse_id.description: Unique identifier for each browsing event (primary key).
browse_id.analysis_use: Used to deduplicate events, count total events (COUNT(*)), and as a stable event identifier for joins to raw logs or debug traces.
business_questions: How many distinct browsing events occurred in a period?
Are there duplicate or retried events present in the dataset (identify by browse_id)?
browse_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but observed max len 8. Use as unique key. Typical patterns: WHERE browse_id = 'BRW05001', SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT browse_id). Avoid casting unless necessary. Do not aggregate browse_id; use it for DISTINCT or JOIN ON.

customer_id.description: Logical customer identifier for the user who generated the event (may be NULL for anonymous sessions).
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter and group to compute per-customer event counts, unique visitors (COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id)), sessionization, and join key to customer master tables for enrichment.
business_questions: Which customers have the highest browsing activity?
How many unique customers searched for 'tomato' last week?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Nullable—handle NULLs in queries (e.g., WHERE customer_id IS NOT NULL). Common joins: JOIN ecom_customers USING(customer_id) or ON ecom_customers.customer_id = ecom_browsing_history.customer_id. Use COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) for unique users; consider COALESCE(customer_id,'anonymous') for grouping.

sku.description: Stock-keeping unit indicating the product referenced by the event (may be NULL for pure category/search events).
sku.analysis_use: Group and filter to measure product interest (views/search-to-view mapping), top-N SKU reports, and join to products/product_variants for metadata and conversion metrics.
business_questions: Which SKUs receive the most product views?
For SKU 'TOM001', how many searches preceded a view within the same session?
sku.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but small distinct count (7). Nullable—filter WHERE sku IS NOT NULL for product-level metrics. Joins: JOIN products ON products.sku = ecom_browsing_history.sku. Use GROUP BY sku ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC for top-N. Normalize case/trim if product keys vary.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the browsing event occurred (event time).
event_ts.analysis_use: Primary temporal field for filtering and time-series aggregation (hour/day/week), sessionization, recency calculations, and ordering events within sessions.
business_questions: How many events happened per hour/day this week?
What is the peak hour for SEARCH events?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: datetime2. Use range filters: WHERE event_ts BETWEEN '2025-10-23' AND '2025-10-24'. Use DATEPART/CAST/TRUNC equivalents for grouping (DATE(event_ts) or CONVERT(date,event_ts)). Handle NULLs—fallback to created_at if event_ts is missing. For NL2SQL parsing: expect phrases like 'last 7 days', 'between X and Y', 'hour of day'.

event_type.description: Categorical field describing the kind of browsing event (e.g., SEARCH, VIEW_PRODUCT, CATEGORY_CLICK).
event_type.analysis_use: Used for grouping, funnel analysis (sequence of events), filters to isolate specific behaviors, and distribution metrics.
business_questions: What proportion of events are SEARCH vs VIEW_PRODUCT vs CATEGORY_CLICK?
How many VIEW_PRODUCT events did we have for a specific SKU last month?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with low cardinality (3). Use simple equality filters (event_type = 'SEARCH') or IN lists. Consider mapping synonyms in NL2SQL (e.g., 'product view' -> VIEW_PRODUCT, 'category click' -> CATEGORY_CLICK). GROUP BY event_type for distribution.

search_query.description: The search string entered by the user when event_type = SEARCH (may be NULL otherwise).
