search_query.analysis_use: Aggregate and rank top queries, analyze query-to-product mapping, detect spelling/normalization needs, and feed query analytics (intent, long-tail). Useful for conversion tracking when joined with subsequent events.
business_questions: What are the top search queries and their frequencies?
Which search queries lead most often to product views for SKU-level items?
search_query.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Nullable; often only populated for SEARCH events—filter event_type = 'SEARCH' when analyzing. Normalize with LOWER(TRIM(search_query)) for grouping and matching. Use LIKE or full-text search for fuzzy matching; beware of stopwords. Typical NL2SQL filters: 'search for tomato' -> WHERE LOWER(search_query) LIKE '%tomato%'.

device_type.description: Client device type used for the event (ios, android, web).
device_type.analysis_use: Group and filter to understand device-specific behavior, compare engagement and conversions across platforms, and segment reports.
business_questions: What percent of SEARCH events originate from mobile (ios+android) vs web?
Which device type has the highest average events per user?
device_type.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with small set of known values. Use simple equality or IN clauses (device_type IN ('ios','android')). Map synonyms (mobile -> ios+android) in NL2SQL. Handle NULLs by grouping them as 'unknown' or excluding them as requested.

referrer_source.description: Traffic source/referrer that brought the user to the site (e.g., FB Ads, Google, WhatsApp, Direct).
referrer_source.analysis_use: Attribution and channel analysis: group by referrer_source to measure volume and downstream engagement (views, searches), ROI proxies, and campaign performance.
business_questions: Which referrer source drives the most searches and product views?
How does engagement (views per session) differ for FB Ads vs Organic (Direct/Google)?
referrer_source.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with low cardinality. Use GROUP BY referrer_source and JOIN to acquisition/campaign tables if available. Normalize naming (e.g., 'FB Ads' vs 'Facebook Ads'). Use WHERE referrer_source = 'FB Ads' or referrer_source IN (...) for filtering.

created_at.description: Row insertion or ingestion timestamp (when the record was created in the ETL system).
created_at.analysis_use: Data freshness checks, ETL lag calculations (compare created_at vs event_ts), partitioning/archival by ingestion date, and troubleshooting backfills or late-arriving events.
business_questions: What is the typical lag between event_ts and created_at?
Which records were ingested in the last 24 hours?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2. Use for ETL/time-based partition filters: WHERE created_at >= DATEADD(day,-1,GETDATE()). For lag analysis: SELECT AVG(DATEDIFF(second,event_ts,created_at)). Prefer event_ts for behavioral analysis and created_at for ingestion/freshness.

cart_item_id.description: Surrogate unique identifier for each cart line item (primary key).
cart_item_id.analysis_use: Use for deduplication and row-level joins. Not typically aggregated except to count distinct items. Useful as a stable identifier when joining back to logs or other ETL tables.
business_questions: How many unique cart items were created in a time period?
Which cart items were updated most frequently (join on updated_at)?
cart_item_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NOT NULL; unique PK. Use equality or IN filters for precise retrieval. Avoid range predicates. When generating SQL, reference as primary key; no casting needed.

cart_id.description: Identifier of the parent cart that the item belongs to (FK to ecom_carts).
cart_id.analysis_use: Frequent grouping key to compute items per cart, total cart value (sum qty*price_at_add), and to join with ecom_carts and downstream orders. Useful for session-level analysis and conversion funnel joins.
business_questions: How many items does each cart contain on average?
Which carts contain a specific SKU or variant?
cart_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; FK to zuna_etl.ecom_carts.cart_id. Use equality/IN filters and GROUP BY cart_id for aggregation. Be mindful of NULLs — exclude or treat as anonymous carts. Consider joining to ecom_carts on exact string equality.

sku.description: Stock keeping unit code representing the product added to the cart (natural product key).
sku.analysis_use: Primary attribute for product-level aggregations: counts, frequency, top SKUs, SKU-level conversion when joined to orders. Good for GROUP BY and top-N queries. Low cardinality with skew—use top-skew optimization for NL2SQL (LIMIT + ORDER BY count).
business_questions: Which SKUs are most frequently added to carts?
Which SKUs have the highest average quantity per cart item?
sku.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; low distinct count (10). When interpreting user requests like 'top products' default to COUNT(*) grouped by sku and ORDER BY count DESC. Join to products or product_variants using sku if available; if both sku and variant_id exist, clarify which to prefer.

qty.description: Quantity of the SKU added to the cart for this cart item (decimal with 4 fractional digits).
qty.analysis_use: Used in aggregations (SUM/AVG) for total items and average quantity per item/cart. Common filter for thresholds (qty > 1) or distribution analysis (histogram). Multiply with price_at_add to compute line value.
business_questions: What is the total quantity of items added to carts in a period?
How many line items have quantity greater than 1 (bulk adds)?
qty.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) NULL. Use numeric comparisons and aggregate functions (SUM(qty), AVG(qty)). Be careful with nulls — treat as 0 or exclude depending on intent. If computing revenue-like metrics, cast/multiply by price_at_add appropriately and consider datatype promotion.

added_at.description: Timestamp when the cart item was first added to the cart.
added_at.analysis_use: Time-series analysis: trending when items are added (hour/day/week), sessionization with carts, cohort creation based on add time, and latency calculations between add and update/checkout.
business_questions: At what times of day are items most frequently added to carts?
How long do items remain in carts on average (added_at to updated_at or to checkout)?
added_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 NULL. Use range predicates for time window filters (added_at BETWEEN ...). For bucketing use DATEPART or cast to date. Handle time zone considerations externally. Ensure use of appropriate indexing on datetime when filtering by ranges.

updated_at.description: Timestamp of the last update to the cart item (quantity change, reservation change, etc.).
updated_at.analysis_use: Detect edits to cart items, compute time-to-edit or time-to-checkout, identify churn (items removed if joined with cart state), and audit activity. Useful for finding recently updated items.
business_questions: Which items were modified shortly before checkout?
How often are cart items updated after initial addition?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 NULL. Use for ordering (latest updates) and time-difference calculations (DATEDIFF between added_at and updated_at). Consider null semantics if not updated (may equal added_at or be null).

reserved_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the item was reserved (e.g., held in inventory) when added to the cart.
reserved_flag.analysis_use: Filter/group by reservation status to analyze reserved vs non-reserved items, compute reservation rate by SKU/time, and reconcile against inventory reservations. Useful in joins with inventory tables.
business_questions: What share of cart items are reserved vs not reserved?
Which SKUs have the highest reservation rates?
reserved_flag.nl2sql_hints: bit NULL. Use boolean filters reserved_flag = 1 or = 0; account for NULLs explicitly. For aggregations use SUM(CASE WHEN reserved_flag=1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) or COUNT with WHERE. Be aware some SQL dialects require CAST or explicit boolean handling.

price_at_add.description: Snapshot of the item's price when it was added to the cart (bigint).
price_at_add.analysis_use: Used to compute line value (qty * price_at_add), historic price analysis, discount/repricing detection (when compared to current price/pricing tables), and average price per SKU in carts.
business_questions: What was the average price of items added to carts during a campaign?
How much potential revenue is sitting in carts (sum qty * price_at_add)?
price_at_add.nl2sql_hints: bigint NULL. Multiply with qty (decimal) to calculate line_value; cast if necessary to avoid truncation. Watch for currency units (assume integer cents or whole units) — clarify with domain experts. Use GROUP BY sku or cart_id for aggregations.

variant_id.description: Identifier for the product variant associated with the cart item (e.g., size/color), complementary to sku.
variant_id.analysis_use: Join key to product_variant or product tables to get variant attributes (size, color). Useful to disambiguate SKUs that map to multiple variants and to perform variant-level popularity and stock reconciliation.
business_questions: Which variants (size/color) are most frequently added to carts?
Are there variant-level stock issues indicated by high reservation counts?
variant_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL. Moderate distinctness. Prefer exact string join to product_variants if present. When user queries mention 'variant' or 'size/color', map to variant_id. Watch for cases where sku alone suffices—ask follow-up or join both.

notes.description: Freeform or numeric notes related to the cart item. In the provided sample this column is numeric type and entirely NULL.
notes.analysis_use: Currently not useful (100% null). If populated, could be used for annotations or numeric flags; treat with caution because declared decimal type suggests numeric data rather than text.
business_questions: Are there any numeric notes or special instructions attached to cart items?
Has notes ever been used to flag special handling or promotions?
notes.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) NULL but 100% null in sample. Likely unused placeholder; NL2SQL systems should avoid referencing unless user explicitly asks and should treat as nullable. Confirm intended semantics before using in aggregates or filters.

cart_id.description: Unique identifier for the cart (primary key).
cart_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier; used for point lookups, deduplication, joins to cart_items and orders, and grouping at cart-level.
business_questions: Retrieve full cart record by cart_id (debugging/support).
How many unique carts were created in a date range?
Which carts converted to orders (join by cart_id)?
cart_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) (max len observed 8). Always use equality filters for point lookups (WHERE cart_id = '...'). Use this as the join key to ecom_cart_items and ecom_orders when those tables store cart_id. Prefer exact matches, avoid LIKE unless necessary.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who owns the cart (nullable).
customer_id.analysis_use: Join key to ecom_customers for demographics or retention analysis; grouping to compute carts per customer; filter for customer-level cohorts.
business_questions: How many carts does a customer create on average?
What is abandonment rate by customer cohort or segment?
Which customers have the highest total cart value?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Nullable — account for NULLs when joining (use LEFT JOIN if you want carts with no customer). Use equality or IN predicates for customer cohorts. Consider indexing customer_id for fast group/joins.

session_id.description: Session identifier associated with the cart (nullable).
session_id.analysis_use: Session-level grouping to track multiple carts per session, measure session conversion, and correlate session activity with cart changes.
business_questions: How many carts are created per session on average?
What is conversion rate per session (session to order)?
session_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Can be used for grouping or filtering; treat as string equality. Sessions may be repeated; deduplicate if you want unique sessions (e.g., COUNT(DISTINCT session_id)).

created_at.description: Timestamp when the cart was created.
created_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trend analysis, time-binning (hour/day/week), funnel timing, and cohort definitions.
business_questions: How many carts were created per day/week/month?
How does abandonment rate change over time (by created_at)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2. Use range predicates (BETWEEN or >=/<=) and date truncation functions (CAST/CONVERT/DATE_TRUNC depending on SQL dialect) for grouping. Watch timezone normalization if upstream data mixes zones. Indexing on created_at benefits time-window queries.

last_updated_at.description: Timestamp of the last update to the cart (e.g., when items changed or status updated).
last_updated_at.analysis_use: Used to measure cart activity recency, calculate time-to-abandon or time-between-updates, and find stale carts.
business_questions: How long after creation are carts typically updated?
Which carts have not been updated recently (stale) and may be abandoned soon?
last_updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2. Useful in comparisons with created_at or abandoned_at. Use as a range filter for recent activity queries. Consider indexing if queries filter by last_updated_at.

total_items.description: Total number of items in the cart (aggregate count of cart_items).
total_items.analysis_use: Grouping and distribution analysis; input for binning (1 item, 2 items, etc.); correlate with total_amount and abandonment.
business_questions: What is the distribution of total_items across carts?
Are carts with more items more or less likely to be abandoned?
total_items.nl2sql_hints: Type: bigint but small cardinality (8 distinct values). Good candidate for GROUP BY and histogram queries. When joining with cart_items, ensure consistency if total_items is computed vs stored (use cart_items counts to validate).

total_amount.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM, AVG), segmentation (high-value carts), AOV calculations, and revenue forecasting when joined to orders. Correlate with coupon usage and device_type.
business_questions: What is the average cart value by device_type or coupon_code?
What share of cart value is abandoned (sum of total_amount for abandoned carts)?
total_amount.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use SUM/AVG/MEDIAN for aggregations. Be explicit about currency — currency column currently single-valued (INR) but join to pricing/product tables if multi-currency in other datasets. Beware of NULL total_amount values.

coupon_code.description: Coupon code applied to the cart (nullable).
coupon_code.analysis_use: Group by coupon to measure usage frequency and effect on cart value and conversion; filter to analyze coupon-specific campaigns.
business_questions: How many carts used each coupon and what is the conversion rate for each?
Do certain coupons lead to higher average cart value?
coupon_code.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) with only 2 distinct values in sample and ~37% NULL. Treat NULL as 'no coupon'. Use equality or IN predicates. Join to ecom_coupons for coupon metadata. Low cardinality makes GROUP BY efficient; consider CASE WHEN coupon_code IS NULL THEN 'NONE' END for nicer reporting.

currency.description: Currency code for the cart monetary values (e.g., INR).
currency.analysis_use: Filter/group by currency in multi-currency datasets, convert amounts if needed. In this dataset currency is constant (INR).
business_questions: What is the revenue by currency?
Are conversion rates or average cart values different across currencies?
currency.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) but observed max len 3 and single value 'INR'. If system expands to multi-currency, ensure joins to currency conversions or normalization. Currently can often be ignored for filtering unless future data adds currencies.

device_type.description: Device/platform used to create the cart (web/ios/android).
device_type.analysis_use: Segmentation and comparison (abandonment rate, AOV) by device. Useful for UX and platform-specific optimizations.
business_questions: Which device_type has the highest abandonment rate?
Which device_type has the highest average cart value?
device_type.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) with three distinct values. Use equality filters or GROUP BY device_type. Consider normalizing values (lowercase) if data is inconsistent. Low cardinality — good for indexed filtered queries and GROUP BY.

abandoned_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the cart was abandoned (True) or not (False).
abandoned_flag.analysis_use: Primary filter for abandonment analysis, compute abandonment rates, aggregate abandoned value, and identify targets for remarketing.
business_questions: What percent of carts are abandoned overall and by device/coupon?
What is the total abandoned revenue (sum total_amount where abandoned_flag = True)?
abandoned_flag.nl2sql_hints: Type: bit (boolean). Use explicit boolean predicates (WHERE abandoned_flag = 1 or = 0) or IS TRUE/FALSE depending on dialect. Combine with abandoned_at for timing analyses. Ensure NULL semantics — treat NULL as unknown (explicit check if present).

abandoned_at.description: Timestamp when the cart was marked abandoned (nullable).
abandoned_at.analysis_use: Measure time-to-abandon (abandoned_at - created_at), temporal patterns of abandonment, and windowed remarketing triggers. Filter to recent abandonments.
business_questions: How long after cart creation do customers typically abandon a cart?
What times of day have the highest number of abandons?
abandoned_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2 and ~59% NULL (only set when abandoned_flag is True). Use range and date-aggregation functions. When calculating time differences, guard against NULLs. Consider combining with abandoned_flag to ensure consistency (WHERE abandoned_flag = 1 AND abandoned_at IS NOT NULL).

coupon_usage_id.description: Surrogate identifier for the coupon usage event (string).
coupon_usage_id.analysis_use: Use to reference individual usage rows; can be used in deduplication and row-level exports. Not safe to assume uniqueness — treat as non-unique identifier unless cleaned.
business_questions: How many unique coupon usage events exist?
Are there duplicate coupon_usage_id values indicating ingestion issues?
coupon_usage_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; do not assume primary-key semantics. When users ask for 'unique usage id' or count of usage events, prefer COUNT(*) or COUNT(DISTINCT coupon_usage_id) explicitly. If deduplication required, include created_at or applied_ts in the GROUP BY or window functions. Avoid joins on coupon_usage_id unless upstream systems guarantee uniqueness.

coupon_id.description: Identifier of the coupon applied (foreign key to ecom_coupons).
coupon_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering key for coupon-level aggregates (redemption counts, total/avg discount). Join key to ecom_coupons to bring in coupon metadata (type, campaign, issue/expiry dates).
business_questions: Which coupons are redeemed most often?
What is the total discount cost per coupon?
coupon_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as FK when composing joins: JOIN zuna_etl.ecom_coupons USING (coupon_id) or ON ecom_coupon_usage.coupon_id = ecom_coupons.coupon_id. Because values are strings, comparisons should be exact; consider trimming/casing if source inconsistencies exist. Indexing/filtering on coupon_id is beneficial for performance when queries filter by coupon(s). Use GROUP BY coupon_id for aggregates and include coupon name/description by joining coupons table.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who applied the coupon (string, can be joined to ecom_customers).
customer_id.analysis_use: Group/filter for customer-level analytics (top redeemers, average discount received per customer). Join to ecom_customers for segmentation attributes and lifetime value comparisons.
business_questions: Which customers redeem the most coupons during a period?
What is the average discount received per customer segment?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as a grouping/filtering key. For NL2SQL, clarify whether questions mean distinct customers or total redemptions (COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) vs COUNT(*)). When joining to customers, use exact equality on this string column. Consider NULL customer_id handling for guest/checkouts — use IS NULL checks or coalesce in translations.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the coupon application (foreign key to ecom_orders).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to retrieve order totals, statuses, dates and compute discount as percent of order value or determine order-level effects of coupons. Can be used to find orders with multiple coupons.
business_questions: Which orders used coupons and what total discount was applied to those orders?
Do certain orders or order sizes (value buckets) correlate with coupon usage?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Join on order_id to zuna_etl.ecom_orders (ON ecom_coupon_usage.order_id = ecom_orders.order_id). When computing relative impact, cast discount_given and order total to numeric types and handle NULLs. Watch out for multiple coupon rows per order — aggregate discount_given by order_id if the question asks for total discount per order. If users say 'coupon used on order X', match exactly by string; consider normalizing whitespace or case if source inconsistent.

applied_ts.description: Timestamp string when the coupon was applied (stored as nvarchar).
applied_ts.analysis_use: Primary field for event time analysis (redemption time). Must be cast/parsed to datetime for time-series, date bucketing (day/week/month) or range filtering. Use for ordering events when created_at differs from application time.
business_questions: How many coupons were applied per day/week/month?
What is the time distribution of coupon redemptions during a campaign?
applied_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar(50) — critical to CAST/TRY_CAST or parse to datetime in NL2SQL translations: e.g., TRY_CAST(applied_ts AS datetime2) or PARSE depending on SQL dialect. If parsing fails, fallback to created_at. When users ask for 'between dates' or 'last 7 days', convert the string to datetime before filtering (WHERE TRY_CAST(applied_ts AS datetime2) BETWEEN ...). Suggest normalizing applied_ts to proper datetime in ETL and/or adding an indexed computed column used by NL2SQL engines for faster date filters.

discount_given.description: Monetary amount of discount applied for the usage event (decimal(18,4)).
discount_given.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation and statistical analysis: SUM(discount_given) to compute total coupon cost, AVG for average discount, MAX/MIN for extremes, and GROUP BY to analyze by coupon/customer/order/date. Useful to compute discount as percentage of order value after joining to orders.
business_questions: What is the total discount cost incurred by coupons in a period?
What is the average discount amount per redemption for each coupon?
discount_given.nl2sql_hints: Numeric column — use SUM(), AVG(), COUNT() over this field. Handle NULLs explicitly (COALESCE(discount_given,0)) when summing. When computing percentages relative to order totals, ensure consistent scale and handle decimal precision. Watch for negative values (returns/adjustments) — filter or include according to question intent.

created_at.description: Datetime when this row was created in the ETL (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Source-of-truth ingestion timestamp for auditing and incremental ingestion. Use for recency filters when applied_ts is missing or unreliable. Can be used to analyze data arrival patterns and detect late-arriving events.
business_questions: When were coupon usage records created in the system (ingestion timing)?
How many coupon usage rows arrived in each ingestion batch or day?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Already datetime2 — preferred for time-range filters in NL2SQL when applied_ts cannot be parsed. Use directly in WHERE created_at BETWEEN ... or for time bucketing (CONVERT(date, created_at) or DATE_TRUNC depending on dialect). For incremental queries, created_at is a reliable column to use for watermarking; recommend indexing if incremental reads are common.

address_id.description: Unique identifier for the address (primary key).
address_id.analysis_use: Uniquely identifies rows; used for precise joins to address-level events or logs and for de-duplication. Useful as GROUP BY key when listing addresses.
business_questions: Which address entries belong to a single customer (list of address_id by customer)?
Are there duplicate or suspicious address_id values across the dataset?
address_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar primary key; exact-match filters and equality joins are typical. Use address_id in WHERE or JOIN conditions as a unique key. Avoid LIKE on this column unless user query explicitly requests pattern matching.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who owns the address (FK to ecom_customers.customer_id).
customer_id.analysis_use: Main join key to customer table; grouping and counting addresses per customer; filtering addresses for a particular customer; joining to orders/payments to map deliveries to customers.
business_questions: How many addresses does customer CUST123 have?
List recent addresses for a given customer for contact or verification.
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar FK. Use for JOINs to ecom_customers and to filter addresses by customer. Expect some NULLs; queries should handle customer_id IS NULL if necessary. Use exact equality for customer lookups.

label.description: Address label provided by the user (e.g., Home, Work).
label.analysis_use: Low-cardinality grouping/filtering to separate Home vs Work addresses, compute relative proportions, and select preferred delivery addresses if label is used in business logic.
business_questions: What percentage of addresses are labelled 'Home' vs 'Work'?
How many 'Work' addresses are in city X?
label.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar, low cardinality. Support simple equality filters (label = 'Home') and GROUP BY label. Consider mapping synonyms/case variations if user input uses different casing.

addr_line1.description: Primary street address or building name for the address.
addr_line1.analysis_use: Used for display, fuzzy matching or de-duplication of addresses, and grouping by common addr_line1 (e.g., apartment complexes or market names). Can be used in text filters or pattern searches.
business_questions: Which addresses contain 'Main Market' and how many customers live there?
List addresses in a particular street or building for delivery route planning.
addr_line1.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar, mostly unique. Support LIKE/ILIKE or CONTAINS queries for text search. For NL2SQL map free-text address filters to SQL LIKE '%keyword%'. Consider normalizing common tokens (St/Street, Rd/Road) in pre-processing.

addr_line2.description: Secondary address detail (landmark or additional directions). Many repeated values and some nulls.
addr_line2.analysis_use: Useful for grouping by landmark (e.g., Near Bus Stop) to find delivery concentration and common local descriptors; treat null as no landmark.
business_questions: How many addresses include 'Near Bus Stop' as a landmark?
Which neighborhoods use specific addr_line2 markers frequently?
addr_line2.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with ~27% nulls and only a few distinct values. Use IS NULL checks and equality or LIKE for matching. Because values are short and repetitive, exact-match filters often suffice.

city.analysis_use: Primary geographic aggregator for grouping, filtering, regional reporting and join constraints to city-level delivery cost or logistics tables.
business_questions: How many addresses are in Surat vs Vadodara?
Which cities have the most 'Work' addresses?
city.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with small cardinality (~10). Use equality filters and GROUP BY city. Consider handling common misspellings or alternate names in NL2SQL mapping.

state.analysis_use: Mostly static in this dataset but useful for explicit filters, validation and future-proofing for multi-state expansion.
business_questions: Confirm whether all addresses belong to Gujarat
Filter addresses by state when expanding outside the current state
state.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar and currently single-value. NL2SQL should allow state equality but can optimize queries by recognizing low selectivity; avoid state filters when user does not specify them.

pincode.description: Postal code / ZIP code for the address (numeric).
pincode.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation at postal-code level for delivery cost zones, regional analysis and mapping to external regional datasets. Useful in join conditions with logistics/courier tables keyed by pincode.
business_questions: Which pincodes have the highest number of addresses?
How many orders/deliveries are being sent to pincode 385556?
pincode.nl2sql_hints: BIGINT numeric type. May need to treat as string if leading zeros are possible in other datasets. Use equality and IN for filtering multiple pincodes and GROUP BY for distribution. Beware of type mismatches when joining to tables that store pincode as string.

country.analysis_use: Usually constant here; used for validation and future multi-country support, and to filter in global queries.
business_questions: Are any addresses outside India present?
Filter addresses by country for international shipping rules
country.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar, single-value in current data. Use equality match. NL2SQL can default to country='India' only when user context implies it; otherwise include it only if explicitly requested.

latitude.description: Latitude coordinate of the address for geolocation.
latitude.analysis_use: Geospatial calculations: nearest-store, distance/heatmap generation, clustering of customer addresses, mapping addresses to delivery zones. Can be used with longitude to compute Haversine distances.
business_questions: Which customers live within 5 km of warehouse X?
Where are the highest-density address clusters by lat/long?
latitude.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). For distance calculations use appropriate functions or convert to floats; consider indexing strategy for geospatial queries (geography/geometry types or spatial index) if scale rises. Handle NULLs and pair latitude/longitude checks together.

longitude.description: Longitude coordinate of the address for geolocation.
longitude.analysis_use: Paired with latitude for geographic joins, distance calculations and mapping visualizations. Used in proximity and routing calculations.
business_questions: List addresses sorted by distance from a given longitude/latitude point.
Find addresses within bounding box or polygon for a delivery zone.
longitude.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Use together with latitude; consider casting to geography type for spatial queries. NL2SQL should generate Haversine or spatial-indexed queries depending on backend capabilities.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the address record was created.
created_at.analysis_use: Temporal filtering and trend analysis (new addresses over time), recency checks for verification, and ordering to find the most/least recently added addresses.
business_questions: How many addresses were added in the last 30 days?
Show the most recently added addresses for verification purposes.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 type. Use range filters (>=, BETWEEN) for time-window queries and ORDER BY created_at DESC for latest entries. Watch timezone conventions if input queries specify relative times; ensure NL2SQL maps 'last month' to correct date arithmetic functions.

customer_id.description: Unique identifier for each customer; logical primary key for this table (nvarchar up to 50, actual max length observed 8).
customer_id.analysis_use: Join key to customer master and transactional tables; filter to target individual customers; group by to count or list customers. Use equality filters for best performance; suitable for IN-lists for batch targeting.
business_questions: Which customers should be included in a campaign targeting 'Champions'?
Show the full profile and segmentation for a given customer_id.
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key (nvarchar). Use exact equality or IN lists (e.g. WHERE customer_id = 'CUST1000' or WHERE customer_id IN ('CUST1000','CUST1001')). Avoid leading-wildcard LIKE for performance. Consider trimming/casing only if source strings vary; max observed length is 8.

rfm_segment.description: Categorical RFM segment assigned to the customer (e.g., Champions, Regulars, At Risk, Hibernating). Low cardinality (4 distinct).
rfm_segment.analysis_use: Primary grouping/segment filter for aggregations (counts, avg loyalty_score, avg_order_value). Useful for segment-level cohort and trend reports and to build targets for campaigns.
business_questions: How many customers are in each RFM segment and what are their average metrics?
Which segments contain the highest purchase_frequency and avg_order_value?
rfm_segment.nl2sql_hints: Use exact string comparisons (rfm_segment = 'Champions'). Because it is low-cardinality, GROUP BY rfm_segment is efficient. When users use synonyms (e.g., 'inactive' vs 'Hibernating'), map to canonical segment values in NL2SQL parsing. Handle NULLs explicitly (WHERE rfm_segment IS NULL or COALESCE).

loyalty_score.description: Integer-like score (bigint) representing customer loyalty, derived from behavior/points. 59 distinct values observed.
loyalty_score.analysis_use: Aggregation (AVG, MIN, MAX), filters (>= threshold for VIP), distributions and ranking customers. Combine with avg_order_value to prioritize outreach.
business_questions: Which customers have loyalty_score above X and should receive a reward?
What is the average loyalty_score by RFM segment?
loyalty_score.nl2sql_hints: Numeric column; use numeric comparisons and ranges (e.g. WHERE loyalty_score >= 50). Watch for NULLs (filter or COALESCE). Prefer numeric operators, avoid casting to string. Use ORDER BY loyalty_score DESC for top-N queries.

avg_order_value.description: Average order value per customer (decimal(18,4)), pre-computed from transactional history.
avg_order_value.analysis_use: Aggregation (AVG, SUM when weighted by order count), filters for high-value customers (>= threshold), sorting for top customers and computing revenue projections when joined with order counts.
business_questions: Which customers have avg_order_value above $150 and should be targeted for premium product offers?
What is the average avg_order_value per RFM segment?
avg_order_value.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type—use numeric comparisons and aggregations (AVG(avg_order_value), SUM(avg_order_value)). When presenting in user-facing queries, round/format to 2 decimals. Avoid equality checks on decimals; use ranges. Nulls should be handled (WHERE avg_order_value IS NOT NULL or COALESCE).

purchase_frequency.description: Average purchase frequency metric per customer (decimal(18,4)), indicating how often a customer purchases over a period.
purchase_frequency.analysis_use: Filter or rank customers by activity, group by segment to compare behavioral intensity, combine with last_purchase_date to detect lapsed vs frequent buyers.
business_questions: Which customers have purchase_frequency below 0.5 and may be at risk of churning?
What is the median purchase_frequency for each RFM segment?
purchase_frequency.nl2sql_hints: Decimal numeric; use range predicates (>, <, BETWEEN). Combine with last_purchase_date for recency-based filters. Handle NULLs via COALESCE if needed. For NL2SQL parsing, recognize synonyms like 'frequency', 'purchase rate' and map to this column.

last_purchase_date.description: Datetime of the customer's most recent purchase (datetime2). Used to measure recency.
last_purchase_date.analysis_use: Range filters for recency (e.g., last X days), compute days-since-last-purchase by DATEDIFF, cohorting by last purchase month, and identifying lapsed customers for reactivation.
business_questions: Which customers haven't purchased in the last 180 days?
Show customers whose last_purchase_date is within the last 30 days.
last_purchase_date.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 column—support range queries (WHERE last_purchase_date >= '2025-01-01'). Use DATEDIFF or date_trunc equivalents to compute recency. Be explicit about timezones if user queries use local dates. Handle NULLs (customers with no purchases) separately (WHERE last_purchase_date IS NULL).

created_at.description: Datetime when this segmentation row or customer record was created (datetime2). Useful for cohorting by creation date or data lineage.
created_at.analysis_use: Filter or group by customer onboarding cohorts (e.g., customers created in last 30/90 days), measure how quickly customers reach certain loyalty tiers after creation, or audit when segmentation snapshot was created.
business_questions: How many customers created in the last 90 days are already in the 'Champions' segment?
Show trends of average loyalty_score by customer creation month.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2—use range filters and date functions (MONTH, YEAR, DATEDIFF). Clarify whether created_at refers to customer creation or segmentation snapshot in NL2SQL prompts; if ambiguous, map to customer creation. Handle timezone assumptions and NULLs explicitly.

customer_id.description: Surrogate natural identifier for a customer (primary key).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup/join key; grouping/unique counts; base for aggregations joined to transactional tables.
business_questions: Retrieve full customer profile by ID.
Count unique customers over time or by channel.
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but actual max len 8; treat as exact-match key. Use equality predicates or IN lists for lookups. When generating SQL, prefer WHERE customer_id = 'CUST1001'. Use it to join to ecom_orders ON customer_id. Indexing: primary key provides index—use for fast joins.

external_customer_code.description: Customer identifier from an external system (optional).
external_customer_code.analysis_use: Entity resolution across systems, dedup checks, joins to external datasets, grouping by external partner identifier.
business_questions: Which customers map to which external system codes?
Are there customers in external systems not present here?
external_customer_code.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50), distinct per row. May be NULL. Use equality/IS NOT NULL filters. When matching external sources, normalize casing and trim whitespace. If mapping external tables, prefer join on external_customer_code when customer_id is not present.

first_name.analysis_use: Lightweight segmentation, personalization (salutations), fuzzy matching for de-duplication and name-based grouping.
business_questions: How many customers share the same first name (top names)?
Which customers should receive personalized communications?
first_name.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Use LIKE or equality for name filters. Beware of nulls and varying capitalization; normalize (UPPER/LOWER) for comparisons. Avoid using for joins—not unique.

last_name.analysis_use: Segmentation and de-duplication; combined with first_name for identity heuristics.
business_questions: What are the most common surnames?
Identify customers with matching full name and missing contact info.
last_name.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Similar to first_name: normalize case, handle NULLs, use combined expressions (first_name + ' ' + last_name) for full-name filters. Not suitable as join key.

email.description: Primary customer email address used for login and communication.
email.analysis_use: Unique lookups, contactability checks, marketing segmentation, duplicate detection; join key to email-based systems.
business_questions: Which customers have invalid or missing email addresses?
How many opted-in customers have an email for campaigns?
email.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) and distinct for each row. Use pattern checks (LIKE '%@%') or proper regex validation in application layer; in SQL Server use TRY_PARSE/CHARINDEX for simple checks. Null-safe comparisons required. Consider indexing if used for frequent lookups (unique index if emails are unique).

phone.description: Customer phone number, likely with country code and separators.
phone.analysis_use: Contactability checks, SMS campaign segmentation, identity verification, de-duplication.
business_questions: How many customers have a valid phone number?
Which customers should be targeted via SMS (marketing_opt_in true and phone present)?
phone.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but max observed 14. Normalize by stripping spaces/characters for matching. Use LIKE or sanitized equality. Null-safe and format-aware; consider storing a normalized numeric representation for efficient matching and indexing.

dob.description: Date of birth stored as nvarchar (format appears YYYY-MM-DD).
dob.analysis_use: Derive age cohorts, birthday campaigns, demographic segmentation. Must be cast to date to compute age or DOB-based ranges.
business_questions: How many customers are in each age bracket (e.g., 18-24, 25-34)?
List customers with birthdays this month for targeted offers.
dob.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — use TRY_CAST(dob AS date) or TRY_CONVERT(date,dob,23) in SQL Server to avoid errors. For NL2SQL, map user intents like 'age' or 'born before 1990' into CAST/TRY_CAST expressions. Consider backfill to a proper DATE column for performance and correctness.

gender.description: Customer self-reported gender with low cardinality (Other/Male/Female).
gender.analysis_use: Grouping and segmentation for demographic analysis and targeted campaigns.
business_questions: What is the gender breakdown of active customers?
Does conversion/adoption differ by gender?
gender.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but small set of values; use direct equality or IN filters. Consider standardizing values (trim/case). Low-cardinality — aggregation GROUP BY gender is cheap. Watch for NULLs representing unknown.

created_at.description: Timestamp when customer record was created (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Cohort analysis, acquisition funnel, growth over time, retention cohorts (by signup date).
business_questions: How many customers were created each month/week?
What is the 30-day retention for customers who signed up in a specific month?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 — use direct range predicates (WHERE created_at BETWEEN ...). For NL2SQL date phrases like 'last 30 days' translate to GETDATE()/DATEADD or parameterized boundaries. Index on created_at can speed range queries; consider a composite index with status or marketing_opt_in for common filters.

last_login.description: Timestamp of the customer's last login (datetime2) — engagement metric.
last_login.analysis_use: Recency analysis, active user counts, churn detection, segmentation by recency (R in RFM).
business_questions: Which customers have logged in during the last 7/30/90 days?
List customers who haven't logged in for more than 180 days (candidate for re-engagement).
last_login.nl2sql_hints: Use range predicates and datediff/datediff(day, last_login, GETDATE()) for recency buckets. NULL last_login indicates never logged in — handle explicitly. Index last_login for fast recency queries if table grows.

marketing_opt_in.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the customer has opted into marketing (bit).
marketing_opt_in.analysis_use: Filter for compliant marketing lists, calculate opt-in rates, segment tests/campaigns.
business_questions: How many opted-in customers do we have by channel or language?
What percent of new signups opt into marketing?
marketing_opt_in.nl2sql_hints: SQL Server bit type: queries use WHERE marketing_opt_in = 1 or = 0. Handle NULL as unknown; prefer explicit IS TRUE semantics in NL2SQL generation. Consider indexing if frequently used in WHERE clauses combined with other filters.

preferred_language.analysis_use: Segmentation for localized content, communication routing and campaign targeting.
business_questions: How many customers prefer Hindi vs English vs Gujarati?
Target customers preferring Gujarati for a localized promotion.
preferred_language.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality nvarchar. Use equality/IN filters. Normalize values for comparisons. Use GROUP BY preferred_language for distribution queries.

status.analysis_use: Filter active users for marketing/transactions, compute active ratios, retention of active cohort.
business_questions: How many active customers exist and how has that trended over time?
What percent of inactive users have logged in in the last 90 days?
status.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but only 2 values present — use WHERE status = 'ACTIVE'. Consider converting to a smaller enum-like type or adding a computed bit column for faster boolean filtering. Use in composite indexes for queries that frequently filter by status + created_at.

source_channel.description: Acquisition channel for the customer (Organic, Referral, Website, Mobile App).
source_channel.analysis_use: Attribution, channel performance, acquisition cost analysis when joined with spend data, segmentation for channel-specific campaigns.
business_questions: Which channel delivers the most customers and what are their opt-in rates?
Compare retention or order frequency by acquisition channel.
source_channel.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with limited distinct values. Use GROUP BY and ORDER BY counts to rank channels. For NL2SQL, map user terms like 'signup channel' to this column. Consider standardizing common synonyms (e.g., 'app' -> 'Mobile App').

map_id.description: Unique identifier for the frequently-bought mapping record. Appears to be a mapping code (e.g., FRQ007001) and is unique per row in this snapshot.
map_id.analysis_use: Used as a stable row identifier when updating or deleting mappings; not typically used for aggregation. Useful for de-duplication and audits.
business_questions: When was this specific mapping (map_id) last updated?
Which specific mappings changed between two exports (identify by map_id)?
map_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key (nvarchar). Use equality or IN filters on map_id for targeted lookups. If deduplication or merge logic is required, use map_id as the unique identifier. Avoid numeric operations; do exact matches or prefix/LIKE only if needed.

sku.description: The base SKU for which we are listing frequently-bought-together products. Low cardinality in this table (6 distinct SKUs) and distribution is skewed toward a few top SKUs.
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filtering field for queries that ask for recommendations for a given product. Use GROUP BY sku to produce per-SKU summaries, filter WHERE sku = 'XXX' for single-SKU recommendations, or WHERE sku IN (...) for multiple SKUs.
business_questions: What are the top recommended SKUs for this specific sku?
For which base SKUs do we have the strongest co-purchase signals?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar; comparisons are exact string matches. When generating recommendations, filter by sku and ORDER BY co_purchase_rate DESC; use LIMIT/top N. Consider joining sku to products or ecom_products to fetch metadata. Because cardinality is small in this table, queries constrained by sku will be fast but ensure joins to larger product tables use proper indexes on their sku columns.

freq_with_sku.description: The SKU that is frequently purchased together with the base sku (the co-purchased partner SKU). Also low-cardinality and skewed.
freq_with_sku.analysis_use: Used as the partner dimension in pair analyses and recommendations. Grouping by freq_with_sku across all base SKUs can identify generally popular co-purchase targets. Join key to product master for enrichment.
business_questions: Which SKUs are most often recommended as companions across many base SKUs?
Is this freq_with_sku frequently paired with multiple different base SKUs (broad cross-sell candidate)?
freq_with_sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar; use equality/IN filters and joins to product tables on the sku column name (ensure consistent casing/format). When asked for bi-directional relationships (e.g., find if sku A -> B also B -> A), query both columns or normalize pair ordering.

co_purchase_rate.description: Numeric score (decimal(18,4)) representing the strength of the co-purchase relationship between sku and freq_with_sku. Higher values indicate stronger co-purchase propensity (rankable metric).
co_purchase_rate.analysis_use: Primary numeric metric for ranking, thresholding and aggregations (avg, min, max). Use in ORDER BY to get top co-purchases, or WHERE co_purchase_rate >= X to filter strong signals. Can be averaged by sku or freq_with_sku to identify overall propensity.
business_questions: What are the top 5 SKU pairs by co_purchase_rate?
Which base SKUs have the highest average co_purchase_rate across their partners?
co_purchase_rate.nl2sql_hints: Use numeric comparisons and ORDER BY co_purchase_rate DESC for rankings. Avoid casting; field is decimal so treat as numeric. For percentile or bucket analyses, use appropriate numeric functions. When joining to sales/count-based tables, consider normalizing scales if co_purchase_rate is derived differently than raw counts.

last_updated.description: Timestamp when the mapping was last refreshed. Stored as nvarchar(50) but appears to follow ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format in the data.
last_updated.analysis_use: Filter for recency (e.g., only use mappings updated in the last N days) after casting to datetime. Order by recency to prefer newer signals. Useful for maintenance windows and for validating staleness of recommendations.
business_questions: Which mappings were updated in the last 7 days?
Are strong co-purchase signals recent or stale (compare co_purchase_rate with last_updated)?
last_updated.nl2sql_hints: Because it's nvarchar, cast to a proper datetime (e.g., CAST(last_updated AS datetime) or TRY_CAST in SQL Server) before temporal comparisons. Be defensive in NL2SQL: when user asks about 'recent' or date ranges, translate to a cast and handle parse failures with TRY_CAST/ISDATE checks. Prefer ISO format assumptions but validate in production; consider normalizing this column to a datetime in ETL.

notification_id.description: Unique identifier for each notification event. Primary key for the table.
notification_id.analysis_use: Use for row-level retrieval, deduplication, and as the unique key when joining or auditing. Not used for grouping due to high cardinality.
business_questions: Show details for a specific notification by notification_id (audit/troubleshooting).
Has a notification_id been sent multiple times (duplicates/retries)?
notification_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string primary key. Use equality filters (WHERE notification_id = '...') for exact lookups. Avoid using this column for grouping/aggregation. Ensure NL2SQL preserves exact string match and handles case sensitivity depending on DB collation.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the recipient customer (nullable).
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter/group notifications by customer, compute per-customer counts, join to customer profile tables for enrichment (demographics, segmentation).
business_questions: Which customers received the most FAILED notifications in the last 30 days?
How many notifications did a given customer receive and what were their delivery outcomes?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Nullable; use LEFT JOIN when combining with customer tables (ecom_customers). When filtering, handle NULLs explicitly (WHERE customer_id IS NULL or IS NOT NULL). Use equality and IN filters; consider normalizing or validating customer_id format (e.g., 'CUST%').

notification_type.description: Channel used to send the notification (EMAIL, SMS, PUSH).
notification_type.analysis_use: Key dimension for grouping and aggregations to compare volume and delivery performance across channels. Low cardinality makes it efficient for GROUP BY and filtering.
business_questions: Which channel has the highest delivery failure rate?
How does notification volume by channel change over time?
notification_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field — prefer equality/IN filters or GROUP BY. Be careful with string casing; use normalized values or upper() if necessary. In NL2SQL mappings, map synonyms (e.g., 'push' -> 'PUSH').

title.description: Notification subject or template title (e.g., 'Order Update', 'New Offer').
title.analysis_use: Dimension for template-level performance analysis (open/delivery/conversion when joined to behavioral data). Group by title to compare effectiveness and failure rates of different templates.
business_questions: Which titles/templates have the highest delivered rates?
Do certain titles correlate with increased order activity after sending?
title.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality and often templated; use equality or GROUP BY. Titles may be truncated — confirm max length when reporting full text. For fuzzy matching (e.g., find templates containing 'Offer'), use LIKE or full-text search if supported.

message.description: Content body (templated) of the notification message. Repeated values indicate standard templates.
message.analysis_use: Analyze message template performance, sentiment, and correlation to conversions. Use for text-based filtering or sampling when troubleshooting delivery content-related issues.
business_questions: Which message variants are associated with more order conversions?
Are certain message texts producing higher failure or spam rates?
message.nl2sql_hints: Also low-cardinality here. Stored as nvarchar; consider full-text indexing for substring searches. When used in filters, prefer exact matches for templates or LIKE for partial matches. Trim/normalize whitespace in NL2SQL generation.

sent_ts.description: Timestamp string representing when the notification was sent (stored as nvarchar).
sent_ts.analysis_use: Temporal analyses (send time distribution, hourly/daily trends). Must be parsed/cast to datetime for interval aggregations and comparisons.
business_questions: How many notifications were sent per hour/day over a given date range?
What is the average time between created_at and sent_ts (if both populated)?
sent_ts.nl2sql_hints: Critical: stored as string — convert to datetime (e.g., TRY_CAST(sent_ts AS datetime2) or CONVERT). Handle malformed or NULL values and timezone considerations. Prefer using created_at (datetime2) if sent_ts is unreliable. When building NL2SQL queries for time ranges, cast once (in a subquery or CTE) to avoid repeated conversions.

delivery_status.description: Result of the send attempt (e.g., DELIVERED, FAILED, SENT).
delivery_status.analysis_use: Primary metric for delivery performance — compute counts, rates, and trends. Use for filtering problematic deliveries and triggering alerts.
business_questions: What percentage of notifications were DELIVERED vs FAILED in the last month?
Which channels or templates have the highest FAILED rates?
delivery_status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality; use GROUP BY delivery_status and CASE expressions to compute success/failure rates. Normalize text values in NL2SQL (upper-case) to avoid mismatches. Consider mapping SENT -> in-flight vs DELIVERED for final success metrics.

linked_order_id.description: Optional reference to an order related to the notification (nullable).
linked_order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders (ecom_orders) to measure notification-to-order relationships, conversions, and troubleshooting of order communications.
business_questions: How many order-related notifications failed for orders created in the last 7 days?
Which orders had multiple notifications and what were their delivery outcomes?
linked_order_id.nl2sql_hints: Nullable string key — use LEFT JOIN to ecom_orders on linked_order_id = ecom_orders.order_id (or matching id column). Validate naming conventions (prefix EORD) in NL2SQL patterns. When joining, guard for unmatched values and use EXISTS or aggregated joins for counts.

created_at.description: Datetime2 indicating when the notification record was created in the system (reliable datetime column).
created_at.analysis_use: Primary temporal column for recency, retention, and time-window filters. Use for trend analysis and TTL/purging logic. More reliable for range queries than sent_ts since it's native datetime.
business_questions: Count notifications created in each day/week/month for trend analysis.
Find notifications created in a specific date range for auditing or debugging.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Use directly in WHERE clauses for date ranges (e.g., WHERE created_at BETWEEN ...). Prefer created_at over sent_ts for performance because it's a datetime column and can be indexed. When combining with sent_ts, cast sent_ts to datetime to avoid mixing types.

order_id.description: Unique identifier for the order (primary key).
order_id.analysis_use: Used as the row identifier for joins to order_lines, payments, shipments; not typically aggregated but used for counts and deduplication.
business_questions: How many unique orders were placed in a given period?
Which orders correspond to a given customer or shipment?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) PK; exact-match filters and joins on order_id are common. Use equality comparisons rather than LIKE. Expect high cardinality — index/PK present.

order_number.description: Human-facing order reference (may include business-formatted sequence).
order_number.analysis_use: Useful for reporting, lookup and linking to external systems; can be used for grouping if numbers encode period/series.
business_questions: Show order details for order number ORDR20250001.
How many orders exist per order series (if encoded in order_number)?
order_number.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar; may contain patterned prefixes. Prefer exact match or prefix search. Not a reliable join key unless known in other systems.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who placed the order (FK to ecom_customers).
customer_id.analysis_use: Group and aggregate orders by customer for RFM, lifetime value, repeat purchase rate; join to customer dimension for demographics/segments.
business_questions: What is the total revenue per customer?
Which customers have placed more than X orders in the last 6 months?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar FK — join with zuna_etl.ecom_customers.customer_id. Can be NULL; watch for null-handling in joins (use LEFT JOIN when keeping orders without customer).

order_ts.description: Timestamp of the order event stored as nvarchar (string representation of datetime).
order_ts.analysis_use: Primary field for order event time slicing if parsed to datetime; used for time-based aggregations (hour/day/month) and ordering events.
business_questions: How many orders were placed by hour/day/month?
Peak ordering hours for Web vs Mobile App?
order_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar(50) — must be cast/parsed to datetime in queries (e.g., TRY_CAST/CONVERT) to enable range filters and time grouping. Canonical created_at exists as datetime2; choose created_at when accurate and typed correctly. Be cautious with inconsistent string formats.

channel.description: Sales channel through which the order was placed (e.g., Web, Mobile App).
channel.analysis_use: Group orders and revenue by channel; use for channel performance, conversion funnel comparisons and cohort analyses.
business_questions: Which channel drives the most revenue and orders?
Is average order value different between Web and Mobile App?
channel.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality text field — safe to GROUP BY or filter with equality. Values: 'Web', 'Mobile App'. Consider standardizing case/trim in predicates.

order_status.description: Current lifecycle status of the order (PLACED, CONFIRMED, PACKED, SHIPPED, CANCELLED, etc.).
order_status.analysis_use: Filter for completed vs cancelled orders, compute fulfillment rates, stage-based funnel analyses and time-in-status when joined with order events.
business_questions: What percentage of orders are cancelled vs shipped?
How many orders are currently in PACKED status by fulfillment centre?
order_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical low-cardinality column; use for WHERE or GROUP BY. Be explicit about which statuses count as 'completed' (e.g., SHIPPED/DELIVERED) vs terminal cancelled/refunded states.

payment_status.description: Payment state of the order (PENDING, PAID, REFUNDED).
payment_status.analysis_use: Filter for paid revenue vs pending/refunded; compute refund rates and cash flow metrics.
business_questions: What is total paid revenue vs refunded amounts over time?
How many orders remain in PENDING payment status?
payment_status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical. Use in conjunction with monetary columns to restrict revenue to PAID. For refunded analysis, join or compare to ecom_refunds/ecom_payments if deeper detail needed.

delivery_slot_id.description: Identifier for scheduled delivery slot for the order (links to delivery_slots table).
delivery_slot_id.analysis_use: Analyze delivery slot demand, fulfillment performance and on-time delivery by slot; group by slot for operational planning.
business_questions: Which delivery slots are most popular and generate the most revenue?
Are cancellations higher for specific delivery slots?
delivery_slot_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar; joinable to delivery_slots table for human-readable windows. Moderate cardinality — equality and GROUP BY are common. Nulls possible for orders without scheduled slot.

delivery_address_id.description: Identifier of the delivery address used for the order (likely links to customer address table).
delivery_address_id.analysis_use: Geographic/area-level analysis when joined to address table (city, pincode), multi-address behaviour per customer, clustering deliveries for route planning.
business_questions: Which areas (postal codes) generate the most orders/revenue?
Do certain addresses have higher return or cancellation rates?
delivery_address_id.nl2sql_hints: High cardinality nvarchar key. Join to ecom_customer_addresses (use LEFT JOIN if some addresses missing). Avoid grouping on raw id for reporting; join to address attributes first.

total_amount.description: Gross order total before tax, discounts and shipping (decimal).
total_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute gross GMV, average order size; used in top-line revenue and trend analysis.
business_questions: What is daily/weekly gross merchandise value (GMV)?
What is average total_amount by channel or campaign?
total_amount.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) — use SUM(), AVG() and appropriate casting. When combining with payment_status, restrict to PAID if calculating realized revenue.

tax_amount.analysis_use: Tax liability reporting, margins and net revenue calculations; breakdown of order composition.
business_questions: How much tax is collected per region or month?
What is average tax rate relative to total_amount?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) — include in SUM() aggregates. Consider calculating implied tax rate as tax_amount / total_amount, guarding division by zero and nulls.

discount_amount.description: Total discount applied to the order (decimal).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Measure promotion cost, coupon effectiveness, impact on average order value and margin.
business_questions: How much discount is given by coupon_code or source_campaign?
Does higher discount correlate with higher order volume or lower net revenue?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: decimal; combine with coupon_code and source_campaign. Watch nulls — treat null as 0 for sums if appropriate.

shipping_charge.description: Shipping fee charged to the customer for the order (decimal).
shipping_charge.analysis_use: Analyze shipping revenue vs cost, effect on net amount, and free-shipping promotions impact.
business_questions: What is total shipping revenue and average shipping charge by fulfilment centre?
How often is shipping waived for promotions?
shipping_charge.nl2sql_hints: decimal; can aggregate. When calculating net margin, include shipping_charge appropriately (may be revenue or cost depending on accounting).

net_amount.description: Net payable amount after tax, discount and shipping (decimal).
net_amount.analysis_use: Primary metric for realized revenue / financial reporting; aggregate to compute revenue, AOV, and customer lifetime value.
business_questions: What is total net revenue by day/channel/campaign?
What is average net_amount for first-time vs repeat customers?
net_amount.nl2sql_hints: decimal — use SUM(net_amount) for revenue. Ensure alignment with payment_status (PAID) when computing realized revenue and with refunds adjustments from ecom_refunds.

coupon_code.description: Applied coupon code for the order (categorical), nullable (33% null).
coupon_code.analysis_use: Assess coupon performance, redemption rates, and impact on order value and conversion.
business_questions: Which coupon codes drive the most incremental revenue or orders?
What is average discount_amount for orders using NEWUSER vs FRESH10?
coupon_code.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality (2 values) with nulls. Use COALESCE to group nulls as 'NONE' if needed. Join to promotions table if present for details.

source_campaign.description: Acquisition source/campaign attributed to the order (e.g., Google, Organic, Referral, FB Ads). Nullable (~19.8%).
source_campaign.analysis_use: Campaign/channel attribution, cost-per-order (when joined to marketing spend), and acquisition performance analysis.
business_questions: Which acquisition channels produce the highest net revenue and AOV?
How do refund or cancellation rates vary by campaign?
source_campaign.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with nulls. Standardize values before grouping. Consider using attribution windows and joining to tracking/marketing tables for deeper analysis.

fulfillment_centre_id.description: Identifier/name of the fulfilment centre handling the order (low-cardinality).
fulfillment_centre_id.analysis_use: Operations and logistics analysis by fulfilment centre, including throughput, average fulfillment time and regional performance.
business_questions: Which fulfillment centre ships the most orders and revenue?
Is there a difference in cancellation/refund rates between centers?
fulfillment_centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality nvarchar; use equality filters and GROUP BY. Join to a centres table for metadata if available. Good candidate for indexing on operational queries.

created_at.description: Datetime when the order record was created in the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Reliable typed timestamp for time-based analyses, ingestion time, event ordering and auditing; use for time-series aggregation when order_ts is unreliable.
business_questions: How many orders were created per day/week/month?
How long between created_at and updated_at on average?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 — preferred for range filters and GROUP BY by date parts. Use date functions (CAST/CONVERT/DATEPART) to bucket by day/week/month.

updated_at.description: Datetime when the order record was last updated (datetime2).
updated_at.analysis_use: Track changes, compute latency between creation and fulfillment, detect recent updates for incremental ETL or CDC processes.
business_questions: What is the average time from created_at to updated_at (time-to-update)?
Which orders were updated in the last 24 hours?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 — use for incremental extraction (WHERE updated_at > last_run). Beware of timezone assumptions; use UTC-normalized times if necessary.

log_id.description: Primary key for this log row; unique identifier for the payment status change event.
log_id.analysis_use: Used for deduplication, selecting specific events, ordering; not useful for grouping or aggregations except counting unique events.
business_questions: Fetch the full event record for a particular log id (debugging).
How many unique log events exist for a given payment_id?
log_id.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match filters on log_id (WHERE log_id = 'PLOG14001') are common. log_id is nvarchar; avoid implicit type casts. Use equality, not LIKE. Because it's the PK, queries selecting by log_id should be highly selective and fast if indexed.

payment_id.description: Identifier of the payment associated with this status change (natural join key to payments table).
payment_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to payment metadata and orders. Useful for grouping (events per payment), counting retries per payment, and filtering to a single payment's history.
business_questions: Show status transition history for a given payment_id.
Which payments experienced more than N status change events (flapping)?
payment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key (nvarchar). Typical predicates: equality or IN lists. For joins, join on payment_id exactly (avoid trimming); consider indexing payment_id. Watch out for occasional nulls — filter payment_id IS NOT NULL for joins.

status_before.description: Payment status before this change (enumerated values such as PENDING, PAID, INITIATED).
status_before.analysis_use: Group and pivoting for transition matrices (count of from->to). Filter to analyze transitions originating from a specific status. Low-cardinality column — efficient for group by and aggregation.
business_questions: How often do transitions originate from PENDING versus INITIATED?
For events originating from PENDING, what are the common resulting statuses?
status_before.nl2sql_hints: Use equality filters and GROUP BY. When constructing transition queries include both status_before and status_after in GROUP BY. Status values are textual; consider normalizing case in queries if data inconsistent.

status_after.description: Payment status after this change (e.g., FAILED, PENDING, PAID).
status_after.analysis_use: Primary target for success/failure rate calculations, pivot tables, and identifying end states. Useful in WHERE clauses to count failures or successes and in GROUP BY for distribution.
business_questions: What proportion of updates end in FAILED during the last 7 days?
How many payments transitioned to PAID on their final logged attempt?
status_after.nl2sql_hints: Filter or group by status_after (e.g., WHERE status_after = 'FAILED'). For trendlines use status_after with time windows. Normalize values if future variants appear (e.g., 'failed' vs 'FAILED').

updated_at.description: Timestamp when the status update was recorded/occurred (datetime2).
updated_at.analysis_use: Time-series analysis, filtering by date ranges, latency calculations (updated_at - created_at), ordering events. Useful for rolling windows and aggregations by day/hour.
business_questions: How many status updates occurred per hour/day in the last month?
What is the trend of FAILED transitions over time?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Use range predicates (BETWEEN or >= AND <) for time filters to leverage indexes. Avoid functions on updated_at (e.g., CAST/CONVERT) in WHERE clauses to keep range scans sargable. Use date_truncing/grouping for time buckets in GROUP BY.

source.description: Origin of the status update (e.g., Manual, PG Webhook).
source.analysis_use: Categorical grouping to compare channels (manual vs gateway) for reliability, failure and retry rates. Low cardinality — ideal for group by and simple filters.
business_questions: Does PG Webhook produce more FAILED responses than Manual updates?
Which source triggers more retries on average?
source.nl2sql_hints: Filter with equality (WHERE source = 'PG Webhook') and include in GROUP BY. Source values are short strings; account for NULLs. Consider mapping synonyms (e.g., 'Webhook' vs 'PG Webhook').

response_code.description: Numeric response code from payment gateway or internal handling (HTTP-like codes such as 200, 402, 500).
response_code.analysis_use: Group by response_code to identify dominant error codes, correlate with status_after and retry_count. Low-cardinality makes it efficient for aggregation.
business_questions: Which response_code correlates with most FAILED transitions?
How many attempts returned 500 (server error) versus 402 (payment required)?
response_code.nl2sql_hints: Stored as bigint; use numeric comparisons and GROUP BY. Map common codes to categories (success/error) in queries. Watch for NULLs. Filtering by response_code is selective when specific codes are queried.

response_body.description: Intended to store the payment gateway response body but currently defined as decimal(18,4) and 100% NULL in dataset — likely incorrect type or unused.
response_body.analysis_use: If populated, it would provide gateway response details for debugging; currently unusable for analysis. Should be retyped to text/varchar or JSON to be useful.
business_questions: What error message did the gateway return for a failed attempt?
Are there recurring response body messages that indicate a systematic issue?
response_body.nl2sql_hints: Currently NULL and decimal; NL2SQL should not expect text here. If schema is updated to varchar/JSON, treat it as non-indexed searchable text (LIKE/JSON functions). Do not attempt numeric aggregations on this column.

retry_count.description: Number of retries attempted for this status change (stored as decimal but contains small integer values like 0-3).
retry_count.analysis_use: Aggregate (AVG, MAX) retry behavior per payment or per source/response_code. Filter to find high-retry events and analyze correlation with success/failure.
business_questions: What is the average retry_count before a payment reaches PAID?
Which payments exceeded the retry threshold (e.g., > 3) and still failed?
retry_count.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal(18,4) but values are integers — cast to integer in queries for comparisons (e.g., CAST(retry_count AS int) > 1). Consider normalizing schema to integer for performance. Use GROUP BY and aggregates.

processed_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the log event has been processed by downstream systems (bit).
processed_flag.analysis_use: Filter to find unprocessed events, measure backlog, and track processing throughput. Useful as an operational filter in ETL or monitoring queries.
business_questions: How many logged events are still unprocessed?
What proportion of FAILED events have been marked processed?
processed_flag.nl2sql_hints: Use WHERE processed_flag = 0/1 or WHERE processed_flag IS NULL depending on DB client. Beware of tri-state if NULLs present; treat NULL as unprocessed unless defined otherwise. Boolean filters are highly selective.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the log row was created in the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Used to measure ingestion latency (updated_at - created_at), filter by create date, and trace event arrival patterns. May be used instead of or alongside updated_at when distinguishing event time vs processing time.
business_questions: What is the average time between created_at and updated_at for each event?
How many events were created per day/week?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Use range filters for created_at (BETWEEN/>= AND <) to maintain sargability. For bounding windows use created_at for ingestion-time windows, updated_at for actual status change time. Avoid applying functions in WHERE to preserve index usage.

notes.description: Freeform notes column intended for comments; currently defined as decimal(18,4) and 100% NULL — likely mis-typed or unused.
notes.analysis_use: If populated with text, useful for human-readable context for certain events. As decimal now, not useful; schema should be changed to varchar/text if notes are required.
business_questions: Which events have manual notes indicating special handling or errors?
Find events with notes containing a particular keyword for audit.
notes.nl2sql_hints: Currently numeric and NULL — do not expect content. If migrated to varchar/JSON, NL2SQL should treat it as a searchable text field (LIKE or full-text). Avoid aggregations on freeform notes.

payment_id.description: Surrogate unique identifier for each payment transaction (primary key).
payment_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for deduplication and joins to payment status/history tables. Use for exact lookups and linking refunds or logs to a single payment.
business_questions: Retrieve the details for a specific payment_id for investigation.
How many payment attempts exist for a given payment_id (if retries stored)?
payment_id.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match key; use equality filters (WHERE payment_id = '...') for point lookups. Treat as string (nvarchar). Indexing this column is critical for fast single-payment lookups.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the payment (FK to ecom_orders). Nullable.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to ecom_orders for enriching payments with order/customer/product attributes. Grouping/aggregating payments by order, customer or order date after join.
business_questions: Which payments correspond to cancelled or returned orders?
What is the payment-to-order match rate (payments without matching order)?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Nullable join key; when writing NL2SQL, include LEFT JOIN if you want payments without orders. Use equality joins on string keys; consider trimming/case-normalization if inconsistent. Index on order_id speeds aggregations by order.

payment_ts.description: Timestamp of the payment event stored as nvarchar (string). Values follow a 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' pattern.
payment_ts.analysis_use: Primary event time for time-series analyses, daily/hourly aggregations, and computing latency to settlement after casting to datetime; filtering by payment date range.
business_questions: How many payments occurred per day/hour in a date range?
What is the distribution of payment attempts over business hours vs off-hours?
payment_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — cast/convert to datetime (e.g., TRY_CAST(payment_ts AS datetime2)) before temporal filters or date_trunc. Use pattern matching only if necessary. Validate or filter out invalid strings to avoid cast errors.

amount.description: Monetary amount associated with the payment (decimal(18,4)).
amount.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM, AVG), filtering (amount ranges), grouping by mode/gateway/status for revenue and exposure analysis.
business_questions: What is the total settled amount by gateway in the last month?
What is the average payment amount for UPI vs CARD vs COD?
amount.nl2sql_hints: Numeric column — safe for SUM/AVG. Check nulls before aggregations (use COALESCE(amount,0)). When joining to orders, ensure not to double-count amounts for multi-payment orders (use DISTINCT order grouping or join logic).

payment_mode.description: High-level mode of payment (COD, NETBANKING, UPI, CARD).
payment_mode.analysis_use: Categorical grouping and filtering; analyze mode-specific failure and settlement rates and average amounts.
business_questions: Which payment mode has the highest failure rate?
How does average order value differ by payment_mode?
payment_mode.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — ideal for GROUP BY. Use exact value matching in WHERE (e.g., WHERE payment_mode = 'UPI'). Consider normalizing values or mapping synonyms in NL2SQL rules if user uses 'credit card' vs 'CARD'.

payment_gateway.description: The payment processor/gateway used (e.g., Razorpay, Paytm, BankTransfer).
payment_gateway.analysis_use: Group/filter by gateway for reliability and settlement performance; compute gateway-specific fees or failure rates when combined with external fee tables.
business_questions: Which gateway processed the highest volume/amount last quarter?
Which gateway has the longest average time-to-settlement?
