payment_gateway.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — use GROUP BY/ORDER BY. Map user synonyms in NL2SQL ('Razorpay' == 'razor pay') and support filtering by gateway. Consider joining to a gateway metadata table if present.

gateway_ref_no.description: Reference number returned by the gateway for the transaction (external id).
gateway_ref_no.analysis_use: Reconciliation between internal payments and gateway reports; deduplication of gateway events and cross-checking settlement logs.
business_questions: Which payments are missing gateway_ref_no or have duplicate gateway_ref_no?
Find the gateway reference for payment failures for reconciliation with gateway logs.
gateway_ref_no.nl2sql_hints: Unique-ish identifier stored as string. Use equality or pattern searches for partial matches. Useful for joins to external gateway logs; treat as text and ensure exact matching. Watch for leading/trailing whitespace.

status.description: Execution status of the payment attempt (PENDING, FAILED, SUCCESS).
status.analysis_use: Primary classification for outcome analysis — group counts/amounts by status to compute success/failure rates and monitor PENDING backlog.
business_questions: What is the success rate of payments in the last 30 days?
How many PENDING payments exist older than 24 hours?
status.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality; use for WHERE filters and GROUP BY. When users ask about 'failed' vs 'returned' clarify semantics. Consider mapping synonyms in NL2SQL (e.g., 'successful' → 'SUCCESS').

settlement_status.description: Shows whether the payment has been settled to the merchant (SETTLED or UNSETTLED).
settlement_status.analysis_use: Aggregation of settled vs unsettled amounts and counts; financial reconciliation and cash-flow forecasting.
business_questions: How much money is currently UNSETTLED by gateway?
What percent of payments are settled within X days?
settlement_status.nl2sql_hints: Binary categorical; use for filtering/grouping. Combine with settlement_date and payment_ts (cast payment_ts) to compute settlement latency. Consider indexing if frequent filter target.

settlement_date.description: Datetime when the payment was settled (datetime2).
settlement_date.analysis_use: Temporal filtering and latency calculations (settlement_date - payment_ts). Used to build settlement timelines and daily reconciliation reports.
business_questions: What is the average time-to-settlement per gateway or per payment_mode?
Show settled payment amounts by settlement_date for reconciliation with bank statements.
settlement_date.nl2sql_hints: Already datetime2 — use directly in temporal filters/aggregations. When computing latency, cast payment_ts to datetime and handle NULL settlement_date for UNSETTLED rows. Use date functions or date_trunc equivalents depending on SQL dialect.

refund_eligible_flag.description: Boolean indicating whether the payment is eligible for refund (bit).
refund_eligible_flag.analysis_use: Filter for potential refund exposure and compute counts/amounts of refund-eligible payments.
business_questions: How much amount is refund-eligible currently?
Which gateways produce the most refund-eligible payments?
refund_eligible_flag.nl2sql_hints: Bit/boolean — use WHERE refund_eligible_flag = 1/TRUE. When translating NL to SQL support synonyms like 'eligible for refund' → refund_eligible_flag = TRUE. Combine with status/settlement_status to determine actionable refunds.

processed_by.description: User or system that processed the payment record. In current data the value is constant ('system').
processed_by.analysis_use: Audit/source tracking; detect multiple processors if present. Currently low informational value due to single distinct value.
business_questions: Which system or user processed these payments?
Are there payments created by manual agents versus automated system?
processed_by.nl2sql_hints: Usually string; treat as optional audit field. If queries filter by processed_by, be aware that current data returns only 'system'. Use equality checks. Not a join key.

created_at.description: Datetime when the payment record was created in the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Record ingestion time for audit and backlog detection; filter new records or compute delays between created_at and payment_ts.
business_questions: How many payments were created in the system in the last 24 hours?
Is there a backlog of payments created but not processed/settled?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 — use for temporal filtering and auditing. When computing event timelines compare created_at with payment_ts and settlement_date. Be cautious if created_at differs from payment_ts semantics (ingestion vs event time).

notes.description: Notes field — DDL shows decimal(18,4) but current dataset is 100% NULL (no values).
notes.analysis_use: Intended for free-form notes or numeric adjustments; currently unusable due to nulls and unexpected data type. If populated, could provide manual annotations or adjustments to payments.
business_questions: Are there manual notes associated with payments indicating reasons for failure?
Are there adjustment amounts or comments recorded for specific payments?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Data type mismatch: DDL shows decimal but name suggests text; confirm actual semantic type. Expect many NULLs — guard queries using IS NOT NULL. If intended as text, cast or alter column; NL2SQL should not assume content unless schema is verified.

event_id.description: Unique identifier for each product event record (primary key).
event_id.analysis_use: Used as a unique row identifier. Rarely aggregated except for DISTINCT counts. Useful when selecting a specific event or deduplicating.
business_questions: What are the details of event EVNT06001?
How many unique events were recorded in a time window?
event_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar primary key (max len 50). Use equality filters for single-event retrieval (WHERE event_id = '...'). Avoid using event_id for grouping at scale (high cardinality). Use explicit quoting for string literals and be mindful of collation/special characters when matching.

sku.description: Product identifier / stock-keeping unit affected by the event.
sku.analysis_use: Primary field for grouping and filtering (per-SKU event counts, latest event per SKU). Ideal join key to product catalog tables (e.g., products, ecom_products). Low cardinality — good for selective filters and fast group-bys.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest frequency of OUT_OF_STOCK events?
Show latest event per SKU with event type and timestamp.
sku.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar, often used in WHERE and JOIN clauses. Expect a small, bounded set of SKUs; NL2SQL should allow IN-list filters (e.g., sku IN ('POT002','APP009')). Normalize casing when matching and handle NULLs (some rows may have NULL sku). Consider suggesting an index on sku for faster lookups in larger datasets.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the product event actually occurred (source event time).
event_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time-series analysis, ordering events, window functions to find previous/next events per SKU, and computing event rates over time buckets (hour/day). Useful for filtering by event occurrence period.
business_questions: How many PRICE_DROP events occurred last week by day?
What was the first BACK_IN_STOCK event for SKU POT002 in October 2025?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 type. NL2SQL should map natural-language time periods to explicit predicates on event_ts (e.g., WHERE event_ts BETWEEN ...). Use DATEADD/CAST/TRUNC functions when generating buckets. Prefer filtering on event_ts rather than created_at if asking about when change happened. Be careful with timezone assumptions — event_ts appears timezone-naive.

event_type.description: Categorical type of the event (BACK_IN_STOCK, PRICE_DROP, OUT_OF_STOCK).
event_type.analysis_use: Common grouping/filter dimension for counts, proportions, and pivoting. Low-cardinality ideal for discrete filters and conditional aggregates.
business_questions: What percentage of events were PRICE_DROP vs OUT_OF_STOCK in November?
Which event type drives the most activity for inventory_bot?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with 3 distinct values. NL2SQL should translate phrases like 'price drops' to event_type = 'PRICE_DROP'. Use IN lists for multi-type queries. Consider using CASE statements for custom groupings (e.g., inventory-related vs pricing-related).

old_value.description: Previous numeric value associated with the event (e.g., previous price or quantity). Currently populated entirely with NULLs.
old_value.analysis_use: When populated, used to compute deltas (old_value → new_value), averages or distributions of changes, and detect magnitude of price or stock adjustments. Currently not usable as all values are NULL.
business_questions: What was the average price decrease (old_value - new_value) for PRICE_DROP events?
How often did the old_value equal zero (indicating new listing)?
old_value.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) but currently NULL for all rows; NL2SQL should check for IS NOT NULL before numeric operations to avoid null-propagation. If users ask about deltas, generate expressions like COALESCE(old_value,0) - COALESCE(new_value,0) but prefer IS NOT NULL predicates to indicate meaningful data.

new_value.description: New numeric value after the event (e.g., new price or resulting quantity). Currently populated entirely with NULLs.
new_value.analysis_use: Used with old_value to calculate change magnitude, sign, percentages, and detect significant price/stock movements once populated. Also useful for identifying absolute new price levels.
business_questions: Which PRICE_DROP events resulted in a new price below X?
What is the median new_value after PRICE_DROP for a given SKU?
new_value.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) but 100% NULL in current snapshot. NL2SQL should guard numeric filters with IS NOT NULL and may need to CAST strings to numeric if users supply numeric thresholds. Suggest treating absent numeric data as unavailable rather than zero.

triggered_by.description: Source or agent that triggered the event (system, pricing_bot, inventory_bot).
triggered_by.analysis_use: Categorical filter and grouping to attribute events to automation or manual systems. Useful for root-cause analyses and SLA/agent performance metrics.
business_questions: How many BACK_IN_STOCK events were generated by inventory_bot vs system?
Which agent is responsible for the most PRICE_DROP events?
triggered_by.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with three dominant values. NL2SQL should allow mapping natural language references like 'pricing bot' to triggered_by = 'pricing_bot'. Use explicit equality/IN filters and consider aliasing human-friendly labels (e.g., 'pricing bot' → 'pricing_bot'). Handle NULL triggers defensively.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the event record was inserted into this table (ingestion/recorded time).
created_at.analysis_use: Used to measure ingestion latency (created_at - event_ts), filter recent arrivals, and to build incremental extraction (e.g., WHERE created_at > last_run). Useful for operational monitoring and SLA checks.
business_questions: What is the average ingestion delay between event_ts and created_at?
List events ingested in the last 24 hours and their types.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2; preferred for incremental ETL predicates. NL2SQL should distinguish queries about when an event occurred vs when it was ingested — map 'when recorded' to created_at and 'when happened' to event_ts. Use range predicates on created_at for efficient incremental queries and recommend indexing on created_at for larger data volumes.

date.description: The date of the daily snapshot (datetime2). Values appear at midnight and represent the day for which the metrics apply.
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for filtering and grouping (DATE_TRUNC/day, week, month, year). Essential for range filters, time-series aggregations, lag/lead calculations, rolling windows and time-based joins to other daily tables.
business_questions: What are weekly and monthly trends in net_sales?
Which dates had anomalously high discount or delivery_fee impacts?
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as date-only dimension (cast or truncate time portion if necessary). Use BETWEEN or >=/< for ranges and GROUP BY DATEPART/YEAR/MONTH for aggregation. Because it is unique per row, queries filtering by date can use point lookups; consider index on date if table grows. Be explicit about timezone awareness if converting to dates.

product_id.description: Identifier for the product (nvarchar(50)). In this dataset it's constant ('TOM001'), indicating a single-product dataset.
product_id.analysis_use: Used for grouping or filtering by product when multiple products exist. Will be the join key to product metadata tables (products, ecom_products, b2b_products). Currently not selective (cardinality=1), so product filters may be no-ops.
business_questions: What are the sales metrics for product TOM001 over time?
How does this product perform versus other products (after joining to multi-product tables)?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; watch for case sensitivity and padding. If schema-wide joins expect a different product id format, normalize (trim/upper). Because current cardinality is 1, product filters will not reduce result sets; verify multi-product presence before relying on product-level grouping. Not declared as FK — prefer explicit join conditions.

total_orders.description: Aggregate count or sum representing total orders for the product on that date (stored as decimal(18,4)).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate with SUM, AVG, MAX, or used directly per-day. Useful for per-order metrics (net_sales / total_orders). Filtering on thresholds (e.g., total_orders > X) to find high-activity days.
business_questions: Which days had the highest number of orders for the product?
How does average orders per day vary month-over-month?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as decimal, it represents a count and may be aggregated with SUM or averaged. When used in integer contexts cast to appropriate type. Watch for nulls. To compute per-order averages use safe division (handle zero/null). Prefer grouping by date for per-day totals and aggregating across date ranges for summaries.

total_customers.description: Number of distinct customers associated with the product sales that day (decimal(18,4)). Likely an aggregate distinct count.
total_customers.analysis_use: Used to compute per-customer metrics (net_sales / total_customers), retention/unique buyer trends, and correlation with orders. Useful for identifying days with many unique buyers vs repeat buyers.
business_questions: How many unique customers purchased the product each day?
Are increases in net_sales driven by more customers or higher spend per customer?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal but represents a count—cast when necessary. Because it may be calculated (distinct count), be cautious if re-aggregating across date ranges to avoid double counting; where possible prefer source-level distinct counts. Handle zero or null values in averaged metrics and divisions.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the product on that date (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Used to measure volume trends, compute average kg per order/customer, and inventory planning. Aggregatable across dates to get total kilograms sold in a period.
business_questions: What is the total kg sold per week/month for inventory forecasting?
What is the average kg sold per order and is that changing over time?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Unit-aware: values are in kg; ensure units are clear in queries. SUM is appropriate for totals; divide by total_orders or total_customers for per-order/customer metrics. Watch for nulls. When comparing with other tables use consistent units (convert grams/lbs if needed).

gross_sales.description: Gross monetary sales for the product on that date before discounts, delivery fees, taxes (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for top-line calculations, percentage metrics (discount/gross_sales), trend analysis, and ratio computations with net_sales. Aggregate with SUM for period totals.
business_questions: What was the gross revenue by month for the product?
What percentage of gross sales is being discounted on average?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Monetary value—be careful with SUM precision. Use appropriate currency context and formatting in results. Combine with discount_amount/delivery_fee/tax_amount to reconcile net_sales. Ensure NULL-safe arithmetic (COALESCE) and handle negative values if refunds are represented here or elsewhere.

discount_amount.description: Total discount amount applied to product sales for the date (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Analyze promotion impact, compute discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales), and track promotion cost over time. Aggregate for total discount burden in period.
business_questions: How much was given away in discounts per month and as a percent of gross sales?
Do discounts correlate with higher quantity sold or more unique customers?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Use COALESCE for NULL safety. Compute ratios with gross_sales but guard against division by zero. When producing percent change ensure consistent time windows. If negative discounts (surcharges) are possible, handle sign semantics explicitly.

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fees charged for the product on that date (decimal(18,4)). May be fees charged to customer or fees recognized as revenue depending on ETL semantics.
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Assess contribution of delivery fees to revenue, analyze net revenue composition, and measure average delivery fee per order. Can also be used to estimate shipping cost coverage.
business_questions: How much revenue comes from delivery fees and how has it changed over time?
What is the average delivery fee per order for this product?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Clarify whether this is billed to customers or absorbed; naming alone may be ambiguous. Use SUM and compute per-order averages (divide by total_orders). Handle NULLs and negative values consistently. When joining to shipping tables ensure consistent attribution by date and order identifiers if available.

tax_amount.description: Total tax amount collected on product sales for the date (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Tax component analysis of revenue, reconciliation with tax reporting systems, and percentage of gross sales represented by taxes. Useful for compliance and finance reconciliations.
business_questions: How much tax was collected per month for the product?
What percent of gross sales is taxes and has tax burden changed over time?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Monetary value; use COALESCE for safe arithmetic. When calculating net_sales components, confirm whether tax is included or excluded in gross_sales. Aggregate with SUM and be careful when joining to tax reporting tables by tax period rather than calendar date if jurisdictions differ.

net_sales.description: Net sales for the product on that date after discounts, delivery, and taxes - final recognized sales (decimal(18,4)).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line revenue metric for profitability and trend analysis. Aggregate across periods for performance, compute per-order/customer averages, and compare against gross_sales to determine deductions.
business_questions: What are the net sales for the product this month and how does it compare to the previous month?
Which days or campaigns produced the highest net sales and what were their discount/tax impacts?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Use as main target metric in queries (SUM/AVG). Verify that net_sales = gross_sales - discount_amount +/- delivery_fee +/- tax_amount per ETL semantics before relying on decomposition; if not, avoid implicit assumptions. Use COALESCE to avoid null propagation. When building NL2SQL prompts, clarify whether users want gross or net figures and whether delivery_fee should be treated as revenue or cost.

refund_id.description: Surrogate identifier for the refund event, stored as nvarchar(50). Primary key (unique for each refund row).
refund_id.analysis_use: Used for deduplication, record-level counts, direct lookups and joins when referencing specific refund records. Ideal for retrieving single-refund details.
business_questions: Retrieve all fields for a specific refund_id for investigation.
Count distinct refund_ids to measure number of refunds in a period.
refund_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string in queries. Use equality filters for point lookups (WHERE refund_id = 'ERF7001'). Highly selective — index is recommended for direct lookups.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the refund; nullable string and foreign key to ecom_orders.order_id.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to order-level tables to get customer, order_total, items. Grouping and aggregation by order_id to detect multiple refunds per order.
business_questions: Which orders have multiple refunds and what is the total refunded per order?
Link refunds to customer data via orders to compute per-customer refund amount.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; use LEFT/INNER JOINs to ecom_orders depending on whether orphan refunds should be kept. Consider indexing order_id to speed joins and filters (e.g., WHERE order_id = 'EORDR5048'). Handle NULLs explicitly.

payment_id.description: Identifier for the payment transaction associated with the refund, stored as nvarchar(50).
payment_id.analysis_use: Join key to payment/gateway tables to validate payment/refund reconciliation, measure refunds by payment method or gateway transaction.
business_questions: Which payment transactions have associated refunds and what are their amounts?
What is the distribution of refunds by payment gateway (when joined with payments/gateway table)?
payment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; join to ecom_payments on payment_id. Values often reuse a pattern (EPAY...), so filters like LIKE 'EPAY%' are possible but equality matches are preferable. Nullable — handle NULLs in joins.

refund_ts.description: Timestamp of the refund event as a string (nvarchar). Appears to contain datetime-like values but stored as text.
refund_ts.analysis_use: Must be parsed/cast into a datetime to support time-based grouping (day/week/month), windowing, and ordering. Useful for event time series analyses if converted.
business_questions: What is the daily/weekly/monthly trend of refund counts and amounts (requires casting to datetime)?
How long after order placement do refunds typically occur (requires join to order timestamp)?
refund_ts.nl2sql_hints: Because it is nvarchar, use CAST/CONVERT to datetime (e.g., TRY_CAST(refund_ts AS datetime2)) in WHERE/GROUP BY. Expect consistent ISO-like formatting but add guards (TRY_CAST) for dirty values. For performance, consider migrating to a datetime column and indexing it.

refund_amount.description: Monetary amount refunded for the refund event (decimal(18,4)).
refund_amount.analysis_use: Primary metric for aggregations: SUM(refund_amount), AVG(refund_amount), MAX/MIN. Group by time, reason, mode, status for financial analysis.
business_questions: What is total refunded amount by month, reason, or refund_mode?
What is the average refund amount for returns vs cancellations?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Numeric aggregation safe; watch for NULLs — use COALESCE(refund_amount,0) when summing. For currency, ensure consistent scale; joins to orders may require currency matching if multi-currency environments exist.

refund_mode.description: Method used to issue the refund (ACCOUNT_CREDIT or PG_REFUND). Low cardinality string column.
refund_mode.analysis_use: Categorical grouping (counts and sums) to analyze operational and financial impact by refund method.
business_questions: What share of refunded value is processed via ACCOUNT_CREDIT vs PG_REFUND?
Is one refund_mode associated with higher failure rates or longer processing times?
refund_mode.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Low cardinality => good candidate for an indexed column or included in composite indexes for common filters (e.g., WHERE refund_mode = 'PG_REFUND'). Use exact matches; consider standardized values in predicates.

gateway_ref_no.description: Reference number assigned by payment gateway for the refund transaction (varchar).
gateway_ref_no.analysis_use: Reconciliation with gateway logs, troubleshooting, deduplication and forensic tracing of refund operations.
business_questions: Which gateway references correspond to failed refunds and need reprocessing?
Match gateway_ref_no to gateway reports to confirm settlement of refunds.
gateway_ref_no.nl2sql_hints: String comparisons; often unique per gateway transaction. Use equality to match with gateway extracts. Consider indexing if frequent lookups occur; beware of variable patterns/lengths.

status.description: Current state of the refund (COMPLETED, INITIATED, FAILED). Low cardinality with three values.
status.analysis_use: Filter and group to monitor operational health (failure rates), SLA adherence, and reconciliation. Used in WHERE clauses and aggregates (counts, sums by status).
business_questions: What percentage of refunds are failing versus completing?
How much monetary value is stuck in INITIATED state vs COMPLETED?
status.nl2sql_hints: Use exact string matches (WHERE status = 'FAILED'). Low cardinality — good candidate for filter first in queries to reduce result sets. Consider boolean flags or an indexed enum-like column for performance.

processed_by.description: Identifier of the team or user who processed the refund (all current values: finance_team).
processed_by.analysis_use: Auditing and operational ownership. If expanded to multiple processors, used for SLA and workload distribution analysis.
business_questions: Which team handled refunds and are there differences in processing speed or success rates?
Filter refunds processed by a particular operator for audit/troubleshooting.
processed_by.nl2sql_hints: String field; presently single value so low discriminative power. For NL2SQL, avoid using as primary filter unless specified. Normalize when multiple processors exist to allow joins to users/teams table.

processed_at.description: Datetime when the refund was processed in the system (datetime2).
processed_at.analysis_use: Calculate processing latency (processed_at - refund_ts or order_ts), time to complete refunds, SLA compliance, and time-based reporting using native datetime operations.
business_questions: What is the average time to process a refund from request to completion?
How many refunds were processed in a given time window (by hour/day)?
processed_at.nl2sql_hints: Native datetime type — use directly in WHERE, GROUP BY, date_trunc, DATEDIFF, and window functions. When refund_ts is a string, cast and compare to processed_at carefully. Index processed_at for time-range queries.

reason.description: Business reason for the refund (Quality Issue, Order Cancelled, Customer Return). Low cardinality and business-categorical.
reason.analysis_use: Group and filter to analyze drivers of refunds, compute average refund_amount per reason, and prioritize quality or process improvements.
business_questions: What are the most common reasons for refunds and how much do they cost?
Do certain reasons have higher failure rates or longer processing times?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string — use for GROUP BY and filtering. Ensure consistent labels; consider mapping to normalized reason codes for robust NL2SQL parsing (e.g., 'Quality Issue' -> 'QUALITY').

notes.description: Column defined as decimal(18,4) but currently 100% NULL. Appears to be an incorrect type for a 'notes' field which typically would be text.
notes.analysis_use: Currently unusable (all NULL). If repurposed to textual notes, it would support qualitative context for refunds. As-is, contributes nothing to analysis.
business_questions: N/A in current state — no useful info stored.
If repurposed: what free-text comments accompany refunds and do they explain patterns?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Treat as NULL numeric now; queries should ignore this column. Recommend DDL change: drop or alter type to nvarchar(max) if storing comments. NL2SQL systems should not expect notes data in numeric form and should handle missing data gracefully.

return_item_id.description: Unique identifier for this returned item record (primary key).
return_item_id.analysis_use: Used as the primary row identifier; useful for deduplication, exact lookups, and joining if other tables reference this return_item_id. Also used to count distinct returned items.
business_questions: How many distinct return items were processed in a time window?
Fetch the full details for a specific returned item (by return_item_id).
return_item_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar primary key (max observed length 8). Use direct equality filters (WHERE return_item_id = 'RIT11001') for point lookups. Expect uniqueness so DISTINCT on this column is redundant. If provided by user, prefer exact-match queries rather than LIKE unless partial id specified.

return_id.description: Identifier for the overall return (a return can contain multiple return_item rows).
return_id.analysis_use: Group or aggregate return items by return_id to compute per-return metrics (total qty per return, count of items per return). Join key to returns-level table for customer, reason, and return date.
business_questions: Which returns contain the largest number of returned items or quantities?
Show all items for a given return_id to inspect dispositions and restock decisions.
return_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar nullable (max observed length 8) with moderate cardinality. Use equality or IN filters to restrict to specific returns. When joining, use exact match on return_id; consider returning aggregates grouped by return_id. Nulls are possible—handle with IS NULL checks if asking for items without return linkage.

order_item_id.description: Identifier of the original order line / item that was returned (links returned item back to the order).
order_item_id.analysis_use: Join to order_items/ecom_order_lines to get original order context: order_id, price, product_id. Grouping by order_item_id can detect repeated returns for same order line.
business_questions: Which order lines have been returned multiple times or have multiple returned quantities?
For a given order_item_id, what was the returned quantity and disposition?
order_item_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar nullable (max observed length 7) with high cardinality (53 distinct). Use exact equality joins to order_items. Be careful that some order_item_id values may be NULL; include IS NOT NULL if required. If users reference 'order item' in NL, map to order_item_id and consider joins to ecom_order_lines/order_items tables.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit of the returned product; short product code used for product-level analytics.
sku.analysis_use: Filter and group by sku to compute return rates and volumes per product, combine with products table to enrich with product name/category, and identify top-returned SKUs.
business_questions: What are the top SKUs by returned quantity and count?
For SKU 'ONI003', what proportion of returns are restocked vs discarded?
sku.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar nullable (max len 6 observed) with low cardinality (10 distinct). Use equality or IN for SKU filters. Consider case sensitivity of SKU depending on DB collation; normalize case in NL2SQL if users may provide mixed-case SKUs. Join key to product tables often via sku column—explicit mapping needed if product tables use a different column name (e.g., product_code).

qty.description: Quantity of units returned for this return item line (decimal with 4 decimal places).
qty.analysis_use: Primary measure for aggregation (SUM(qty), AVG(qty)), used for counts of units returned, ratios and KPI calculations (returned quantity per order, restocking volume). Filterable (e.g., qty > X).
business_questions: What is the total quantity returned per SKU or per disposition?
Which returned items have unusually large quantities (identify outliers)?
qty.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) nullable. Aggregations should use SUM(qty) and proper numeric formatting. Beware of fractional quantities due to decimal scale; if business expects integers, consider rounding. When users ask for 'count of returns' distinguish COUNT(*) (rows) vs SUM(qty) (units).

inspected_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the returned item was inspected.
inspected_flag.analysis_use: Filter and group by inspection status to measure inspection rates, inspection backlog, and link inspection outcomes to dispositions.
business_questions: What proportion of returned items have been inspected?
Among inspected items, how many were marked restockable vs discarded?
inspected_flag.nl2sql_hints: bit nullable. In SQL Server use WHERE inspected_flag = 1 or 0, or IS NULL checks. Users may phrase booleans as 'inspected', 'not inspected', 'pending inspection'—map natural language to (inspected_flag = 1), (inspected_flag = 0), or (inspected_flag IS NULL).

inspected_at.description: Timestamp when the returned item inspection occurred.
inspected_at.analysis_use: Time-series analyses: inspection throughput over time, average time-to-inspection (when combined with return creation time from returns table), window filters (inspected_at BETWEEN X AND Y), and recent inspection activity.
business_questions: How many items were inspected in the last 7 days?
What is the average time between return creation and inspected_at (requires join to returns)?
inspected_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 nullable and distinct per row. Use range predicates and date functions. When users ask relative-time queries ('last week', 'last 30 days'), translate to inspected_at >= DATEADD(...) or appropriate DB date expressions. Handle time zones only if additional metadata present; otherwise assume stored times are DB-local.

restockable_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the inspected returned item can be restocked into inventory.
restockable_flag.analysis_use: Critical for operational KPIs: compute volumes and values of restockable items, decide inventory movements, and combine with product cost/pricing to estimate financial impact.
business_questions: What quantity/value of returned items are restockable versus non-restockable?
Which SKUs have the highest proportion of restockable returns?
restockable_flag.nl2sql_hints: bit nullable. Use WHERE restockable_flag = 1/0. For percent calculations use SUM(CASE WHEN restockable_flag = 1 THEN qty ELSE 0 END) / SUM(qty). When users ask 'restockable' map to restockable_flag = 1 and consider NULL semantics if present.

disposition.description: Categorical outcome assigned after inspection (e.g., RESTOCK, DISCARD, RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER).
disposition.analysis_use: Group by disposition to measure outcome distribution and compute flows for inventory, supplier returns, or disposal. Useful for pivot tables and trend analysis by disposition over time or SKU.
business_questions: What proportion of returned items are RESTOCK vs DISCARD vs RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER?
For items with disposition RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER, which SKUs are most frequently returned to supplier?
disposition.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar nullable with small set of values (3 distinct). Normalize values (trim, uppercase) when matching. Use equality or IN filters (e.g., WHERE disposition IN ('RESTOCK','DISCARD')). When users ask for 'sent back to supplier' map to disposition = 'RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER'. Consider handling unknown/NULL dispositions (uninspected or pending).

notes.description: Freeform notes or comments related to the return item. Column currently typed as decimal but observed to be 100% NULL—likely unused or mis-typed.
notes.analysis_use: If populated, could be used for qualitative analysis or text search to capture inspector comments. As-is it contains no data and cannot be relied upon for analysis.
business_questions: Show any inspection notes for a given return_item_id (if notes were populated).
Search returned items for a keyword mentioned in notes (requires text content).
notes.nl2sql_hints: Declared as decimal(18,4) but all values are NULL in sample—treat as unused. If users reference 'notes', confirm field actually contains text; if not, map to other comment fields in returns or tickets tables. Avoid numeric aggregations on this column; check schema correctness (should likely be nvarchar).

review_id.description: Unique identifier for each review (primary key).
review_id.analysis_use: Primary key for row-level lookups, deduplication and joins to any review-specific artifacts. Use in WHERE for single-review retrieval.
business_questions: Retrieve full review details for a given review_id.
Detect duplicate or missing review rows by review_id.
review_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Exact match filter (WHERE review_id = '...') is appropriate. Since cardinality equals rowcount, queries filtering by review_id should be selective and fast; consider indexing if frequent point lookups are common.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who submitted the review; FK to ecom_customers.customer_id.
customer_id.analysis_use: Join key to customer metadata for segment analysis, grouping by customer to get counts and avg rating, filter to reviews from specific customers or segments.
business_questions: What are the average ratings by customer segment (joined with ecom_customers)?
Which customers submitted the most helpful reviews?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Use JOIN ecom_customers ON ecom_reviews.customer_id = ecom_customers.customer_id. Handle possible NULLs. For NL2SQL map 'customer', 'customer id', 'user' to this column; when filtering by segments, route through ecom_customers.

sku.description: Product SKU for the reviewed item.
sku.analysis_use: Group and aggregate (COUNT, AVG(rating), SUM(helpful_count)) by SKU; join to product tables for names/categories; filter for specific SKUs or SKU lists.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the lowest average rating?
How many reviews does SKU X have and what is its average helpful_count?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) but actual max len 6; low cardinality (~10). Common filters: WHERE sku = 'BTR006' or WHERE sku IN (...). Join to products/product_variants on sku or product tables—ensure exact matching case/format. Consider indexing sku for aggregation-heavy queries.

rating.description: Numeric rating provided by the customer (bigint).
rating.analysis_use: Primary metric for sentiment: compute AVG(rating), distribution (COUNT per rating), rate-based filters (e.g., rating >=4), and time-series averages.
business_questions: What is the average rating overall and per SKU?
What proportion of reviews are 4 or 5 stars?
rating.nl2sql_hints: Type: bigint. Use numeric aggregations (AVG(), COUNT(), GROUP BY rating). Because distinct values are limited, GROUP BY rating is efficient. When asking for 'positive/negative' use thresholds (rating >=4). Watch for NULLs when aggregating; use COALESCE or WHERE rating IS NOT NULL if needed.

title.description: Short review title summarizing the customer's impression.
title.analysis_use: Categorical grouping to count common themes, frequency analysis, simple sentiment proxies, and filters for templated responses.
business_questions: What are the most common review titles for a product or SKU?
Are certain titles (e.g., 'Too ripe') concentrated in particular SKUs or time periods?
title.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50); low cardinality (4 distinct). Use GROUP BY title for frequency. For text-matching use exact matches or LIKE for partials. Map natural language requests like 'show reviews titled X' to WHERE title = 'X'.

review_text.description: Full review text left by the customer; here appears templated with a few repeated phrases.
review_text.analysis_use: Text analysis (frequency counts, basic sentiment proxies), filters for keywords (LIKE '%packaging%'), and grouping to find common complaints/praises. Useful for surfacing examples in dashboards.
business_questions: Which reviews mention 'packaging' or 'overripe' and how do those reviews rate?
Show examples of 5-star reviews for SKU X.
review_text.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(57); low distinct values in this dataset (4). For NL2SQL use LIKE/ILIKE or full text search where supported. When translating NL to SQL, treat this as free text—allow queries like WHERE review_text LIKE '%fresh%' and return review_text in SELECT. Beware of truncation if UI expects longer text.

review_ts.description: Timestamp of the review as stored originally (nvarchar).
review_ts.analysis_use: Time-based analysis (counts over intervals) after parsing/casting to datetime; use for order-of-event if created_at is not the desired field.
business_questions: How many reviews were posted per day using review_ts?
What are the most recent reviews for a given SKU or customer?
review_ts.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) but contains timestamp strings (format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'). Cast to datetime in queries: CAST(review_ts AS datetime2) or TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT depending on DB. Prefer created_at if review_ts parsing is unreliable. NL2SQL should detect date phrases (e.g., 'last 7 days') and map to CAST(review_ts ...) or created_at when appropriate.

verified_purchase_flag.description: Indicates whether the review is from a verified purchase (bit).
verified_purchase_flag.analysis_use: Filter to compare verified vs non-verified review behavior, compute rates and averages conditional on verified status.
business_questions: What is the average rating for verified purchases vs non-verified?
What proportion of reviews are verified purchases?
verified_purchase_flag.nl2sql_hints: Type: bit. In this dataset the flag is always True (no variance) — NL2SQL systems should detect this and avoid returning empty groups. Use WHERE verified_purchase_flag = 1 for filters. Be robust to both boolean literals (TRUE/FALSE) and numeric (1/0) depending on SQL dialect.

helpful_count.description: Number of customers who marked the review as helpful (bigint).
helpful_count.analysis_use: Ranking/sorting reviews by helpfulness, aggregating helpful_count per SKU or customer, identifying influential reviews.
business_questions: Which reviews have the highest helpful_count overall and per SKU?
What is the total helpful_count accumulated by reviews for SKU X?
helpful_count.nl2sql_hints: Type: bigint. Use ORDER BY helpful_count DESC for 'most helpful' queries. When aggregating use SUM(helpful_count) and handle NULLs with COALESCE(helpful_count, 0). For percentile queries use window functions (ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), NTILE).

responded_by.description: Intended to represent who responded to the review (likely user id) but currently typed decimal and contains 100% NULLs.
responded_by.analysis_use: When populated, used to measure response rates, response times and staff interactions. Currently unusable until corrected and populated.
business_questions: Which staff members respond to reviews most frequently?
What percentage of reviews receive a response?
responded_by.nl2sql_hints: Type currently decimal(18,4) with all NULLs — treat as data-quality issue. NL2SQL should not expect values; if present, treat as identifier and convert to appropriate type (nvarchar/int). For response analysis join on staff or users table using corrected type. Use IS NOT NULL to detect responded reviews.

response_text.description: Text of the response to the review; currently mis-typed as decimal(18,4) and 100% NULL.
response_text.analysis_use: When populated, used to show company replies, analyze response quality, and compute response coverage. Currently unusable.
business_questions: Show review and corresponding company response for reviews with negative ratings.
How long after a review is a response posted and what is the sentiment of the response?
response_text.nl2sql_hints: Type currently decimal(18,4) but should be nvarchar/text. Treat as a data-quality/ETL fix. NL2SQL should map 'response' and 'reply' to this column if corrected. Use LIKE or full-text search for content-based filters.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp for the review row (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Reliable datetime for ordering and time-series (prefer this over review_ts if review_ts is inconsistent). Use for windowing (daily/weekly/monthly aggregations) and filtering (last N days).
business_questions: How many reviews were created in the last 30 days?
Show trends in average rating by week/month using created_at.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2. Use DATEPART, CAST, CONVERT or date_trunc equivalents for interval grouping depending on dialect. Prefer created_at for time range filters if review_ts requires parsing. Use BETWEEN for inclusive ranges and timezone-aware conversions if needed in application layer.

date.description: The calendar date for the aggregated daily metrics (datetime2, primary key). Represents the day the sales metrics belong to.
date.analysis_use: Primary grouping and ordering key for all time-series queries, date range filtering, window functions (moving averages, period-over-period), rollups to week/month/year. Use as join key to other daily aggregates.
business_questions: Show sales (gross/net) for a specified date range or compare two dates.
Calculate 7-day or 30-day moving averages ending on a given date.
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as DATE (strip time portion) for grouping; cast datetime2 to date if necessary. Use BETWEEN or explicit >= and < next-day for filters. Because it's the PK, expect uniqueness — avoid asking for DISTINCT date unless explicitly needed.

order_channel.description: The channel through which orders were placed (nvarchar(50)). In this table all rows are 'Website'.
order_channel.analysis_use: Potential filter/group column for multi-channel comparisons; in this table current cardinality is 1 so it is not useful for segmentation unless future data adds channels.
business_questions: Filter or compare website sales versus other channels (if/when present).
Confirm channel-specific KPIs for Website across dates.
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Value distribution is constant ('Website'). NL2SQL should prefer not to include redundant WHERE order_channel = 'Website' unless user asks for channel-specific filtering. Expect simple equality filters; not a reliable join key.

total_orders.description: Total number of orders on that date (stored as decimal(18,4) though conceptually a count).
total_orders.analysis_use: Used for aggregations (SUM for totals, AVG for average orders per day), rate calculations (orders per customer), denominators for AOV (gross_sales/total_orders) and other per-order metrics. Filtering on thresholds (e.g., high-order days) and grouping by order volume bands.
business_questions: How many orders were received on a given date or over a range?
What is the average number of orders per day for the last month?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal — cast to integer if expecting integer semantics (e.g., CAST(total_orders AS BIGINT)). Handle NULLs (days with no data). For AOV, protect against division by zero by checking total_orders > 0.

total_customers.description: Number of unique customers who purchased on that date (decimal stored).
total_customers.analysis_use: Used for customer-level KPIs, average orders per customer, retention/cohort inputs when joined with customer tables, and correlation with total_orders and sales.
business_questions: How many unique customers purchased on a particular day?
Are increases in revenue driven by more customers or higher spend per customer?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: Also decimal; cast to integer for count-like operations. When calculating rates (orders per customer) ensure division by non-zero. If nulls present treat as 0 or exclude depending on business rule.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity shipped/sold on that date measured in kilograms (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute average kg per order or per customer, trend analysis for volume forecasts, and correlation with revenue or delivery cost. Useful for logistics and capacity planning.
business_questions: What was the total weight sold each day and how is it trending?
What is the average kg sold per order or per customer?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal with four decimal places — use SUM and AVG directly. When joining to per-item or per-order tables, join on date and be aware of differing aggregation granularities (order-level vs daily). Consider unit conversions if other tables use grams or units.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (revenue before discounts, fees and taxes) for the date (decimal monetary value).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for trend analysis, aggregation (SUM, AVG), share calculations (discount% = discount_amount/gross_sales), and comparisons across periods.
business_questions: What were the gross sales on a specified date or period?
How much did gross sales grow month-over-month?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Monetary decimal — preserve scale. For percentage calculations guard against zero gross_sales. When users ask for currency formatting, perform formatting at presentation layer; NL2SQL should return raw numeric values. Use appropriate SUM/AVG and grouping by date parts.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied on that date (decimal monetary value).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute discount rate (discount_amount/gross_sales), to analyze promotion effectiveness, and to aggregate discounts across periods.
business_questions: What was the total discount given yesterday and how did that impact net sales?
Which days have the highest discount as a percentage of gross sales?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal monetary field. For ratio calculations ensure gross_sales > 0. When users ask for discount breakdowns by campaign or promo, join to coupon or order-level tables — this table only gives totals.

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fees collected on that date (decimal monetary value).
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Used to evaluate delivery revenue, unit delivery fee per order, impact on net sales, and to compare delivery fee revenue vs delivery cost (if cost data available elsewhere).
business_questions: How much was collected in delivery fees on a given day?
What is the average delivery fee collected per order?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Decimal. May be large relative to other monetary fields. When computing per-order metrics divide by total_orders with a guard for zero. If users request net margin after delivery cost, delivery cost must be joined from logistics or cost tables.

tax_amount.description: Total taxes charged on that date (decimal monetary value).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute tax as percentage of gross sales, to reconcile tax collected over periods, and for compliance or VAT reporting when aggregated by tax period.
business_questions: How much tax was collected this week or month?
What percentage of gross sales is being taken as tax?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal. For regulatory reports, group by fiscal periods (month/quarter). Ensure proper aggregation (SUM) and time-zone/fiscal calendar alignment if required.

net_sales.description: Net sales for the date after discounts, fees and taxes (decimal monetary value).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line revenue metric for daily reporting, trend analysis, period comparisons and as input to profitability analyses when combined with cost data.
business_questions: What were the net sales each day for the last 30 days?
How did net sales change after discount campaigns?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal. When users ask for net contribution per order use net_sales/total_orders with zero-division checks. Verify whether net_sales is pre- or post-delivery/tax per business definition before combining with other tables.

created_at.description: Timestamp when this row was created/loaded into ETL (datetime2). Only 2 distinct values indicating batch loads.
created_at.analysis_use: Used for data lineage and diagnosing ingestion time; not typically used for business metrics. Can be used to filter by load batch if auditing or reprocessing rows.
business_questions: When was the daily summary for a given date last loaded?
Which rows were loaded in a specific ETL batch?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 with only a couple distinct values—treat as metadata. Avoid using created_at for time-series analysis of sales. If filtering by created_at, use exact equality or range. Consider that created_at is not a reliable proxy for the business date.

year.description: Calendar year for the monthly aggregate (integer/bigint). Part of the composite primary key.
year.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering by year; used in ORDER BY or WHERE to limit analysis to specific years; join key to yearly rollup tables.
business_questions: What are the totals for 2024 versus previous years (when available)?
How does month-over-month performance for a specific year compare?
year.nl2sql_hints: Bigint; treat as integer for grouping and filtering. Use in WHERE (e.g., WHERE year = 2024) and GROUP BY. Because sample has only 2024, NL2SQL systems should allow year filters but warn of limited range. Use exact match rather than textual comparisons.

month.description: Calendar month number (1-12) for the aggregate. Second part of the composite primary key.
month.analysis_use: Primary grouping dimension for monthly time series; combine with year to form a date or use in ORDER BY to ensure chronological ordering; useful for month-over-month delta calculations.
business_questions: Which month had the highest net sales in the year?
What is the month-over-month percentage change in total_orders?
month.nl2sql_hints: Bigint; when returning chronological results, ORDER BY year, month. For time functions, consider casting to a date (e.g., DATEFROMPARTS(year, month, 1) in SQL Server) to produce proper date axes. Be mindful that month is numeric (not padded), so formatting may be required for display.

order_channel.description: Sales channel for the row (nvarchar(50)). In the sample data it's always 'Website'.
order_channel.analysis_use: Slice by channel when multiple channels are present; used as a grouping or filter dimension for channel-specific reporting and comparisons with other channel tables.
business_questions: How much of monthly sales came from the Website channel versus other channels?
Are order counts different across channels in a given month?
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Text field; in this table it appears single-valued. NL2SQL should handle values case-sensitively depending on DB collation. Use equality filters (e.g., WHERE order_channel = 'Website'). If nulls exist, handle them explicitly (IS NULL) in queries. Not a reliable join key to customer/order tables — use channel fields from transactional tables instead.

total_orders.description: Pre-aggregated total number of orders for the month (numeric stored as decimal(18,4)).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM across months or years), ratios (orders per customer), and trend analyses. Can be used as denominator for average calculations (e.g., average order value).
business_questions: How many orders were placed in March 2024?
What is the average number of orders per month in 2024?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal; when expecting integer counts, cast/round appropriately (e.g., CAST(total_orders AS BIGINT) or ROUND). In NL2SQL generation, prefer SUM(total_orders) for totals and use explicit casting for COUNT-like semantics. Watch for NULL values — use COALESCE(total_orders,0).

total_customers.analysis_use: Customer reach and penetration metrics, churn/retention approximations, and segmentation when compared across months. Used as denominator for per-customer averages.
business_questions: How many unique customers ordered in June 2024?
What is the average revenue per customer for July 2024 (net_sales / total_customers)?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal though conceptually an integer. CAST or ROUND when using for counts. Use COALESCE(total_customers, 0) to avoid NULLs. If precise unique counts are needed, join to transactional customer-level tables rather than relying solely on this pre-aggregation.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the month (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Volume trends, fulfillment/weight-based costing, and average weight per order (total_qty_kg / total_orders). Useful for logistics planning (delivery capacity).
business_questions: What was the total kg sold in November 2024?
What is the average kg per order in each month?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal — aggregate with SUM(total_qty_kg). For derived ratios, ensure denominator handling and casting. Use units in query outputs to avoid ambiguity. Watch for NULLs and use COALESCE(total_qty_kg,0).

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (pre-discount) for the month (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for top-line reporting; used in aggregates, YTD calculations, and to compute average order value (gross_sales / total_orders). Used with discount_amount to calculate realized revenue.
business_questions: What were the monthly gross sales and which month had the highest gross sales?
How much did gross sales grow MoM or YoY (when multiple years available)?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal with 4 fractional digits. Use SUM(gross_sales) for rollups. When computing per-order or per-customer metrics, divide carefully and guard divide-by-zero. Ensure currency formatting is applied at presentation. Use COALESCE(gross_sales, 0) to handle NULLs.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied for the month (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Assess promotional costs, discount as percentage of gross_sales, and the impact of discounts on revenue. Useful to compute net_sales = gross_sales - discount_amount + delivery_fee + tax_amount (verify sign conventions).
business_questions: What total discount was given in August 2024 and what percent of gross sales was it?
How did discounts affect average order value over the year?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal; may be zero or NULL. When computing percentage of gross sales, use safe division with COALESCE(gross_sales,0) and NULL handling: CASE WHEN gross_sales = 0 THEN NULL ELSE discount_amount/gross_sales END. Confirm sign convention (positive = amount reduced from gross) before doing arithmetic.

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fees charged in the month (decimal(18,4)).
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Revenue or offset to shipping cost analysis, used in net revenue calculations and to evaluate shipping income vs shipping cost where shipping cost is available elsewhere.
business_questions: How much in delivery fees was collected in October 2024?
What portion of total revenue is covered by delivery fees each month?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Decimal; may need to be considered as revenue or pass-through depending on business logic. Use COALESCE(delivery_fee,0). For net calculations, confirm whether delivery fees are included in net_sales or need to be added/subtracted explicitly.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the month (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Tax reporting and compliance, calculation of net revenue after taxes, and reconciling tax collected with tax remittance data.
business_questions: How much tax was collected in each month of 2024?
Is tax_amount changing proportionally with gross_sales month-over-month?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal; treat as monetary value. Use SUM(tax_amount) and COALESCE(tax_amount,0). For comparisons with gross_sales, ensure both are in the same currency/context. Avoid using tax_amount as tax rate — it's an absolute amount.

net_sales.description: Net sales for the month after discounts (and possibly after fees/tax depending on pipeline logic) (decimal(18,4)).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line monthly sales figure for revenue reporting and comparisons. Use in trend analysis, YTD calculations, and per-customer/per-order metrics.
business_questions: What were net sales each month and which month delivered the highest net revenue?
What is net sales per customer or per order for each month?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal. Verify and document formula used to compute net_sales (gross - discount + delivery + tax? or gross - discount - tax?). For NL2SQL, avoid deriving net_sales from components unless explicitly requested — prefer using net_sales column. Use COALESCE(net_sales,0) and SUM(net_sales) for aggregations.

created_at.description: ETL row creation timestamp (datetime2). In the sample this is constant and likely indicates when the monthly snapshot was created.
created_at.analysis_use: Auditing, freshness checks, and filtering by snapshot run. Not used for the business time dimension (use year/month instead).
business_questions: When was this monthly snapshot generated?
Which snapshots are stale or need refresh based on their created_at timestamps?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2; do not use created_at as primary business date for reporting — use year/month. For freshness filters, use WHERE created_at >= '<timestamp>' or compare to current time. Be aware that created_at may be identical across rows if snapshots are bulk-produced.

year.description: Calendar year for the aggregated metrics. Primary key of the table representing the period of aggregation.
year.analysis_use: Used for filtering and grouping (e.g., WHERE year = 2024, GROUP BY year, or comparing multiple years). Ideal as a join key to lower-granularity tables when mapped to transaction dates.
business_questions: What were the key sales metrics for the year 2024?
How has net sales changed year-over-year (requires multiple year rows)?
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as integer/bigint. Use equality or range predicates (e.g., year = 2024, year BETWEEN 2020 AND 2024). When joining to transactional tables that store full timestamps, map with YEAR(transaction_date) = year or use date range filters (transaction_date >= 'YYYY-01-01' AND transaction_date < 'YYYY+1-01-01') for sargability.

order_channel.description: The sales channel (e.g., Website, Mobile app, POS) for which the yearly aggregates are computed.
order_channel.analysis_use: Filter or group to compare performance across channels. Useful for channel-specific KPIs and channel-mix analysis.
business_questions: How much revenue did the Website/channel generate in 2024?
Which channel contributed the most orders or customers in the year?
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Case-sensitive depending on DB collation—use case-insensitive matches where appropriate (e.g., LOWER(order_channel) = 'website'). Use IN for multiple channels. Be aware that the field is nullable; handle NULLs explicitly (IS NULL or COALESCE(order_channel,'Unknown')).

total_orders.description: Total number of orders (aggregated) for the year and channel. Stored as decimal(18,4) even though counts are typically integers, indicating it may be aggregated or averaged in upstream processing.
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) or report directly as the yearly count; compute derived metrics like average order value (gross_sales / total_orders). Filter or compare across years/channels.
business_questions: How many orders did we process in the year for a given channel?
What is the average order value when combining total_orders with gross_sales?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Although numeric, total_orders may contain fractional values because of decimal type—cast to integer if you expect counts (CAST(total_orders AS BIGINT) or ROUND). Use NULL-safe computations (e.g., COALESCE(total_orders,0)) when dividing to compute averages. Be careful to prevent division by zero.

total_customers.description: Number of distinct customers captured in the year for the channel (stored as decimal(18,4) similar to total_orders).
total_customers.analysis_use: Used for customer-level KPIs such as customers per year, customer penetration, reactivation analyses when combined with other tables. Can compute metrics like average orders per customer (total_orders / total_customers).
business_questions: How many unique customers purchased via the channel in the year?
What is the average number of orders per customer for the year?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: May be fractional due to decimal type; cast/round when necessary. Use COALESCE(total_customers,0) to avoid null-based errors. For segmentation or cohort analysis, join with ecom_customers or customer-level aggregates rather than relying solely on this column.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the aggregated year and channel (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Useful for volume-based KPIs, logistics planning, per-kg revenue metrics (gross_sales / total_qty_kg), and capacity forecasting. Can be compared against inventory or shipping metrics.
business_questions: What was the total weight (kg) of product shipped/sold in the year?
What is revenue per kg (gross_sales divided by total_qty_kg)?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Units are kilograms—clarify unit assumptions with consumers. Use COALESCE(total_qty_kg,0) and guard divisions for zero. When joining to product-level or shipment tables, ensure consistent unit conversions before comparisons.

gross_sales.description: Total gross revenue for the year before discounts, taxes and delivery fees (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for topline. Summarize, compare year-over-year, compute percentages (discount/gross_sales), and feed into dashboards and financial reconciliation.
business_questions: What was the total gross sales for the year by channel?
What percentage of gross sales was reduced by discounts?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Monetary decimal—preserve precision and format in outputs. Use proper rounding/CAST for reporting (e.g., ROUND(gross_sales,2)). Be explicit about currency if multi-currency context exists. Use COALESCE(gross_sales,0) in arithmetic expressions.

discount_amount.description: Total monetary value of discounts applied during the year (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Measure discount impact, compute discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales), and test promotional effectiveness. Compare against marketing spend or coupon usage tables.
business_questions: How much money was given away in discounts this year?
What is the discount rate as a percentage of gross sales?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal money field—use COALESCE(discount_amount,0). When calculating percentages, ensure gross_sales is non-zero. To validate promotions, join or reconcile with ecom_coupons or ecom_coupon_usage using period filters.

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery/shipping fees charged to customers in the year (decimal(18,4)).
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Part of revenue components; used to analyze contribution of delivery fees to net sales and to offset shipping costs. Can be compared to shipping cost data from logistics tables.
business_questions: How much revenue came from delivery fees during the year?
Are delivery fees covering logistics costs (compare against shipping expense data)?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: COALESCE(delivery_fee,0) and ROUND for presentation. If delivery fee is sometimes negative (refund/adjustment), handle signs explicitly. To reconcile, filter/join with dispatch/shipment tables by year range.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected on orders for the year (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Tax reporting and reconciliation; part of gross-to-net calculations. Important for regulatory reporting and VAT/sales tax analysis.
business_questions: How much tax was collected in the year?
What is the effective tax rate relative to gross sales?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: COALESCE(tax_amount,0). Taxes may require special handling if multi-jurisdictional—join with tax or order-level tables to break out by region. Use rounding appropriate for currency.

net_sales.description: Net sales for the year after adjusting gross sales for discounts, delivery fees and taxes (decimal(18,4)).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line sales metric for revenue reporting and comparisons. Used for KPI calculations, growth rates, and financial dashboards.
business_questions: What were the net sales for the year for each order channel?
How do net sales compare to gross sales and year-over-year performance?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal monetary field—use COALESCE(net_sales,0) and ROUND when presenting. When validating, net_sales should be reconcilable to gross_sales - discount_amount + delivery_fee + tax_amount (verify sign conventions). Use explicit expressions in NL2SQL to compute deltas and percentages.

created_at.description: Timestamp indicating when this aggregated row was generated or inserted (ETL snapshot time).
created_at.analysis_use: Used to check currency/staleness of the snapshot, to filter snapshots by ETL date, and for auditing. Not a business metric but critical for data freshness checks.
business_questions: When was this yearly aggregate last updated?
Is the snapshot for year X up to date with the latest ETL run?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 field—use standard date/time comparisons and timezone considerations if applicable. For joins or comparisons to transaction dates, prefer comparing by year ranges rather than direct equality. Use CAST/CONVERT for formatting in output. Be aware some snapshots may be generated after the period end (e.g., created_at in 2025 for year=2024).

keyword_id.description: Unique identifier for the keyword record (string). Primary key of the table.
keyword_id.analysis_use: Used as the unique row identifier; not typically used for grouping or aggregation. Useful for deduplication and selecting single records.
business_questions: What is the record detail for keyword_id = 'KEYW1001'?
Are there duplicate keyword_id values (data integrity check)?
keyword_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) NOT NULL. Exact-match key; use equality predicates (WHERE keyword_id = '...'). No need to cast; beware of leading/trailing whitespace and case depending on collation. Use direct PK lookup for fast point queries.

keyword.description: The search term or keyword string (e.g., 'potato', 'organic veg').
keyword.analysis_use: Primary categorical attribute for grouping, filtering and ranking. Use GROUP BY keyword, COUNT(*) and aggregate metrics (AVG conversion_rate, SUM search_volume). Also for text filtering (LIKE, ILIKE depending on DB) and fuzzy matching.
business_questions: Which keywords have the highest aggregate search volume?
Which keywords have the best conversion rate on average?
keyword.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) NULL. Low cardinality (9 distinct); use GROUP BY keyword for compact result sets. For text filters use case-insensitive comparisons or LOWER(keyword) when user queries may vary in case. Support substring matches with LIKE '%<term>%'. Beware NULLs — include WHERE keyword IS NOT NULL when grouping/filtering.

search_volume.description: Numeric count indicating search volume or estimated searches for the keyword (bigint).
search_volume.analysis_use: Used for numeric aggregations (SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN), ranking (ORDER BY search_volume DESC), and correlation with conversion_rate. Useful for computing weighted metrics (e.g., volume-weighted conversion).
business_questions: What are the top N keywords by search_volume?
How does search_volume correlate with conversion_rate across keywords?
search_volume.nl2sql_hints: Type: bigint NULL. Use numeric comparisons and aggregations without casting. Handle NULLs with COALESCE(search_volume,0) if summing. For percentile or distribution queries use appropriate DB functions (PERCENTILE_CONT/NTILE). When users ask for thresholds (e.g., >100), ensure integer comparison. Consider summing by keyword or time for trend queries.

conversion_rate.description: Conversion rate for the keyword represented as a decimal (decimal(18,4)), likely a percentage value (e.g., 2.4800 meaning 2.48%).
conversion_rate.analysis_use: Used for ranking and averaging (AVG conversion_rate), filtering (e.g., conversion_rate > X) and deriving metrics such as conversion uplift or expected conversions = search_volume * conversion_rate/100 if conversion_rate is percent.
business_questions: Which keywords have conversion_rate above a business threshold (e.g., >5%)?
Which keywords have high conversion_rate but low search_volume (high ROI opportunities)?
conversion_rate.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4) NULL. Confirm whether values are stored as percentages (e.g., 2.48) or fractions (0.0248). When combining with search_volume to estimate conversions, convert appropriately (e.g., search_volume * conversion_rate / 100). Use COALESCE(conversion_rate,0) to handle NULLs. For comparisons, avoid implicit string casts; treat as numeric. Cast to numeric precision if your DB requires it for arithmetic.

top_sku.description: The SKU most associated with the keyword (string). Represents the top product shown or clicked for this keyword.
top_sku.analysis_use: Used to join to product and sales tables to analyze product-level performance for keyword-driven traffic; group and filter by top_sku to see which products surface for top keywords.
business_questions: Which SKU appears most often as the top_sku for high-volume keywords?
Do top SKUs for high-converting keywords have higher revenue/sales in product tables?
top_sku.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) NULL. No declared FK; joining requires matching column names in product tables (e.g., products.sku or product_variants.sku). Normalize case and trim whitespace when joining (e.g., UPPER(top_sku) = UPPER(products.sku)). Because top_sku cardinality is low, GROUP BY top_sku produces compact results. Handle NULLs (WHERE top_sku IS NOT NULL) when joining.

created_at.description: Timestamp when this keyword metric record was recorded (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Primary temporal dimension for trend analysis and time-windowed aggregations (GROUP BY date, week, month). Useful for time-series, snapshot comparisons, and incremental ETL/partitioning.
business_questions: How has the search_volume for 'potato' changed over the last 30 days?
Which keywords had spikes in conversion_rate during a specific date range?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2 NULL. Use standard datetime predicates (WHERE created_at BETWEEN ...). For grouping by day/week/month, cast/convert appropriately (e.g., CAST(created_at AS date) or DATE_TRUNC functions). Account for timezone if source data spans regions. For incremental queries use WHERE created_at > last_processed_timestamp. Handle NULL created_at rows explicitly (exclude or treat as unknown).

ticket_id.description: Unique identifier for each support ticket (primary key).
ticket_id.analysis_use: Used to identify rows, count distinct tickets, deduplicate, and join to detail tables (e.g., ecom_ticket_messages). Key for record-level retrieval.
business_questions: Retrieve full ticket record by ticket_id (e.g., details for TICK001002).
How many distinct tickets were created in a date range?
ticket_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NOT NULL; unique. Use equality filters: WHERE ticket_id = 'TICK001001'. When selecting a single ticket prefer direct equality rather than LIKE. Join key to message/detail tables if they reference ticket_id.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who raised the ticket; nullable if ticket not tied to a customer record.
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter and group tickets by customer, join to ecom_customers for attributes (segment, region, lifetime value), compute tickets per customer and identify repeat reporters.
business_questions: Which customers have the most tickets in the last 90 days?
What issues are most common for high-LTV customers (requires join to customers)?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; FK to zuna_etl.ecom_customers.customer_id. Use LEFT JOIN when enriching to preserve tickets without customer_id. Filter/aggregate with WHERE customer_id = 'CUST1072' or GROUP BY customer_id. Consider indexing customer_id for join performance.

subject.description: Categorized short subject of the ticket (e.g., Delivery Delay, Order Issue).
subject.analysis_use: Group and filter to analyze issue types, distribution of subjects and trends over time, subject vs. priority or resolution speed.
business_questions: What proportion of tickets are 'Delivery Delay' versus other subjects?
Which subjects have the highest proportion of high-priority or unresolved tickets?
subject.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; low cardinality (4). Prefer equality or IN filters: WHERE subject = 'Delivery Delay' or subject IN ('Delivery Delay','Refund Query'). Avoid full-text search—subject is categorical.

description.description: Short textual description of the issue. Often templated in this dataset.
description.analysis_use: Used for sampling or identifying frequently occurring phrasing; can be used for simple pattern matching (LIKE) to find specific keywords. For advanced text analysis join with message table and use NLP outside SQL.
business_questions: How many tickets contain 'damaged' in the description?
Which descriptions correlate with higher priority or repeat tickets?
description.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; low variety here but free text in general. Use WHERE description LIKE '%damaged%' for keyword searches. Consider using lower/upper functions for case-insensitive matches. For NL2SQL, avoid assuming unique phrasing; map common phrases to canonical subjects when possible.

status.description: Current lifecycle state of the ticket (OPEN, IN_PROGRESS, RESOLVED, CLOSED).
status.analysis_use: Primary column for operational dashboards: counts by status, SLA measurement, backlog size, and time-in-state analysis (with timestamps from messages or updated_at).
business_questions: How many tickets are currently OPEN or IN_PROGRESS?
What percentage of tickets get RESOLVED within X hours by priority?
status.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; low cardinality (4). Use WHERE status = 'OPEN' or GROUP BY status. If computing resolution times, combine with created_at/updated_at and ensure status semantics (RESOLVED vs CLOSED). Consider normalizing status values in queries (TRIM/UPPER) if data inconsistencies exist.

priority.description: Business priority assigned to the ticket (LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH).
priority.analysis_use: Segment tickets for SLA measurement, resource allocation, and prioritization analysis; useful for weighted counts and filtering critical issues.
business_questions: How many high-priority tickets are open and who are they assigned to?
Average time to first update for HIGH vs LOW priority tickets.
priority.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; low cardinality (3). Use WHERE priority = 'HIGH' or GROUP BY priority. Consider mapping priority to numeric weights externally for weighted aggregations. Ensure consistent values (e.g., no 'High' vs 'HIGH').

created_at.description: Timestamp when the ticket was created.
created_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trend analysis (tickets per day/week/month), cohort analysis, and SLA start point for age/resolution calculations.
business_questions: How many tickets were created per day over the last 30 days?
Are ticket volumes increasing month-over-month for 'Delivery Delay' subjects?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 NULL; use date casting for grouping: CAST(created_at AS DATE) or DATEPART/YEAR/MONTH depending on DB.
