For range filters use BETWEEN or >= AND < patterns (e.g., WHERE created_at >= '2025-08-01' AND created_at < '2025-09-01'). Consider indexing created_at for time-range queries.

updated_at.description: Timestamp of the last update to the ticket record.
updated_at.analysis_use: Used to compute time-to-update or approximate resolution time (when paired with status or messages). Useful for recency filtering and to measure agent responsiveness.
business_questions: What is the median time between created_at and updated_at for resolved tickets?
Which tickets have not been updated in the last X days?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 NULL; often used together with created_at to compute durations: DATEDIFF(second, created_at, updated_at) or similar. Use WHERE updated_at IS NULL to find never-updated tickets. Beware that updated_at may change for any metadata update — for true resolution time use explicit resolved_at if available in messages table.

order_id.description: References an order related to the ticket (nullable); ties ticket to a specific purchase or order event.
order_id.analysis_use: Join to orders to analyze order-related issues, group tickets by order attributes (order date, fulfillment center), and find problematic products or order batches.
business_questions: How many tickets are associated with orders placed in the last 30 days?
Which orders (or SKUs/orders by product) have generated multiple tickets?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; semi-cardinal. Join to ecom_orders (or relevant order table) on order_id for enrichment. Use LEFT JOIN to avoid dropping tickets without an order. Filter with WHERE order_id = 'EORD2018' or GROUP BY order_id. Consider indexing order_id for join/filter performance.

assigned_to.description: Identifier of the support agent or owner responsible for the ticket (e.g., ops_manager, support_agent_1).
assigned_to.analysis_use: Used to measure agent workload, performance (time to update/resolve), distribution of high-priority tickets, and capacity planning.
business_questions: How many open tickets does each agent currently have?
Which agent resolves tickets fastest for HIGH priority?
assigned_to.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) NULL; low cardinality (3). Use GROUP BY assigned_to for load reports and join to a users/agents table if available for richer metadata. Use WHERE assigned_to IS NULL to find unassigned tickets. Consider filtering using exact matching: WHERE assigned_to = 'ops_manager'.

ticket_message_id.description: Unique identifier for each ticket message (nvarchar(50)). Presently no PK constraint enforced in DDL.
ticket_message_id.analysis_use: Use to uniquely identify rows when de-duplicating or reconstructing message threads. Useful for ordering if message_ts missing or ambiguous. Can be used as a grouping key for message-level joins or updates.
business_questions: Which specific message (by id) was sent during a ticket escalation?
Are there duplicate or missing ticket_message_id values indicating data issues?
ticket_message_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string primary-key candidate. Expect high cardinality; prefer equality filters. If NL2SQL query references message ids, match exact string. Consider creating a primary key or unique index to improve join performance and deterministic ordering.

ticket_id.description: Identifier of the ticket to which the message belongs (foreign key to ecom_ticket.ticket_id). nvarchar(50).
ticket_id.analysis_use: Principal join key to ecom_ticket and other ticket-related tables. Grouping and aggregations (message counts per ticket) and filters (messages for a specific ticket). Partitioning/indexing candidate for frequent ticket-level queries.
business_questions: How many messages does ticket TICK001010 have and who sent them?
Which tickets have no messages or very high message counts indicating complex issues?
ticket_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as the main join/filter key. Use equality or IN filters for ticket-scoped queries. Ensure join uses exact string match; consider trimming/case-normalization if inconsistent. Recommend index on ticket_id to speed ticket-level aggregations and joins.

message_ts.description: Message timestamp stored as nvarchar(50) (format examples look like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'). Represents when the message content was created/sent (may be same or different from created_at).
message_ts.analysis_use: After parsing to datetime, used for ordering messages within a ticket, calculating response times, filtering by message send time, and analyzing messaging patterns by time-of-day. Currently a string, so conversion needed for range queries and temporal aggregations.
business_questions: What is the time ordering of messages on ticket TICK001017?
What is the hourly distribution of messages (peak support hours)?
message_ts.nl2sql_hints: Convert nvarchar timestamp to a datetime/datetime2 in queries (e.g., TRY_CAST or CAST) before doing comparisons or date functions. If both message_ts and created_at exist, clarify which reflects sender time vs ingestion time. For NL2SQL, prefer explicit 'message_ts as datetime' conversions or use created_at if guaranteed accurate.

sender.description: Who sent the message (small set of values, e.g., 'support' or 'customer'). nvarchar(50) but only 2 distinct values observed.
sender.analysis_use: Primary dimension for segmenting messages — count by sender, compute response metrics (time from customer message to next support message), and SLA compliance by sender. Useful in stepwise conversation reconstruction (identify who replied).
business_questions: What proportion of messages are from support vs customers?
What is the average number of support replies per ticket?
sender.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a low-cardinality categorical column. Use equality checks in WHERE (e.g., sender = 'support') or CASE statements for aggregations. Be cautious of typos/case differences; normalize values during extraction or compare lower(sender).

message_text.description: Text body of the message (nvarchar(50)). In this dataset only 4 distinct messages are present; likely templated or truncated content.
message_text.analysis_use: Used for content classification, identifying common responses, detecting escalations or resolution confirmations, and simple keyword searches. Because of low cardinality here, can be grouped to find most common messages and inspect templates.
business_questions: Which canned responses are used most frequently by support?
How often do customers request attachments or provide photos (based on message_text like 'Can you share a photo?')?
message_text.nl2sql_hints: If doing text filters, use LIKE / CONTAINS if full-text enabled; otherwise equality or pattern matching. Be aware of truncation (max_len 29) — longer messages may be truncated. Consider migrating to a larger text column if message bodies can be longer.

attachment_url.description: Intended to store a URL to an attachment; currently typed as decimal(18,4) and 100% NULL in the sample. Column appears mis-typed and unused.
attachment_url.analysis_use: If populated and corrected to a string type, useful to determine which messages include attachments, count attachments per ticket, and analyze multimedia requests. As-is it cannot be meaningfully used.
business_questions: How many messages include attachments and which tickets contain attachments?
What types of attachments do customers send and how often are they requested by support?
attachment_url.nl2sql_hints: Treat as null/unused in current queries. For NL2SQL, assume no values unless schema is corrected. Recommend schema change to NVARCHAR or VARCHAR for URLs and backfill if attachments are present in source. Do not attempt numeric comparisons in queries until type fixed.

created_at.description: Row ingestion/creation timestamp (datetime2). Represents when the message row was created in the ETL or database.
created_at.analysis_use: Reliable for chronological queries, ingestion-time audits, filtering by ingestion window. Useful as fallback when message_ts is missing or not parseable. Can be used for time-based aggregations, SLA windowing, and retention analysis.
business_questions: Which messages were created in the last 7 days (recent activity)?
Are there delays between message_ts and created_at indicating ingestion latency?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Use as a native datetime for range filters, order by, and date_trunc/grouping. When NL2SQL asks for time-based metrics and message_ts is string, prefer created_at unless the user explicitly requests sender-provided timestamp. Consider exposing both and clarifying their semantics in NL2SQL prompts.

vendor_id.description: Unique identifier for a vendor (logical primary key).
vendor_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping and join key for all vendor-level analyses. Use for exact-match filters, joins to transactional tables, and counts of vendors.
business_questions: Show all details for vendor_id = 'ECOMV3001'.
How many transactions are associated with each vendor_id (when joined to orders/payments)?
vendor_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as exact-match string. Normalization: real values are short (<=9 chars) so consider mapping to varchar(9). Queries should use equality or IN lists; avoid LIKE unless partial id provided. Ensure joins use the same datatype in other tables (cast if necessary).

vendor_name.analysis_use: Reporting label for groups; group-by or display field. Use for fuzzy search, partial matches, and dedup detection.
business_questions: Find vendors whose name contains 'Tara' or 'Bajaj'.
List names of inactive vendors in Ahmedabad.
vendor_name.nl2sql_hints: Support case-insensitive and partial (LIKE) matching. Beware of small variations/typos; NL2SQL should allow fuzzy/contains search. Avoid using vendor_name as a join key.

vendor_category.description: Categorical classification of vendor (Ad Agency, Courier, Influencer, Marketing, Tech).
vendor_category.analysis_use: High-value grouping/filtering dimension for segmentation and aggregation (counts, joins to category-specific KPIs). Low cardinality => good candidate for indexing and fast aggregation.
business_questions: How many vendors belong to each vendor_category?
Show all ACTIVE vendors in the 'Courier' category.
vendor_category.nl2sql_hints: Use equality or IN for filters. NL2SQL systems should map synonyms (e.g., 'couriers' -> 'Courier') and handle pluralization. Consider offering suggestions when category value not exact.

phone.description: Vendor contact phone number (country code + digits, spaces present).
phone.analysis_use: Contact list export, duplicate detection, and validation. Useful for outreach filters (e.g., vendors missing phone).
business_questions: List phone numbers for vendors in Gandhinagar.
Which vendors do not have a phone number or have malformed phone formats?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Phone strings contain formatting (spaces, leading +). NL2SQL should support normalization (strip non-digit chars) for comparisons and dedup. Use IS NULL/IS NOT NULL to detect missing values; use LIKE/regex for format checks.

email.analysis_use: Contact exports, validation/uniqueness checks, and filtering for communication campaigns. Use to find vendors missing or with malformed emails.
business_questions: Export emails of all ACTIVE vendors in Marketing category.
Which vendors have invalid-looking email addresses?
email.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; support case-insensitive matching. NL2SQL should allow patterns (contains '@', domain filters). Be aware of data issues (commas or special chars). Use IS NULL/IS NOT NULL for presence checks.

billing_address.analysis_use: Used for invoicing, geolocation parsing (if normalized), and compliance addresses. Useful for generating mailing lists or extracting city/pincode via parsing.
business_questions: Provide billing addresses for vendors with outstanding invoices (when joined).
Which vendors have addresses in a given district/pincode?
billing_address.nl2sql_hints: Free-text field — use LIKE or full-text when supported. If NL2SQL user asks by region/pincode, system should attempt to parse or recommend extracting city/pincode into separate columns.

gst_no.description: Vendor GST number stored as bigint (tax identifier).
gst_no.analysis_use: Compliance/Tax verification, joins or matching to invoices/credit notes, uniqueness checks. Useful to detect missing or invalid GST entries.
business_questions: Which vendors are missing GST numbers?
List vendors with GST numbers matching a given pattern or exact GST.
gst_no.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as bigint, GST numbers can contain leading zeros in some contexts — verify format. NL2SQL should accept numeric equality and also support casting to string when matching against text fields in other tables. Consider recommending string storage in DDL to preserve formatting.

pan_no.description: Vendor PAN (Permanent Account Number) as nvarchar (typical length ~10).
pan_no.analysis_use: Compliance checks, identity matching with financial systems, uniqueness/dedup. Useful in regulatory reports and vendor verification.
business_questions: Find vendors with missing or duplicate PAN numbers.
Show vendor details for PAN = 'BALTL1862A'.
pan_no.nl2sql_hints: PAN is alphanumeric and usually fixed-length (~10). Use exact-match or uppercasing during comparisons. NL2SQL should normalize case and trim whitespace. Recommend enforcing length/format validations upstream.

city.description: Vendor city (normalized to a small set; 9 distinct values present).
city.analysis_use: Geographic grouping and filtering for regional reporting and logistics. Good for aggregation (vendor counts by city) and joins to region-level tables.
business_questions: How many vendors are located in Ahmedabad vs Anand?
List all INACTIVE vendors in Bharuch.
city.nl2sql_hints: Use exact-match or IN lists. NL2SQL should map synonyms or common misspellings and support case-insensitive matching. Consider providing autocompletion from distinct city values.

status.description: Lifecycle status of vendor: ACTIVE or INACTIVE.
status.analysis_use: Primary filter to include/exclude vendors from operational reports, onboarding or communications. Useful for counts, segmentation and policy-driven automations.
business_questions: How many ACTIVE vendors do we currently have?
List vendor_ids and contact details for INACTIVE vendors for re-onboarding.
status.nl2sql_hints: Binary low-cardinality filter — prefer equality checks. NL2SQL should accept synonyms (e.g., 'enabled' -> ACTIVE). Consider indexing for frequent status-based filters. Use explicit handling of NULL if present.

created_at.description: Datetime when the vendor row was created/loaded in ETL (currently a single load timestamp).
created_at.analysis_use: Source system load metadata rather than vendor onboarding date given single distinct value. Not useful for time-series analysis unless future rows show variance.
business_questions: Show vendors created after a given date (only meaningful if column varies).
When was this vendor record last loaded or created?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as datetime2. NL2SQL systems should be aware that in this dataset created_at is constant; time filters will return either all or none. If users ask for onboarding timelines, warn that created_at may reflect ETL load time not true onboarding date.

wishlist_id.description: Unique identifier for the wishlist entry (nvarchar(50)). In this dataset it appears unique per row (distinct = 100) but there is no declared primary key constraint.
wishlist_id.analysis_use: Use for row-level identification, de-duplication, and as a stable identifier when exporting or updating wishlist records. Rarely used for aggregation except counting distinct wishlist entries.
business_questions: How many unique wishlist entries exist in a given period?
Are there duplicate wishlist_id values indicating data integrity issues?
wishlist_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (nvarchar). Expect per-row uniqueness but confirm with COUNT(*) vs COUNT(DISTINCT). When filtering by wishlist_id quote values (e.g., WHERE wishlist_id = 'WISH04001'). Avoid relying on it as a join key to product/customer tables — join on customer_id or sku instead.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who created the wishlist entry (nvarchar(50)). 65 distinct customers across 100 rows, nullable.
customer_id.analysis_use: Common grouping key for per-customer aggregations (wishlist count, priority items per customer). Join key to customer master tables for enrichment (ecom_customers or b2b_customers). Useful in funnels and segmentation.
business_questions: Which customers have the most wishlist items and who should be targeted for re-engagement?
What percentage of customers add priority items to their wishlist?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string in queries and joins. Use explicit joins like JOIN ecom_customers c ON ecom_wishlist.customer_id = c.customer_id. Handle NULLs (WHERE customer_id IS NOT NULL) when counting customers. Consider indexing for frequent grouping/filtering if dataset grows.

sku.description: Stock-keeping unit for the product added to the wishlist (nvarchar(50)). Low cardinality (7 distinct SKUs) with strong skew toward a few top SKUs.
sku.analysis_use: Primary attribute for popularity analysis (counts, ranks). Good for low-cardinality aggregation and heatmaps. Join key to product / product_variant tables to fetch product name, category, price, availability.
business_questions: Which SKUs are most added to wishlists (top N SKUs)?
Are certain SKUs disproportionately marked as priority?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. When joining to product master be explicit about which products table to use (e.g., products.sku or product_variants.sku). Because cardinality is low, use GROUP BY sku ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT/TOP. Handle SKU normalization if different tables use variant codes or prefixes.

added_at.description: Timestamp (datetime2) when the SKU was added to the wishlist. Distinct for most rows (100 distinct values). Nullable.
added_at.analysis_use: Temporal analysis: trends over time, time-of-day or day-of-week patterns, windowed funnels. Useful for cohorting users by first wishlist date and measuring time-to-purchase after wishlist addition (requires joins).
business_questions: How many wishlist adds occurred per day/week/month?
Are there hourly or daily patterns in wishlist additions that correlate with campaigns?
added_at.nl2sql_hints: Use as datetime; prefer ISO format in filters (e.g., WHERE added_at >= '2025-10-01' AND added_at < '2025-11-01'). For SQL Server use CONVERT/CAST if timezone normalization is necessary. When grouping by period, use DATEPART/CONVERT or appropriate date_trunc equivalents depending on dialect. Watch for NULLs when filtering by date.

priority_flag.description: Boolean/bit indicating whether the wishlist item is marked as priority (bit). Distribution approximately 46% True, 54% False.
priority_flag.analysis_use: Filter or weight wishlist items by priority to find high-intent products/customers. Can be used in scoring or segmentation to prioritize outreach and promotions.
business_questions: What percent of wishlist items are marked as priority?
Which customers have high counts of priority wishlist items?
priority_flag.nl2sql_hints: SQL Server bit type: filter using WHERE priority_flag = 1 (True) or = 0 (False), or WHERE priority_flag IS TRUE/FALSE depending on dialect. Be explicit about Boolean semantics in NL2SQL mappings (map 'priority' to bit value 1). Handle NULLs (treat as unknown or default to False depending on business rule).

notes.description: Decimal(18,4) column named 'notes' that is fully NULL in this dataset (100% nulls). Likely a legacy or mis-typed column; semantics unclear (could be rating, score, or notes placeholder).
notes.analysis_use: Currently unusable (all NULL). If later populated, could be numeric metadata (e.g., internal score) but confirm intended meaning before use in aggregation or filters.
business_questions: If populated: what is the distribution of 'notes' values across wishlist items?
If populated: do 'notes' correlate with priority_flag or conversion?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Treat as numeric/decimal when present. Because all values are NULL, guard queries against dividing by COUNT(notes) and use COALESCE or IS NOT NULL checks. Confirm column intent with data owners before exposing in analytics; may need to be dropped or repurposed.

item_id.description: Unique identifier for each inventory item/record (primary key).
item_id.analysis_use: Exact-match filtering, point lookups, deduplication and linking when mapping a physical inventory record to other systems. Use for selecting single-record details and as grouping key when required.
business_questions: What are the details (sku, batch, stock, cost) for item INV5001?
When was a specific item added and last updated?
item_id.nl2sql_hints: Exact equality searches (WHERE item_id = 'INV5001') are most efficient. Treat as string (nvarchar) — match case-insensitively per DB collation. Avoid partial LIKE unless searching prefix patterns; prefer exact or IN lists for access.

sku.description: Stock keeping unit — product-level code mapping the inventory record to product/master data.
sku.analysis_use: Group and aggregate (SUM total_stock_units, AVG cost_per_unit) by SKU, filter to product(s), join to product/product_variants tables to enrich dimensions.
business_questions: How many units are on hand per SKU?
Which SKUs have the highest inventory value?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use exact matches, IN-lists, or GROUP BY sku. When joining to product tables, ensure matching data types/lengths (nvarchar); consider trimming/normalizing whitespace. Because sku cardinality is low (10), GROUP BY will produce small result sets.

name.description: Human-readable product name associated with the inventory item.
name.analysis_use: Label results in reports, group by name for readability, filter by partial name (LIKE) for ad-hoc queries, but prefer sku for deterministic joins.
business_questions: Show inventory breakdown by product name for reporting dashboards.
Find all items containing 'Onion' in the name that are low in stock.
name.nl2sql_hints: Name is nvarchar — use LIKE '%term%' for substring search (slower) or exact match for deterministic filters. Prefer joining on sku rather than name to avoid ambiguity and spelling variations.

total_stock_units.description: Quantity on hand for the inventory item (decimal, supports fractional units).
total_stock_units.analysis_use: Primary metric for aggregation (SUM), threshold filtering (<=, <), trend analysis (received_at vs current), and for calculating inventory value (total_stock_units * cost_per_unit). Used in ORDER BY for highest/lowest stock.
business_questions: What is the total available stock across all items or per SKU?
Which items have stock below the reorder threshold?
total_stock_units.nl2sql_hints: Use numeric comparisons and SUM() aggregations. Be mindful of decimal precision when comparing to integer thresholds — cast where necessary. For low-stock queries, filter WHERE total_stock_units < X. Avoid grouping by raw decimal when unnecessary; aggregate first.

batch_id.description: Identifier for a received production/shipment batch for traceability.
batch_id.analysis_use: Filter/group by batch for batch-level recalls, aggregations of stock per batch, join candidate to batch-level inspection/quality tables, and to track expiry/mfg relationships.
business_questions: How much stock is associated with batch BATCH6982?
Which batches are associated with recent quality inspection failures?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string for equality/IN comparisons. Because batch_id is nearly unique, GROUP BY batch_id yields many rows. Useful join key if other tables store batch_id; normalize case and trimming if necessary.

received_at.description: Timestamp when the inventory was received into stock (datetime2).
received_at.analysis_use: Filter by receipt window, compute lead times, perform time-series of receipts, and identify recent receipts. Useful for inventory age calculations (current_date - received_at).
business_questions: How much stock was received last week by supplier?
What are the receipt trends over the past month?
received_at.nl2sql_hints: Use date functions and ranges (WHERE received_at BETWEEN '2025-09-01' AND '2025-09-30', or CAST/CONVERT to date). Timezone is DB-local; use CONVERT(date, received_at) for date-only grouping. Use indexed datetime ranges for efficient scans if indexed.

expiry_date.description: Expiry date for the inventory item/batch (datetime2).
expiry_date.analysis_use: Filter for near-expiry or expired items, group by expiry buckets, compute at-risk quantity, and drive first-expiry-first-out (FEFO) rules.
business_questions: Which items expire in the next 7 days and what quantities are affected?
What is the total value of expired stock?
expiry_date.nl2sql_hints: Compare using date arithmetic (expiry_date < GETDATE() or BETWEEN). Convert to DATE (CAST(expiry_date AS date)) for bucket grouping. Handle NULL expiry_date (non-perishables) explicitly in WHERE clauses.

supplier.description: Name of the supplier who provided the inventory (categorical, 3 main suppliers).
supplier.analysis_use: Group by supplier for supplier performance and exposure, filter supplier-specific receipts, compute average cost or total units supplied per supplier.
business_questions: Which supplier supplied the most units this month?
Which supplier's batches are expiring soon?
supplier.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — GROUP BY supplier is efficient. Use equality/IN filters (WHERE supplier = 'Kumar Veg Wholesalers'). Normalize naming variations if present; consider joining to a suppliers master table if available.

cost_per_unit.description: Unit cost for the inventory item (decimal) used for valuation.
cost_per_unit.analysis_use: Multiply by total_stock_units to compute inventory value; aggregate (AVG, MIN, MAX) to analyze cost distribution; track cost changes over time with created_at/updated_at.
business_questions: What is the total inventory value by SKU?
How has average cost_per_unit changed for a SKU over time?
cost_per_unit.nl2sql_hints: Decimal arithmetic — use SUM(total_stock_units * cost_per_unit) for valuation. Watch precision/scale and rounding when aggregating; CAST if needed. Filter cost ranges (WHERE cost_per_unit BETWEEN X AND Y) for outlier detection.

mfg_date.description: Manufacturing/production date of the inventory item (datetime2).
mfg_date.analysis_use: Compute product age (received_at - mfg_date), determine shelf-life checks, filter for recent production batches, and reconcile manufacturing dates with expiry for quality analysis.
business_questions: Which batches were manufactured within the last 30 days?
What is the average age at receipt for items by supplier?
mfg_date.nl2sql_hints: Use date arithmetic and conversions (DATEDIFF, CAST to date). Handle NULLs and ensure comparisons account for time components. Use BETWEEN and DATEDIFF for age buckets.

created_at.description: Timestamp when this inventory record was created in the system.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and lineage; track ingestion velocity; filter newly created records for ETL or incremental processing and to correlate with received_at for data quality checks.
business_questions: Which inventory records were created in the last day for incremental ETL?
Are there items with created_at significantly after received_at indicating late ingestion?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Use for incremental extraction (WHERE created_at > @last_sync). Exact datetime filters and ranges with CONVERT/CAST to date for daily batching. Keep in mind created_at may differ from received_at.

updated_at.description: Last update timestamp for the inventory record.
updated_at.analysis_use: Change tracking, detect recent adjustments, and determine records that changed since last ETL or UI refresh. Useful for identifying manual stock edits or automated adjustments.
business_questions: Which inventory items were updated in the last 24 hours?
Show items where stock or cost was changed in the past week.
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Use for incremental syncs (WHERE updated_at > @last_sync). Filter ranges and use ORDER BY updated_at DESC for recent changes. Be cautious about timezone and ensure consistency with created_at/received_at semantics.

order_item_id.description: Unique identifier for the order line item (primary key).
order_item_id.analysis_use: Used as a unique row identifier. Rarely aggregated but used for de-duplication and row-level joins. Useful when retrieving individual line details.
business_questions: Retrieve details for a specific order item by ID.
Verify uniqueness and lineage for ETL debugging.
order_item_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) primary key, not null. Expect high cardinality; use direct equality when filtering (WHERE order_item_id = '...'). Avoid using LIKE for primary key. When returning single-row results use exact match predicates.

order_id.description: Identifier for the parent order (foreign key to ecom_orders.order_id).
order_id.analysis_use: Key join column to order-level table to aggregate line totals, attach customer or order-status attributes, and compute order-level KPIs. Also used to group line items into their orders.
business_questions: What is the total order value per order (sum(line_total) grouped by order_id)?
How many items are in each order (count(order_item_id) or sum(qty_units))?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50), nullable. Join to zuna_etl.ecom_orders on order_id. Prefer equality joins. Consider filters like WHERE order_id IN (...) or WHERE order_id = '...'. Nulls possible; handle using INNER JOIN if you only want items with orders, or LEFT JOIN if you need orphan items.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit — marketplace/product code identifying item family.
sku.analysis_use: Common grouping/filtering key to compute sales by product, top-sellers, price distributions, and inventory reconciliation. Low cardinality (10 distinct) — efficient GROUP BY target.
business_questions: Which SKUs generated the most revenue in the last month?
What is the average unit price and quantity sold per SKU?
sku.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50), nullable. Often used with equality or IN lists. For user queries accept SKU patterns but prefer exact matches. Map sku to product tables when joining external product metadata. Use GROUP BY sku for roll-ups.

product_name.description: Human-readable product name for the line item.
product_name.analysis_use: Label for reports and dashboards; used for grouping in user-facing outputs. Potentially redundant with SKU and variant; used when joining to product master is not available.
business_questions: Show top product names by revenue or units sold.
Display product names in order detail reports.
product_name.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50), nullable. Can contain similar values for same SKU — prefer grouping by sku/variant_id for stable results. When matching on product name, be mindful of casing or minor variations; consider using LOWER() or LIKE for fuzzy matches.

variant_id.description: Identifier for a specific variant of the product (size, packaging, etc.).
variant_id.analysis_use: Used to disambiguate SKUs with multiple variants; grouping and joins to variant-level product metadata. Good for inventory and variant-level pricing analysis.
business_questions: Which product variants sell better (units/revenue) than their siblings?
What is revenue per variant_id over time?
variant_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50), nullable. Use as grouping key or join key to variant/product tables. Treat as distinct from sku — when both exist prefer variant_id for variant-level analysis. Use equality or IN predicates.

qty_units.description: Quantity in countable units (e.g., number of packs or items).
qty_units.analysis_use: Sum or average for volume metrics, inventory impact, and unit-based KPIs. Useful for counts and per-order item quantities.
business_questions: How many units of SKU X were sold this week?
What is the average units per order item?
qty_units.nl2sql_hints: bigint, nullable. Aggregate with SUM(qty_units), AVG(qty_units) and GROUP BY sku/order_id. Handle NULLs by coalescing to 0 (e.g., COALESCE(qty_units,0)) if required. Use integer arithmetic for counts.

qty_kg.description: Quantity expressed in kilograms (decimal with 4 decimal points).
qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate for weight-based sales, pricing per kg, logistics capacity planning and per-order weight calculations.
business_questions: What is the total weight (kg) shipped per day or per batch?
What is the average price per kg for a given SKU?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4), nullable. Use SUM(qty_kg) and AVG(qty_kg). When converting between units and kg, ensure unit consistency. Coalesce NULLs to 0 when summing. Watch rounding when presenting results.

unit_price.description: Price per unit (or per kg depending on item) at the time of order.
unit_price.analysis_use: Used to compute revenue, margin (with cost joins), price analytics, promotions detection and price distribution per SKU/variant.
business_questions: What is the average unit price per SKU over the last quarter?
Identify orders/line items where unit_price deviates from expected price list.
unit_price.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4), nullable. Use arithmetic: SUM(unit_price * qty_units) or SUM(line_total) depending on consistency. Beware of NULLs. For comparisons to price master join on sku/variant_id and compare decimals with tolerance for floating rounding when necessary.

line_total.description: Total monetary value for the line (unit_price * quantity minus discounts plus taxes depending on business logic).
line_total.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric at line level. Aggregate to compute order revenue, daily revenue, revenue by SKU, etc. Used in financial KPIs and reporting.
business_questions: Total revenue by date, SKU, or order (sum(line_total)).
Which lines/orders have unusually high line_total (potential fraud or pricing errors)?
line_total.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4), nullable. Use SUM(line_total) for revenue roll-ups. Validate consistency with unit_price * qty_units (or qty_kg) when auditing. Note that discount_amount is 0 in the snapshot — adjust logic if discount column populated in other periods.

tax_amount.description: Tax charged for the line item.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liability by period, by SKU, or by order. Can be used to validate tax calculations and for tax-reporting reconciliation.
business_questions: How much tax was collected by day/region (when joined to order address)?
What is the average tax rate applied per SKU or category?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4), nullable. SUM(tax_amount) for total tax. Compute tax rate as tax_amount / NULLIF(line_total,0). Handle division by zero. Join to orders for jurisdiction if needed to attribute tax to regions.

discount_amount.description: Discount applied on the line item (currently constant 0.0 in the snapshot).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to measure discount impact on revenue; currently provides no variance but still useful to include in ETL and future queries.
business_questions: How much discount was given per SKU or order (sum(discount_amount))?
Which orders received discounts and how much did discounts reduce revenue?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4), nullable. Currently all values are 0.0 — queries filtering on discount_amount > 0 will return empty in this snapshot. Still include in SELECT calculations (line_total + discount_amount + tax_amount) depending on accounting rules. Use COALESCE(discount_amount,0) when summing.

batch_id.description: Identifier for the picking/fulfillment batch that processed this item (warehouse tracking).
batch_id.analysis_use: Group and filter for warehouse/picking performance, batch-level throughput, and linking to picking_batches or dispatch tables for SLA analysis.
business_questions: Which batches had the highest number of items or highest value shipped?
Are there batches that consistently show longer pick-to-ship times?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50), nullable, high cardinality (~214 distinct). Use equality or IN for specific batch lookups. For performance, avoid full-text searches on batch_id; treat it as an identifier. Join to picking_batches or dispatch tables on batch_id when available.

picked_at.description: Timestamp when the item was picked in the warehouse (fulfillment event).
picked_at.analysis_use: Compute fulfillment latency (picked_at - created_at), pick velocity, and daily/hourly pick volumes. Useful for SLA and operational dashboards.
business_questions: What is the average time from order creation to item picked?
How many items were picked per hour/day for capacity planning?
picked_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2, nullable. Use DATEPART/CAST/CONVERT for bucketing by day/hour. When computing intervals use DATEDIFF or subtract timestamps depending on SQL dialect. Be mindful of timezone assumptions; timestamps may be in UTC or local time — confirm source. Handle NULLs when items not yet picked.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the order item record was created (typically when the order was placed).
created_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for sales/time series analysis. Use for time-window filtering, trend analysis, cohorting and aggregations by day/week/month.
business_questions: How much revenue was booked per day/week/month?
How has the average unit price evolved over time for each SKU?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2, nullable. Use as time filter (WHERE created_at BETWEEN ...). When combining with picked_at compute time deltas. Use DATE_TRUNC/CONVERT/CAST depending on DB to bucket times. Beware of timezone normalization. For performance, queries should filter on this column when analyzing date ranges to reduce scanned rows.

summary_id.description: Surrogate/unique identifier for the daily summary row. Primary key of the table.
summary_id.analysis_use: Used to uniquely select or reference a single summary row (point lookups). Rarely used in aggregations; useful for joins when referencing this exact summary record from logs or ETL tables.
business_questions: Show me the record for summary_id = 'SUM1001' to inspect the stored aggregates.
Has the ETL produced a new summary row with summary_id X for a given date?
summary_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) primary key; use exact equality comparisons (summary_id = '...') for direct lookups. Avoid LIKE unless user asks for pattern. Not suitable as grouping key — group by store_id/date instead.

store_id.description: Identifier for the POS store the summary applies to (logical store key).
store_id.analysis_use: Common filter and group-by field for cross-store comparisons, ranking, and per-store time series. Join key to pos_stores for metadata (name, region) or to transactional tables for reconciliation.
business_questions: What are total_sales per store for the last week?
Which store had the highest avg_txn_value yesterday?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with low cardinality. NL2SQL should map synonyms like 'store', 'location' to store_id. When grouping or filtering, prefer equality or IN lists. For joins, use store_id = pos_stores.store_id. Consider normalizing case/prefix differences if natural language refers to 'AHD' vs 'STORE_AHD_001'.

date.description: The date (day) that the summary covers. Stored as datetime2 but semantically a date (midnight times observed).
date.analysis_use: Primary temporal dimension for time series, filtering by ranges, grouping by day/week/month/weekday. Often needs casting/truncation to date for grouping or range checks.
business_questions: Show total_sales by day for the last 30 days.
Compare weekend vs weekday total_txns for a given month.
date.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 — treat as DATE for most NL2SQL date-range requests (use CAST(date as date) or CONVERT(date,...)). Translate phrases like 'last week', 'month-to-date' to appropriate date filters. Be explicit about inclusive/exclusive ranges. Use date_trunc or DATEPART equivalents when user requests monthly/weekly aggregates.

total_txns.description: Total number of transactions (stored as decimal) for the day/store summary. Represents transaction count.
total_txns.analysis_use: Aggregation and trend metrics (SUM, AVG), denominator for per-transaction metrics and for computing average transaction values if recomputing, and used for anomaly detection.
business_questions: How many transactions did store STORE_AHD_001 have yesterday?
What is the trend of total_txns over the last 7 days for each store?
total_txns.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal(18,4) — treat as numeric/integer when used as counts (CAST/ROUND if necessary). When NL refers to 'num transactions' or 'transaction count', map to total_txns. Guard against division by zero when computing avg (total_sales/total_txns).

total_sales.description: Total gross sales amount for the day and store (monetary value).
total_sales.analysis_use: Primary KPI for revenue reporting, aggregations (SUM), averages, comparisons, and as numerator for ratios (refund rate, tax rate). Used in top-N queries and trend analyses.
business_questions: Which days had the highest total_sales in November?
What are total_sales per store for the previous month?
total_sales.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Map natural language terms 'sales', 'revenue', 'gross sales' to this column. Use SUM(total_sales) for range queries; ensure currency formatting happens at presentation layer. Watch for NULLs — coalesce to 0 in aggregations (COALESCE(total_sales,0)).

total_tax.description: Total tax amount collected for the day/store.
total_tax.analysis_use: Used to compute tax rates (total_tax / total_sales), tax trends, and to reconcile tax reporting. Aggregate by period or store for tax remittance calculations.
business_questions: What is the tax collected by each store last week?
What percent of sales was tax on average last month?
total_tax.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Map 'tax', 'VAT', 'GST' in NL to total_tax if context matches. Use COALESCE(total_tax,0) in calculations and guard against division by zero when computing ratios.

total_refunds.description: Total amount refunded to customers for the day/store.
total_refunds.analysis_use: Compute refund rate (total_refunds / total_sales or per transaction), trend refunds over time, and reconcile refund-related metrics against returns/returns_lines tables.
business_questions: How much was refunded across all stores last month?
What is the refund rate for STORE_RJT_001 in the last 30 days?
total_refunds.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Map words like 'refunds', 'returns value' to this column. Use COALESCE(total_refunds,0) and ensure ratio denominators are non-zero. For deeper refund drill-downs join with returns/pos_returns tables.

avg_txn_value.description: Precomputed average transaction value for the day/store (probably total_sales / total_txns). Stored for convenience/fast read.
avg_txn_value.analysis_use: Used as a KPI for ticket size, to trend changes in customer spend, and to compare across stores or days. Can be validated or recomputed from total_sales/total_txns.
business_questions: What is the average transaction value across stores this week?
Did avg_txn_value increase after the promotion last Saturday?
avg_txn_value.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). NL2SQL should allow either using stored avg_txn_value or deriving it as total_sales / NULLIF(total_txns,0). Be explicit when user asks to 'recompute' or 'validate' average values. Use COALESCE and guard against divisions by zero.

created_at.description: ETL insertion or summary creation timestamp (when this summary row was written).
created_at.analysis_use: Used to validate ETL freshness, audit when summaries were created, and to detect late-arriving data. Can be filtered to find recently inserted summaries.
business_questions: When was the daily summary for 2025-11-22 created?
Show me summaries created in the last 24 hours (to check ETL runs).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2. Map NL phrases like 'created', 'generated', 'ETL run time' to created_at. Use range filters on created_at; consider timezone awareness if user refers to 'local time'. For 'latest' use ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 1.

notes.description: Free-text notes for the summary row. In this dataset it is constant ('Daily summary').
notes.analysis_use: Typically low analytical value here; can be used for metadata filtering if different note values are populated (e.g., 'recalc', 'adjusted'). For current data it is mostly informational.
business_questions: Show rows annotated as 'Daily summary' (or any other note values).
Are there any summaries marked with special notes (e.g., 'adjusted')?
notes.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Treat as optional filterable text. NLP should handle exact or LIKE matches when user asks for notes content. Expect low cardinality — consider exact equality checks first.

adjustment_id.description: Surrogate unique identifier for the inventory adjustment event (primary key).
adjustment_id.analysis_use: Used as a unique row identifier; useful for retrieving single events, deduplication, and joins when adjustments need to be referenced. Not typically aggregated.
business_questions: Show the details of adjustment PAD3002.
Is there more than one record with the same adjustment_id (duplication check)?
adjustment_id.nl2sql_hints: String (nvarchar). Exact-match filters should use equality. Treat as primary key — prefer WHERE adjustment_id = '...' for single-row retrievals. Avoid using LIKE on this column in typical queries.

pos_sku.description: Point-of-sale SKU identifier for the product adjusted.
pos_sku.analysis_use: Key grouping/filtering dimension for SKU-level aggregates (sum qty_change, count adjustments). Join key to product/master SKU tables for product metadata and pricing.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest total shrinkage (sum of negative qty_change)?
How many adjustments have been made for SKU APP009_001 in the last month?
pos_sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; may need exact matches. When joining to product tables ensure type alignment (cast if needed). Use GROUP BY pos_sku for SKU aggregates. Top-N filters by frequency should use ORDER BY SUM(qty_change) or COUNT(*) and LIMIT.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store where the adjustment occurred.
store_id.analysis_use: Filter and group by store for regional/store-level metrics (total adjustments, shrinkage by store). Useful join key to store/master store tables.
business_questions: Which store has the highest number of shrinkage adjustments?
What is the total qty_change for STORE_RJT_001 over the last 30 days?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar field; join predicates should ensure matching types. Common patterns: GROUP BY store_id, WHERE store_id = 'STORE_RJT_001'. Consider composite filters with pos_sku and adjustment_ts for per-store per-SKU analyses.

adjustment_ts.description: Timestamp of when the adjustment occurred as recorded (stored as nvarchar formatted like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
adjustment_ts.analysis_use: Temporal filtering and grouping (by day/hour/week) after casting to datetime. Sequence analyses (order of adjustments) and rate calculations require converting to proper datetime.
business_questions: How many adjustments occurred per day in the last month?
At what hours do most adjustments occur (hourly distribution)?
adjustment_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — convert to datetime (e.g., TRY_CONVERT(datetime, adjustment_ts, 120) or CAST when format is consistent). Use the casted value in WHERE and GROUP BY for time-based queries. Watch for malformed strings; use TRY_ conversions to avoid failures. If many queries use this column, consider storing a proper datetime column or a persisted computed column.

qty_change.description: Net change in inventory quantity caused by the adjustment; can be negative (removal) or positive (addition).
qty_change.analysis_use: Primary numeric metric to sum/avg/median per SKU/store/reason. Identify shrinkage (negative sums), large single adjustments, and compute derived fields (expected_after = inventory_before + qty_change).
business_questions: What is the total net qty_change by reason for the last quarter?
Which individual adjustments had the largest absolute qty_change?
qty_change.nl2sql_hints: Stored as bigint — aggregate with SUM(qty_change), AVG(qty_change), and use ABS(qty_change) for magnitude queries. When validating inventory consistency, check inventory_before + qty_change = inventory_after. Beware of nulls: treat NULL as missing; use COALESCE(qty_change,0) where appropriate.

reason.description: Categorized reason for the adjustment (e.g., Shrinkage, Stock Count Correction, Damage).
reason.analysis_use: Group and filter adjustments by reason to analyze root causes, frequency and volume per reason. Use in pivoting or percentages calculations.
business_questions: What proportion of inventory variance is labeled as Shrinkage vs Damage?
How does average qty_change differ by reason?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality categorical nvarchar column — good for GROUP BY and CASE statements. Normalize/canonicalize values if joining to a reasons master table. Use string equality; consider trimming/collation issues in comparisons.

adjusted_by.description: User identifier for the person who made the adjustment.
adjusted_by.analysis_use: Identify frequent adjusters, compute per-user adjustment volumes and counts, surface suspicious behavior, and join to user directory for names/roles.
business_questions: Which users create the most adjustments and what are their average adjustment sizes?
Are there users who consistently make large negative adjustments?
adjusted_by.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar; join to pos_users (or equivalent) on user id. When evaluating suspicious patterns, combine with approval_by, adjustment_ts and qty_change thresholds. Use GROUP BY adjusted_by for counts/sums.

approval_by.description: User identifier for the approver of the adjustment.
approval_by.analysis_use: Used to check approval patterns, identify who approves large or frequent adjustments, and measure segregation of duties (same person adjusting and approving).
business_questions: Which approvers sign off on the highest value or volume of adjustments?
How often is the adjuster also the approver (segregation of duties violations)?
approval_by.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar; joinable to user directory. For segregation checks use queries like WHERE adjusted_by = approval_by. Consider NULL approvals — treat as unapproved when performing approval-rate calculations.

inventory_before.description: Recorded on-hand inventory count for the SKU at the store immediately before the adjustment.
inventory_before.analysis_use: Validate adjustments (inventory_before + qty_change => inventory_after), compute risk (low before counts), and calculate percent change. Useful for detecting negative before counts or impossible states.
business_questions: How many adjustments occurred when inventory_before was below a safety threshold (e.g., < 10)?
Are there adjustments that result in negative inventory_before or inventory_after?
inventory_before.nl2sql_hints: bigint numeric. Use in arithmetic expressions and validations. Watch for NULLs. Prefer queries that check inventory_before + qty_change = inventory_after for integrity; use COALESCE to guard nulls.

inventory_after.description: Recorded on-hand inventory count for the SKU at the store immediately after the adjustment.
inventory_after.analysis_use: Final state used for reconciliation with expected inventory. Compare to inventory_before and qty_change to validate correctness and detect anomalies.
business_questions: List adjustments where inventory_after does not equal inventory_before + qty_change.
What are the final on-hand counts for SKUs after adjustments on a given day?
inventory_after.nl2sql_hints: bigint numeric. Use in consistency checks and final-state reporting. When joining to product_inventory, ensure timeframe alignment (inventory snapshots vs point adjustments).

created_at.description: System timestamp when the adjustment record was created in the ETL/destination (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ETL lineage (ingestion time). Use for identifying recently loaded rows, latency calculations between adjustment_ts and created_at, and incremental extraction (WHERE created_at > last_run).
business_questions: What is the delay between the adjustment event (adjustment_ts) and when it was created in the system (created_at)?
Which adjustments were created in the last 24 hours?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Proper datetime2 type — use directly in WHERE/GROUP BY. For performance in incremental extracts use an index on created_at. When comparing with adjustment_ts (which is nvarchar), CAST/TRY_CONVERT adjustment_ts to datetime before computing differences.

redemption_id.description: Unique identifier for each loyalty redemption record (primary key).
redemption_id.analysis_use: Used as the unique identifier for de-duplication and linking individual redemption events. Not typically aggregated; used in COUNT DISTINCT or to identify specific records.
business_questions: How many unique redemptions occurred in a period?
Can I retrieve details for a specific redemption by its id?
redemption_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) but actual max len 6; treat as string. Use equality or IN predicates for lookup. Because it's PK and unique, expect single-row results. Avoid range filters; use exact matches. If generating WHERE redemption_id = 'LR4000', ensure quotes and exact casing if DB is case-sensitive.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who redeemed loyalty points; links to customer master data.
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filtering dimension for customer-level analyses, frequency, lifetime value of redemptions, joining to customer tables for segmentation.
business_questions: Which customers redeem the most points or get the highest total discount?
How many customers redeemed loyalty points in a given period?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 8). Often used in GROUP BY and JOINs to customer tables. Expect NULLs; handle with IS NULL or explicit exclusion. Use equality, IN (small lists), or GROUP BY for aggregation (COUNT, SUM). Consider join to ecom_customers/b2b_customers on customer_id.

pos_txn_id.description: POS transaction identifier associated with the redemption; ties the redemption to a specific sale transaction.
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Join key to POS transaction and transaction line tables to evaluate revenue offset, determine basket context, and compute percent-of-transactions using loyalty.
business_questions: Which transactions included a loyalty redemption and what was the sale total for those transactions?
What proportion of POS transactions used loyalty redemptions?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 9). Join to pos_transactions or pos_transaction_lines on pos_txn_id. Use LEFT JOIN if some transactions may be missing in POS schema. Use EXISTS or INNER JOIN when identifying transactions with redemptions. Handle NULLs if redemption not tied to a transaction.

loyalty_points_used.description: Number of loyalty points consumed in the redemption event.
loyalty_points_used.analysis_use: Numeric metric for aggregation (SUM, AVG, MAX), filtering (e.g., redemptions over X points), and conversion to monetary value when combined with discount_amount.
business_questions: What is the total number of loyalty points redeemed over a period?
What is the average and median points used per redemption?
loyalty_points_used.nl2sql_hints: bigint nullable. Use SUM(loyalty_points_used), AVG, MAX, and GROUP BY. When converting points to value, join or compute points-to-currency rate via discount_amount/loyalty_points_used (avoid division by zero/NULL). Use WHERE loyalty_points_used IS NOT NULL for numeric aggregations if desired.

discount_amount.description: Monetary discount applied due to loyalty redemption (decimal with 4 fractional digits).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Primary financial metric: aggregate monetary impact (SUM), average discount per redemption, per-customer/store totals, and correlation with points used.
business_questions: What is the total monetary impact (sum of discount amount) of loyalty redemptions over time?
What is the average discount amount per redemption and per store?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) nullable. Use SUM(discount_amount), AVG(discount_amount). Be cautious about NULLs; use COALESCE(discount_amount,0) when summing if semantics require. For conversions or ratios (discount/points), guard against NULLs and zero points. When formatting results, round to currency precision.

redeemed_at.description: Timestamp when the loyalty points were redeemed (datetime2).
redeemed_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for temporal analyses: trends, windowing (daily/weekly/monthly), peak-hours analysis, and time-based filters.
business_questions: How do redemptions trend by day, hour, or month?
What are peak redemption hours and are there store-level time patterns?
redeemed_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 nullable. Use date functions (CAST or CONVERT to date, DATEPART, FORMAT depending on dialect) for grouping by day/month/hour. Use range predicates for efficient filtering (redeemed_at >= '2025-11-01' AND redeemed_at < '2025-12-01'). Consider indexing/partitioning on redeemed_at for larger datasets. Beware of timezone considerations — ensure redeemed_at is in consistent timezone.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store/location where the redemption occurred.
store_id.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering dimension for store-level KPIs, comparisons, and joins to store metadata (pos_stores) for region or operational analysis.
business_questions: Which stores have the highest number of redemptions and highest total discount amounts?
What is the average discount per redemption by store?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 13) with low cardinality (4 values). Ideal for GROUP BY and WHERE filters. Join to pos_stores on store_id to get store attributes. Because of low cardinality, queries grouped by store will be efficient; ensure joins use the same datatype and normalization (e.g., trimming whitespace).

created_by.description: Identifier for the user or process that created the redemption record (likely POS user).
created_by.analysis_use: Used for audit and operational queries: which operators created redemptions, error investigation, and operator-level activity metrics.
business_questions: Which POS users create the most loyalty redemptions?
Are there user-level anomalies (e.g., operators approving large discounts)?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 6) with very low cardinality. Use GROUP BY created_by for counts. Treat as string in WHERE clauses. If linking to user metadata, join to pos_users on created_by. Consider case sensitivity and trimming when joining.

approval_id.description: Identifier for an approval associated with the redemption (audit/manager approval reference).
approval_id.analysis_use: Audit dimension for filtering redemptions that required approval and investigating approval patterns; can be used to join to approval workflow tables if present.
business_questions: Which redemptions required approval and are there patterns in approved amounts?
Are there approvals linked to specific approvers or types that correlate with high discounts?
approval_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 6). Mostly unique per record (49 distinct). Use existence checks (approval_id IS NOT NULL) to identify approved redemptions. Join to approval/audit tables when present. Because values are mostly unique, grouping by approval_id is less useful unless aggregating approval metadata.

notes.description: Free-text notes about the redemption; appears constant ('Loyalty redemption') in current snapshot.
notes.analysis_use: Minimal analytical value in current data because it's constant; can be used for human-readable audit or to filter by specific note values if future data varies.
business_questions: Are there special notes or flags associated with redemptions requiring follow-up?
Do specific note values correspond to exceptions or manual adjustments?
notes.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 18) but currently constant. Use equality filters only if non-constant values appear. Treat as nullable string. Avoid heavy reliance on this column for grouping given its low variability.

date.description: The date/time the aggregated metrics relate to. Stored as datetime2 (no timezone). In practice this appears to be one row per calendar date (time portion zeroed).
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for filtering, grouping and ordering. Used to aggregate to week/month/quarter, calculate moving averages, detect missing dates, and join with other date-keyed tables.
business_questions: What were net sales for a given date or date range?
How do sales vary by day of week / month?
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a DATE for grouping (use CAST(date AS DATE) / CONVERT(DATE, date) / DATE_TRUNC depending on SQL dialect). Be cautious of datetime component — use truncation when grouping by calendar dates. Use date range filters (date >= X AND date < Y) rather than applying functions to column for better index usage. If asking for time buckets, specify desired granularity (day/week/month) explicitly.

product_id.description: Identifier of the product for which metrics are recorded (nvarchar(50)). Currently only one distinct value (TOM001). Nullable in DDL but populated in data.
product_id.analysis_use: Filter or group by product. Join key to product master tables for descriptive attributes (name, category, vendor). When only one product exists, queries can omit filter but should still support multi-product patterns.
business_questions: What are the daily sales for product TOM001?
How do product-level sales compare across products (if table populated for many products)?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Use exact equality filters (product_id = 'TOM001') or IN lists. Be aware it's nvarchar(50) — trimming or collations may matter. If product_id is nullable, use WHERE product_id IS NOT NULL when joining to master tables. For future scalability, expect joins on product_id to pos_products.product_id or products.product_id; recommend advising engine to prefer equality filters and to suggest normalization to a shorter type when possible.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold for that product on the date, measured in kilograms (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM), averages, rate calculations (price per kg = net_sales / total_qty_kg), trend analysis, and correlation with sales amounts. Filter for thresholds (e.g., days with zero or very low volume).
business_questions: What is the total quantity (kg) sold for a given period?
What was the average kg sold per day and which days had unusually high/low kg sold?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Be cautious dividing by total_qty_kg — handle zero or NULL with CASE or NULLIF to avoid division by zero (e.g., net_sales / NULLIF(total_qty_kg, 0)). Use SUM(total_qty_kg) for periods. Use COALESCE(total_qty_kg, 0) when you want to treat missing as zero.

gross_sales.description: Gross monetary sales amount for that product on the date before discounts and taxes (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) to compute total gross revenue; used with discount_amount and tax_amount to reconcile net_sales. Useful for margin and revenue mix calculations. Filter for large gross sales days.
business_questions: What is total gross revenue for this product over a selected month?
Which days had the highest gross sales and how do they correlate with promotions?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Monetary values are decimal(18,4) — maintain precision in aggregates. When comparing values across tables, ensure currencies and scales align. Use COALESCE(gross_sales, 0) for aggregations if NULLs should be treated as zero. If asking for percent contributions, compute SUM(gross_sales) over the desired window and divide by the period total.

discount_amount.description: Total discount applied to sales for that product on the date (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to understand promotion impact, compute discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales), and identify days with higher-than-normal discounting. Use to reconcile gross to net sales and to measure ROI of promotions.
business_questions: How much discount was given for this product last month in total and as a percent of gross sales?
Which dates had the largest discount amounts, and did those dates have higher net sales afterwards?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: When computing rates, guard against division by zero (use NULLIF(gross_sales, 0)). Use SUM(discount_amount) for period totals. Remember discount_amount can be NULL — use COALESCE for summation or comparisons.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for that product on the date (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax collected, compute tax rate relative to gross or net sales, and reconcile with fiscal reports. Useful when splitting revenue by tax status or region (when joined with product/location metadata).
business_questions: How much tax was collected for this product over a given quarter?
What is the effective tax rate (tax_amount / gross_sales) over time?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Treat as decimal(18,4) and handle NULLs with COALESCE(tax_amount, 0). When computing percentages, use SUM(tax_amount) / NULLIF(SUM(gross_sales), 0). If queries require tax breakdown by jurisdiction, join on product/location metadata as this table does not include tax jurisdiction.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount for that product on the date after discounts and including taxes (decimal(18,4)). Represents the final revenue recognized in this ETL table.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary KPI for revenue trend analysis (SUM, AVG), used to compute price per kg (net_sales / total_qty_kg), rolling averages, and to compare against gross_sales to validate discounts/tax reconciliation.
business_questions: What are daily net sales and how have they changed over the last 30 days?
What is the net revenue per kg and how does it vary across promotional periods?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Use SUM(net_sales) for period reporting; ensure precision when aggregating decimal(18,4). Validate relations: often net_sales ≈ gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount (verify on data). Use COALESCE(net_sales, 0) when aggregating. For NL2SQL, prefer explicit arithmetic expressions and guard clauses (e.g., NULLIF) when calculating derived metrics like price per kg.

pos_sku.description: Point-of-sale specific SKU code (POS-level item variant identifier). Often includes suffixes (e.g., size/pack) and is distinct per POS item.
pos_sku.analysis_use: Used for joins to POS-specific transactions, aggregations of POS sales, filtering and grouping POS-level metrics; can be used to display POS item labels. Good for deduping POS-level data.
business_questions: Which pos_sku items sold the most in POS transaction lines?
Are there POS items with multiple pos_sku variants mapping to the same master sku?
pos_sku.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Often used as a join key to pos_transaction_lines.pos_sku; watch for exact string matching and trailing whitespace. Because values are short (max len 10 observed), use equality filters for performance. Consider normalizing case if queries are case-sensitive.

sku.description: Canonical SKU (business product identifier). Declared NOT NULL and acts as the logical primary key in this extract.
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping key for product-level analysis, joins to master products, product_variants, pricing and sales tables. Aggregations (SUM, AVG) typically grouped by sku.
business_questions: What is the total stock_qty per sku?
Which SKUs require reorder based on reorder_point?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) NOT NULL. Use as the primary join column to products, product_pricing, product_inventory and sales tables. Should be treated as unique in this table; equality joins are most common. If multiple tables contain sku, prefer joining on sku rather than pos_sku for canonical results.

store_id.description: Store identifier for the POS extract (store location code). In this data extract there is only one distinct value.
store_id.analysis_use: Filter to a specific store; useful when comparing multiple stores (not applicable here since single store). When joining multi-store datasets, use to partition queries or to aggregate by store.
business_questions: Which products are stocked at STORE_AHD_001?
How does stock at STORE_AHD_001 compare to other stores (after joining multi-store inventory)?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Single distinct value in this table—be careful when writing NL2SQL that assumes multiple stores. When composing cross-store queries, ensure correct join/filter on store_id exists in the other table.

plucode.description: Numeric PLU code often used by scale or POS devices to identify produce/weighed items.
plucode.analysis_use: Filtering or joining to other systems that use PLU codes (scales, legacy POS). Can be aggregated or used to detect duplicates between PLU and sku mappings.
business_questions: Which SKUs share the same PLU code?
What is the inventory for PLU 4011?
plucode.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). When writing NL2SQL, compare numeric equality or cast if input is provided as string. Watch for trailing decimals (e.g., 4011.0000).

name.description: Human-readable product name as presented in POS (may include pack/weight info).
name.analysis_use: Used for reports, filters by product description, grouping for display and fuzzy matching to master product names. Useful in NL2SQL when users reference product names instead of SKUs.
business_questions: Show me all products with 'Apple' in the name and their stock quantities.
What are the names and prices for SKUs that are out of stock?
name.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Natural language queries often reference parts of name; use LIKE or CONTAINS. Be cautious with special characters and case sensitivity. Prefer allowing substring matches for user input.

uom.description: Unit of measure or pack-size for the POS item (e.g., kg, 250g, 500g).
uom.analysis_use: Filter/group by package size for promotion analysis or inventory units normalization. Useful when converting quantities to a standard base unit for aggregation.
business_questions: How much stock do we have in 'kg' UOM items vs packaged items?
List products sold as 250g packs and their prices.
uom.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) with low cardinality. Use equality filters; consider normalizing values (e.g., 'kg' vs 'Kg') in NL2SQL. If unit conversion is needed, join to a unit-conversion table or handle conversions in query logic.

pos_price.description: Current POS selling price for the item (per POS UOM).
pos_price.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute price lists, price distribution, average price, or compare with other channels. Used in revenue calculations (when multiplied by sold quantity after joining sales).
business_questions: What are the top 5 most expensive POS items?
What is the average pos_price for vegetables (filter by name pattern)?
pos_price.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Watch for NULLs. When computing revenue, join to sales to multiply price by quantity at the time of sale or use transaction-level price if captured there. For range queries, ensure numeric comparisons use proper casting if input is string.

tax_rate.description: Tax rate applied at POS for the item (decimal). In this extract it is constant at 0.05.
tax_rate.analysis_use: Calculate tax liabilities on sales or verify correct tax application across products. Low variance means limited analytical discrimination in this table alone.
business_questions: Which items have a non-standard tax rate?
What is the total tax owed if all stock_qty items are sold at pos_price?
tax_rate.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Often constant—watch for NULLs or default values. Use numeric arithmetic for tax computations. If joining to tax tables, use sku or product category as join keys since tax_rate per item may be duplicated.

stock_qty.description: Current POS stock quantity for the item (in the unit indicated by uom).
stock_qty.analysis_use: Primary column for inventory analyses: aggregate totals, detect out-of-stock, compute days-of-supply when combined with sales velocity, and identify reorder candidates (stock_qty < reorder_point).
business_questions: Which products are out of stock (stock_qty = 0)?
Which SKUs have stock_qty below the reorder_point?
stock_qty.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Be careful with NULLs; use COALESCE to treat NULL as 0 if appropriate. For comparisons to reorder_point use numeric comparisons. If combining with sales rates, ensure UOM alignment before computation.

reorder_point.description: Threshold quantity that triggers reorder for the POS item.
reorder_point.analysis_use: Flagging items for replenishment (stock_qty < reorder_point), calculating safety stock gaps and prioritizing purchase orders.
business_questions: Which SKUs need replenishment because stock_qty is below reorder_point?
How many units must be ordered to reach reorder_point for each SKU?
reorder_point.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use in numeric comparisons. Handle NULL reorder_point (treat as no reorder rule) by checking IS NOT NULL. When generating reorder quantities, compute reorder_point - stock_qty and cap at zero.

active_flag.description: Indicator whether the POS product is active. In this extract all values are NULL (no active/deactivated state provided).
active_flag.analysis_use: When populated, used to filter active products for sales and reporting. Currently not useful due to 100% NULLs—may indicate missing upstream data.
business_questions: Which POS products are marked inactive and should be excluded from promotions?
How many active SKUs are currently listed at POS?
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Type: bit. Because values are currently NULL, NL2SQL should not assume false/true default—explicitly handle NULLs (IS TRUE / IS FALSE / IS NULL). If the UI asks for 'active products', translate to WHERE active_flag = 1 OR active_flag IS NULL depending on business rules.

updated_at.description: Timestamp of the last update for the POS product record (price/stock/name changes).
updated_at.analysis_use: Filter or order by recency (recently changed items), detect stale records, incremental ETL watermarking and audit queries to find when data was last refreshed.
business_questions: Which SKUs were updated in the last 7 days?
What items had price changes in the last month (use updated_at + joins to history if available)?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2. Use range filters (BETWEEN, >=) for recency queries. Consider timezone normalization if source systems vary. When building NL2SQL for 'recent' queries, map natural language intervals (today, last week) to appropriate datetime arithmetic.

sku.description: Product identifier (SKU) used to attribute sales metrics to specific products. Declared nvarchar(50) but observed max length is 6 and all values are distinct in this sample.
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping key for aggregates (SUM, AVG) and top-N queries; used for joins to product master or inventory tables; used for filtering specific products or sets of products. Because it is textual, exact-match filters and IN lists are common.
business_questions: What are the top 10 SKUs by net sales?
Show sales metrics for a given SKU or list of SKUs (e.g., 'APP009').
How many SKUs contribute to X% of total POS net sales?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (nvarchar). Use exact-match or IN for filtering; beware of collation/case-sensitivity and leading/trailing spaces—use TRIM/UPPER if necessary. If the metadata indicates this is a primary key but the DDL lacks a constraint, queries can still rely on uniqueness in practice but consider validating uniqueness. When joining to other tables use sku and ensure types match (varchar vs nvarchar) or cast appropriately.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales amount for the SKU before discounts and (apparently) before removing discounts. Decimal(18,4) for monetary precision.
gross_sales.analysis_use: Commonly aggregated (SUM(gross_sales)) to compute total revenue; used to compute ranking, share of gross sales, and per-SKU averages. Used as denominator when computing discount rate or contribution percentages. Can be filtered by ranges to find high/low grossing SKUs.
business_questions: Which SKUs generate the most gross revenue at POS?
What is the total gross sales across all SKUs for a report?
Which SKUs have gross sales above a threshold (e.g., > 1,000,000)?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal(18,4) — use SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN. Watch for NULLs: use COALESCE(gross_sales,0) when aggregating. Use appropriate rounding/formatting in results. When writing NL2SQL, interpret comparative phrases ('greater than 1M') as numeric filters and convert currency tokens to numeric literals. Avoid implicit casts; compare decimals to decimals.

discount_amount.description: Total discount amount applied to the SKU at POS. Decimal(18,4).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregated to find total discount exposure per SKU or overall; combined with gross_sales to compute discount_rate = discount_amount / gross_sales; used to analyze promotional impacts and margin erosion. Filterable to identify SKUs with large absolute discounts.
business_questions: Which SKUs received the largest discounts in absolute terms?
What is the discount percentage for SKU X (discount_amount / gross_sales)?
Which products have discount amounts that exceed a threshold or are unusually high relative to gross sales?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type — use SUM, division for percentages. Guard against division by zero and NULLs (COALESCE(gross_sales,0) and handle zero). Natural language asking for 'discount rate' should be translated to (discount_amount / gross_sales) * 100 with rounding. Consider that discounts may be negative or positive depending on business (validate sign).

tax_amount.description: Taxes collected for the SKU. Decimal(18,4). In the sample it appears stored separately and not included in net_sales calculation.
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregated to compute total tax liability per SKU or overall, calculate effective tax rate relative to gross or net; used for compliance reporting and reconciliation against tax remittances. Filterable to find SKUs with unusually high tax amounts.
business_questions: How much tax was collected per SKU and in total?
What is the effective tax as a percentage of gross sales for SKU Y?
Which SKUs contribute most to the POS tax liability?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) — aggregate with SUM. Check whether tax is included in net_sales externally (sample shows net = gross - discount) — if NL asks for 'net after tax' verify definition. Handle NULLs with COALESCE. When users ask 'tax rate', translate to tax_amount / gross_sales or tax_amount / net_sales depending on definition; clarify if tax is included in prices.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount for the SKU after discounts (observed = gross_sales - discount_amount). Decimal(18,4).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary measure for revenue-based rankings and aggregation (SUM(net_sales)). Used to compute contribution to total revenue, growth, and performance metrics. Often used in ordering/top-N and percentage-share calculations. Validate that net_sales equals gross - discount in production data or treat as authoritative if guaranteed.
