business_questions: Which SKUs contribute most to net revenue?
What is total POS net sales across all SKUs?
How much did net sales change if compared against previous snapshots (when joined to time-series tables)?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) — use SUM, ORDER BY SUM(net_sales) DESC for top-N. If natural language asks for 'net sales' ensure whether tax should be included or not; sample suggests tax is excluded. Use COALESCE(net_sales,0) for safe aggregation. If data model guarantees net = gross - discount, consider checking consistency: add a derived check WHERE ABS(net_sales - (gross_sales - discount_amount)) > tolerance to detect anomalies. Avoid double-counting when joining with transactional tables—ensure aggregation levels align (SKU-level vs transaction-level).

pos_return_id.description: Unique identifier for the POS return event/line (string). In the sample each value appears distinct for each row.
pos_return_id.analysis_use: Deduplication, identifying a single return record, grouping for counts per return event, traceability for auditing and joins when other systems reference the same ID.
business_questions: How many unique return records were created in a given period?
Are there duplicate pos_return_id values indicating ETL issues or retransmissions?
pos_return_id.nl2sql_hints: Treated as nvarchar; do not assume PK constraints exist — use DISTINCT or GROUP BY for uniqueness checks. If joining to other systems, match exactly on this string. Watch for leading/trailing spaces; use TRIM in generated SQL when necessary.

pos_txn_id.description: Identifier of the original POS transaction associated with the return (string).
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Join key to pos_transactions (and transaction_lines) to fetch sale amount, customer, store, and timestamp. Use for grouping returns by sale or calculating return rate per transaction.
business_questions: Which sales transactions have associated returns and what is the refund amount per transaction?
What proportion of transactions result in returns for a given period or store?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar; use exact-match joins to pos_transactions.pos_txn_id. Allow for nulls; use LEFT JOIN when selecting transactions with/without returns. Normalize case/trim if upstream systems differ.

return_ts.description: Timestamp of the return event as stored by the POS system, currently as nvarchar (text).
return_ts.analysis_use: Primary event time for return occurrence — use for fine-grained timeline analysis after casting/parsing to datetime. Useful for inferring business hour patterns or return latency when compared to sale time.
business_questions: What time-of-day do returns most frequently occur?
How long after the original sale do returns typically occur (when joined to sale timestamp)?
return_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar(50) — always CAST/TRY_CAST or parse to datetime in NL2SQL queries (e.g., TRY_CONVERT(datetime2, return_ts, 120)). Provide default fallback to created_at if parsing fails. Accept common query patterns: 'between YYYY-MM-DD and YYYY-MM-DD', 'last 7 days', 'group by date(return_ts)'.

sku.description: Stock keeping unit code of the returned product (string).
sku.analysis_use: Group and aggregate returns by product. Join to products/pos_products to enrich with product name, category, price, and inventory attributes. Filter to inspect performance of particular SKUs.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest return counts and associated refund amounts?
Are particular SKUs returning for specific reasons more often?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to product tables (pos_products, products). String matching should be exact; consider normalizing case. Provide convenience synonyms in NL2SQL parser (e.g., 'item', 'product', 'SKU'). Limit result sets with 'top N' or ORDER BY SUM(refund_amount) DESC for common queries.

qty.description: Quantity of units returned for this line (decimal but represents integer counts in practice).
qty.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to measure returned unit volume, average qty per return, and calculate per-unit refund when combined with refund_amount. Useful to compute inventory impact.
business_questions: How many units were returned in total by SKU or time period?
What is the average quantity returned per return event?
qty.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal(18,4) but typically integer values — use SUM(qty) and CAST to integer if needed for display. Watch for decimal formatting in user queries; use ROUND or CAST when presenting counts.

reason.description: Categorical reason code for the return (e.g., Wrong Item, Customer Return, Damaged).
reason.analysis_use: Group by reason to understand drivers of returns. Filter to analyze specific cause-related trends and to inform quality/fulfillment improvements.
business_questions: What percentage of returns are due to 'Damaged' vs 'Wrong Item'?
Which SKUs are most often returned for a specific reason?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Categorical text; treat as low-cardinality dimension. Support synonyms in NL2SQL (e.g., 'damaged', 'wrong item', 'customer return') and map to canonical values if needed. Use GROUP BY reason and COUNT or SUM(refund_amount).

refund_amount.description: Monetary amount refunded for this return line (decimal).
refund_amount.analysis_use: Primary financial metric: SUM(refund_amount) to compute total refunds, AVG for average refund, and SUM grouped by SKU/reason/operator/time for financial reporting and reconciliation.
business_questions: What is the total refunded amount by store/operator/SKU over a period?
What is the average refund per return and per SKU?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) — use SUM(refund_amount) and proper rounding/formatting in results (e.g., 2 decimal places). When joining to invoices/payments, ensure currency alignment. Watch for nulls; treat null refund_amount as 0 or exclude depending on business logic.

processed_by.description: Identifier of the POS user/operator who processed the return (string).
processed_by.analysis_use: Assess staff-level metrics: number of returns processed, refund amounts handled, and potential fraud/abuse detection. Join to pos_users or HR tables to get readable names and roles.
business_questions: Which operators process the most returns or highest total refunds?
Are return patterns clustered around specific operators (training or fraud signals)?
processed_by.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to pos_users (or staff) tables. Handle nulls and use LEFT JOINs for exposure of unlinked records. Normalize format if other tables use different ID schemes (e.g., case sensitivity).

approval_required_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether managerial approval was required for the return (bit).
approval_required_flag.analysis_use: Filter and group to measure how many returns require approval and the associated refund exposure. Evaluate approval frequency by operator, SKU, and reason.
business_questions: What share of refunds required approval and how much money did those represent?
Which SKUs or reasons most often require approval?
approval_required_flag.nl2sql_hints: Bit type — queries will typically use = 1 or = 0. Account for NULLs (explicitly check IS NULL if relevant). Allow natural language phrases like 'requires approval' -> approval_required_flag = 1.

restock_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the returned item was restocked into inventory (bit).
restock_flag.analysis_use: Measure inventory impact of returns, differentiate refunds that result in restock vs those that do not (e.g., damaged). Use to compute net inventory adjustments and cost of returns.
business_questions: What proportion of returned units were restocked vs not restocked?
Do certain reasons or SKUs have lower restock rates (e.g., damaged goods)?
restock_flag.nl2sql_hints: Bit type — treat as boolean in NL2SQL (restocked / not restocked). Consider NULL semantics. Join with product_inventory to adjust quantities only for restock_flag = 1.

created_at.description: Datetime2 indicating when this record was created in the ETL/warehouse (reliable datetime type).
created_at.analysis_use: Primary ingestion/event timestamp for time-based analysis when return_ts is missing/invalid. Use for incremental ETL, watermarking, and temporal grouping (day/week/month).
business_questions: How many returns were recorded/created per day over the last month?
What is the trend of returns over time (weekly/monthly)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 — use DATEPART/CAST/CONVERT for grouping by date, week or month. Prefer created_at where return_ts parsing is unreliable. Support time-range phrases in NL2SQL (e.g., 'last 7 days' -> created_at >= DATEADD(day, -7, GETDATE())).

date.description: The day for which the sales aggregates are reported (datetime2, stored at midnight). Acts as the time dimension for the aggregated metrics.
date.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filtering key for time-series analysis. Used in date-range filters, truncation (to month/week), and window functions (lags, moving averages).
business_questions: What are daily/weekly/monthly sales trends for a store?
Which dates had anomalously high discounts or tax amounts?
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a date/datetime when parsing natural language (support 'between', 'last N days', 'month', 'year to date'). Use date truncation functions (e.g., CONVERT(date,...), CAST(... AS date), DATEFROMPARTS, or SQL Server's DATEADD/DATEDIFF) for grouping. Use inclusive/exclusive semantics explicitly when generating BETWEEN queries. Because primary key includes date, prefer filtering on date ranges to limit scans.

store_id.description: Identifier for the store reporting the daily metrics (nvarchar(50); three distinct values in this dataset).
store_id.analysis_use: Dimension for grouping and filtering. Commonly used to filter to one or many stores or to group for comparisons and ranking.
business_questions: Which store generated the highest net sales in a given period?
How do total bills compare across stores on a specific date?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Use exact matches or IN lists for multi-store queries. Map common synonyms (store, outlet, location). Normalize casing only if source is inconsistent. Use this as an equality filter to leverage composite primary key for efficient retrieval.

total_bills.description: Total number of bills/transactions for that store on that date (stored as decimal(18,4) though logically an integer count).
total_bills.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM, AVG), rate calculations (sales per bill), and trend analysis. Can be used as a denominator for per-bill metrics.
business_questions: What is the average number of bills per day for each store?
What is average gross/net sales per bill by store and date range?
total_bills.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as decimal, treat as integer counts when producing natural language answers. Consider casting/rounding in generated SQL (e.g., CAST(total_bills AS bigint) or ROUND). Handle NULLs (treat as zero) when computing per-bill averages to avoid division by zero.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for that store/day (decimal with high cardinality).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregations and unit-based KPIs (kg per bill, kg per store per period), trend detection, and correlation with sales amounts.
business_questions: How many kilograms were sold by store last week?
What is the average kg sold per bill or per day for each store?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Keep decimal precision when aggregating. Support units in NL2SQL parsing (kg, kilograms). When combining with price metrics, ensure consistent units and consider division by total_bills or other denominators with NULL/zero checks.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales amount for the store on that date before discounts/taxes (decimal).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Key measure for revenue trend analysis, aggregation, comparisons, and as a component when computing net or effective prices.
business_questions: What were gross sales per store last month?
Which date had the highest gross sales across all stores?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Map natural language terms like 'sales', 'revenue' carefully — distinguish between 'gross sales' and 'net sales'. Use SUM(gross_sales) for period totals. Watch for NULLs and cast decimals consistently in expressions.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied on that date for the store (monetary amount as decimal).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregation to compute total discounts, discount rate (discount/gross_sales), and to identify promotional periods or anomalies.
business_questions: How much discount did each store give during the promotion period?
What is the average discount rate (discount/gross sales) by store?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: When computing rates, guard against division by zero (gross_sales might be zero). Normalize phrasing like 'discount', 'promotional discount', or 'coupon' to this column. Use COALESCE(discount_amount,0) in generated SQL.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for that store on that date (decimal).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate for tax reporting, compute tax rates (tax/gross_sales), and reconcile with accounting systems.
business_questions: What was the total tax collected by store last quarter?
What is the effective tax rate (tax/gross_sales) over time?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Treat as monetary decimal. Consider localization words like 'VAT', 'GST', 'tax'. Use defensive SQL (COALESCE) and ensure correct numeric precision in computed ratios. Combine with date filters for tax-period queries.

net_sales.description: Final net sales amount for the store on that date (likely gross minus discounts plus tax or pre-calculated net metric).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary KPI for revenue/earnings analyses, ranking, trends, and dashboards. Use SUM(net_sales) and AVG(net_sales) across periods or stores.
business_questions: What is net revenue by store and by month?
Which store contributed the most net sales year-to-date?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Do not assume net_sales calculation formula—it's precomputed; avoid recomputing unless explicitly requested. Use direct aggregation SUM(net_sales) for totals. When NL asks for 'revenue', clarify whether it means gross or net. Handle NULLs with COALESCE(net_sales,0).

created_at.description: ETL/record creation timestamp (datetime2). In this dataset it is constant, indicating an ETL or load timestamp rather than row-level event time.
created_at.analysis_use: Primarily metadata for data freshness and ETL monitoring. Generally not used for business aggregation; may be used to filter data loads or identify last update.
business_questions: When was this daily-sales dataset last refreshed?
Was a particular day's data reloaded after a certain timestamp?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Treat as metadata. When NL queries ask about data freshness use MAX(created_at). Be aware that created_at may not vary per row — check distinct values before generating predicates. Avoid joining on created_at unless explicitly required.

year.description: Calendar year for the monthly aggregation (e.g., 2024). Part of the composite key identifying the row.
year.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filtering dimension for time-based analysis. Use together with month to filter or group at monthly granularity. Can be used to compute year-over-year if table contained multiple years.
business_questions: What are the monthly sales for each store in 2024?
What is the year-to-date net sales per store for 2024?
year.nl2sql_hints: Type: bigint (integer). Low cardinality (currently only 2024). Always use year + month together for precise monthly filtering. When users ask 'for 2024' ensure the WHERE filters year = 2024. If natural language says 'year-to-date' compute using month <= current_month and year = requested_year.

month.description: Month number (1-12) for the aggregation. Part of the composite key with year and store_id.
month.analysis_use: Used to group and order results within a year (e.g., monthly trends). Combined with year to define time windows, seasonal analyses, and MoM growth calculations.
business_questions: What were net sales by month for STORE_AHD_01?
Which month had the highest gross sales across all stores?
month.nl2sql_hints: Type: bigint. Values represent months 1-12. When natural language references month names (e.g., 'March'), translate to numeric month (3). For ranges like 'Q1' map to months 1-3. Use ORDER BY year, month for chronological results.

store_id.description: Identifier for the store (e.g., STORE_AHD_01). Part of composite key and primary store dimension for analysis.
store_id.analysis_use: Main dimension for slicing and dicing metrics (store-level KPIs, comparisons, filters). Join key to pos_stores to pull store metadata like region or store name.
business_questions: Which store contributed the most to total net sales in June?
How do average bills compare between STORE_SUR_02 and STORE_VAD_03 for 2024?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) (string). Use exact equality for store-specific queries (store_id = 'STORE_AHD_01') or IN lists for multiple stores. Consider joins to pos_stores on store_id; ensure casing and trimming match source. Because store_id is part of the composite primary key, prefer WHERE store_id = ... plus year/month to target single rows.

total_bills.description: Number of bills/receipts issued in the month for the store. Numeric, stored as decimal(18,4) though logically integer counts.
total_bills.analysis_use: Aggregate and average calculations (SUM for totals, AVG per month across stores, compute average ticket value using gross_sales/total_bills). Filter low-activity stores or abnormal bill counts.
business_questions: What is the average number of bills per month for each store?
Which months had unusually high or low bill counts for STORE_VAD_03?
total_bills.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4) but used as a count—cast to integer if needed or use ROUND. Handle NULLs with COALESCE(total_bills, 0) when computing averages or ratios to avoid division by NULL. When computing average ticket, guard against total_bills = 0.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the store-month. Decimal(18,4).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Used for volume analysis, yield per bill (total_qty_kg/total_bills), inventory planning, and price-per-kg computations (gross_sales/total_qty_kg).
business_questions: How much quantity (kg) did STORE_SUR_02 sell in April 2024?
What is the average price per kg by store for the year?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use COALESCE(total_qty_kg, 0) to avoid NULLs in calculations. Be cautious about division by zero when using as denominator. Consider rounding domain-specific outputs to 2 decimals for price-per-kg and 4 or 3 for quantities depending on business needs.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (pre-discount and pre-tax) for the store-month. Decimal(18,4).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Core revenue metric used in SUM and comparisons, basis for computing discounts/taxes as rates (discount_amount/gross_sales). Used in trend analysis, share calculation, and top/bottom store identification.
business_questions: What is the monthly gross sales trend for all stores?
Which store had the largest gross sales decline month-over-month?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use SUM(gross_sales) for rollups. When users ask percent change, compute (current - prior)/prior with NULL guards. Use ROUND(...) when formatting currency; consider casting to DECIMAL(18,2) for presentation. Handle NULLs with COALESCE(gross_sales, 0).

discount_amount.description: Total discount amount given in the month for the store. Decimal(18,4).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Measure of promotion/discount spend; compute discount rate (discount_amount/gross_sales), impact on net sales, and trend analysis. Useful to evaluate promotion effectiveness by comparing lift vs. discount cost.
business_questions: What percentage of gross sales was given as discounts for STORE_AHD_01 in May?
Which month had the highest discount spend for the year for each store?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use COALESCE(discount_amount, 0). When computing rates, ensure gross_sales != 0; apply safe-division patterns. Interpret negative or unexpected discount values carefully (data quality).

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the store-month. Decimal(18,4).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Tax compliance and tax-per-sale analyses; compute tax rate (tax_amount/gross_sales) and monitor anomalies. Useful for reconciling with payments and accounting systems.
business_questions: What was the tax collected by store in July 2024?
Is the effective tax rate stable month-to-month for each store?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use COALESCE(tax_amount, 0). For ratio computations, check gross_sales > 0. Ensure currency/locale conventions are respected when rounding for display.

net_sales.description: Net sales after discounts and taxes (final revenue) for the store-month. Decimal(18,4).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary profitability/revenue metric for ranking and trend analysis. Summed for YTD/MTD totals, used to compute store contribution and growth metrics.
business_questions: Which store had the highest net sales year-to-date?
What is the month-over-month percentage change in net sales for the chain?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Type: decimal(18,4). Use SUM(net_sales) for aggregates. Prefer net_sales as the canonical revenue metric unless user explicitly asks for gross. Use COALESCE(net_sales, 0). When comparing channels, align on the same granularity (month/year). Consider indexing on (year, month, store_id) for frequent lookups.

created_at.description: ETL or row insertion timestamp indicating when the row was created/last updated in the ETL process. Single distinct value in sample data.
created_at.analysis_use: Primarily metadata for data freshness checks and monitoring ETL runs. Not suitable as a business event timestamp for sales date filtering.
business_questions: When was this monthly summary table last refreshed?
Has the ETL produced new monthly rows since the last run?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2. Do not use created_at as proxy for transaction date — use year/month instead. Use created_at for data quality queries (e.g., find stale months). When NL queries request 'latest data' map to MAX(created_at) or filter by the most recent year/month rather than assuming created_at is meaningful for business time.

year.description: Calendar or fiscal year for the summarized metrics. Stored as a bigint (numeric year value).
year.analysis_use: Filter or group-by key for time-based aggregation (yearly comparisons, year-over-year). Use for joins to monthly/daily tables by bounding their dates to the year. Because it's numeric, queries often use equality or range predicates (e.g., year = 2024 or year BETWEEN 2020 AND 2024).
business_questions: Which stores had the highest net sales in year = 2024?
How do metrics compare year-over-year for a given store (needs multiple year rows)?
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as integer-like column (bigint). Use equality or BETWEEN for year filters. When joining to date-based tables, translate year to date ranges (e.g., date >= '2024-01-01' AND date < '2025-01-01'). Be explicit about fiscal vs calendar year if needed.

store_id.description: Store identifier (nvarchar(50)). Logical primary-key component identifying the retail location.
store_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping key for per-store metrics, ordering/ranking, and joins to store dimension and other POS tables. Use in WHERE clauses to filter specific stores or IN-lists for comparisons.
business_questions: How does STORE_AHD_01 compare to STORE_SUR_02 in net sales for 2024?
What stores exceed a threshold of net sales in the specified year?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Use exact string matching or IN lists. Be mindful of casing and trailing spaces; store_id is nvarchar so consider trimming. Prefer joining with fully qualified pos_stores.store_id. If matching against other systems, canonicalize punctuation/case. If implementing predicates, index lookup on (store_id, year) is optimal.

total_bills.description: Total number of bills/receipts for the store in the year. Stored as decimal(18,4) though semantically an integer count (likely aggregated as SUM of counts).
total_bills.analysis_use: Aggregate metric used to compute average basket value (gross_sales / total_bills) and to analyze transaction volume trends. Useful as denominator for per-bill metrics.
business_questions: What is the average bill value for a store in 2024 (gross_sales / total_bills)?
Which store had the most transactions (bills) in the year?
total_bills.nl2sql_hints: Although decimal, treat as integer count — cast to BIGINT/INT when computing averages or counts to avoid fractional bills. Guard against division by zero when computing per-bill metrics (use NULLIF(total_bills,0)). In NL2SQL generation, suggest explicit CAST(total_bills AS bigint) if integer logic is expected.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the store over the year (decimal(18,4)).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Used to measure volume sold, compute kg per bill (total_qty_kg / total_bills), or kg per monetary unit (total_qty_kg / net_sales). Good for inventory planning and unit economics.
business_questions: What is the average kg sold per bill at STORE_VAD_03 in 2024?
Which store sold the highest total quantity (kg) in the year?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Use as numeric aggregation input; watch units (kg). Use NULLIF for division by zero when deriving ratios. When joining to product-level quantity, ensure consistent units and convert if necessary.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (pre-discount, pre-tax) for the store in the year (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for top-line calculations and to compute discount_rate = discount_amount / gross_sales and tax_rate = tax_amount / gross_sales. Aggregate, rank, and filter by thresholds.
business_questions: Which store had the highest gross sales in 2024?
What is the discount percentage of gross sales for each store?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Monetary decimal; use SUM when computing across multiple rows (if joining to other tables). Protect against division by zero when computing ratios. When comparing across channels, ensure currency consistency and rounding (use ROUND or CAST to desired precision).

discount_amount.description: Total discounts given for the store during the year (decimal(18,4)).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to calculate discount rates, measure promotional spend, and reconcile with gross/net sales. Can be compared across stores or as % of gross to assess discounting behavior.
business_questions: What percentage of gross sales was given as discounts by STORE_SUR_02 in 2024?
Which store increased discount spend the most (requires multiple years)?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Monetary decimal; often used in ratio calculations (discount_amount / gross_sales). Use COALESCE/NULLIF to avoid division errors. Consider generating derived columns (discount_rate) in NL2SQL when requested by user.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected/charged for the store in the year (decimal(18,4)).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute tax rates relative to gross sales and to validate net sales calculations. Useful for tax reporting and reconciliation across regions if stores have different tax regimes.
business_questions: What is the effective tax rate (tax_amount / gross_sales) by store for 2024?
Does net_sales equal gross - discount + tax for each store?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Monetary decimal. When deriving net_sales check formula net = gross - discount + tax. For comparisons across jurisdictions, join to store tax-region dimension to apply correct tax rules.

net_sales.description: Net sales after discounts and taxes for the store in the year (decimal(18,4)). Observed to follow net_sales = gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line sales metric for ranking, trend analysis, share calculations, and KPI dashboards. Use as target metric for comparisons and for percent-of-total calculations.
business_questions: Rank stores by net_sales for the year 2024.
What share of consolidated net sales does each store contribute?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Monetary decimal. Verify consistency with derived calculation (gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount). Use SUM(net_sales) for totals. When users ask for percent-of-total, produce SUM(net_sales) OVER() or SUM(net_sales)/total for ratio.

created_at.description: ETL or row insertion/update timestamp (datetime2) indicating when the row was created/loaded into the table.
created_at.analysis_use: Used for data freshness checks, ETL monitoring, and to filter for the latest load. Not typically used for business period calculations (use year instead). Useful to debug stale or missing year rows.
business_questions: When was the yearly rollup for 2024 loaded for STORE_AHD_01?
Have yearly aggregates been refreshed since a specified date (data freshness)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 type — use standard datetime predicates (>=, <=). For NL2SQL, prefer explicit timezone/format handling if needed. When users ask for the latest data, translate to WHERE created_at = (SELECT MAX(created_at) FROM zuna_etl.pos_sales_yearly) or filter by date range. Do not substitute created_at for year-based time filtering.

shift_id.description: Unique identifier for the shift closure record (string token like 'SHIFTCLS1000').
shift_id.analysis_use: Primarily an identifier for grouping or selecting single-shift records; can be used to join if transactional tables record shift_id. Use as GROUP BY when counting shifts and when fetching details for a specific shift.
business_questions: Show details for shift SHIFTCLS1001.
How many shifts exist in a date range and list their IDs?
shift_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar/text. Exact-match filters (WHERE shift_id = '...') are common. If joining to transactions, confirm whether transactions include shift_id (string) or require matching by terminal and timestamp range. Consider indexing if queries frequently filter by shift_id.

terminal_id.description: Identifier of the POS terminal where the shift occurred (foreign key to pos_terminals).
terminal_id.analysis_use: Common filter and group key for per-terminal aggregation (sum of total_sales, discrepancies). Join key to pos_terminals for terminal/store metadata. Low cardinality — beneficial for GROUP BY and rollups.
business_questions: What is total sales per terminal for the last month?
Which terminals have the highest frequency of cash shortfalls?
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to zuna_etl.pos_terminals. Treat as nvarchar; comparisons should match exact formatting (e.g., 'TERM-001-4'). Because cardinality is low, queries grouping by terminal are efficient but consider indexes on terminal_id if table grows.

user_id.description: POS user/operator identifier who closed the shift (e.g., PU1003).
user_id.analysis_use: Filter or group to compute per-operator metrics (sales per operator, discrepancy rates). Joinable to pos_users for names/roles.
business_questions: Which user generated the highest average total_sales per shift?
Which users have recurring discrepancies in counted_cash?
user_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; join to pos_users using exact match. Low cardinality — consider using in GROUP BY. If user names are needed, join to pos_users; NL2SQL should map 'user' to user_id and be aware of possible synonyms (operator, cashier).

shift_start_ts.description: Shift start timestamp recorded as nvarchar (sample format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
shift_start_ts.analysis_use: Filter by date/time ranges, compute shift duration with shift_end_ts, bucket shifts into days/weeks/months after casting to datetime. Useful for ordering and trend analysis.
business_questions: List all shifts that started on 2025-11-23.
How many morning shifts (start before 12:00) vs evening shifts are there?
shift_start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — cast to DATETIME/DATE in SQL (e.g., CAST(shift_start_ts AS DATETIME) or TRY_CAST) before comparisons, grouping, or date functions. NL2SQL should detect string timestamp format and include CAST/CONVERT in generated SQL. Be defensive: use TRY_CAST or database-specific safe parsing if input may contain malformed values.

shift_end_ts.description: Shift end timestamp recorded as nvarchar (sample format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
shift_end_ts.analysis_use: Used with shift_start_ts to calculate shift duration, to filter shifts that ended in a period, and to detect overnight shifts. Important for reconciling transactions by timestamp windows.
business_questions: Which shifts ended after midnight?
What is the average shift duration for terminal TERM-001-4?
shift_end_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string — cast to DATETIME before use. When computing duration, use TIMESTAMPDIFF or DATETIME subtraction after casting. NL2SQL should generate safe casts and handle time-zone considerations if relevant.

total_sales.description: Total monetary sales recorded for the shift (decimal).
total_sales.analysis_use: Primary numeric measure for aggregation (SUM, AVG), ranking shifts/terminals/users, and trend analysis. Useful as denominator for ratios (refund rate).
business_questions: What are the top 10 shifts by total_sales in the last quarter?
What is the average total_sales per shift by terminal?
total_sales.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4) — use SUM(total_sales), AVG(total_sales). Watch for nulls; include COALESCE(total_sales,0) when summing across possibly missing values. When joining to transaction-level data, ensure not double-counting if totals are derived there as well.

cash_collected.description: Amount of cash recorded as collected during the shift (decimal).
cash_collected.analysis_use: Aggregate and compare with expected_cash and counted_cash to detect cash handling issues. Group by terminal/user to identify patterns of cash intake. Component of total_sales breakdown.
business_questions: How much cash did terminal TERM-001-2 collect last week?
Which users collect the largest cash volumes per shift?
cash_collected.nl2sql_hints: decimal — use SUM/AVG. Expect correlation with expected_cash; create computed columns like (counted_cash - expected_cash) or (expected_cash - counted_cash). Use COALESCE for null safety.

card_collected.description: Amount collected via card payments during the shift (decimal).
card_collected.analysis_use: Used in payment mix analysis (card vs cash), aggregate reporting, and reconciliation with payment processor data.
business_questions: What percentage of total_sales was card_collected by terminal over the last month?
Which shifts had card_collected > 1000?
card_collected.nl2sql_hints: decimal — use SUM(card_collected) and compute ratios (SUM(card_collected)/SUM(total_sales)). Handle nulls with COALESCE. If comparing to external payment logs, join using terminal and timestamp windows.

refunds_amt.description: Total refund amount processed during the shift (decimal).
refunds_amt.analysis_use: Measure of returns exposure; used to calculate net sales (total_sales - refunds_amt), refund rates, and to spot shifts with unusually high refunds.
business_questions: Which shifts had refunds_amt greater than 5% of total_sales?
What is the total refunds_amt per store for the month?
refunds_amt.nl2sql_hints: decimal — use SUM(refunds_amt) and compute ratios to total_sales. Watch for potential nulls. For outlier detection, sort by refunds_amt / NULLIF(total_sales,0).

expected_cash.description: Calculated amount of cash expected in the till at shift close (decimal), likely derived from sales/cash transactions and refunds.
expected_cash.analysis_use: Key reconciliation value — used with counted_cash to compute short/over amounts. Aggregate by terminal/user to identify recurring cash variance issues.
business_questions: Which shifts have expected_cash differing from counted_cash, and by how much?
What is the average expected_cash per shift by terminal?
expected_cash.nl2sql_hints: decimal — expect formulas like expected_cash = cash_collected ± adjustments. Use (expected_cash - counted_cash) or ABS() to find magnitude of discrepancies. Use COALESCE and handle decimal precision in comparisons.

counted_cash.description: Actual cash counted in the till at shift close (decimal).
counted_cash.analysis_use: Used to compute discrepancies (counted_cash - expected_cash). Critical for auditing and identifying cash handling issues per shift/user/terminal.
business_questions: List shifts where counted_cash < expected_cash (cash shortfall).
What is the total cash over/short per user this month?
counted_cash.nl2sql_hints: decimal — compute discrepancy columns: (counted_cash - expected_cash) AS cash_variance. Use WHERE counted_cash IS NOT NULL when computing variance; apply COALESCE to avoid null propagation. Consider indexing on terminal_id and date range rather than on counted_cash.

discrepancies_note.description: Intended to capture notes about discrepancies but currently defined as decimal and contains 100% nulls in sample; likely a schema mismatch (should be text) or unused.
discrepancies_note.analysis_use: If populated as text, it would be used for qualitative explanations of over/short incidents, searchable for keywords (theft, drawer jam, reconciliation). As currently numeric and null, it provides no analytical value.
business_questions: Show notes for shifts with cash variance greater than 100.
How many discrepancy notes mention 'counting error' (if stored as text)?
discrepancies_note.nl2sql_hints: Schema issue: currently decimal but semantically should be varchar/text. NL2SQL should not assume numeric operations on this column. If present as text in production, use LIKE/ILIKE for keyword searches. If numeric, confirm intended meaning before generating arithmetic operations. Consider recommending a DDL migration to VARCHAR and populate from source.

sync_id.description: Unique identifier for a synchronization attempt (primary key).
sync_id.analysis_use: Used as the unique row key; for counting distinct syncs, linking to other logs that store sync_id, deduplication and detailed drilldowns.
business_questions: How many unique sync attempts occurred in a time window?
Show details for a specific sync (errors, counts, timestamps).
sync_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (nvarchar). Use exact equality or IN lists. Avoid using LIKE for equality. It's unique so GROUP BY sync_id is typically unnecessary except for deduplication checks.

terminal_id.description: Identifier of the POS terminal that initiated the sync.
terminal_id.analysis_use: Filter/group by to compute per-terminal success/failure rates, aggregate records_sent/received per terminal, identify problematic terminals and trend analysis by terminal.
business_questions: Which terminal has the highest failure rate?
What is the average records_sent per terminal over the last 30 days?
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Column is nvarchar and low cardinality; use equality and GROUP BY. Prefer joining to pos_terminals or pos_stores on terminal_id for descriptive metadata. Normalize casing/whitespace if data is inconsistent.

sync_ts.description: Timestamp of the sync attempt as stored (nvarchar containing datetime-like string).
sync_ts.analysis_use: Primary temporal field for ordering sync events; used for time-range filters, windowing and trend analysis after casting to datetime. Can compute frequency or rates per time unit once normalized.
business_questions: How many syncs occurred per hour/day?
Are there daily patterns in sync failures (time-of-day hotspots)?
sync_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; cast to datetime (e.g., TRY_CONVERT(datetime2, sync_ts, 120) or appropriate format) before comparisons and aggregations. Handle NULLs and malformed values with TRY_ functions. Use created_at as fallback if sync_ts missing or unreliable.

sync_status.description: Outcome of the sync attempt (e.g., SUCCESS or FAILED).
sync_status.analysis_use: Filter/group to compute success rates, trend failures, and to join with retry_count or error_message for root-cause analysis.
business_questions: What percent of syncs are SUCCESS vs FAILED over the last month?
For FAILED syncs, what are the common retry_count values or errors?
sync_status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string. Use equality (WHERE sync_status = 'FAILED') or GROUP BY for aggregated counts. Consider normalizing values/casing. Use as join/filter key for diagnostic queries.

records_sent.description: Number of records sent from the terminal in the sync (bigint).
records_sent.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX) to understand throughput per sync/terminal and detect anomalous volumes or missing data.
business_questions: What is the average number of records sent per successful sync?
Are there syncs with unusually high or low records_sent that need investigation?
records_sent.nl2sql_hints: Numeric type (bigint). Use SUM/AVG, GROUP BY terminal_id or date buckets. Watch for NULLs; coalesce to 0 if semantic. Use WHERE records_sent IS NOT NULL for accurate aggregates.

records_received.description: Number of records the receiver acknowledged/processed (bigint).
records_received.analysis_use: Compare with records_sent to compute loss/round-trip discrepancies, compute success ratios per sync, and as a quality metric.
business_questions: What is the average ratio of records_received to records_sent?
Which syncs show discrepancies between sent and received counts?
records_received.nl2sql_hints: Numeric (bigint). Use arithmetic (records_received/records_sent) with NULL and zero guards. Cast to decimal for ratio calculations to avoid integer division truncation.

error_message.description: Short error text explaining failure reason (nullable).
error_message.analysis_use: Aggregate by error_message to find frequent causes, filter to inspect failed syncs and correlate with retry_count and terminal_id.
business_questions: What are the top error messages causing sync failures?
Are particular terminals consistently reporting the same error message?
error_message.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar with many NULLs; use WHERE error_message IS NOT NULL. Values may be repeated and short; consider normalization (trim, lower) before grouping. For text searches, use LIKE or full-text if supported; be careful with exact matching if messages change slightly.

retry_count.description: Number of retries attempted for this sync (decimal(18,4) but small integer-like values).
retry_count.analysis_use: Distribution analysis, correlate retry_count with success/failure and time to success, detect terminals requiring many retries.
business_questions: What is the distribution of retry_count for failed vs. successful syncs?
How many syncs required more than N retries?
retry_count.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal but values appear integer-like; cast to INT where appropriate (e.g., CAST(retry_count AS INT)). Handle NULLs. Use GROUP BY CAST(retry_count AS INT) for clearer buckets.

last_success_ts.description: Timestamp of the last successful sync for the terminal or sync_id context, stored as nvarchar datetime-like string.
last_success_ts.analysis_use: Identify recency of successful syncs per terminal, compute time since last success (after casting), and detect stale terminals.
business_questions: When was the last successful sync for each terminal?
Which terminals have not synced successfully in the last X hours/days?
last_success_ts.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar containing datetime-like strings. Cast using TRY_CONVERT/TRY_PARSE to datetime/datetime2 before date arithmetic. Use COALESCE with created_at if last_success_ts is NULL. Beware of timezone assumptions — data appears local/timestamp strings without zone.

created_at.description: Record insertion timestamp in datetime2 representing when the log row was created in the system.
created_at.analysis_use: Reliable datetime for ingestion-time based filtering, ordering and retention policies; use for temporal analytics when sync_ts is missing or untrusted.
business_questions: How many sync entries were created per day?
What is the average lag between created_at and sync_ts (after casting)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Proper datetime2 type — use directly for WHERE/ORDER BY/DATE bucket GROUP BY. Prefer created_at for ingestion-time filters. When interpreting sync_ts or last_success_ts, compute and compare to created_at to detect clock skew or late-arriving records.

terminal_id.description: Surrogate/business key identifying the terminal (PK). Example values: TERM-001-1 ...
terminal_id.analysis_use: Primary join key for linking terminal metadata to transactions, shifts and logs. Use for grouping, filtering, and distinct counts of terminals.
business_questions: List all metadata for a given terminal_id.
How many transactions were processed by terminal_id X?
When was terminal_id created and who is assigned to it?
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Data type nvarchar(50) (max len 10 used). Treat as string key in WHERE and JOIN clauses. Use exact equality or IN for sets. Indexing on terminal_id is critical for fast joins (it's PK).

store_id.description: Foreign key linking terminal to the store where it is installed. Values reference pos_stores.store_id.
store_id.analysis_use: Join key to pos_stores for store attributes; group by store to count terminals per store; filter by store for store-scoped analyses.
business_questions: How many terminals are present in each store?
Which terminals belong to STORE_AHD_001?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Use JOIN zuna_etl.pos_stores ON pos_terminals.store_id = pos_stores.store_id. Watch for NULL store_id (nullable). Use GROUP BY store_id for counts. Consider trimming/pattern matching if store_id contains variable formats.

terminal_code.description: Human-readable terminal code often combining store shorthand and terminal label (e.g., STORE_AH-T1).
terminal_code.analysis_use: User-friendly identifier for reports and dashboards; filter or group by terminal_code in addition to terminal_id; useful in UI display and exports.
business_questions: Show terminal_code and device_serial for all active terminals.
Find terminals by terminal_code pattern (e.g., '-T1').
terminal_code.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). May be used with LIKE for partial matches. Prefer terminal_id for deterministic joins; include terminal_code in SELECT for readability. Watch for inconsistent formatting — normalize if necessary.

device_serial.description: Manufacturer serial number of the POS device (hardware identifier).
device_serial.analysis_use: Uniquely identifies hardware for warranty, RMA and device-level tracking; join or filter when reconciling hardware inventory or support tickets.
business_questions: Which terminal_id has serial SN103622?
List terminals with duplicate or missing device_serial (potential data quality issue).
device_serial.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Use equality/IN for lookup. Check for uniqueness; if duplicates exist, investigate. Use string trimming; treat NULL or empty as missing hardware mapping.

pos_software_version.description: Version string of POS software installed on the terminal (e.g., v3.2.1).
pos_software_version.analysis_use: Filter/group to find terminals by version, assess upgrade compliance, or join with release metadata to determine known-bug exposure.
business_questions: Which terminals are not on the latest POS software version?
How many terminals run v3.2.1?
pos_software_version.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Values may be semantic version strings; consider normalizing (major/minor/patch) for comparisons. Because values can be non-numeric, use string operations or parse to components for range queries. Current data shows a single value; watch for future diversity.

assigned_user_id.description: Identifier of the user (operator/technician) assigned responsibility for the terminal.
assigned_user_id.analysis_use: Attribute terminal ownership; group counts of terminals per user; filter by user for operational queries; join to pos_users for details.
business_questions: Which terminals are assigned to PU1001?
How many terminals does each assigned user manage?
assigned_user_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Nullable. Use JOIN to pos_users if available. Use GROUP BY assigned_user_id for counts; expect low cardinality. Watch for reassignments over time (no history in this table).

last_online_ts.description: Timestamp when the terminal was last seen online. Stored as nvarchar but contains datetime-like strings.
last_online_ts.analysis_use: Compute recency/uptime; filter terminals that haven't checked in within a threshold; sort to find most/least recently online. Requires casting to DATETIME/DATETIME2 for time arithmetic.
business_questions: Which terminals have not come online in the last 24 hours?
Show terminals ordered by most recent last_online_ts.
last_online_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar(50) with values like '2025-11-25 12:13:55'. Cast using TRY_CONVERT(datetime2, last_online_ts) or safe parsing in NL2SQL to allow time comparisons. Handle NULLs and malformed strings. Avoid string comparisons for temporal logic.

status.description: Operational state of the terminal (e.g., ACTIVE, INACTIVE, DECOMMISSIONED). Current data shows 'ACTIVE' for all rows.
status.analysis_use: Filter to active terminals for production metrics or to identify decommissioned/inactive devices. Group by status for counts.
business_questions: How many active vs inactive terminals do we have?
List terminals that are not ACTIVE.
status.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50). Use exact matches (e.g., WHERE status = 'ACTIVE'). Normalize case/whitespace if user input may vary. Consider mapping synonyms (e.g., 'active','Active').

location_lat.description: Latitude of the terminal's physical location (decimal with 4 fractional digits).
location_lat.analysis_use: Geospatial plotting, distance calculations, clustering of terminals. Group or filter by lat-long ranges for regional analyses.
business_questions: Plot all terminal locations on a map for a given region.
Which terminals are within X km of a given coordinate?
location_lat.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Use together with location_long for geospatial computations. For distance calculations, cast to float or use spatial functions if database supports geography types. NULL-safe: some terminals may lack coordinates.

location_long.description: Longitude of the terminal's physical location (decimal with 4 fractional digits).
location_long.analysis_use: Paired with location_lat for mapping and geospatial queries; can be used to compute proximity to store or other terminals.
business_questions: Find terminals closest to a given longitude/latitude.
Group terminals by geographic clusters (e.g., city-level bounding boxes).
location_long.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Combined with location_lat for geospatial logic. Consider converting to geography data type for efficient spatial queries and indexing. Beware precision limits with 4 decimal places (~11 m resolution).

created_at.description: Datetime when the terminal record was created (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit when terminals were provisioned; cohort analysis by creation date; filter for recently added terminals.
business_questions: Which terminals were added in the last 30 days?
Show terminal onboarding trend by week/month based on created_at.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2. Use direct datetime comparisons, DATEPART/DATEDIFF for time-based grouping. Consider timezone handling if upstream systems use different zones. Use created_at for temporal joins with provisioning events.

pos_line_id.description: Unique identifier for the transaction line (string). Appears unique for each row in this dataset.
pos_line_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, row-level joins, and line-level grouping. Useful to count distinct lines and to identify or filter specific line items.
business_questions: How many distinct line items were recorded in a period?
Can we retrieve the details for a particular line id for debugging or reconciliation?
pos_line_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string (nvarchar). Although distinct count equals row count, there is no declared PK — consider recommending a primary key constraint. Use exact matches and equality filters (WHERE pos_line_id = '...') rather than LIKE. When generating joins, prefer pos_line_id for direct line-level lookups.

pos_txn_id.description: Identifier of the parent POS transaction for the line (string). Repeats across multiple lines belonging to the same transaction.
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Group by to roll line items into transaction-level metrics (total transaction value, total tax, item count). Join key to transaction header table for store/customer metadata.
business_questions: What is the average number of line items per transaction?
Which transactions contributed the most revenue or tax?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: String type; expect many-to-one relationship to a transactions table. When writing NL2SQL, infer joins to pos_transactions on pos_txn_id. Use GROUP BY pos_txn_id when converting line-level to transaction-level aggregates. Consider indexing pos_txn_id for performance on common joins/filters.

sku.description: Stock-keeping unit identifier for the product sold (string). Low cardinality with a few SKUs making up most rows.
sku.analysis_use: Primary dimension for product-level aggregations: total qty, revenue, average price, tax per SKU. Join to product dimension tables for name/category.
business_questions: Which SKUs are top sellers by quantity and revenue?
What is the average unit price and margin proxies per SKU?
sku.nl2sql_hints: String type; use exact equality for SKU matching. Join on sku to pos_products/products/product_variants. Aggregate queries commonly use GROUP BY sku, ORDER BY SUM(line_total) DESC or SUM(qty) DESC. Because cardinality is small, GROUP BY on sku will be efficient, but consider an index on sku for joins.

qty.description: Quantity sold for the line (decimal). Can be whole numbers in practice but stored as decimal(18,4).
qty.analysis_use: Aggregated (SUM) to compute units sold, average quantity per line (AVG), distribution (COUNT WHERE qty > X). Used in revenue reconstructions (unit_price * qty) and inventory impact calculations.
business_questions: How many units of each SKU were sold in the given period?
What are typical quantities per line (e.g., single-unit vs bulk purchases)?
qty.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal type; use SUM(qty) for totals and AVG(qty) for average. Be explicit in NL2SQL when comparing decimals (e.g., WHERE qty >= 1). When reconstructing totals use qty * unit_price rather than trusting line_total if data quality is a concern.

unit_price.description: Unit selling price applied to the line (decimal).
unit_price.analysis_use: Used to compute revenue per unit, to detect pricing anomalies (compare to product master pricing), and aggregate average unit price by SKU or period.
business_questions: What is the average unit price per SKU and how does it vary over time?
Are there price outliers or incorrect unit prices compared to price lists?
unit_price.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type; common aggregations include AVG(unit_price) and MIN/MAX for outlier detection. Watch for NULLs; ensure explicit casts if joining to price lists with different numeric types. Use filters to exclude zero or negative prices if those indicate errors.

line_total.description: Total monetary amount for the line (decimal), generally qty * unit_price minus discounts plus taxes depending on definition.
line_total.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for sales analytics (SUM(line_total)). Useful for per-line profitability proxies and reconciliation to transaction totals and daily sales snapshots.
business_questions: What is total revenue by SKU / batch / time window?
How do line-level totals aggregate to transaction totals and daily sales?
line_total.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type; prefer SUM(line_total) for revenue aggregations. When validating results, compare SUM(line_total) to SUM(qty * unit_price) and transaction headers for inconsistencies. Be explicit about inclusion/exclusion of tax/discount if business logic requires.

discount_amt.description: Discount applied to the line (bigint). In this dataset the column is constant 0 for all rows.
discount_amt.analysis_use: Used to measure discount impact on revenue, to filter discounted vs non-discounted lines, and to analyze promotion effectiveness. Here it is effectively unused and can be ignored unless future data changes.
business_questions: What is the total discount given in the period by SKU or promotion?
Which transactions or SKUs received the most discount amounts?
discount_amt.nl2sql_hints: Integer type but currently constant zero — NL2SQL should handle this as a possible no-op and still include it in schema for future-proofing. When generating queries, include discount_amt in revenue calculations only if non-zero or flagged by the user. Consider noting to data engineers that a default 0 may be redundant.

tax_amt.description: Tax amount applied to the line (decimal).
tax_amt.analysis_use: Aggregate SUM(tax_amt) for total tax liability, compute average tax per SKU or transaction, and validate tax calculations against tax rules.
business_questions: How much tax was collected per SKU or per day?
Are tax rates applied consistently across SKUs and transactions?
tax_amt.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type; commonly aggregated with SUM(tax_amt). For rate calculations, compute tax_amt / line_total or tax_amt / (unit_price * qty) while guarding for zero denominators. NL2SQL should format decimals appropriately and may need rounding functions for presentation (e.g., ROUND).

variant_info.description: Field intended for variant attributes (stored as decimal in the DDL), but currently entirely NULL in this snapshot.
variant_info.analysis_use: If populated, would be used to disambiguate variants of a SKU (size, color) in product-level analyses. Currently unusable due to 100% NULLs and unexpected numeric type.
business_questions: How do sales vary by variant of a product?
Which variants have higher returns or different pricing?
variant_info.nl2sql_hints: Column type is decimal but content is NULL — NL2SQL should avoid relying on this column. Recommend schema review: variant_info may be better as nvarchar/JSON or a FK to product_variants. If queries reference variant, prefer joining to product_variants via sku+variant key instead.

expiry_date.description: Intended expiry/Best Before date for the batch/product line (datetime). Currently 100% NULL.
expiry_date.analysis_use: When present, enables expiry-based analyses such as proximity-to-expiry sales, spoilage tracking, and batch quality monitoring. Currently cannot be used in analysis due to missing values.
business_questions: How many units sold close to expiry (e.g., within 7 days of expiry)?
Which batches/products had sales after their expiry date?
expiry_date.nl2sql_hints: Datetime type; NL2SQL should include null-safe filters (e.g., WHERE expiry_date IS NOT NULL AND expiry_date BETWEEN ...). Because all values are NULL in this snapshot, queries that reference expiry_date should guard for empty results and not assume populated values.

batch_id.description: Batch identifier associated with the line (string). Highly unique per line in this sample, indicating per-item or small-batch tracking.
batch_id.analysis_use: Traceability and inventory reconciliation: group by batch to see units sold, revenue and tax per batch. Useful for recalls and expiry investigations when combined with inventory/batch master data.
business_questions: Which batches contributed most to revenue or quantity sold?
Are there any batches with anomalous pricing or tax amounts?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: String type; join candidate to inventory or batch master tables (inventory_items, product_inventory). Because batch_id is nearly unique, GROUP BY batch_id may produce many small groups — use filters by product or date to reduce result size. Consider indexing batch_id for traceability queries.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the line was created/ingested (datetime).
created_at.analysis_use: Time-based filtering and grouping (daily/weekly/monthly sales), trend analysis, and temporal joins to snapshots or other time-series tables (pos_sales_daily). Useful for incremental ingestion and change data capture.
business_questions: What is the trend of sales/revenue over time (daily/weekly/monthly)?
Which time windows have the highest sales volumes (hour of day / day of week)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime type; NL2SQL should support time-range filters (WHERE created_at BETWEEN ...), date truncation functions (CAST/CONVERT or DATEPART) for grouping, and timezone considerations if cross-system. Recommend indexing created_at for time-window queries. Handle NULLs explicitly if present.

pos_txn_id.description: Unique identifier for the POS transaction (header level).
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for joins to line-level tables (pos_transaction_lines), deduplication, counting distinct transactions, and grouping by transaction for session-based analytics.
business_questions: How many unique transactions occurred in a given period?
Which transactions should be investigated for reconciliation differences vs. line-level totals?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as the natural primary key when present; expect NVARCHAR values (string comparisons). Use exact equality joins on pos_txn_id. If generating time filters, cast other fields (txn_ts/created_at) instead of pos_txn_id. Consider indexing this column; if guaranteed unique, set as primary key in DDL.

terminal_id.description: Identifier of the POS terminal where the transaction originated.
terminal_id.analysis_use: Group and filter for terminal-level performance, join to pos_terminals to get store/location attributes, and detect terminal-specific anomalies.
business_questions: Which terminals have the highest sales volume?
Are there terminals with unusually high tax or failed syncs?
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality string (NVARCHAR). This is a foreign key to pos_terminals. Use joins on terminal_id to enrich with store metadata. Indexing/partitioning on terminal_id is useful for terminal-based queries and filters.

pos_user_id.description: Identifier for the cashier / POS user who processed the transaction.
pos_user_id.analysis_use: Aggregate sales by cashier, compute productivity metrics (transactions / revenue per cashier), and detect user-specific patterns.
business_questions: What is total sales and average ticket per cashier?
Which cashier handled the most transactions during a shift?
pos_user_id.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR, low cardinality. Joinable to pos_users for user metadata. Use as GROUP BY or filter. Consider indexing if many queries filter by pos_user_id.

txn_ts.description: POS transaction timestamp as string (NVARCHAR); likely when transaction occurred.
txn_ts.analysis_use: Primary time field for POS event time-based analysis (hourly/daily aggregations) after casting to a datetime type. Useful for sessionization and TTL filters.
business_questions: How many transactions happened per hour/day?
What is the hourly distribution of sales and payment modes?
txn_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as NVARCHAR — critical to CAST/CONVERT to DATETIME/DATE for correct time-based filtering, grouping and ordering. Be aware of format consistency (observed format 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss'). Prefer created_at if txn_ts quality is uncertain. Provide explicit CAST in generated SQL (e.g., TRY_CAST(txn_ts AS datetime2)).

txn_type.description: Type of transaction (e.g., SALE, REFUND). In sample data it's always 'SALE'.
txn_type.analysis_use: Filter by transaction type to separate sales, refunds, or other flows; compute net revenue when combined with lines or sign logic.
business_questions: What proportion of transactions are sales vs refunds?
How do sales vs refunds impact net revenue by period?
txn_type.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR; values may be low-cardinality. Check for constant values in the dataset. Use equality filters. When computing net amounts, be careful to interpret types (refunds may require subtracting amounts).

txn_total.description: Total monetary amount for the transaction (decimal(18,4)).
txn_total.analysis_use: Primary measure for revenue and financial KPIs (SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX). Base metric for per-transaction analytics, reconciliation with line totals and daily aggregations.
business_questions: What is total revenue and average transaction value over a period?
What is the distribution of transaction sizes (min/max/median)?
txn_total.nl2sql_hints: Decimal numeric type — safe for aggregation. When combining with txn_type, apply sign rules if negative values or refunds exist. Check for NULLs. For NL2SQL, use SUM(txn_total) and CAST as needed for formatting.

tax_total.description: Total tax amount applied to the transaction (decimal(18,4)).
tax_total.analysis_use: Aggregate tax collected by period/store/payment mode; verify tax calculations vs expected tax rates; reconcile with accounting.
business_questions: How much tax was collected by store/terminal/period?
Is the tax/sales ratio within expected thresholds?
tax_total.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type. Summarize with SUM(tax_total). When estimating tax rates, compute tax_total / txn_total but guard against division by zero and NULLs.

discount_total.description: Total discount applied to the transaction (decimal). In this sample it is always 0.0000.
discount_total.analysis_use: Measure promotional impact, compute net revenue after discounts, detect whether discounts are being applied at transaction level or only at line level.
business_questions: What is total discount given by period/store?
Which transactions or promotions led to the largest discounts?
discount_total.nl2sql_hints: Decimal, constant zero in sample — might be populated at scale or left for line-level discounts. Consider checking pos_transaction_lines for discounts. If always zero, queries can ignore or exclude the column; confirm with full dataset before optimizing.

payment_mode.description: Mode of payment (e.g., CASH, UPI, CARD).
payment_mode.analysis_use: Break down revenue by payment instrument, compute counts and amounts by payment_mode, detect payment processing issues and trends.
business_questions: How much revenue is collected via each payment mode?
Has the share of digital payments increased over time?
payment_mode.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR, low cardinality. Use for GROUP BY and filtering. Watch out for value normalization (e.g., 'CARD' vs 'CREDIT_CARD'); consider mapping synonyms. Indexing helps when frequently filtered.

external_txn_ref.description: External/payment processor reference or id for the transaction (string).
external_txn_ref.analysis_use: Correlation with external payment gateway records, reconciliation, fraud investigation and deduplication between systems.
business_questions: Which transactions failed or succeeded at the external processor?
Are there duplicate external refs indicating reconciliation issues?
external_txn_ref.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR potentially unique per external system. Use equality joins with external payment logs. May require normalization/trimming. Watch for NULLs or repeated values across refunds/voids.

shift_id.description: Identifier for the work shift during which the transaction occurred (e.g., SHIFT1).
shift_id.analysis_use: Aggregate sales by shift to understand shift-level performance, staffing optimization and shift-based anomalies.
business_questions: Which shift generates the highest sales and number of transactions?
Are there shifts with unusual tax or average transaction values?
shift_id.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR low-cardinality. Use as GROUP BY or filter. Validate shift definitions against shift scheduling system if available. Map to shift start/end times if needed for time-range queries.

cashier_note.description: Free form or numeric note associated with cashier (column is decimal and currently 100% NULL in sample).
cashier_note.analysis_use: Currently unusable in sample. If populated, could store short numeric codes or amounts; otherwise consider deprecating or converting to a textual notes column.
business_questions: Are there manual notes associated to transactions that require follow-up?
Are any non-null cashier notes correlated with transaction exceptions?
cashier_note.nl2sql_hints: Column typed decimal but appears intended for textual notes — schema mismatch. For NL2SQL, treat as nullable; avoid using in text searches. Recommend reviewing DDL: change to NVARCHAR if notes are textual, or remove if unused.

status.analysis_use: Filter out incomplete/voided/failed transactions; compute counts of completion rates; monitor for transaction lifecycle issues.
business_questions: How many transactions are completed vs voided or pending?
Are there stores or terminals with higher rates of non-completed transactions?
status.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR. In sample it's constant 'COMPLETED'. Always include status filter if business rules require only completed transactions (e.g., WHERE status = 'COMPLETED'). Be tolerant to variations in text (case, whitespace).

created_at.description: Datetime when the record was created in the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Reliable ingestion/ETL timestamp for freshness checks, time-based partitioning, ordering and auditing. Use for time-range filters when txn_ts is unreliable.
business_questions: When were transactions ingested into the system (latency/ETL delays)?
How many transactions were created in a given day/hour for operational dashboards?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime2 — preferred for time operations. Use for WHERE created_at BETWEEN X AND Y, GROUP BY CAST(created_at AS DATE) etc. For NL2SQL, prefer created_at if txn_ts is text or malformed.

synced_flag.description: Boolean indicating whether the transaction has been synced to downstream systems (bit).
synced_flag.analysis_use: Filter to show only synced or unsynced transactions for reconciliation and ETL health monitoring.
business_questions: How many transactions remain unsynced to downstream systems?
Are there patterns (by terminal or date) in unsynced transactions?
synced_flag.nl2sql_hints: Bit type — use WHERE synced_flag = 0/1 or WHERE synced_flag = CAST(1 AS bit). In NL2SQL grammars, map natural language phrases like 'unsynced' to synced_flag = 0. Remember sample shows all True; confirm variability in full dataset.

pos_user_id.description: Unique identifier for a POS user (alphanumeric id like 'PU1001').
pos_user_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for joins to transactional and shift tables; grouping and counting users; filtering specific user(s).
business_questions: How many transactions did pos_user_id = 'PU1001' process in the last week?
List all shifts and transactions attributed to a given pos_user_id.
pos_user_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50); values are short strings. Use exact equality for lookups and joins. Watch for leading/trailing whitespace and case sensitivity depending on DB collation. Consider indexing pos_user_id in production for frequent joins.

name.description: Human readable name of the POS user.
name.analysis_use: Display/label in reports; grouping Aggregation by name (useful in small datasets); de-duplication when name is used with other keys.
business_questions: Show the full names of users who have not logged in for 30 days.
Which named users are Supervisors vs Cashiers?
name.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Avoid using name as a join key — prefer pos_user_id. Expect variations in formatting; consider trimming and normalization for matching.

role.description: User role or job function at POS (e.g., Cashier, Supervisor).
role.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering to compute role-level metrics, headcount by role, and role-based access checks in queries.
business_questions: How many Supervisors and Cashiers exist per store?
Are Supervisors logging in more frequently than Cashiers?
role.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50) but low cardinality. Use equality/IN filters; consider normalizing roles or mapping synonyms. Good candidate for a small lookup table in schema for integrity.

phone.description: Contact phone number for the POS user, stored as string (includes country code).
phone.analysis_use: Used for contact lists, deduplication, and verification. Can be used to detect multiple accounts with same phone.
business_questions: Which users have missing or invalid phone numbers?
Are there multiple pos_user_id accounts tied to the same phone number?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Treat as string (no numeric aggregation). Normalize formatting when searching (strip spaces, punctuation). May be unique; consider applying uniqueness constraint if appropriate.

email.description: User email address used for notifications and identification.
email.analysis_use: Filtering and contact lists; deduplication; potential login/notification target. Useful to join with audit systems storing user email.
business_questions: List users with a corporate email address vs generic domains.
How many active users have not verified or provided an email?
email.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Use LIKE for domain-based filtering (e.g., '%@example.com'). Email should not be used as primary join key if pos_user_id exists. Beware of case sensitivity depending on collation.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store where the POS user is assigned (e.g., 'STORE_AHD_001').
store_id.analysis_use: Critical for grouping users by store, filtering by store, and joining to pos_stores or sales tables for store-level analysis.
business_questions: Which users are assigned to STORE_RJT_001?
How many users per store are active vs inactive?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(50). Use exact equality for joins to pos_stores. Be mindful that users may move stores — consider historical store assignment table if needed. Normalize store_id values before joins.

active_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the user account is active (bit).
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter active vs inactive users; compute active headcount; exclude inactive users from operational reports.
business_questions: How many active POS users exist across all stores?
Which inactive users have recent last_login timestamps (possible reactivation)?
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Type: bit (nullable). Treat NULL as inactive or handle explicitly. Use simple WHERE active_flag = 1 for filtering. Consider adding NOT NULL with default in production to avoid ambiguity.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the POS user record was created (onboarded).
created_at.analysis_use: Time-series analysis for onboarding trends, cohort analysis, and aging of accounts; filter by date ranges.
business_questions: How many users were created each month in the last year?
What is the onboarding trend per store over time?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2 (nullable). Use range filters and date functions (DATEPART/CAST to date) for grouping by day/week/month. Indexing on created_at helps time-based reports.

last_login.description: Timestamp of the user's last successful login to the POS system.
last_login.analysis_use: Recency and inactivity calculations (e.g., last_login < now() - X days); identify dormant accounts; correlate last_login with activity-level metrics.
business_questions: Which users have not logged in in the past 90 days?
What is the average days-since-last-login by role or store?
last_login.nl2sql_hints: Type: datetime2 (nullable). Use date arithmetic and ranges. Beware of timezone handling if logins are recorded in different zones. Nulls indicate never-logged-in; handle explicitly in queries.

pin_hash.description: Cryptographic hash of the user's PIN used for authentication (non-reversible).
pin_hash.analysis_use: Authentication verification (compare hashed inputs externally); audit and security checks for duplicate hashes (possible reuse); storage of sensitive credential material (do not display).
business_questions: Are there reused PINs across different users (same hash)?
When was the PIN last changed (requires a PIN audit trail table; not present here)?
pin_hash.nl2sql_hints: Type: nvarchar(96). Sensitive data — avoid returning in plain query results unless necessary and authorized. Do not use pin_hash for joins to other business tables. Treat as opaque string; consider masking in result sets and applying strict access controls.

vendor_id.description: Unique identifier for each vendor (primary key). Alphanumeric code like 'POSV1001'.
vendor_id.analysis_use: Primary join key for connecting vendors to transactional/fact tables; used for exact-match filters, deduplication, listing. Highly selective so ideal for joins and WHERE filters.
business_questions: What are the contact details for vendor 'POSV1003'?
Show sales and orders for vendor POSV1002 (when joined to orders/transactions).
vendor_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as NVARCHAR/text in NL2SQL; use equality filters or IN lists for vendor selection. Exact-match joins should use vendor_id; avoid LIKE unless pattern matching required. Ensure quoting for string literals and consider normalizing casing if comparisons are case-sensitive in DB collation.

vendor_name.analysis_use: Used for display, grouping, fuzzy matching, sorting, and text search; possible aggregation by vendor_name for reporting. Can be used in WHERE with LIKE/full-text for name-based search.
business_questions: List vendors whose name contains 'Bobal'.
Group vendors by name for reporting or export.
vendor_name.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR with variable lengths and punctuation. Support case-insensitive searches and use LIKE '%keyword%' or full-text search for complex name queries. Be careful with commas/special characters in names—escape them as needed.

phone.description: Vendor primary phone number (includes country code and formatting).
phone.analysis_use: Contactability checks, export contact lists, detect missing or malformed numbers, group by area code if normalized, and filter vendors by phone prefix (country/region).
business_questions: Which vendors are missing phone numbers?
Show vendor contact list including phone numbers for a regional sales rep.
phone.nl2sql_hints: Stored as NVARCHAR; expect variable formatting ('+91 7xxxxxxxxx', spaces). Normalize before numeric operations (remove spaces and punctuation) or use LIKE '+91%' for country-code filtering. Use IS NULL/IS NOT NULL for missing-value checks.

email.description: Vendor contact email address used for notifications and billing.
email.analysis_use: Used for contact lists, validation checks (missing/invalid), deduplication, and search by domain. Useful for integrations and automated notifications.
business_questions: Which vendors do not have an email on file?
List vendors with emails at a particular domain (e.g., '@vendor.in').
email.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR; watch for punctuation and occasional malformed entries (commas inside addresses). Use LIKE '%@domain' for domain filters, validate with a simple regex pattern in SQL if supported, and treat NULL/empty strings separately.

address.description: Free-form street address (mixed components: street, area, postal code).
address.analysis_use: Display/contacting vendors, basic geographic grouping if parsed, data-quality audits for completeness, and geocoding prior to spatial analyses. Can be used with LIKE for substring matching.
business_questions: Which vendors are located in 'Ramagundam' or with postal code '438508'?
