List full addresses for vendors to generate delivery labels.
address.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR free-form text. For NL2SQL, use LIKE '%keyword%' for partial matches or pattern extraction functions to parse postal codes. Consider normalizing into separate columns (street, city, postal_code) for more precise queries and joins.

city.description: City where the vendor is located (normalized to a modest set of values).
city.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation by city, geographic segmentation, filters for region-specific reports and joins to store/region tables.
business_questions: How many vendors are in Gandhinagar and Anand?
Show vendor distribution by city to plan regional supply coverage.
city.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR with low cardinality—good candidate for GROUP BY and filters. Use exact matches or IN lists (e.g., WHERE city = 'Gandhinagar' or WHERE city IN (...)). Beware of slight spelling variations; consider a normalization/dictionary table for consistent joins.

gst_no.description: Vendor GST (tax) number stored as bigint.
gst_no.analysis_use: Compliance checks, invoice matching, tax reporting, deduplication when combined with other identifiers. Use to join against tax/contract tables if they reference GST numbers.
business_questions: Which vendors are missing a GST number?
Match vendors to tax records or contracts using GST number.
gst_no.nl2sql_hints: Currently bigint — ensure no leading zeros or non-numeric chars exist. For string-like operations (prefix checks, pattern matching), cast to VARCHAR/NVARCHAR. Use IS NULL for missing checks. When exposing to NL2SQL, clarify whether user intends numeric or string comparison; treat as string if matching formatted GST identifiers.

business_type.description: Categorical classification of the vendor's primary business (e.g., Equipment Repair, Hardware).
business_type.analysis_use: Segmentation, grouping, pivoting and filtering for vendor categories, supplier performance by category, and targeted communications.
business_questions: How many vendors are in 'Equipment Repair' vs 'Hardware'?
Show vendors in 'Packaging' to source packaging supplies.
business_type.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR with low cardinality—use GROUP BY or WHERE business_type = '...' or IN lists. Consider mapping synonyms/variants to canonical types for consistent queries. Use exact matches unless supporting fuzzy category matching.

created_at.description: Timestamp of vendor record creation (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Auditing, identifying recently added vendors if values vary, temporal filtering for recently onboarded vendors. Currently constant across rows so not useful until populated properly.
business_questions: Which vendors were created in the last 30 days?
Show vendors onboarded between two dates (if created_at varies).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Datetime type—use DATE/TIME predicates (>, <, BETWEEN). Note: current data shows a single timestamp for all rows (no variance); NL2SQL should handle the case where temporal filters return empty or all rows. Use IS NOT NULL to check presence. When present, prefer parameterized date literals and consider timezone handling.

inventory_id.description: Unique identifier for the inventory record (PK). Likely encodes SKU and centre in its value (e.g., INV-APP009-AHMEDABAD).
inventory_id.analysis_use: Primarily used for unique row identification. Rarely used for aggregation; useful for row-level lookups, deletes/updates and audit. Can be used to infer centre or sku if naming scheme is consistent but better to use structured columns.
business_questions: Show the inventory record details for inventory_id = 'INV-APP009-AHMEDABAD'.
How many inventory records were updated/created for a given inventory_id (join to audit/event logs)?
inventory_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar — exact-match primary-key lookups are common. Avoid parsing semantics from the string; use sku and centre_id columns instead. Use '=' for lookups. If user asks to search by partial patterns, use LIKE on inventory_id but prefer structured columns.

sku.description: Product SKU code; foreign key to products.sku. Low cardinality (10 distinct across 40 rows).
sku.analysis_use: Frequent grouping/filtering key for aggregations (SUM of qty_units, qty_kg, reserved_stock, available_stock). Common join key to products table for descriptive metadata.
business_questions: What is the total available_stock for SKU 'APP009' across all centres?
Which SKUs are below reorder_point across the network?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to zuna_etl.products. Values are short strings; use equality or IN for filtering. When user refers to product names, resolve to SKU via a join. Because sku distinct count is low, group-by on sku will produce compact results.

centre_id.description: Human-readable centre name where inventory is stored (collection/packing/distribution/cold storage). Very low cardinality: 4 centres.
centre_id.analysis_use: Important grouping/filtering dimension for location-level inventory reporting and allocation; useful to compute per-centre totals and to identify centre-specific shortages.
business_questions: Which centres have stockouts or are below reorder points for critical SKUs?
What is the distribution of total qty_kg by centre?
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string with limited distinct values — support quick GROUP BY and equality filters. Consider normalizing to centre_id key in larger datasets but OK as-is for NL2SQL. When user says 'warehouse' or 'distribution centre', map to these strings; provide suggestions/autocomplete if ambiguous.

qty_units.description: Quantity measured in units (integer) present in the inventory record.
qty_units.analysis_use: Primary metric for aggregations (SUM, AVG), comparisons and thresholds. Useful for converting between units and kg when combined with qty_kg. Can be aggregated by SKU/centre to produce total unit stock.
business_questions: What is the total qty_units for SKU 'BRN007' across all centres?
Which SKUs have more than X units available in any centre?
qty_units.nl2sql_hints: Stored as bigint — use numeric aggregations (SUM, AVG). Expect nulls; coalesce to 0 if summing. Use for numeric comparisons in WHERE or HAVING clauses. When user asks for unit-level vs weight-level metrics, clarify which to use (qty_units vs qty_kg).

qty_kg.description: Quantity measured in kilograms (decimal(18,4)) for the inventory record.
qty_kg.analysis_use: Weight-based aggregation and reporting (SUM, AVG) and conversion checks against qty_units. Useful for perishables or when orders are by weight. Enables weight-based replenishment calculations.
business_questions: What is the total qty_kg of perishable products in cold storage?
Which SKUs have less than X kg in total and need urgent replenishment?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Stored as decimal string numeric type — use numeric functions and comparisons. Handle NULLs. When users query weight thresholds or do arithmetic with qty_kg, ensure correct precision. For NL2SQL, if user says 'kgs' or 'kg', map to qty_kg.

reserved_stock.description: Units reserved for orders/allocations and therefore not available for new orders.
reserved_stock.analysis_use: Used to compute available capacity (available_stock often already accounts for reservations) and to measure allocation pressure. Useful for forecasting and allocation conflict detection.
business_questions: How much stock is reserved for SKU 'CAR004' across centres?
Which SKUs have high reserved_stock relative to total qty_units?
reserved_stock.nl2sql_hints: bigint — use in arithmetic (e.g., qty_units - reserved_stock). Verify whether available_stock equals qty_units - reserved_stock; if not, avoid assumptions. When users ask for 'reserved' or 'allocated', map to this column.

available_stock.description: Units currently available for sale/dispatch (after reservations).
available_stock.analysis_use: Primary KPI for replenishment and orderability. Aggregated by SKU/centre for stock availability dashboards. Used in thresholds to trigger reorder (compare with reorder_point).
business_questions: List SKUs with available_stock < reorder_point by centre.
What is the overall available_stock for top-selling SKUs?
available_stock.nl2sql_hints: bigint — use in inequality filters and ORDER BY for top/bottom queries. If discrepancy with qty_units and reserved_stock exists, prefer available_stock for 'sellable' quantity. Use COALESCE when nulls possible.

reorder_point.description: Configured inventory level (units) at which replenishment should be triggered for the record.
reorder_point.analysis_use: Used to detect low-stock conditions (available_stock < reorder_point). Aggregation less common; primarily for row-level threshold checks and alerts.
business_questions: Which inventory records have available_stock below reorder_point and need reorder?
How many SKUs have reorder_point set above average available_stock?
reorder_point.nl2sql_hints: bigint — used in comparisons and boolean predicates. When generating NL2SQL, treat as numeric constant. Users may request 'below reorder point' or 'above reorder point' — translate accordingly. Consider NULL handling: treat NULL as not configured.

lead_time_days.description: Expected supplier/fulfilment lead time in days for replenishing this SKU at this centre. Low cardinality (1-3 days).
lead_time_days.analysis_use: Used with reorder_point and safety_stock to plan reorder timing; used to compute expected arrival dates if combined with order date. Aggregated rarely; used for planning calculations.
business_questions: Which SKUs have lead_time_days >= 3 and are near reorder points?
Show SKUs where lead_time_days is 1 (fast replenishment).
lead_time_days.nl2sql_hints: bigint small-range values — use in arithmetic (lead_time_days + today) when computing expected arrival, or for filtering. If user asks for lead time in business days vs calendar days, NL2SQL cannot convert — clarify. Use equality/inequality filters or GROUP BY for distribution.

safety_stock_kg.description: Safety buffer expressed in kilograms to protect against stockouts. Stored as bigint but name suggests kg.
safety_stock_kg.analysis_use: Used to calculate effective reorder thresholds in weight terms (compare qty_kg to safety_stock_kg). Useful for perishables or weight-based planning.
business_questions: Which SKUs do not meet their safety_stock_kg and need urgent replenishment?
How many kilograms of safety stock are held across all cold-storage centres?
safety_stock_kg.nl2sql_hints: Stored as integer but represents kg — for weight-based logic use qty_kg and safety_stock_kg together. When users request 'safety stock', map to this column and ensure unit alignment. Handle NULLs and unit conversions carefully.

expiry_ts.description: Expiry timestamp as string (nvarchar) — contains datetime-like values; distinct values per row (40).
expiry_ts.analysis_use: Critical for perishables reporting: filter for items expiring within X days, order by expiry for FIFO/FEFO decisions. Must be parsed/cast to a datetime type for date arithmetic.
business_questions: List inventory expiring within the next 7 days.
Which centres have the most kilograms expiring in the next 30 days?
expiry_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — NL2SQL should CAST/CONVERT to datetime when doing date comparisons. Validate format before casting; handle parsing errors. If user queries relative time (e.g., 'next 7 days'), translate to a WHERE clause comparing CAST(expiry_ts AS datetime) BETWEEN CURRENT_DATE AND DATEADD(day, 7, CURRENT_DATE). If casting not possible, return warning.

storage_temp_c.description: Required storage temperature in Celsius (integer). Very low cardinality (10°C and 4°C primarily).
storage_temp_c.analysis_use: Used to filter/group inventory into temperature-controlled buckets (cold vs ambient), allocate to appropriate centres, and compute totals per temperature class.
business_questions: Show all SKUs that require cold storage (storage_temp_c <= 4).
Total qty_kg in cold storage versus ambient storage.
storage_temp_c.nl2sql_hints: bigint but small set of values — use equality or range filters (<= or =). When users say 'cold' or 'ambient', map to appropriate numeric thresholds (e.g., <=4 => cold). Ensure units are integer; handle NULLs as unknown temperature requirements.

price_id.description: Unique identifier for the pricing record (string). Represents a specific price interval/entry.
price_id.analysis_use: Use as a unique row identifier for deduplication and record-level audits; group by price_id when you need row-level details. Useful for joins where specific price record needs referencing.
business_questions: Which price records were created or updated by a given operation or batch (identify by price_id)?
Show full details for price record PR-APP009-1.
price_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar; use equality filters for lookups (WHERE price_id = '...'). If price_id should be unique, NL2SQL should assume single-row result for exact match. When generating UPDATE/DELETE intents, prefer exact matches on price_id. No numeric or datetime casting required.

sku.description: Stock-keeping unit identifier linking price to a product (FK to zuna_etl.products.sku).
sku.analysis_use: Primary column for grouping and filtering (price history per product). Use for joins to product metadata and for aggregations by SKU. Low cardinality — queries filtering by sku are selective if combined with date filters.
business_questions: What are the historical prices for SKU 'APP009'?
Which SKUs have prices above X in the last quarter?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar and the primary join key to products table: JOIN zuna_etl.products p ON product_pricing.sku = p.sku. When users ask for per-product queries, expect to filter on sku. Because sku cardinality is low, combine sku filters with date filters for better selectivity. Beware of trailing/leading whitespace—use TRIM if results seem off.

start_ts.description: Start timestamp for the price interval. Stored as nvarchar but represents a datetime string (e.g., '2024-10-30 10:10:00').
start_ts.analysis_use: Used to filter and determine when a price becomes active; important for temporal queries (active price at a point in time), sorting for price history, and detecting interval overlaps/gaps. Commonly needs to be converted to datetime for range calculations and comparisons.
business_questions: Which prices became effective in November 2024?
What was the first date a price was applied for SKU X?
start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST or TRY_CAST to datetime/datetime2 in generated SQL: e.g., TRY_CAST(start_ts AS datetime2) or CONVERT(datetime2, start_ts, 120) assuming 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MI:SS' format. When writing NL2SQL that filters by date, convert both start_ts and the input literal to datetime. For interval logic use: CAST(start_ts AS datetime2) <= @target AND CAST(end_ts AS datetime2) > @target. Consider handling NULLs or malformed strings with TRY_CAST and fallback logic.

end_ts.description: End timestamp for the price interval. Stored as nvarchar but represents a datetime string; marks when the price stops being effective.
end_ts.analysis_use: Used with start_ts to compute active windows, duration of price intervals, to detect overlaps/gaps, and to filter for prices active within a period.
business_questions: Which prices will expire in the next 30 days?
Are there overlapping pricing intervals for SKU Y?
end_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also requires CAST/TRY_CAST to datetime for comparisons. When querying active prices use both start_ts and end_ts bounds. For open-ended intervals, expect NULL or a distant end date—NL2SQL should handle both. Use interval overlap predicates: NOT (CAST(end_ts AS datetime2) <= @period_start OR CAST(start_ts AS datetime2) >= @period_end).

price_per_unit.description: Monetary price per unit for the SKU for the given interval (decimal(18,4)).
price_per_unit.analysis_use: Primary analytical measure: aggregate (avg/min/max), compute changes between intervals, compute weighted prices when joined with volumes, and detect price anomalies or promotions. Used for filters (e.g., prices > X).
business_questions: What is the average price_per_unit for SKU X over the last 6 months?
Which SKUs had a price_per_unit drop of more than 10% in a month?
price_per_unit.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type — use numeric comparisons and aggregations (AVG/MIN/MAX). When combining with sales tables to compute revenue, be explicit about rounding/scale. When asking for 'current price' at a datetime, ensure the NL2SQL uses the datetime-casted start/end fields to find the active interval before selecting price_per_unit.

currency.description: Currency code for the price (NVARCHAR(50) but values show a single currency 'INR').
currency.analysis_use: Used to validate or filter records by currency; for multi-currency environments it is required to normalize or convert prices for cross-currency analyses. In this dataset it's constant, so little filtering value.
business_questions: Are there any pricing records in currencies other than INR?
Show prices for SKUs where currency != 'INR' (if any).
currency.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar. Because the column is constant in current data, NL2SQL can assume INR by default but should still include currency in generated SQL if user mentions currency. For multi-currency queries, ensure joins to exchange rate tables or conversions are applied outside this table.

min_order_qty.description: Minimum order quantity required for the given price interval (decimal(18,4), but effectively integral values in data).
min_order_qty.analysis_use: Used for filtering and grouping (how many prices require MOQ of 1/5/10), for order policy analytics, and to determine eligibility of a purchase quantity for a price band.
business_questions: How many price records require a minimum order quantity of 10?
For SKU Z, what min_order_qty values have been offered historically?
min_order_qty.nl2sql_hints: Numeric decimal column; treat as integer for business logic though stored as decimal. When answering eligibility questions (e.g., 'can I buy 3 units to get price X?'), NL2SQL should compare requested quantity >= min_order_qty. When grouping by min_order_qty, consider normalizing precision (CAST(min_order_qty AS INT)).

created_by.description: Identifier of the user/system that created the pricing record. Data shows a single value 'pricing_admin'.
created_by.analysis_use: Audit/filtering by creator and provenance. Useful to attribute pricing batches when multiple creators exist. In current dataset it provides little variance but may be important in larger data.
business_questions: Which pricing entries were created by 'pricing_admin' in October 2024?
Show records created by other users (if any) for audit.
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar and a static filter when users ask for creator-based queries. NL2SQL should allow filtering by creator but also tolerate the column being constant. When generating user-facing explanations, mention that all current rows were created by 'pricing_admin'.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the pricing record was created (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Used for auditing, ordering price entries by creation time, and identifying most recent inserts. Useful for detecting late backdated entries when created_at is after start_ts.
business_questions: Which price entries were created in the last 7 days?
Show price records that were backdated (start_ts earlier than created_at).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as datetime2 — no casting required for datetime operations. When comparing created_at with start_ts or end_ts, ensure start_ts/end_ts are cast to datetime2 first. For queries like 'most recent price changes', ORDER BY created_at DESC. When exposing both created_at and start_ts in results, be mindful that start_ts is text and must be converted for proper date arithmetic.

sku.description: Unique product stock-keeping unit identifier for the summarized row. Declared nvarchar(50) but observed max length ~6; this is the primary key for the table.
sku.analysis_use: Used for grouping, exact-match filters, ordering and joins to product master tables and order/line-level tables. Ideal for JOIN predicates and IN-list filters in NL2SQL queries. Also used as label when presenting ranking/leaderboards.
business_questions: Which SKU is our top seller by net_sales?
Show SKU-level metrics for a selected list of SKUs (e.g., ['APP009', 'BRN007']).
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string/key. Use exact equality or IN for filtering; consider case-sensitivity depending on DB collation. Avoid LIKE unless implementing prefix search. Because declared length is larger than actual values, trimming is usually unnecessary but safe to include. Use this column as the primary join key to product master tables.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales amount for the SKU before discounts/fees/taxes (decimal(18,4)).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary measure for revenue analysis: SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN, ranking, percentile. Often used as denominator to compute rates (discount% = discount/gross_sales). Useful in trend/reconciliation when combined with temporal tables.
business_questions: What is the gross sales for SKU APP009?
Rank SKUs by gross_sales to find the top contributors.
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal(18,4) — preserve scale in aggregations; consider COALESCE(gross_sales,0) when summing to avoid null propagation. Watch for division by zero when deriving percentages (guard with NULLIF(gross_sales,0)).

discount.description: Total discount amount applied to sales of the SKU (decimal(18,4)).
discount.analysis_use: Used to compute discount rates (discount/gross_sales), aggregate total discount exposure per SKU or across SKUs, and to identify heavy discounting. Useful for margin and promotion effectiveness analysis.
business_questions: What is the total discount applied to a specific SKU?
Which SKUs have the highest discount as a percentage of gross sales?
discount.nl2sql_hints: Nullable decimal; use COALESCE(discount,0) for sums. When computing rates, guard with NULLIF(gross_sales,0). For NL2SQL, map phrases like 'discount rate' to discount/gross_sales; map 'total discounts' to SUM(discount).

delivery_fee.description: Aggregate delivery or shipping fees collected (decimal(18,4)) tied to the SKU sales.
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Analyzed as a revenue component or cost offset: can be aggregated, used to compute fee-per-unit (if units available elsewhere), or as percent of gross_sales. Helps in evaluating shipping revenue contribution or anomalies.
business_questions: How much delivery fee revenue did SKU COW005 generate?
What percentage of gross sales are delivery fees for each SKU?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Nullable decimal; treat like other monetary metrics. Use COALESCE(delivery_fee,0). NL2SQL should map 'shipping', 'delivery', 'fulfillment fee' phrases to this column where appropriate.

tax.description: Aggregate tax amount collected on sales of the SKU (decimal(18,4)).
tax.analysis_use: Used to compute tax contribution, effective tax rate (tax/gross_sales), and for reconciliation with tax reporting systems. Included in revenue-side analysis when validating reported net sales.
business_questions: What is the total tax collected for SKU BRN007?
What is the effective tax rate (tax as % of gross) by SKU?
tax.nl2sql_hints: Nullable decimal; use COALESCE(tax,0) for aggregates. When asking for 'tax rate' map to tax/gross_sales and guard for zero gross. Clarify whether tax is included in net_sales to avoid misinterpretation.

net_sales.description: Reported net sales amount for the SKU (decimal(18,4)). This is the post-processed revenue figure as stored by the ETL — business definition (how it is derived from gross/discount/delivery/tax) must be confirmed with domain owners.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary KPI for rank/driver analysis (SUM, AVG, TOP N). Used for benchmarking, margin analysis (when combined with cost metrics), and reconciliation against invoices or accounting reports. Also used as the target field when validating ETL transformations.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest net_sales and how do they compare to gross_sales?
Are the net_sales values consistent with gross, discount, delivery_fee and tax (identify mismatches)?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Decimal and nullable — use COALESCE(net_sales,0) in aggregations. Do not assume net_sales is derivable from other columns without business confirmation; NL2SQL should not auto-synthesize net_sales as gross - discount +/- delivery +/- tax. When users ask for 'net', clarify whether they mean this column or a computed net value.

variant_id.description: Surrogate/primary identifier for the product variant (unique string).
variant_id.analysis_use: Primary filter/group key for exact lookups and joins; use for identifying single variant records and de-duplication.
business_questions: What is the full record/details for variant with variant_id = X?
How many transactions reference this specific variant_id across order or inventory tables?
variant_id.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 7 observed) and NOT NULL; use equality filters for best performance (variant_id = 'VAR3001'). Prefer exact match over LIKE. Index or primary key exists — use for point lookups.

sku.description: Product-level SKU that groups multiple variants (nullable). Foreign key to zuna_etl.products.sku.
sku.analysis_use: Join key to product master; group variants by product; filter for all variants under a product.
business_questions: List all variants for product SKU = 'APP009'.
How many variants does each product (sku) have?
sku.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 6 observed). Treat as join key to zuna_etl.products.sku; prefer equality joins. Because sku has limited cardinality, joins followed by aggregations are common. Handle NULLs: some variant rows may have no sku.

variant_name.description: Human-friendly variant label (e.g., Loose, Packed 500g). Low cardinality.
variant_name.analysis_use: Group or filter to separate packaging types (loose vs packed); used in display and product listings.
business_questions: What percentage of variants are 'Loose' vs 'Packed 500g'?
Show price differences between 'Loose' and 'Packed' variants.
variant_name.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with only a few distinct values — good candidate for GROUP BY. Beware of synonyms/case differences (Loose vs loose) and trailing spaces; consider trimming and normalizing strings in NL2SQL generation.

variant_sku.description: Concatenated SKU for the variant (often product SKU plus variant suffix). Appears unique per row.
variant_sku.analysis_use: Exact variant-level identifier for external systems, searching, or joining to variant-specific tables (pricing/inventory). Useful for keyword searches and mappings.
business_questions: Find variant by variant_sku = 'APP009-PACKED500G'.
Which variant_skus have changed price in a given period?
variant_sku.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (pattern like 'SKU-VARIANT'). Support prefix searches (variant_sku LIKE 'APP009%') and exact matches. Consider indexing for fast search. Watch for hyphen/underscore conventions when generating queries.

uom.description: Unit of measure for the variant (e.g., kg, 250g, 500g).
uom.analysis_use: Group or filter by unit type for packaging analytics; convert to canonical units for aggregation (e.g., normalize g to kg).
business_questions: How many variants are sold by weight (kg) versus parcel sizes (250g/500g)?
Show average price per uom type.
uom.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) with few distinct values. NL2SQL should allow normalization (e.g., treat '250g' as 0.25 kg) — may require CASE expressions. Use exact matching or IN lists for filtering (uom IN ('kg','500g')).

units_per_pack.description: Quantity represented by the variant pack (decimal), e.g., 1.0000 or 0.5000.
units_per_pack.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation (AVG, SUM), normalize pricing per unit (price per kg or per pack), filter by pack size.
business_questions: What is the average units_per_pack across variants?
Which variants have units_per_pack <= 0.5?
units_per_pack.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Use numeric comparisons and arithmetic for per-unit price calculations (price_retail/units_per_pack). Watch for NULLs. Consider casting/rounding for presentation.

weight_kg.description: Weight of the variant in kilograms (decimal).
weight_kg.analysis_use: Logistics and shipping calculations, groupings by weight band, compute shipping cost estimates and price-per-kg metrics.
business_questions: What is the total weight if we list one unit of each variant?
Which variants exceed 0.5 kg?
weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Use numeric operators for filters and aggregations. Consistent unit (kg) simplifies queries; if other units appear, normalize prior to aggregation. Handle NULL safely.

barcode.description: Barcode identifier for the variant (string). Likely unique per variant.
barcode.analysis_use: Lookup key for POS/inventory systems; join to scan/event tables; validate duplicate barcodes.
business_questions: Retrieve variant details by barcode = 'BAR1631897'.
Are there any duplicate barcodes across variants?
barcode.nl2sql_hints: nvarchar(50) (max len 10 observed). Use exact matches for lookups. Consider adding an index if POS/barcode lookups are frequent. Trim and normalize leading zeros or formatting differences in NL2SQL mapping.

price_retail.description: Retail selling price for the variant (decimal).
price_retail.analysis_use: Aggregation (AVG, MIN, MAX), price comparison, price-per-unit calculations (price_retail/units_per_pack or /weight_kg), filter for price ranges.
business_questions: What is the average retail price across variants for SKU = 'APP009'?
List variants with retail price above 15.00.
price_retail.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Use numeric comparisons and arithmetic. For currency displays, round to 2 decimals. When computing per-unit price, guard against division by zero or NULL units_per_pack.

price_wholesale.description: Wholesale price for the variant (decimal), used in B2B pricing and margin calculations.
price_wholesale.analysis_use: Compare wholesale vs retail, compute margins and markups, filter by wholesale price bands.
business_questions: What is markup percentage between price_retail and price_wholesale per variant?
Which variants have wholesale price below a given threshold for procurement?
price_wholesale.nl2sql_hints: decimal(18,4). Use arithmetic to compute margins (price_retail - price_wholesale) / price_wholesale. Handle NULL wholesale prices and avoid dividing by zero.

created_at.description: Datetime when the variant record was created (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Time-based filters, trending new variants, incremental ETL watermarking, and aging analyses.
business_questions: How many variants were added in Q4 2023?
List variants created after 2024-01-01.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: datetime2 — use BETWEEN or >=/<= for ranges. Use proper timezone awareness if system integrates data across timezones. Can be used as incremental extraction key if monotonic; ensure indexes on created_at if queried by range frequently.

active_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the variant is active (bit).
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter out inactive variants from catalogs, joins, and reporting; used for lifecycle analyses.
business_questions: Which variants are currently active?
How many inactive variants exist (if any)?
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: bit type — filter with active_flag = 1 or active_flag = 0. In sample data this is all True; NL2SQL generators should still include the flag in filters as default (e.g., WHERE active_flag = 1). Beware of NULL — use IS TRUE/IS FALSE logic if necessary.

sku.description: Unique product identifier (stock keeping unit). Primary key for this table.
sku.analysis_use: Exact-match filter and join key to link product attributes to orders, inventory, pricing, sales and returns. Used for counts, de-duplication and lookups.
business_questions: What are the product attributes for SKU 'APP009'?
Which SKUs appear in recent orders but are missing catalog attributes?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use exact equality or IN lists for filtering; case/collation may affect matches (nvarchar). Prefer SKU for joins to other tables (order_lines, product_pricing, product_inventory). Ensure query uses index on sku (PK) for performance.

product_name.analysis_use: Free-text filters, LIKE or full-text searches; grouping/labeling in reports; display column in results. Useful for fuzzy matching and deduplication checks.
business_questions: Show all products whose name contains 'Apple' or 'Bitter Gourd'.
Which product names have inconsistent naming patterns that require normalization?
product_name.nl2sql_hints: Expect substring searches (LIKE '%term%' or full-text). Watch for parentheses and unit annotations in name—users may phrase queries by unit (e.g., '1kg'). Prefer filtering on subcategory/uom for structured queries to avoid fragile text matches.

product_type.description: High-level product type/category such as 'Vegetable' or 'Fruit'.
product_type.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation (counts, shares) and filter to segment products by type. Low cardinality makes it ideal for GROUP BY and WHERE filters.
business_questions: How many SKUs are vegetables vs fruits?
What is the distribution of shelf life across product types?
product_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality column — use GROUP BY product_type for summaries. Map natural language synonyms (e.g., 'produce' -> product_type = 'Vegetable'/'Fruit').

category.analysis_use: Top-level grouping; in this dataset category is constant. Useful join/label when multiple categories exist in wider schema.
business_questions: List all products in the 'Fresh Produce' category.
Are there products across multiple categories in the catalog?
category.nl2sql_hints: Currently constant across rows—may be redundant for filtering. Still include in SELECT for completeness. NL2SQL systems should treat this as low-importance filter unless schema-wide there are multiple categories.

subcategory.analysis_use: Primary grouping for assortment analysis, stock-keeping, and merchandising. Useful for aggregating counts and joining to taxonomy tables if present.
business_questions: How many SKUs exist per subcategory?
Which subcategories have the shortest average shelf life?
subcategory.nl2sql_hints: Good candidate for GROUP BY. Beware of naming variants—users may query by natural-language subcategory names; consider normalizing or using lower/trim in comparisons.

brand.analysis_use: Brand-level aggregations, filtering and reporting. In current data brand is constant.
business_questions: Which SKUs are from 'Daily Greens'?
How many brands are represented in the catalog?
brand.nl2sql_hints: Low variability here—treat as grouping attribute but expect many datasets to have larger cardinality. If users ask 'by brand' but brand is null/constant, return explanatory note (not available).

uom.description: Unit of measure for the product (e.g., 'kg', '250g', '500g').
uom.analysis_use: Filter and group for packaging/fulfillment logic and conversion calculations. Important for weight normalization and shipping calculations.
business_questions: How many SKUs are sold per kg vs per 250g pack?
Show SKUs available in 500g packaging.
uom.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical; when users ask about weights, consider normalizing between units or use gross/net weight columns where available. Expect synonyms (e.g., 'kilogram' -> 'kg').

gross_weight_kg.description: Gross weight of the packaged product in kilograms (decimal).
gross_weight_kg.analysis_use: Numeric filtering, aggregation (min/max/avg), and calculations for shipping/volumetric rules. Useful to compute total shipment weight by joining with order quantities.
business_questions: Which SKUs have gross weight > 0.75 kg?
What is the average gross weight of items in the 'Vegetable' product_type?
gross_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal type — use numeric comparisons and range filters. Note low distinct values in sample data; queries with equality will match few values. When combining with quantity multiplication, ensure proper casting.

net_weight_kg.analysis_use: Similar to gross_weight_kg — used for pricing per kg computations, promotions, and shipping weight calculations. Good for unit-price normalization.
business_questions: Which SKUs have net weight equal to 1.0 kg?
Calculate price per kg for SKUs when joined to price table.
net_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal comparisons are safe; watch for floating point equality due to scale. Prefer BETWEEN or ROUND for user-facing equality checks (e.g., net_weight_kg BETWEEN 0.9999 and 1.0001).

pack_size.description: Number of units in one pack (bigint).
pack_size.analysis_use: Aggregation for package-level inventory and order fulfillment logic. Multiply by net/gross weight to compute total pack weight.
business_questions: Which products have a pack size greater than 1?
Compute total gross weight per pack for shipping estimates.
pack_size.nl2sql_hints: Integer comparisons and arithmetic. In this dataset pack_size is constant 1 so queries filtering on pack_size > 1 will return none; NL2SQL should handle that gracefully.

hs_code.description: Harmonized System (HS) tariff code for customs (decimal in DDL) — currently all NULL.
hs_code.analysis_use: When populated, used for trade reporting, tax, and customs classification. Not usable in current dataset as it's fully null.
business_questions: List SKUs and their HS codes for export documentation.
Which SKUs share the same HS code?
hs_code.nl2sql_hints: Field is decimal but often stored as string in other systems; treat carefully when matching on prefixes. Because it's 100% NULL here, queries filtering hs_code IS NOT NULL will return no rows. NL2SQL should surface that hs_code is missing if user requests it.

shelf_life_days.description: Shelf life of the product in days (bigint).
shelf_life_days.analysis_use: Filter and group to manage replenishment priorities, aging, promotions and expiry risk. Key for perishable goods planning.
business_questions: Which SKUs have shelf life <= 5 days and need fast turnover?
Average shelf life by subcategory.
shelf_life_days.nl2sql_hints: Integer comparisons (<=, >=). Low distinct values—GROUP BY gives small sets. When users specify windows (e.g., 'less than a week'), translate to numeric threshold (<=7).

storage_temp_c.description: Recommended storage temperature in degrees Celsius (bigint).
storage_temp_c.analysis_use: Filter SKUs by required storage conditions (cold chain vs ambient). Group to determine volume of cold-storage required.
business_questions: Which products require refrigeration (storage_temp_c <= 10)?
How many SKUs need storage at or below a given temperature?
storage_temp_c.nl2sql_hints: Use numeric comparison. Consider mapping terms like 'refrigerated', 'cold' to temperature thresholds in NL2SQL translations. Low-cardinality; GROUP BY storage_temp_c is useful.

is_perishable_flag.description: Boolean flag indicating whether a SKU is perishable (bit).
is_perishable_flag.analysis_use: Primary filter for perishability-related analyses (inventory aging, handling rules, returns). In current dataset all rows are True.
business_questions: Show only perishable products needing special handling.
Count perishable vs non-perishable SKUs in the catalog.
is_perishable_flag.nl2sql_hints: Use boolean checks (is_perishable_flag = 1 / = 0 or IS TRUE/FALSE depending on SQL dialect). If data is constant, inform users results may be trivial. Map natural-language 'perishable' to this flag in NL2SQL parsing.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the product record was created in the ETL/catalog (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Time-based filtering and ordering to find recent adds or historical catalog snapshots. Useful for incremental syncs and change detection.
business_questions: Which products were added in the last 30 days?
Show products created between two dates.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Use proper datetime literals/format in WHERE (e.g., 'YYYY-MM-DD' or parameterized). Support date range queries and functions (DATEPART, >=, BETWEEN). Consider time zone interpretation if user queries 'today'/'last month'.

default_image_url.description: Intended to store product image URL but currently defined as decimal and 100% NULL in data.
default_image_url.analysis_use: When corrected and populated, used for UI rendering and to check missing imagery. Currently unusable.
business_questions: Which SKUs are missing a product image?
Provide default image URLs for products for the product page.
default_image_url.nl2sql_hints: DDL/type mismatch: should be nvarchar/varchar but currently decimal(18,4) and null. NL2SQL should not assume URL content is present. If user requests image URL, return NULL or indicate missing data. Recommend schema correction before relying on this field.

return_id.description: Surrogate primary key identifying each return transaction (nvarchar PK).
return_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping key for any return-level aggregation, used for deduplication and joins to other return-detail tables (if exist).
business_questions: How many unique returns occurred in period X?
Fetch full details for a specific return_id for investigations.
return_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as stable unique identifier in WHERE clauses and JOINs. Treat as string; exact-match equality is appropriate. Consider indexing if table grows. watch for leading/trailing whitespace in string comparisons.

order_id.description: Order identifier (nullable string) that this return is associated with; may correspond to different order tables across channels.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to order tables to enrich returns with customer/channel/order value; group by order_id to detect multiple returns per order; filter to analyze returns for a particular order.
business_questions: Which orders have multiple returns?
What is the total refund associated with a particular order_id?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: NULLs present — use IS NOT NULL when joining. Normalize join path: determine whether to join to b2b_orders, ecom_orders or pos_transactions depending on order_id patterns (prefixes like EORD may indicate channel). Use equality joins on nvarchar; if channel-coded prefixes exist, use LEFT(prefix) or LIKE to route to proper order table.

request_ts.description: Timestamp (stored as nvarchar) when the return request was created/submitted; appears in ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
request_ts.analysis_use: Filter and group returns by request date/time, compute durations (after converting to datetime), trend analyses over time windows and hourly patterns.
business_questions: How many return requests were created each day/week/month?
Are there time-of-day patterns in return requests (peak hours)?
request_ts.nl2sql_hints: Column is nvarchar: convert to datetime for time-based grouping: e.g. TRY_CONVERT(datetime2, request_ts) or CAST when safe. Use explicit timezone handling if needed. Validate format consistency; use NULL-safe conversions (TRY_CONVERT) to avoid errors on malformed values.

reason_code.description: Categorical reason for the return (QUALITY, WRONG_ITEM, NOT_AS_DESCRIBED, DAMAGED).
reason_code.analysis_use: Group/aggregate counts and refund sums by reason_code; filter to analyze specific reason subsets; use as dimension in pivoting.
business_questions: Which reason_code accounts for the highest number of returns?
What is the average refund amount by reason_code?
reason_code.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — ideal for GROUP BY and filtering. Use equality/IN lists. Consider mapping to canonical taxonomy if joining with other channels (ensure consistent values). Handle NULLs (if any) with COALESCE.

requested_items_json.description: Intended to hold JSON with item-level details requested for return, stored as nvarchar but currently contains '[]' for all rows.
requested_items_json.analysis_use: If populated, can be parsed to explode item-level return lines for item-level analyses; currently not useful (empty array) but keep support for future item-level queries.
business_questions: Which SKUs/items are most frequently returned (if item details present)?
How many items were requested to be returned per return transaction?
requested_items_json.nl2sql_hints: Currently always '[]' — avoid parsing until populated. When populated, use JSON functions (JSON_VALUE, OPENJSON) after ensuring column is NVARCHAR(max) or sufficient length. NL2SQL should guard for empty arrays and invalid JSON (use TRY/CATCH or JSON validation functions).

status.description: Lifecycle status of the return request (REQUESTED, APPROVED, REJECTED, COMPLETED).
status.analysis_use: Filter and group to compute counts, rates (approval/rejection), and funnel conversion; time series of statuses; joinable with timelines to compute status transition durations.
business_questions: What is the approval rate of return requests?
How many returns are in each status today/this month?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical, low-cardinality — use in WHERE/GROUP BY. When computing rates, use counts partitioned by status. Be explicit about status semantics (e.g., COMPLETED likely implies processed and refunded). Consider combining status with timestamps to infer state-change times if separate event log not present.

approved_by.description: Identifier/role of the person who approved the return (nullable, two observed values: ops_manager, quality_head).
approved_by.analysis_use: Attribute for approval analytics: approval volumes by approver, average refunds approved by approver, and service-level assessments (who approves fastest). NULL indicates not yet approved or auto flows.
business_questions: Which approver has approved the highest total refund value?
What is the distribution of approvals between ops_manager and quality_head?
approved_by.nl2sql_hints: Nullable — use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL to detect unapproved returns. Standardize values before grouping (trim, lowercase). Treat as string in joins (if you have a users table, map to user/role). Use COALESCE('unapproved') when reporting.

pickup_scheduled_ts.description: Scheduled timestamp for pickup collection (stored as nvarchar ISO-like string).
pickup_scheduled_ts.analysis_use: Used to measure lead time between request and scheduled pickup, workload planning (pickups per day/hour), and to detect missed pickups by comparing with received_at_centre_ts.
business_questions: How many pickups are scheduled per day and are pickup slots overloaded?
What is the average time between request_ts and pickup_scheduled_ts?
pickup_scheduled_ts.nl2sql_hints: Convert to datetime for interval calculations (TRY_CONVERT). Be cautious about NULLs. Use comparisons in WHERE after conversion. For NL2SQL, prefer explicit datetime conversion in generated SQL and fallback when conversion fails.

received_at_centre_ts.description: Timestamp when the returned item(s) were received at processing centre (stored as nvarchar).
received_at_centre_ts.analysis_use: Key for measuring actual transit/processing time (e.g., pickup_scheduled -> received_at_centre), returns processing latency, SLA compliance and time-to-close computations.
business_questions: What is the median time from pickup_scheduled_ts to received_at_centre_ts?
Which returns exceed SLA for receipt at centre?
received_at_centre_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also nvarchar — convert before date arithmetic. Some rows may have received_at_centre_ts later than created_at; validate ordering. Use TRY_CONVERT and filter out NULLs when calculating durations.

refund_amount.description: Monetary refund amount issued for the return (decimal(18,4)).
refund_amount.analysis_use: Primary measure for financial aggregation: sum, avg, count, distribution of refunds; used in revenue leakage and refund exposure analyses and joins with credit_note for reconciliation.
business_questions: What is the total refunded amount per month/channel/reason?
What is the average refund per return and per order?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Treat as numeric for SUM/AVG. Watch for negative/zero values (validate). When joining to credit notes do reconciliation (SUM(refund_amount) vs credit_note amounts). For NL2SQL, map currency context if needed; include CAST only if format mismatch occurs.

credit_note_id.description: Reference identifier to a credit note (nullable string) issued for the return; almost unique per return.
credit_note_id.analysis_use: Join key to credit note/accounting tables to reconcile refunds and accounting entries, verify issuance, and fetch accounting timestamps/status.
business_questions: Which returns have an associated credit note and which do not?
Reconcile refund_amount with the corresponding credit_note value for audit.
credit_note_id.nl2sql_hints: Nullable and near-unique — use LEFT JOIN to credit note table (b2b_credit_notes). Use equality joins on nvarchar. Handle missing credit_note_id with IS NULL check; when matching, trim whitespace and ensure consistent case if needed.

created_at.description: Database/ETL creation timestamp (datetime2) indicating when the record was inserted/recorded in this table.
created_at.analysis_use: Use for ingestion-time audits, incremental extraction (WHERE created_at > last_run), and to filter/partition data by load date. Also useful to detect late-arriving changes vs request_ts.
business_questions: How many return records were ingested per day/week (ETL monitoring)?
Are there late-arriving returns where created_at is much later than request_ts?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Native datetime2 — use directly for time-based filtering and partitioning. When comparing to nvarchar timestamp columns convert those to datetime for consistent comparisons. Use created_at for incremental queries and high-performance filters (indexing recommended).

resolution_notes.description: Intended to hold textual resolution notes, but currently declared as decimal and contains only NULLs — indicates schema/data quality issue.
resolution_notes.analysis_use: When corrected to text, useful for qualitative analysis, exception review and training classification (NLP). As-is it provides no analytical value and likely needs column type correction or population.
business_questions: What reasons or patterns are documented in resolution notes for rejected returns?
Can resolution notes be used to classify root causes for returns?
resolution_notes.nl2sql_hints: Current DDL type is decimal(18,4) but semantics are textual — verify and correct schema to NVARCHAR(MAX) before using in NL2SQL generation. Until fixed, treat as always NULL. NL2SQL should not attempt text operations on this column unless schema corrected.

shipment_id.description: Primary key. Unique identifier for each shipment record (nvarchar up to 50, observed max length 8). Examples: SHIP8001.
shipment_id.analysis_use: Use as unique identifier for row-level operations, de-duplication, joins to shipment-tracking/event tables, or to fetch full shipment details. Not typically aggregated but used to count shipments.
business_questions: How many shipments were generated in a given period?
Which shipment(s) correspond to a specific order or tracking number?
shipment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key; use equality or IN clauses for lookups. Performance: ensure PK/index exists for fast point lookups. Do not attempt numeric operations.

order_id.description: Foreign key linking a shipment to an order in ecom_orders (nvarchar up to 50). Distinct values ~100, nullable.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to order metadata (value, customer, address). Grouping by order to compute shipments per order, join to returns/payments for reconciliation, filter shipments by order attributes (e.g., customer segment).
business_questions: Which shipments correspond to a particular order or customer?
How often are orders split across multiple shipments?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Use for joins to zuna_etl.ecom_orders.order_id. Watch for NULLs if some shipments are not tied to orders. Consider LEFT JOIN when starting from shipments to preserve rows with missing order_id.

carrier_id.description: Identifier/name of carrier handling the shipment (nvarchar). Very low cardinality (3 values observed: LocalCourier, ThirdPartyLogistics, InHouseFleet).
carrier_id.analysis_use: Group and aggregate performance metrics (delivery time, on-time rate, shipping cost) by carrier. Filter for carrier-specific SLA and cost analyses.
business_questions: Which carrier has the best on-time delivery performance?
Which carrier has the lowest average shipping charge per kg?
carrier_id.nl2sql_hints: Categorical grouping recommended. Low cardinality makes GROUP BY efficient. Ensure consistent naming (normalization) — treat values as case-sensitive depending on DB. Carrier_id may be joined to a carriers/partners table if present.

tracking_no.description: Carrier-provided tracking number for the shipment (nvarchar). High cardinality — unique per shipment in practice.
tracking_no.analysis_use: Used for lookups, external tracking reconciliation, linking to tracking-event logs, and as a filter to find a shipment. Not appropriate for aggregation except counts of null/filled tracking numbers.
business_questions: What is the tracking history for a given tracking number?
Which shipments are missing a tracking number?
tracking_no.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; use equality or pattern searches (LIKE) for partial matches. Be mindful of possible duplicates across carriers — consider pairing with carrier_id when joining tracking events.

shipment_date.description: Timestamp when the shipment was dispatched or created (datetime2). Distinct across most rows.
shipment_date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for fulfillment/time-to-ship analyses. Use for temporal grouping (day/week/month), computing transit times (actual_delivery_date - shipment_date), and trend analysis.
business_questions: How many shipments were dispatched per day/week/month?
What is the trend in average dispatch volume over time?
shipment_date.nl2sql_hints: Use as datetime in WHERE (range/filter) and GROUP BY (DATEPART or cast to date). For transit time calculations, prefer DATEDIFF or TIMESTAMPDIFF functions against actual_delivery_date. Beware of timezone normalization if present in other tables.

expected_delivery_date.description: Carrier-expected delivery timestamp (datetime2). Used as SLA target for deliveries.
expected_delivery_date.analysis_use: Compute on-time vs delayed deliveries by comparing with actual_delivery_date; group by expected_delivery_date to analyze SLA fulfillment and forecasting accuracy.
business_questions: What percentage of shipments met the expected delivery date?
Are expected delivery dates becoming less accurate over time?
expected_delivery_date.nl2sql_hints: Compare expected_delivery_date to actual_delivery_date using <=/> operators. When actual_delivery_date is NULL, interpret as 'not delivered yet' (exclude or treat separately). Use cast to date for day-level SLA metrics.

actual_delivery_date.description: Timestamp when the shipment was actually delivered (datetime2). Contains ~41% NULLs indicating pending or missing deliveries.
actual_delivery_date.analysis_use: Key for computing delivery latency, delay (actual - expected), and delivery performance metrics. Filter to identify undelivered shipments (NULL values).
business_questions: What is the average time-to-delivery for delivered shipments?
How many shipments remain undelivered or pending?
actual_delivery_date.nl2sql_hints: Handle NULLs explicitly; use IS NULL for pending/delayed identification. For latency compute DATEDIFF (day/minute) between shipment_date and actual_delivery_date. For on-time flags, compare with expected_delivery_date only when actual_delivery_date IS NOT NULL.

shipment_status.description: Current lifecycle status of the shipment (e.g., OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, IN_TRANSIT, PICKED, DELIVERED). Low cardinality (4 values).
shipment_status.analysis_use: Filter and group to monitor pipeline (how many in each state), identify stuck shipments, and compute status-based KPIs (e.g., percent delivered).
business_questions: How many shipments are currently OUT_FOR_DELIVERY vs IN_TRANSIT?
Which pickup centres have the highest proportion of DELIVERED shipments?
shipment_status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical column; use exact string matches. Consider normalizing status values or mapping to enumerations. Use in WHERE clauses to quickly isolate subsets (e.g., WHERE shipment_status = 'DELIVERED').

pickup_centre_id.description: Name/ID of the pickup or distribution centre that handled the shipment (nvarchar). Very low cardinality observed (e.g., Surat Distribution Centre, Ahmedabad Collection Centre).
pickup_centre_id.analysis_use: Group by pickup centre to compare throughput, average processing-to-shipment time, and local performance. Useful in capacity planning and regional SLA analysis.
business_questions: Which pickup centre processes the most shipments per day?
Does delivery performance differ by pickup centre?
pickup_centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality grouping is efficient. Values may be free-text names — consider joining to a centers reference table if available for standardized IDs. Use in GROUP BY or filters.

package_weight_kg.description: Declared weight of the package in kilograms (decimal(18,4)). Continuous variable with ~99 distinct values.
package_weight_kg.analysis_use: Used for cost-per-kg analysis, binning shipments into weight brackets, regression of shipping_charge against weight, and fraud/validation checks (weight vs order items).
business_questions: How does shipping charge scale with package weight?
What proportion of shipments fall into heavy-weight brackets (> X kg)?
package_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Use numeric comparisons and GROUP BY weight ranges (CASE or buckets). Beware of precision (4 decimal places) when aggregating; cast if needed. Consider creating derived columns for weight bins for faster NL2SQL answers.

package_dimensions_json.description: JSON field intended to store package dimensions (currently observed value '{}' for all rows). Stored as nvarchar(50).
package_dimensions_json.analysis_use: Currently not useful (empty JSON). If populated, could be parsed to extract length/width/height for volumetric weight calculations, dimensional shipping cost calculations, and size-based analytics.
business_questions: What are the dimensional weight and volumetric surcharges by carrier (requires dimensions)?
Are there correlations between package dimensions and damage or returns?
package_dimensions_json.nl2sql_hints: Currently contains empty JSON — treat as missing. If schema evolves to store JSON, use JSON parsing functions (JSON_VALUE/OPENJSON) or ETL to structured columns for efficient queries. NL2SQL should not assume structured subfields exist unless validated.

shipping_charge.description: Monetary charge applied for shipping (decimal(18,4)). Distinct per shipment.
shipping_charge.analysis_use: Aggregate total shipping cost, compute average shipping charge, analyze charge per kg or per carrier, and reconcile charges against invoices or payments.
business_questions: What is the average shipping charge by carrier and by weight bracket?
What is the total shipping expense for a given period?
shipping_charge.nl2sql_hints: Use numeric aggregations (SUM, AVG). When joining to orders to compute % of order value, ensure consistent currency units. Consider rounding/display formatting for result presentation.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the shipment record was created in the system (datetime2).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit/time-tracking dimension for record creation, useful for latency between record creation and shipment_date, and for incremental syncs (CDC). Good for temporal filters (recently created shipments).
business_questions: How long after creation are shipments dispatched (shipment_date - created_at)?
How many shipment records were created in a given time window?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Use as datetime. For incremental fetching, filter on created_at. To compute lags, use DATEDIFF between created_at and shipment_date. Watch for nulls if present (none observed).

last_update.description: Text field capturing last update timestamp or metadata as nvarchar(50) (stored as string timestamps in many rows).
last_update.analysis_use: Used for last-modified audit filtering if cast to datetime. Helpful for incremental syncs or detecting recent status changes, but must be parsed/cast to datetime for temporal operations.
business_questions: Which shipments had status changes within the last X hours/days?
What is the most recent update timestamp for a given shipment?
last_update.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — convert/cast to datetime when performing temporal comparisons (TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to handle malformed values). Prefer using created_at or properly typed timestamp columns for performance; treat last_update as fallback/audit text. When building NL2SQL templates, account for potential parsing errors and advise type conversion.
